<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Software Testing (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Software Testing** is a systematic, planned, and disciplined process of evaluating a software system or its component(s) with the intent to find whether it satisfies the specified requirements (validation) and to identify defects (verification). According to **ANSI/IEEE Standard 1059-1993**, testing is *"the process of analyzing a software item to detect the differences between existing and required conditions (that is, defects/errors) and to evaluate the features of the software item."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** Software Testing is the process of exercising or evaluating a software system or its components by manual or automated means to verify that it satisfies specified requirements OR to identify differences between expected and actual results.

> [!NOTE]
> **Myth Buster:** Testing does NOT prove that a software is defect-free. As per **Dijkstra's famous aphorism**: *"Program testing can be used to show the presence of bugs, but never to show their absence!"* Testing is therefore a *sampling-based risk reduction* activity, not a guarantee.

---

## 1.2 Core Software Testing Terminology (The 7 Golden Terms)

The following terms form the **foundation vocabulary** of KTU's Software Testing paper. They are frequently asked as 3-mark direct questions.

| # | Term | Formal Definition |
|---|------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Error (Mistake)** | A **human action** that produces an incorrect result. It is the *root cause* — committed by developers or users. |
| 2 | **Defect (Bug/Fault)** | An **anomaly** in the software product. The *manifestation* of an error caught by a developer before release. |
| 3 | **Failure** | A **deviation of the software** from its expected delivery or service. It is observed/experienced by the *end user* at runtime. |
| 4 | **Test Case** | A set of **pre-conditions, inputs, actions, expected results, and post-conditions** developed to verify a specific requirement. |
| 5 | **Test Suite** | A **collection of test cases** grouped for execution in a specific testing cycle. |
| 6 | **Test Plan** | A **document** describing the scope, approach, resources, and schedule of intended test activities. |
| 7 | **Test Oracle** | A **mechanism** (manual or automated) that determines whether the system passed or failed a test by comparing actual vs. expected output. |

> [!TIP]
> **Error → Defect → Failure** is the *cause-manifestation-impact* chain. **Error** (developer makes mistake) → **Defect** (bug is present in code) → **Failure** (user encounters the bug during execution).

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### 🏥 The Hospital Diagnosis Analogy
Think of software testing like a **medical diagnostic process** for a patient:

- **Error (Mistake)** = The patient's *unhealthy lifestyle* (root cause — root = bad diet, lack of exercise).
- **Defect (Bug)** = The *symptom observed in the body* (e.g., high blood pressure detected).
- **Failure** = The *heart attack that actually happens* in the field (the real-world impact).
- **Test Case** = A *specific medical test* (e.g., ECG, blood test) with defined input (blood sample) and expected result.
- **Test Plan** = The *overall diagnostic protocol* designed by the doctor.
- **Verification** = Asking *"Did the doctor follow the right diagnostic procedure?"* (Are we checking the patient *correctly*?)
- **Validation** = Asking *"Is this the right patient with the right disease being treated with the right medicine?"* (Are we treating the *right* condition?)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why testing is essential:** A study by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** reports that software bugs cost the U.S. economy approximately **$59.5 billion annually**. Hence, testing is not optional — it is an *engineering insurance policy*.

---

## 1.4 The Need for Testing — Key Justifications

1. **Defect Reduction:** Catching defects early is **10x–100x cheaper** than catching them post-release (based on Boehm's cost-of-defect curve).
2. **Reliability & Trust:** Builds confidence in the software for mission-critical domains (aerospace, healthcare, banking).
3. **Contractual & Legal Compliance:** Many industries (FDA for medical devices, DO-178C for avionics) **mandate** verification and validation.
4. **Quality Assurance (QA):** Testing is the operational arm of the QA process.
5. **Risk Management:** Quantifies residual risk before deployment.

---

## 1.5 Testing as a *Process* — Not an Ad-hoc Activity

KTU emphasizes that testing must follow a **Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC)** with defined phases, deliverables, and exit criteria. The next section unfolds STLC and Methodology in depth.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC) — Phases in Detail

The **Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC)** is a sequence of well-defined activities performed by the testing team to ensure software quality. STLC begins **as soon as requirements are baselined** and runs *in parallel* with the SDLC (or in V-Model, the test phases correspond to development phases).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Frequently Asked:** *"List the phases of STLC and explain any two."* This is a classic 7-mark question pattern.

### The Six Phases of STLC (ISTQB-Standard)

1. **Requirement Analysis (Requirement Phase Testing)**
   - Identify testable requirements.
   - Define types of tests required (functional, non-functional).
   - Deliverable: **Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM)**.

2. **Test Planning**
   - Define test strategy, scope, resources, schedule, entry/exit criteria.
   - Deliverable: **Test Plan Document**.

3. **Test Case Development**
   - Author detailed test cases and test scripts.
   - Prepare test data.
   - Deliverable: **Test Cases, Test Scripts, Test Data**.

4. **Test Environment Setup**
   - Configure hardware/software, network, database, stubs/drivers.
   - Often done in parallel with Test Case Development.

5. **Test Execution**
   - Execute test cases, log defects, retest fixed defects, perform regression.
   - Deliverable: **Test Execution Report, Defect Reports**.

6. **Test Closure**
   - Evaluate cycle completion against exit criteria.
   - Document lessons learned, archive test artifacts.
   - Deliverable: **Test Closure Report**.

### Entry and Exit Criteria (Per Phase)

| STLC Phase | Entry Criteria | Exit Criteria |
|------------|----------------|---------------|
| Requirement Analysis | Requirements Document (SRS) baselined | RTM signed off |
| Test Planning | RTM available | Test Plan approved |
| Test Case Development | Approved Test Plan | Test cases reviewed & signed off |
| Test Environment Setup | Test cases available | Environment ready & smoke test passed |
| Test Execution | Test cases + Environment ready | All tests executed; defects logged |
| Test Closure | All tests executed | Test Closure Report signed off |

---

## 2.2 Software Testing Methodology

A **Testing Methodology** is a *framework* that defines the *strategy, approach, and order* in which testing activities are conducted. The KTU syllabus emphasizes three classical models:

### A. The Waterfall Methodology
Testing happens **only at the end** of the development cycle. **Disadvantage:** Defects found late → expensive to fix.

### B. The V-Model (Verification & Validation Model) — *Most Important for KTU*
The V-Model is an **extension of the waterfall** where each development phase has a **corresponding testing phase**. It visually demonstrates the relationship between development and testing activities.

### C. The Agile / Iterative Methodology
Testing is **continuous**, integrated into each iteration/sprint. Quick feedback loops.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU's Module 1, **V-Model is the centerpiece** — it unifies Verification and Validation in a single elegant diagram (covered in Section 4).

---

## 2.3 Verification vs. Validation (The Core Distinction)

This is the **single most important conceptual pair** in the entire KTU Software Testing syllabus. Examiners love it.

| Aspect | **Verification** | **Validation** |
|--------|------------------|----------------|
| **Definition** | *Are we building the product **right**?* | *Are we building the **right** product?* |
| **Focus** | Internal consistency, adherence to design/specs | Real-world user needs and intended use |
| **Approach** | **Static** (no code execution) | **Dynamic** (code is executed) |
| **Activities** | Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, static analysis | Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance testing |
| **When** | Throughout development, *before* code is run | After code is built, *during/after* execution |
| **Question Answered** | Does the product conform to specifications? | Does the product meet user expectations? |
| **Cost to Fix Defect** | Relatively **low** (caught early) | Relatively **high** (caught late) |
| **Output** | Reviewed documents, signed-off specs | Executed test reports, UAT sign-off |
| **KTU Example** | Inspecting the SRS for completeness | Running the application to see if it works |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "Verification = testing" and "Validation = reviewing." This is the *exact opposite* of the truth. **Verification = Reviews, Validation = Testing (execution).**

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Software Testing Metrics

The following formulas are derived from the **IEEE Standard for Software Test Documentation (829-2008)** and are commonly asked in KTU numerical parts.

| # | Metric | Formula | Purpose |
|---|--------|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $$ DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects Found}}{\text{Size of Module/Software (KLOC)}} $$ | Measures defects per 1000 lines of code. Lower is better. |
| 2 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $$ DRE = \frac{\text{Defects found before release}}{\text{Defects found before release} + \text{Defects found after release}} \times 100\% $$ | Effectiveness of testing phase. Target: **> 95%**. |
| 3 | **Test Case Effectiveness (TCE)** | $$ TCE = \frac{\text{Number of Test Cases that Detected a Defect}}{\text{Total Number of Test Cases Executed}} \times 100\% $$ | Quality of designed test cases. |
| 4 | **Requirement Coverage (RC)** | $$ RC = \frac{\text{Number of Requirements Covered}}{\text{Total Number of Requirements}} \times 100\% $$ | Are we testing all user requirements? |
| 5 | **Test Coverage (TC)** | $$ TC = \frac{\text{Number of Test Cases Executed}}{\text{Total Number of Test Cases Planned}} \times 100\% $$ | Execution progress. |
| 6 | **Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)** | $$ MTTF = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} $$ | Reliability metric. Higher is better. |
| 7 | **Code Coverage (Branch)** | $$ \text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{\text{Executed Branches}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\% $$ | Structural testing metric. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numericals:** Problems on **Defect Density** and **DRE** appear regularly. Always state the *units* (e.g., defects per KLOC, percentage).

---

## 2.5 Verification & Validation Activities — Master Mapping

Verification is performed at **four major checkpoints** during development. Each checkpoint uses specific static techniques:

| Verification Checkpoint | Input Artifact | Verification Techniques | Defects Caught |
|------------------------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Requirements Verification** | SRS / BRS | Reviews, Walkthroughs, Inspections, Prototyping | Ambiguity, incompleteness, inconsistency |
| **High-Level Design Verification** | HLD / System Design | Design Reviews, Architecture Inspection, Checklist-based review | Design flaws, interface mismatches, performance issues at architecture level |
| **Low-Level Design Verification** | LLD / Detailed Design | Detailed design walkthroughs, peer reviews, code-level review readiness check | Logic flaws, module-level design errors, data flow issues |
| **Code Verification** | Source Code | Code inspections, static analysis (lint, complexity), pair programming, code review meetings | Syntax errors, logical errors, coding standard violations |

Validation, on the other hand, is performed at **four testing levels**:

| Validation Level | Scope | Performed By |
|------------------|-------|--------------|
| **Unit Testing** | Individual modules/functions | Developer |
| **Integration Testing** | Interfaces between modules | Developer/Tester |
| **System Testing** | Complete integrated system | Independent Test Team |
| **Acceptance Testing (UAT)** | Business requirements, user perspective | End User / Customer |

---

## 2.6 Why the V&V Pairing Works

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The V-Model is heavily used in **safety-critical industries** like avionics (DO-178C), medical devices (IEC 62304), and railway signaling (EN 50128). The strict phase-to-test correspondence ensures *traceability* and *auditability* — both mandatory in regulated industries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Verification of Requirements — Step-by-Step

Verification of requirements ensures that the **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)** correctly, completely, and unambiguously captures the user's needs and is testable.

### Step-by-Step Process

1. **Identify Stakeholders & Roles**
   - Author, Reviewer, Moderator, Recorder, Reader (IEEE 1028 standard).
   
2. **Choose Verification Technique**
   - **Walkthrough:** Informal, author-led, peer-driven.
   - **Inspection:** Formal, IEEE 1028 standard, with roles (Moderator, Reader, Recorder, Reviewer, Author). Uses entry/exit criteria.
   - **Review:** Manager-led technical review with checklists.
   
3. **Apply Checklist-Based Evaluation**
   The requirements are checked against standard quality attributes:

   | Quality Attribute | Verification Question |
   |-------------------|----------------------|
   | Correctness | Does each requirement represent what the user actually needs? |
   | Unambiguity | Is it interpreted in **only one way** by all readers? |
   | Completeness | Are all possible inputs, scenarios, error conditions covered? |
   | Consistency | Are there **no conflicting** requirements? |
   | Verifiability | Can each requirement be verified by a test? |
   | Traceability | Is each requirement uniquely numbered & traceable? |
   | Modifiability | Can requirements be changed in a controlled way? |

4. **Log Issues & Update SRS**
   Issues are categorized as **Defects (to fix)**, **Clarifications (to explain)**, or **Change Requests (to be baselined)**.

5. **Conduct Re-Verification**
   After fixes, the modified sections of the SRS are re-reviewed to confirm defect closure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Walkthrough is **informal** with **no formal entry/exit criteria**; Inspection is **formal** with **strict roles and metrics** like *preparation rate* and *defect density* per page.

---

## 3.2 Verification of High-Level Design (HLD) — Step-by-Step

HLD describes the **system architecture** — modules, their responsibilities, interfaces, and data flow.

### Step-by-Step Process

1. **Receive the HLD Document** containing:
   - System architecture diagrams (block diagrams, data flow diagrams).
   - Module decomposition.
   - Database design overview.
   - Interface definitions between major modules.
   - Technology stack decisions.

2. **Conduct Architecture Review**
   - Verify modularity (high cohesion, low coupling).
   - Verify that HLD satisfies **all functional and non-functional requirements** (performance, security, scalability).

3. **Apply Specific Verification Checks**:

   $$ \text{Coupling} = f(\text{inter-module dependencies}) \quad \text{Goal: Minimize} $$

   $$ \text{Cohesion} = f(\text{intra-module responsibility focus}) \quad \text{Goal: Maximize} $$

4. **Check Design for Traceability**
   Every HLD element must trace back to one or more **SRS requirements** (forward traceability) and forward to one or more **LLD elements**.

5. **Document Defects & Sign Off**
   - HLD Review Report with the list of issues.
   - Sign-off by stakeholders before proceeding to LLD.

---

## 3.3 Verification of Low-Level Design (LLD) — Step-by-Step

LLD details the **internal logic** of each module: algorithms, data structures, internal interfaces, control flow.

### Step-by-Step Process

1. **Receive the LLD Document** containing:
   - Detailed module logic (pseudo-code, flowcharts, decision tables).
   - Database schema details (tables, columns, indexes).
   - Internal function signatures, parameter lists, return types.
   - Error-handling strategies.

2. **Conduct Detailed Design Walkthrough**
   - Walk through the algorithm with the team step-by-step.
   - Check boundary conditions (e.g., $x = 0$, $x = \text{MAX\_INT}$, empty list).

3. **Static Verification of Logic**
   - Use **logic-coverage tools** to identify unreachable branches.
   - Apply **mathematical correctness checks** for formulae and conditions.

4. **Cross-Reference with HLD**
   - Every LLD module must map to exactly one HLD module.
   - Interface signatures must match the HLD-level contract.

5. **Sign-off and Authorize Coding**
   - Authorize the developers to write code based on the approved LLD.

---

## 3.4 How to Verify Code — Step-by-Step (Comprehensive)

Code verification is the most **hands-on** form of verification. It uses both **human review** and **automated static analysis**.

### Step 1: Author-Level Review (Self-Review)
- The developer reads their own code against an internal checklist.
- Catches 30–50% of defects *before* peer review.

### Step 2: Peer / Pair Review
- A colleague walks through the code with the author.
- Catches another 15–30% of defects.
- Modern variant: **Pair Programming** (XP methodology).

### Step 3: Code Inspection (Formal)
- **IEEE 1028 Standard.** Roles: Moderator, Reader, Recorder, Reviewer, Author.
- Output: **Defect Log** with severity, type, and location.

### Step 4: Static Analysis (Automated Tools)
- Tools like **SonarQube, ESLint, PMD, FindBugs, Checkstyle** analyze the code **without executing** it.
- Detects:
  - **Cyclomatic Complexity** violations.
  - **Unused variables / dead code.**
  - **Memory leaks, null pointer dereferences.**
  - **Security vulnerabilities** (e.g., SQL injection, buffer overflow).

The **Cyclomatic Complexity** is computed as:

$$ V(G) = E - N + 2P $$

Where:
- $E$ = number of edges in the control flow graph.
- $N$ = number of nodes.
- $P$ = number of connected components (usually $P = 1$).

**Industry benchmark:** $V(G) \leq 10$ per function is considered maintainable.

### Step 5: Code Walkthrough
- Author narrates code line-by-line; team asks questions.

### Step 6: Code Standards Compliance Check
- Verify adherence to:
  - **MISRA-C** (automotive / embedded).
  - **CERT** (security).
  - **PEP-8** (Python), **Google Java Style**, etc.

### Step 7: Re-verification after Refactoring
- After any defect fix, the modified code is re-verified.

---

## 3.5 Validation — Step-by-Step (Four Levels)

### Level 1: Unit Testing
- Test individual functions/methods in isolation.
- Uses **stubs** (simulated called modules) and **drivers** (calling main programs).
- **White-box** technique (code-aware).

### Level 2: Integration Testing
- Test interactions between modules.
- **Approaches:** Big-Bang, Top-Down (uses stubs), Bottom-Up (uses drivers), Sandwich (Hybrid).

### Level 3: System Testing
- Test the **complete, integrated system** against the SRS.
- **Types:** Functional, Performance, Load, Stress, Security, Usability, Compatibility.

### Level 4: Acceptance Testing (UAT)
- **Alpha Testing** — at developer's site by internal users.
- **Beta Testing** — at customer's site by real users.
- **User Acceptance Test (UAT)** sign-off authorizes release.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Verification vs. Validation (Quick Reference)

| S.No. | Parameter | Verification | Validation |
|-------|-----------|--------------|------------|
| 1 | Question | Are we building the product **right**? | Are we building the **right** product? |
| 2 | Type | Static (no execution) | Dynamic (execution) |
| 3 | Phase | Before coding | After coding |
| 4 | Cost | Low (early) | High (late) |
| 5 | Techniques | Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, static analysis | Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance tests |
| 6 | Document | SRS, HLD, LLD, Source Code | Executable code |
| 7 | Detects | Specification/design/code defects | Runtime failures, performance issues |
| 8 | Output | Review reports | Test reports |
| 9 | Tool | Checklists, static analyzers | Test automation tools (Selenium, JUnit) |
| 10 | Goal | Conformance to specs | Conformance to user needs |

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: Defect Density & DRE Calculation (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A software module has 5,000 lines of code (5 KLOC). During testing, 20 defects were found. After release, 5 more defects were reported by users. Calculate:
1. **Defect Density (DD)**
2. **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)**

**Solution:**

$$ DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects Found}}{\text{Size in KLOC}} = \frac{20}{5} = 4 \text{ defects/KLOC} $$

$$ DRE = \frac{20}{20 + 5} \times 100\% = \frac{20}{25} \times 100\% = 80\% $$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A DRE of 80% means the testing process is *moderately effective*. Best-in-class organizations target **DRE > 95%**.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The V-Model (Verification & Validation Model) — Block Architecture Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The V-Model is the **single most diagrammed concept** in KTU Software Testing exams. Master this.

```mermaid
graph LR
    REQ[Requirements Specification] --> HLD[High Level Design]
    HLD --> LLD[Low Level Design]
    LLD --> COD[Coding]
    
    COD -.->. UTEST[Unit Testing]
    LLD -.->. ITEST[Integration Testing]
    HLD -.->. STEST[System Testing]
    REQ -.->. ATEST[Acceptance Testing]
    
    ATEST --> UTEST
    UTEST --> ITEST
    ITEST --> STEST
    STEST --> ATEST
    
    UTEST --> RESULT1[Verified Unit]
    ITEST --> RESULT2[Integrated System]
    STEST --> RESULT3[System Validated]
    ATEST --> RESULT4[Customer Accepted]
    
    style REQ fill:#FFE4B5
    style HLD fill:#FFE4B5
    style LLD fill:#FFE4B5
    style COD fill:#FFE4B5
    style UTEST fill:#90EE90
    style ITEST fill:#90EE90
    style STEST fill:#90EE90
    style ATEST fill:#90EE90
    style RESULT1 fill:#87CEEB
    style RESULT2 fill:#87CEEB
    style RESULT3 fill:#87CEEB
    style RESULT4 fill:#87CEEB
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The **left arm** of the V shows the *development phases* (Verification activities). The **right arm** shows the *corresponding testing phases* (Validation activities). The bottom of the V is the *coding* phase.

---

## 4.4 Error → Defect → Failure Chain — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Error Human Mistake] --> B[Defect Bug in Code]
    B --> C[Failure Observed at Runtime]
    
    A --> A1[Example: Wrong operator used in condition]
    B --> B1[Example: IF x greater than 10 instead of IF x greater than equal 10]
    C --> C1[Example: User gets incorrect output for x equals 10]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1:** *Define Software Testing. Differentiate between Verification and Validation.* `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Software Testing is the process of exercising or evaluating a software system or its component(s) by manual or automated means to verify that it satisfies specified requirements OR to identify differences between expected and actual results (IEEE Std 829).

**Verification vs. Validation (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| Question | Are we building the product **right**? | Are we building the **right** product? |
| Type | Static (no execution) | Dynamic (execution required) |
| Activities | Reviews, walkthroughs, inspections, static analysis | Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance Testing |

---

### **Question 2:** *Explain the following terms: (a) Test Case, (b) Test Suite, (c) Test Oracle.* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

**(a) Test Case:** A test case is a set of pre-conditions, inputs, actions, expected results, and post-conditions developed for a particular test objective. It documents the *how* of testing a specific requirement.

**(b) Test Suite:** A test suite is a logical collection of test cases grouped together for execution in a single test cycle or for a specific module/feature.

**(c) Test Oracle:** A test oracle is a mechanism (manual or automated) used to determine whether a system has passed or failed a test by comparing the actual output with the expected output.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** *Explain the Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC) in detail. List and describe all six phases with their entry criteria, activities, deliverables, and exit criteria.* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** *Describe the first three phases of STLC with their deliverables.*

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 1: Requirement Analysis (2 marks)**
- **Entry Criteria:** Approved Software Requirements Specification (SRS).
- **Activities:** Identify testable requirements, classify functional vs. non-functional, define types of tests required, identify gaps and ambiguities in requirements.
- **Deliverable:** **Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM)** — maps each requirement to test cases.
- **Exit Criteria:** RTM signed off by stakeholders.

**Phase 2: Test Planning (2 marks)**
- **Entry Criteria:** Approved RTM.
- **Activities:** Define test strategy, scope, resources (people, tools, infrastructure), schedule, entry/exit criteria, risk mitigation, test environment requirements.
- **Deliverable:** **Test Plan Document** (per IEEE 829 standard) containing test items, features to be tested, approach, pass/fail criteria, suspension/resumption criteria.
- **Exit Criteria:** Test Plan approved by all stakeholders.

**Phase 3: Test Case Development (2 marks)**
- **Entry Criteria:** Approved Test Plan.
- **Activities:** Author test cases with unique IDs, pre-conditions, steps, test data, expected results. Write automated test scripts (if applicable). Review test cases via walkthrough.
- **Deliverable:** **Test Cases Document, Test Scripts, Test Data** (IEEE 829 standard format).
- **Exit Criteria:** All test cases reviewed and signed off.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
These three phases constitute the *planning and design* portion of STLC, setting the foundation for effective test execution.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** *Describe the remaining three phases of STLC with their deliverables and explain the importance of Test Closure.*

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 4: Test Environment Setup (2 marks)**
- **Entry Criteria:** Approved test cases; hardware/software licenses available.
- **Activities:** Configure test servers, install SUT (System Under Test), set up test data, configure network/firewall, install test tools, create **stubs and drivers** as needed.
- **Deliverable:** **Test Environment Ready** — verified via **Smoke Test** (a quick sanity test of the environment).
- **Exit Criteria:** Smoke test passes; environment stable.

**Phase 5: Test Execution (2.5 marks)**
- **Entry Criteria:** Test cases + Test environment ready.
- **Activities:** Execute test cases in order (high-priority first), log results as Pass/Fail/Blocked, log defects for failures in a **Defect Tracking System** (e.g., JIRA, Bugzilla), perform **Regression Testing** after each fix, perform **Re-testing** of fixed defects.
- **Deliverable:** **Test Execution Report, Defect Log, Updated RTM**.
- **Exit Criteria:** All planned test cases executed; exit criteria of Test Plan met.

**Phase 6: Test Closure (2.5 marks)**
- **Entry Criteria:** All planned tests executed.
- **Activities:** Verify all defects are closed or deferred, archive test artifacts, conduct a **Test Closure Meeting** with stakeholders, document **Lessons Learned**, calculate metrics (DRE, Test Coverage).
- **Deliverable:** **Test Closure Report** (IEEE 829 standard).
- **Exit Criteria:** Test Closure Report approved by management.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Test Closure is critical because it converts *raw test data* into *organizational learning* and ensures formal project sign-off.

---

### **Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks):** *Explain Verification and Validation in detail. Discuss the verification activities for Requirements, High-Level Design, Low-Level Design, and Code with appropriate techniques.* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** *Explain Verification of Requirements and High-Level Design in detail.*

**Model Solution:**

**Verification of Requirements (3.5 marks):**

Requirements verification is the systematic process of evaluating the SRS to ensure it is *correct, complete, unambiguous, consistent, verifiable, traceable, and modifiable*.

**Techniques used:**

1. **Walkthrough:** Informal, author-led meeting where author walks the team through the SRS.
2. **Inspection:** Formal technique (IEEE 1028), with defined roles — *Moderator, Author, Reader, Recorder, Reviewer*. Uses **checklists** and **entry/exit criteria**.
3. **Review:** Manager-led technical review with peer reviewers.
4. **Prototyping:** Build a working model to validate requirements with users.

**Quality Attributes Checked:**
- **Correctness, Unambiguity, Completeness, Consistency, Verifiability, Traceability, Modifiability.**

**Output:** Review Report with defects, clarifications, and change requests. Re-review after fixes.

**Verification of High-Level Design (3.5 marks):**

HLD verification evaluates the system architecture and module decomposition.

**Activities:**
- Conduct **Architecture Review** with stakeholders.
- Verify **modularity** — high cohesion, low coupling.

**Mathematical Goal:**

$$ \text{Coupling} \rightarrow \text{Minimize} $$
$$ \text{Cohesion} \rightarrow \text{Maximize} $$

- Verify HLD **satisfies all non-functional requirements** (performance, security, scalability).
- Check **interface definitions** between major modules.
- Check **database design overview** for normalization (typically up to 3NF).
- Verify **forward traceability** to SRS and **backward traceability** to LLD (when LLD is developed later).

**Output:** HLD Review Report with sign-off.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** *Explain Verification of Low-Level Design and Code with techniques used.*

**Model Solution:**

**Verification of Low-Level Design (3 marks):**

LLD verification focuses on the *internal logic* of each module: algorithms, data structures, control flow.

**Activities:**
- Conduct **Detailed Design Walkthrough** — step-by-step narration by author.
- Apply **Boundary Value Analysis** mentally to algorithms to check edge cases (e.g., empty inputs, maximum values).
- Cross-reference each LLD module to its parent **HLD module** — interface signatures must match.
- Verify **error-handling strategies** are explicit.
- Use **decision tables** and **flowcharts** to check completeness of logic.

**Output:** Detailed Design Review Report; authorization to begin coding.

**Verification of Code (4 marks):**

Code verification is the most hands-on form of verification.

**Techniques Used:**

1. **Author Self-Review (1 mark):**
   - Developer reads own code against a checklist.
   - Catches ~30-50% of defects.

2. **Peer / Pair Review (1 mark):**
   - A colleague walks through the code.
   - Catches additional 15-30% of defects.

3. **Code Inspection (1 mark):**
   - Formal (IEEE 1028) — Moderator, Author, Reader, Recorder, Reviewer.
   - Defects logged in a Defect Log.

4. **Static Analysis (1 mark):**
   - Automated tools like **SonarQube, ESLint, PMD, FindBugs**.
   - Detects unused variables, dead code, **Cyclomatic Complexity > 10**, security flaws.

**Cyclomatic Complexity Formula:**

$$ V(G) = E - N + 2P $$

Where $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components.

**Standard Compliance:** Verify code follows coding standards like **MISRA-C, CERT, PEP-8**.

**Output:** Code Review Report, Static Analysis Report, and final sign-off.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Module 1**
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation:** Verification is STATIC (reviews, walkthroughs); Validation is DYNAMIC (running tests). Writing the reverse will cost you **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **V-Model Direction:** Many students draw the V-Model with verification on the *right* arm. The correct convention is **left arm = development + verification**, **right arm = testing + validation**.
> 3. **Forgetting Deliverables:** STLC questions explicitly demand deliverables per phase. Merely listing phase names without RTM, Test Plan, etc. = **partial credit only**.
> 4. **Confusing Walkthrough vs. Inspection:** Walkthrough = *informal, no formal roles*. Inspection = *formal, defined roles (IEEE 1028)*. Examiners love this distinction.
> 5. **Ignoring Cyclomatic Complexity Formula:** When asked "how to verify code," mentioning *static analysis* without **Cyclomatic Complexity** formula $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Stating "Testing = finding defects" only:** The KTU board expects both dimensions — *finding defects* AND *building confidence* that the software meets requirements.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **The "Night-Before-Exam" 60-Second Revision List**

- ✅ **Testing** = process of finding defects *and* validating that requirements are met (IEEE 1059).
- ✅ **Error → Defect → Failure** is the chain of cause → manifestation → impact.
- ✅ **STLC has 6 phases:** Requirement Analysis → Test Planning → Test Case Development → Test Environment Setup → Test Execution → Test Closure.
- ✅ **STLC Deliverables:** RTM, Test Plan, Test Cases, Test Bed, Execution Report, Test Closure Report.
- ✅ **Verification = "Are we building the product RIGHT?"** → Static, document-based, done *before* code execution.
- ✅ **Validation = "Are we building the RIGHT product?"** → Dynamic, execution-based, done *after* code is built.
- ✅ **V-Model:** Left arm = Development + Verification; Right arm = Testing + Validation; bottom = Coding.
- ✅ **Verification of Requirements:** Walkthrough (informal), Inspection (formal — IEEE 1028), Review, Prototyping.
- ✅ **Verification of HLD:** Architecture review, modularity check (high cohesion, low coupling), NFR compliance.
- ✅ **Verification of LLD:** Detailed design walkthrough, boundary value checks, cross-reference to HLD.
- ✅ **Verification of Code:** Self-review → Peer review → Code inspection (IEEE 1028) → Static analysis (SonarQube, ESLint) → Standards compliance (MISRA-C, CERT).
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity Formula:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ (target: $V(G) \leq 10$).
- ✅ **Validation Levels:** Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance (UAT).
- ✅ **Defect Density:** $DD = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$.
- ✅ **DRE:** $DRE = \frac{\text{Defects found before release}}{\text{Total defects}} \times 100\%$ (target > 95%).
- ✅ **Inspection has 5 roles:** Author, Moderator, Reader, Recorder, Reviewer (IEEE 1028).
- ✅ **Walkthrough is informal** with **no entry/exit criteria**; Inspection is formal with **strict entry/exit criteria**.
- ✅ **Dijkstra's Law:** Testing can show the *presence* of bugs, never their *absence*.
- ✅ **Boehm's Law:** Cost of fixing a defect **multiplies ~10x** at each later SDLC phase.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT862 - Software Testing)**
*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcomes, and revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functional Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Functional Testing** is a type of **black-box testing** technique that validates the software system against its **functional requirements and specifications**. It focuses on verifying that each function of the software application operates in conformance with the requirement specification, ensuring the system delivers the expected outputs for given inputs.

According to the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board) Foundation Level Syllabus**, functional testing is concerned with *what the system does* — i.e., its features, operations, and capabilities — and is independent of the internal implementation or structure of the code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Functional Testing falls under the umbrella of **Dynamic Testing** and **Specification-Based Testing (Black-Box)** techniques. It is a mandatory topic under Module 1 and frequently appears as a 7-mark or 14-mark question in ESE.

The primary objectives of functional testing are:

1. To verify the **correctness** of functional requirements.
2. To validate that the system meets business and user expectations.
3. To identify defects in the functional behavior of the system.
4. To ensure data integrity, computation accuracy, and proper interaction between modules.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you just bought a brand-new **microwave oven**. You don't care how the magnetron works internally, or how the circuit board is designed. What you *do* care about is:

- Does the **Start** button actually start the oven?
- Does setting **2 minutes** at **High Power** heat the food for exactly 2 minutes?
- Does pressing **Stop** halt the operation immediately?
- Does the door sensor prevent operation when the door is open?

That is exactly **Functional Testing**. You (the tester) treat the system as a **black box**, feed it inputs (button presses, timer settings), and verify that the outputs (heat, light, sound) match the **user manual and design specification**.

In software terms:
- **Input** → User actions, API calls, database queries, file uploads.
- **Output** → Screen output, database changes, file generation, returned data, system response.
- **Test** → Compare actual output with expected output (defined in the SRS/BRD).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Functional Testing = *What the system does* (Business Logic).
> Non-Functional Testing = *How well the system does it* (Performance, Security, Usability).

---

### 1.3 Functional vs Non-Functional Testing — Quick Comparison

| Dimension | **Functional Testing** | **Non-Functional Testing** |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | **What** the system does | **How well** the system performs |
| Type | Black-box (Specification-based) | Black-box (Behavior-based) |
| Based on | Functional Requirements (SRS) | Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs) |
| Examples | Unit, Integration, System, UAT, Smoke | Performance, Load, Stress, Security, Usability |
| Metric | Pass / Fail of test cases | Quantitative metrics (e.g., ms, MB, req/sec) |
| Verifies | Feature correctness | Quality attributes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phases / Levels of Functional Testing

Functional testing is organized as a **pyramid of test levels**, each with a specific scope, ownership, and test objective.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Core Topic:** The four primary test levels under functional testing are **Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance Testing**. You MUST know the purpose, basis, and typical defects found at each level.

#### 2.1.1 Component / Unit Testing
- **Definition:** Tests the smallest testable individual software units (functions, methods, classes) in isolation.
- **Performed by:** Developers (and sometimes testers for TDD).
- **Technique:** White-box + Black-box.
- **Defects found:** Logic errors, calculation mistakes, syntax errors, data-flow issues.
- **Tools:** JUnit, NUnit, pytest, xUnit.

#### 2.1.2 Integration Testing
- **Definition:** Tests the interaction and data flow between integrated components/modules.
- **Approaches:**
  - **Big-Bang Integration:** All modules integrated and tested at once. Risky; defects hard to isolate.
  - **Incremental Integration:**
    - *Top-Down:* Top modules tested first with stubs for lower modules.
    - *Bottom-Up:* Lower modules tested first with drivers for upper modules.
    - *Sandwich (Hybrid):* Combines both.
- **Defects found:** Interface mismatches, data format mismatches, call sequencing errors.

#### 2.1.3 System Testing
- **Definition:** Tests the complete, integrated system as a whole against the SRS.
- **Performed by:** Independent QA team.
- **Environment:** Staging / Pre-production environment.
- **Includes functional and non-functional tests** but the **functional sub-set** covers end-to-end workflows.

#### 2.1.4 Acceptance Testing
- **Definition:** Validifies the system against **business requirements and user needs** to determine readiness for release.
- **Types:**
  - **User Acceptance Testing (UAT):** Performed by end-users or client representatives.
  - **Operational Acceptance Testing (OAT):** Performed by system administrators (backup/restore, maintenance).
  - **Contract Acceptance Testing (CAT):** Against contractually agreed criteria.
  - **Regulatory / Compliance Acceptance Testing (RAT):** Verifies legal/regulatory compliance.
  - **Alpha Testing:** Performed at developer's site by internal staff.
  - **Beta Testing:** Performed at customer's site by actual end-users in real environments.

---

### 2.2 Special Functional Test Types

| Test Type | Purpose | When Performed |
|---|---|---|
| **Smoke Testing** | Verifies basic / critical functionalities work after a new build. Determines if build is *stable enough* for further testing. | After every new build / release. |
| **Sanity Testing** | Verifies that a specific bug fix or new functionality works correctly *without re-testing the entire system*. Narrow & deep. | After a bug fix or minor change. |
| **Regression Testing** | Ensures that existing functionalities still work after code changes, enhancements, or bug fixes. | After every modification to the codebase. |
| **Re-Testing (Confirmation Testing)** | Re-execution of a failed test case to confirm a bug is fixed. | After a defect is reported and fixed. |
| **Exploratory Testing** | Simultaneous test design and execution, exploring the system to discover defects. | When time is limited and tester experience is high. |
| **Ad-hoc Testing** | Informal, unstructured testing without test cases. | When tester wants to find defects creatively. |
| **Compatibility Testing** | Validates the system works across different browsers, OS, devices, networks. | Before release. |
| **User Interface (UI) Testing** | Validates the GUI elements (buttons, forms, menus, layout). | System testing phase. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Confusion:** *Smoke vs Sanity vs Regression vs Re-Testing* — These four terms are mixed by students. Memorize the table above and the definitions carefully. Past exams (e.g., KTU Dec 2023) have asked direct comparison questions.

---

### 2.3 Specification-Based (Black-Box) Test Design Techniques

These are the **most exam-relevant** techniques for functional testing under KTU Module 1.

#### 2.3.1 Equivalence Partitioning (EP)
- **Idea:** Divide the input domain into groups of *equivalent* data from which test cases can be derived.
- **Rule:** A test case that catches a defect in one value of an equivalence class will catch the same defect for any other value in that class.
- **Valid Class:** Inputs that should be accepted by the system.
- **Invalid Class:** Inputs that should be rejected.

#### 2.3.2 Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)
- **Idea:** Errors tend to occur at the *boundaries* of input ranges, not at the center.
- **Rule:** Test the exact boundary values, just below, and just above.
- For input range [a, b]: test a−1, a, a+1, b−1, b, b+1.

#### 2.3.3 Decision Table Testing
- **Idea:** For systems with complex business rules involving multiple conditions, decision tables capture the combination of inputs and the corresponding actions.
- **Components:** Conditions (C), Condition values (T/F), Actions, Action values.

#### 2.3.4 State Transition Testing
- **Idea:** Tests the system's behavior when it transitions from one state to another based on events.
- **Useful for:** Systems with finite defined states (e.g., ATM: Idle → Card Inserted → PIN Entered → Authenticated → Transaction).

#### 2.3.5 Use Case Testing
- **Idea:** Test cases are derived from **use cases** (sequences of interactions between actors and the system).
- Each use case = a scenario; each scenario = a set of test cases (including alternate and exception flows).

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Boundary Value Test Count (closed interval [a, b])** | $N_{BVA} = 6$ (for 1 variable) | a−1, a, a+1, b−1, b, b+1 |
| 2 | **Equivalence Classes (1 variable)** | 1 valid + 1 (or more) invalid | Reduces test cases dramatically |
| 3 | **Statement Coverage** | $\text{SC} = \dfrac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | **Branch Coverage** | $\text{BC} = \dfrac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100$ | % |
| 5 | **Decision Table Rule Count** | $N_{rules} = 2^n$ | n = number of conditions |
| 6 | **State Transition Edge Count** | $N_{transitions} = \vert E \vert$ (from state diagram) | Directed graph |
| 7 | **Defect Density** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size (KLOC or FP)}}$ | Per KLOC / per FP |
| 8 | **Test Effectiveness** | $\text{TE} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Found in Test}}{\text{Defects Found in Production}} \times 100$ | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** The most frequently used formulas in Module 1 are **Equivalence Partitioning**, **Boundary Value Analysis** (with worked example), and **Decision Table** (with rule count $2^n$).

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Functional testing is the **backbone of quality assurance** in the software industry. Without it:

- Banking systems could miscalculate interest.
- E-commerce platforms could charge the wrong amount.
- Medical devices could misreport vital signs.
- Aviation software could fail mid-flight.

It is **applied universally** in:
- **Banking & Finance** (transaction accuracy, compliance).
- **Healthcare** (patient record correctness).
- **E-Commerce** (cart, checkout, payment flows).
- **Embedded Systems** (sensor reading correctness).
- **Web & Mobile Apps** (user workflows, API responses).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section works through **worked-out examples** of the most exam-relevant test design techniques: **Equivalence Partitioning, Boundary Value Analysis, Decision Table Testing, and State Transition Testing**. Each step is logically justified, exactly as the KTU board examiner expects.

---

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Equivalence Partitioning + Boundary Value Analysis (Combined)

**Problem (Model KTU Question):**
A program accepts a **numeric input N** such that:

$$
1 \leq N \leq 100
$$

The program classifies N as:
- "Low" if $1 \leq N \leq 33$
- "Medium" if $34 \leq N \leq 66$
- "High" if $67 \leq N \leq 100$

Any input outside $[1, 100]$ is invalid and the program displays "Invalid Input".

**Task:** Design test cases using **Equivalence Partitioning** and **Boundary Value Analysis**.

---

#### Step 1: Identify the Input Domain

The valid input domain is the closed interval $[1, 100]$.
Outside this interval, inputs are **invalid**.

We can write this formally as:

$$
D_{valid} = \{ N \in \mathbb{Z} \mid 1 \leq N \leq 100 \}
$$

$$
D_{invalid} = (-\infty, 0] \cup [101, +\infty)
$$

**Explanation:** The set of valid integers is from 1 to 100 inclusive. Anything less than 1 or greater than 100 is invalid by the specification.

---

#### Step 2: Apply Equivalence Partitioning (EP)

We divide the input domain into equivalence classes. Since the program also has a **3-way classification** (Low / Medium / High), we partition the valid range into **3 valid classes** and additionally create **2 invalid classes** for out-of-range values.

| Class ID | Range | Type |
|---|---|---|
| EC1 | $1 \leq N \leq 33$ | Valid (Low) |
| EC2 | $34 \leq N \leq 66$ | Valid (Medium) |
| EC3 | $67 \leq N \leq 100$ | Valid (High) |
| EC4 | $N < 1$ | Invalid |
| EC5 | $N > 100$ | Invalid |

**Explanation:** The rule of EP says we pick one representative from each class. Choosing 17, 50, 80, 0, and 150 respectively, we have **5 test cases** that exercise all 5 partitions.

---

#### Step 3: Apply Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

BVA focuses on the **edges** of the boundaries. The boundaries occur at N = 1, N = 33, N = 34, N = 66, N = 67, N = 100.

For BVA on the **outer range** [1, 100], we test:

$$
N \in \{0, 1, 2, 99, 100, 101\}
$$

For BVA on **inner boundaries** (class transitions): 33/34 and 66/67, we test:

$$
N \in \{32, 33, 34, 35, 65, 66, 67, 68\}
$$

**Explanation:** The classic BVA rule for a closed interval [a, b] uses 6 values: a−1, a, a+1, b−1, b, b+1. For internal class transitions, we use the same logic on each class boundary.

---

#### Step 4: Construct the Final Test Case Table

| TC# | Input N | Expected Output | Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 17 | "Low" | EP | Representative of EC1 |
| 2 | 50 | "Medium" | EP | Representative of EC2 |
| 3 | 80 | "High" | EP | Representative of EC3 |
| 4 | 0 | "Invalid Input" | EP + BVA | Just below lower bound |
| 5 | 150 | "Invalid Input" | EP + BVA | Just above upper bound |
| 6 | 1 | "Low" | BVA | Exact lower boundary |
| 7 | 2 | "Low" | BVA | Just above lower boundary |
| 8 | 32 | "Low" | BVA | Just below class transition |
| 9 | 33 | "Low" | BVA | Exact boundary of EC1 |
| 10 | 34 | "Medium" | BVA | Exact boundary of EC2 |
| 11 | 35 | "Medium" | BVA | Just above class transition |
| 12 | 66 | "Medium" | BVA | Exact boundary of EC2 |
| 13 | 67 | "High" | BVA | Exact boundary of EC3 |
| 14 | 99 | "High" | BVA | Just below upper boundary |
| 15 | 100 | "High" | BVA | Exact upper boundary |
| 16 | 101 | "Invalid Input" | BVA | Just above upper boundary |

**Explanation:** The above 16 test cases provide **100% BVA coverage** and **EP coverage** for the specification. In KTU exams, drawing a clean test case table like this earns full marks.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Decision Table Testing

**Problem:**
A loan approval system has the following rules:
- **Condition 1:** Applicant age ≥ 21?
- **Condition 2:** Applicant salary ≥ ₹25,000/month?
- **Condition 3:** Applicant has a valid credit score (≥ 700)?

**Decision Rules:**
- Loan is **Approved** only if ALL three conditions are TRUE.
- Otherwise, **Rejected**.

**Task:** Construct the decision table and find the number of rules.

---

#### Step 1: Identify Conditions and Actions

- **Conditions:** C1, C2, C3 (3 conditions).
- **Actions:** Approve Loan, Reject Loan (2 actions).

---

#### Step 2: Compute the Number of Rules

Using the formula:

$$
N_{rules} = 2^n = 2^3 = 8
$$

where $n$ is the number of conditions.

**Explanation:** Each condition has 2 possible values (True/False). For 3 conditions, the total combinations are $2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8$ rules.

---

#### Step 3: Construct the Decision Table

| Rule | C1: Age ≥ 21 | C2: Salary ≥ 25k | C3: Credit ≥ 700 | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | T | T | T | Approve |
| R2 | T | T | F | Reject |
| R3 | T | F | T | Reject |
| R4 | T | F | F | Reject |
| R5 | F | T | T | Reject |
| R6 | F | T | F | Reject |
| R7 | F | F | T | Reject |
| R8 | F | F | F | Reject |

**Explanation:** Only R1 results in approval. All other rules result in rejection. The decision table is exhaustive and captures all business logic.

---

#### Step 4: Simplify (Optional, Advanced)

Rules R2, R3, R4, R5, R6, R7, R8 can be collapsed into a **single rule** "Any condition FALSE ⇒ Reject", but for KTU exam answers, write the full table.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: State Transition Testing (ATM System)

**Problem:** Design test cases for an ATM using state transition testing.

**States:**
- S0: Idle
- S1: Card Inserted
- S2: PIN Verified
- S3: Transaction Selected
- S4: Cash Dispensed

**Events:**
- E1: Insert Card
- E2: Enter PIN (correct)
- E3: Enter PIN (incorrect, 3rd attempt)
- E4: Select Withdraw
- E5: Dispense Cash
- E6: Eject Card
- E7: Cancel

---

#### Step 1: Construct the State Transition Diagram (Logically)

| Current State | Event | Next State |
|---|---|---|
| S0 (Idle) | E1: Insert Card | S1 (Card Inserted) |
| S1 | E2: Enter Correct PIN | S2 (PIN Verified) |
| S1 | E3: Enter Wrong PIN (3rd time) | S0 (Idle, card retained) |
| S2 | E4: Select Withdraw | S3 (Transaction Selected) |
| S3 | E5: Dispense Cash | S4 (Cash Dispensed) |
| S4 | E6: Eject Card | S0 (Idle) |
| S2 | E7: Cancel | S0 (Idle) |

---

#### Step 2: Identify Valid and Invalid Transitions

- **Valid:** All transitions in the table above.
- **Invalid:** e.g., Ejecting a card when in S0 (Idle), or dispensing cash when in S1 (Card Inserted but PIN not verified).

**Explanation:** Each transition yields 1 test case. For **N valid transitions**, we have N test cases. Plus, for each state, we should test **invalid events** to verify the system handles them gracefully.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Technique

| Technique | Best For | Drawback |
|---|---|---|
| **EP** | Reducing redundant inputs. | Misses boundary defects. |
| **BVA** | Catching off-by-one errors. | Not useful for non-numeric data. |
| **Decision Table** | Complex business rules with many conditions. | Becomes huge ($2^n$) for many conditions. |
| **State Transition** | Systems with finite defined states. | Not useful for stateless systems. |
| **Use Case Testing** | Validating user workflows. | Doesn't cover all internal paths. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are designed to be **Mermaid v10+ compatible**. Node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting inside quotes).

### 4.1 Functional Testing Test Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Acceptance Testing] --> B0[System Testing]
    B0 --> C0[Integration Testing]
    C0 --> D0[Component or Unit Testing]

    D0 --> D0A[Scope Smallest Unit]
    D0 --> D0B[Done By Developer]
    D0 --> D0C[Tool JUnit pytest]

    C0 --> C0A[Scope Module Interaction]
    C0 --> C0B[Approaches BigBang TopDown BottomUp]
    C0 --> C0C[Tool FitNesse Postman]

    B0 --> B0A[Scope Full Integrated System]
    B0 --> B0B[Done By Independent QA]
    B0 --> B0C[Environment Staging PreProd]

    A0 --> A0A[Scope Business Requirements]
    A0 --> A0B[Done By End User Client]
    A0 --> A0C[Types UAT OAT CAT RAT Alpha Beta]
```

**Diagram Explanation:** The classic **V-Model / Test Pyramid** for functional testing. The base of the pyramid (Unit Testing) has the largest number of tests and smallest scope, while the apex (Acceptance Testing) has the fewest tests but validates business acceptance.

---

### 4.2 Functional Testing Process Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Step 1 Analyze Requirements SRS] --> P2[Step 2 Identify Test Conditions]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Design Test Cases using EP BVA DT ST]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Build Test Data and Test Harness]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Execute Test Cases on System]
    P5 --> P6{Step 6 Compare Actual vs Expected}
    P6 --> P7A[Step 7a Test Passed Log and Close]
    P6 --> P7B[Step 7b Test Failed Log Defect and Track]
    P7B --> P8[Step 8 Fix Defect by Developer]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 ReTest the Defect]
    P9 --> P6
    P7A --> P10[Step 10 Report Test Closure Summary]
```

**Diagram Explanation:** The end-to-end functional testing lifecycle. The **decision diamond** shows the test result evaluation. Failed tests loop back to developer fix and re-test. Passed tests move to closure reporting.

---

### 4.3 Black-Box Test Design Techniques Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    T0[Specification Based Black Box Techniques] --> T1[Equivalence Partitioning]
    T0 --> T2[Boundary Value Analysis]
    T0 --> T3[Decision Table Testing]
    T0 --> T4[State Transition Testing]
    T0 --> T5[Use Case Testing]

    T1 --> T1A[Divide Input into Equivalent Classes]
    T1A --> T1A1[One Representative per Class]

    T2 --> T2A[Test Boundary Values]
    T2A --> T2A1[a Minus 1 a a Plus 1 b Minus 1 b b Plus 1]

    T3 --> T3A[List Conditions and Actions]
    T3A --> T3A1[Generate 2 to the n Rules]

    T4 --> T4A[Identify States and Events]
    T4A --> T4A1[Build State Transition Diagram]

    T5 --> T5A[Identify Actors and Scenarios]
    T5A --> T5A1[Derive Test Cases from Use Cases]
```

**Diagram Explanation:** A hierarchical map of the five **specification-based test design techniques** under functional testing. Each branch shows the procedural sub-steps a tester follows.

---

### 4.4 Functional vs Non-Functional Testing Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[Software Testing] --> F[Functional Testing]
    Root --> NF[Non Functional Testing]

    F --> F1[Unit Testing]
    F --> F2[Integration Testing]
    F --> F3[System Testing]
    F --> F4[Acceptance Testing]

    NF --> NF1[Performance Testing]
    NF --> NF2[Security Testing]
    NF --> NF3[Usability Testing]
    NF --> NF4[Reliability Testing]
```

**Diagram Explanation:** Block-level partition of testing types. **Functional** validates *what* the system does; **Non-Functional** validates *how well* it does it. This is a high-yield KTU diagram — draw it from memory if asked.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define Functional Testing. How is it different from Non-Functional Testing?**

**Model Answer:**

Functional Testing is a type of black-box testing technique that validates the **functional behavior** of a software system against its specified requirements. It tests whether the system performs the functions described in the SRS, such as input validation, business logic, data processing, and output generation.

**Difference from Non-Functional Testing:**

| Aspect | Functional | Non-Functional |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | What the system does | How well the system does it |
| Verifies | Features and operations | Quality attributes |
| Example | Login works correctly | Login responds in < 2 seconds |

Functional testing answers "Is the feature correct?", while non-functional testing answers "Is the feature fast, secure, reliable?"

**Marking Scheme (KTU):** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for clear differentiation.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Distinguish between Smoke Testing and Sanity Testing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Smoke Testing | Sanity Testing |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Verify critical functionalities work after a new build. | Verify a specific bug fix or new functionality. |
| Scope | **Wide and shallow** (covers all major features). | **Narrow and deep** (focuses on one area). |
| Performed | At the start of testing cycle on a new build. | After receiving a build with minor changes / fixes. |
| Also Called | Build Verification Testing (BVT). | Test Acceptance. |
| Documented | Documented (test cases exist). | Usually not documented. |

**In short:** Smoke = "Is the build stable enough to test?" Sanity = "Is this specific fix working correctly?"

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions in Module 1 carry **14 marks** and typically have internal choice. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**. Below, two complete alternative questions are provided.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four levels of functional testing in detail. Mention who performs each level, what is tested, and what defects are typically caught.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain **Equivalence Partitioning** and **Boundary Value Analysis** techniques. Design test cases for a program that accepts a **password of length between 8 and 16 characters**, where the first character must be an alphabet (A–Z or a–z).

**Model Answer for (a):**

The four levels of functional testing form a **test pyramid** from the smallest scope to the largest:

1. **Component / Unit Testing (7 marks for full answer with examples):**
   - Tests the **smallest individual units** of code (functions, methods, classes).
   - Performed by: **Developers** in their own IDEs.
   - Defects caught: **logic errors**, arithmetic mistakes, conditional branching errors, syntax issues.
   - Tools: JUnit, NUnit, pytest, xUnit.
   - Usually automated and part of CI/CD.

2. **Integration Testing:**
   - Tests the **interaction between modules/components**.
   - Performed by: Developers or independent testers.
   - Defects caught: **interface mismatches**, data format issues, call sequencing errors, missing function calls.
   - Approaches: **Big-Bang, Top-Down (with stubs), Bottom-Up (with drivers), Sandwich (Hybrid)**.

3. **System Testing:**
   - Tests the **complete integrated system** against the SRS.
   - Performed by: **Independent QA team** in a staging/pre-production environment.
   - Defects caught: end-to-end workflow failures, integration issues at system level, requirement gaps.
   - Includes both functional and non-functional sub-tests.

4. **Acceptance Testing:**
   - Validates the system meets **business and user needs** before release.
   - Performed by: **End users, customers, or client representatives**.
   - Types: **UAT (User Acceptance), OAT (Operational), CAT (Contract), RAT (Regulatory), Alpha, Beta**.
   - Defects caught: business logic gaps, usability issues, contractual non-compliance.

**Marking Scheme:** Each level = 1.5 marks + 0.5 for diagram. Add a clean test pyramid diagram for full 7 marks.

---

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Problem Restated:** Input password P must satisfy:
- Length: $8 \leq \text{len}(P) \leq 16$
- First character: alphabet (A–Z or a–z)
- Program accepts if valid, rejects otherwise.

**Step 1: Equivalence Partitioning**

| EC | Description | Type | Representative |
|---|---|---|---|
| EC1 | Length 8–16, first char alphabet | Valid | "Hello123" |
| EC2 | Length < 8 | Invalid | "Hi1" |
| EC3 | Length > 16 | Invalid | "abcdefghijklmnopq1" |
| EC4 | Length OK, first char non-alphabet | Invalid | "1Hello12" |

**Step 2: Boundary Value Analysis**

For the length boundary (8 and 16), apply the formula:

$$
N_{BVA} = \{7, 8, 9, 15, 16, 17\}
$$

**Step 3: Test Case Table**

| TC# | Password Input | Expected | Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | "Hello123" | Accept | EP (EC1) |
| 2 | "Hi1" | Reject | EP (EC2) |
| 3 | "abcdefghijklmnopq1" | Reject | EP (EC3) |
| 4 | "1Hello12" | Reject | EP (EC4) |
| 5 | "1234567" (7 chars) | Reject | BVA |
| 6 | "12345678" (8 chars, no alphabet start) | Reject | BVA |
| 7 | "A2345678" (8 chars, valid start) | Accept | BVA |
| 8 | "A23456789012345" (15 chars) | Accept | BVA |
| 9 | "A234567890123456" (16 chars) | Accept | BVA |
| 10 | "A2345678901234567" (17 chars) | Reject | BVA |

**Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for EP classes + 2 marks for BVA values + 2 marks for test case table + 1 mark for correctness.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Test Pyramid** concept. Describe the characteristics, advantages, and disadvantages of each level.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Decision Table Testing** with a suitable example. Construct the decision table for a **library book issue** system that issues books only if the user has a **valid membership** AND has **returned the previous book** AND the **book count limit (≤ 5)** is not exceeded.

**Model Answer for (a):**

The **Test Pyramid** is a concept introduced by **Mike Cohn** that suggests organizing tests in layers, with the largest number at the base (unit tests) and the smallest at the apex (UI/E2E tests).

**Characteristics of each level:**

1. **Unit Tests (Base — widest layer):**
   - **Characteristics:** Fast, isolated, automated, code-level.
   - **Advantages:** Quick feedback, easy to debug, low cost.
   - **Disadvantages:** Doesn't catch integration issues.

2. **Service/API Tests (Middle layer):**
   - **Characteristics:** Tests business logic via API.
   - **Advantages:** Faster than UI, stable.
   - **Disadvantages:** Requires API access, may miss UI issues.

3. **UI / End-to-End Tests (Top — narrowest layer):**
   - **Characteristics:** Slow, brittle, test the full stack.
   - **Advantages:** High confidence, tests real user flows.
   - **Disadvantages:** Slow execution, high maintenance, expensive.

**Why a pyramid?** Because the lower the level, the **faster, cheaper, and more reliable** the tests are. UI tests are kept minimal to avoid flakiness.

**Marking Scheme:** 3 marks for concept + 2 marks for layer description + 2 marks for advantages/disadvantages.

---

**Model Answer for (b):**

**Step 1: Identify Conditions and Actions**

- **Conditions:**
  - C1: User has valid membership (T/F)
  - C2: User has returned the previous book (T/F)
  - C3: Book count ≤ 5 (T/F)
- **Actions:**
  - A1: Issue Book
  - A2: Reject Request

**Step 2: Compute number of rules**

$$
N_{rules} = 2^3 = 8
$$

**Step 3: Construct Decision Table**

| Rule | C1: Valid Membership | C2: Previous Book Returned | C3: Count Within Limit | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | T | T | T | Issue Book |
| R2 | T | T | F | Reject |
| R3 | T | F | T | Reject |
| R4 | T | F | F | Reject |
| R5 | F | T | T | Reject |
| R6 | F | T | F | Reject |
| R7 | F | F | T | Reject |
| R8 | F | F | F | Reject |

**Explanation:** Only when ALL three conditions are TRUE (Rule R1) is the book issued. Any single condition being FALSE results in rejection. This is a strict "AND" business rule.

**Marking Scheme:** 1.5 marks for conditions identification + 1.5 marks for rule count formula + 4 marks for correct table.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Smoke vs Sanity:** Students often define them as "the same". They are NOT. Smoke = wide/shallow on new build. Sanity = narrow/deep on a fix.
> 2. **Missing the BVA count:** The 6-value rule (a−1, a, a+1, b−1, b, b+1) is mandatory. Forgetting b+1 loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Decision table missing actions:** Many students only fill the conditions. **Always include the action column.**
> 4. **Test levels confusion:** "Acceptance testing is done by developers" is WRONG. UAT is done by end users.
> 5. **No diagram in long answers:** Always include a **Test Pyramid** or **Process Flow** diagram in 14-mark answers to secure 1–2 extra marks.
> 6. **EP without BVA = incomplete answer:** Most 7-mark questions ask for BOTH EP and BVA combined. Don't miss either.
> 7. **No test case table:** When asked to "design test cases", students describe them in text. **Always present a tabular format** with TC#, Input, Expected Output, and Technique.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Functional Testing**

- [x] **Definition:** Functional Testing = Black-box testing that validates the *functional requirements* of the system (what it does).
- [x] **Test Pyramid (Bottom to Top):** Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance.
- [x] **Test Levels:**
  - **Unit:** Developer, smallest unit, JUnit.
  - **Integration:** Interaction between modules. Big-Bang, Top-Down, Bottom-Up, Sandwich.
  - **System:** Independent QA, end-to-end, against SRS.
  - **Acceptance:** End-user, UAT/OAT/CAT/RAT, Alpha/Beta.
- [x] **Smoke:** Wide + shallow, on new build, build verification.
- [x] **Sanity:** Narrow + deep, on a specific fix.
- [x] **Regression:** After any change, ensure nothing broke.
- [x] **Re-Testing:** Re-run a failed test after a fix.
- [x] **EP:** Divide input into equivalent classes. 1 valid + 1+ invalid classes.
- [x] **BVA:** Test 6 values for closed interval [a, b]: a−1, a, a+1, b−1, b, b+1.
- [x] **Decision Table:** $N_{rules} = 2^n$ rules for n conditions.
- [x] **State Transition:** Test all valid transitions + invalid events at each state.
- [x] **Use Case Testing:** Test from actor-scenario perspective.
- [x] **Key Formulae:** Statement Coverage, Branch Coverage, Defect Density.
- [x] **Difference:** Functional (What) vs Non-Functional (How well).
- [x] **Tools:** JUnit, Selenium, QTP/UFT, TestRail, Postman.
- [x] **Diagram to Memorize:** Test Pyramid + Test Process Flow.
- [x] **Common Mistake:** Saying UAT is done by developers (WRONG — by end users).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non Functional Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non Functional Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Functional Testing (NFT)** is a type of software testing that verifies the **non-functional attributes** of a system — characteristics such as *performance, usability, reliability, security, portability, and compatibility* — rather than specific functional behaviors. It answers the question: **"How WELL does the system work?"** as opposed to *"Does it work?"*

According to the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board)** standard adopted by KTU's testing syllabus, non-functional testing is a sub-category of **system testing** that measures and verifies parameters which are **not directly related to a specific function or user action**, but rather to the overall quality of the product across dimensions defined by software quality standards such as **ISO/IEC 25010**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Buying a Car**
> Imagine you test-drive a car. **Functional testing** checks whether the car *starts, moves forward, brakes, and turns the steering wheel* (does each part do its job?). **Non-Functional Testing** checks:
> - **Performance:** How fast does it go from 0 to 100 km/h?
> - **Load:** Can it carry 5 passengers plus luggage up a hill?
> - **Stress:** What happens if you overload the trunk with 500 kg?
> - **Usability:** Is the dashboard easy to read at night?
> - **Security:** Can a thief break in within 60 seconds?
> - **Reliability:** Will the engine still work after 100,000 km?

The car *functions*, but NFT tells you **how good the experience is** under various real-world conditions.

## 1.3 Key Quality Attributes (ISO/IEC 25010 Standard)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following **eight quality characteristics** of non-functional testing:

| # | Attribute | What it Measures |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Performance** | Response time, throughput under expected workload |
| 2 | **Load Handling** | Behavior under anticipated peak user/concurrent load |
| 3 | **Stress Tolerance** | Behavior beyond peak limits (breaking point) |
| 4 | **Scalability** | Ability to scale up/down with growing demands |
| 5 | **Security** | Resistance to unauthorized access, attacks |
| 6 | **Usability** | Ease of use, learnability, user satisfaction |
| 7 | **Compatibility** | Behavior across browsers, OS, devices |
| 8 | **Reliability/Recovery** | Stability over time, failure recovery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome **CO1** of PEITT862 expects students to *"demonstrate fundamental knowledge of software testing and quality assurance,"* making non-functional testing a **Module 1 high-priority** topic for short-answer and descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Non-Functional Testing Types

Non-functional testing is not a single activity — it is a **family of testing techniques**, each targeting a specific quality attribute.

### 2.1.1 Performance Testing
- **Goal:** Determine the system's performance metrics (response time, throughput) under expected workload.
- **Core Idea:** Measure baseline performance in a controlled, repeatable environment.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used by SREs (Site Reliability Engineers) to establish **SLAs (Service Level Agreements)** — e.g., *"99th percentile response time must be below 300 ms."*

### 2.1.2 Load Testing
- **Goal:** Verify system behavior under **anticipated peak load** (normal + slightly elevated).
- **Why it matters:** Most production failures occur at peak business hours (e.g., 9 AM login rush, festival sale traffic).
- **Difference from Performance:** Load testing is a *subset* of performance testing that focuses on **volume**.

### 2.1.3 Stress Testing
- **Goal:** Push the system **beyond its designed capacity** to identify the **breaking point** and observe graceful degradation.
- **Why it matters:** Reveals how the system fails (crash hard vs. queue requests) and whether it **recovers** automatically.

### 2.1.4 Spike Testing
- **Goal:** Apply **sudden, dramatic spikes** in load (e.g., 10x traffic in 5 seconds) to test elasticity.

### 2.1.5 Endurance (Soak) Testing
- **Goal:** Run a moderate load for an **extended duration** (hours/days) to detect **memory leaks, resource exhaustion, and degradation**.

### 2.1.6 Security Testing
- **Goal:** Identify vulnerabilities, test authentication, authorization, data encryption, and resistance to attacks (SQL injection, XSS, CSRF).
- **Sub-types:** Penetration testing, vulnerability scanning, ethical hacking.

### 2.1.7 Usability Testing
- **Goal:** Evaluate how intuitive, efficient, and satisfying the system is for real users.
- **Methods:** Task completion time, error rates, SUS (System Usability Scale) questionnaires.

### 2.1.8 Compatibility Testing
- **Goal:** Ensure the software works across **browsers, operating systems, devices, network conditions, and hardware configurations**.

### 2.1.9 Recovery Testing
- **Goal:** Verify the system can **recover from crashes, hardware failures, or network drops** and resume operations with acceptable data integrity.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Metrics Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **core engineering metrics** that the KTU board examiner expects in any performance/load testing numerical or descriptive question. Memorize the units carefully.

| Metric | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|--------|--------|----------------------|------|
| **Response Time** | $T_{r}$ | $T_{r} = T_{end} - T_{start}$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| **Throughput** | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{req}}{T_{total}}$ | requests/sec (RPS) |
| **Latency** | $L$ | Time for first byte to reach client | ms |
| **Concurrent Users** | $N_{u}$ | Active users sending requests at time $t$ | users |
| **Hits per Second** | $HPS$ | $\dfrac{Total\,HTTP\,requests}{Second}$ | hits/s |
| **Transactions per Second** | $TPS$ | $\dfrac{Total\,business\,transactions}{Second}$ | tx/s |
| **Error Rate** | $E_{r}$ | $E_{r} = \dfrac{N_{failed}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | percent (%) |
| **CPU Utilization** | $U_{cpu}$ | $\dfrac{CPU_{used}}{CPU_{total}} \times 100$ | percent (%) |
| **Memory Utilization** | $U_{mem}$ | $\dfrac{Mem_{used}}{Mem_{total}} \times 100$ | percent (%) |
| **Mean Time To Failure** | $MTTF$ | $\dfrac{Total\,operating\,time}{N_{failures}}$ | hours |
| **Mean Time To Recover** | $MTTR$ | $\dfrac{Total\,downtime}{N_{failures}}$ | hours |
| **Availability** | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100$ | percent (%) |

## 2.3 Relationship: Performance vs. Load vs. Stress

The **"Why" and "How"** behind the distinction:

- **Performance Testing** is the *umbrella* — it sets the baseline.
- **Load Testing** is performance testing **at expected peak** (the "happy path" of capacity).
- **Stress Testing** is performance testing **beyond peak** (failure path discovery).
- **Endurance Testing** is performance testing **at sustained moderate load over time** (durability check).
- **Spike Testing** is performance testing **with sudden bursts** (elasticity check).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These distinctions matter because they map to different **operational risks**:
> - Load testing prevents **"Black Friday crashes"** (e-commerce).
> - Stress testing prevents **catastrophic data loss** at the breaking point.
> - Endurance testing prevents **silent slow-death bugs** (memory leaks that show up at hour 72).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Availability Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation is a **frequently asked KTU numerical problem**. You will be expected to compute availability given MTTF and MTTR.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A web server has a **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** of **2000 hours** and a **Mean Time To Recover (MTTR)** of **2 hours**. Compute its **Availability** as a percentage.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given values.**

$$MTTF = 2000 \text{ hours}$$

$$MTTR = 2 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Recall the Availability formula from the metrics table.**

$$A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values into the formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{2000}{2000 + 2} \times 100 \\
  &= \frac{2000}{2002} \times 100 \\
  &= 0.999001 \times 100 \\
  &= 99.9001\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Round and interpret.**

$$A \approx 99.90\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** This is roughly equivalent to a **"three nines"** availability SLA — the server is down approximately **43.8 minutes per month**, which is acceptable for most business applications but insufficient for mission-critical systems (which need **99.999% — five nines**).

### 3.1.3 Downtime Calculation (Extension)

Total hours in a month ≈ **720 hours**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Monthly Downtime} &= (1 - A) \times T_{month} \\
&= (1 - 0.9990) \times 720 \\
&= 0.001 \times 720 \\
&= 0.72 \text{ hours} \\
&= 43.2 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Throughput & Response Time Relationship

### 3.2.1 Little's Law (Queuing Theory Foundation)

For a stable system, the average number of users in the system ($N$) equals the throughput ($\lambda$) multiplied by the average response time ($T_r$):

$$N = \lambda \times T_r$$

**Rearranging for Throughput:**

$$\lambda = \frac{N}{T_r}$$

### 3.2.2 Worked Example

A web application has **500 concurrent users** with an average **response time of 2 seconds**. What is the throughput?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{N}{T_r} \\
&= \frac{500 \text{ users}}{2 \text{ sec}} \\
&= 250 \text{ requests/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
