> **KTU Exam Tip:** Little's Law is the *single most testable formula* on this topic. It demonstrates the **inverse relationship** between response time and throughput: if response time doubles, throughput halves (with constant user load).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Test Selection Decision Table

The KTU examiner often gives a **scenario** and asks students to identify the correct non-functional test type. The following decision matrix resolves common confusion:

| Scenario Clue in Question | Correct NFT Type | Justification Keyword |
|---------------------------|------------------|------------------------|
| "5000 users logging in simultaneously" | **Load Testing** | "Simultaneously" / "Expected peak" |
| "What happens if 50,000 users hit the system?" | **Stress Testing** | "Beyond designed capacity" |
| "Server crashes after 48 hours of normal use" | **Endurance Testing** | "Extended duration" / "Memory leak suspected" |
| "Traffic goes from 100 to 10,000 in 1 minute" | **Spike Testing** | "Sudden burst" |
| "App must work on Chrome, Firefox, Safari" | **Compatibility Testing** | "Different browsers/platforms" |
| "SQL injection in login form" | **Security Testing** | "Unauthorized access" / "Vulnerability" |
| "User cannot find the checkout button" | **Usability Testing** | "Ease of use" / "User experience" |
| "System fails when DB goes down — does it recover?" | **Recovery Testing** | "Recover from failure" |
| "Page takes 8 seconds to load — measure baseline" | **Performance Testing** | "Measure response/throughput" |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Functional vs. Non-Functional Testing

| Aspect | Functional Testing | Non-Functional Testing |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| **Question Answered** | "Does it work?" | "How well does it work?" |
| **Focus** | Specific features/functions | System-wide quality attributes |
| **Executed At** | Unit, Integration, System, UAT levels | Mostly System & Acceptance levels |
| **Test Basis** | Requirements/Specifications | Design, Architecture, Standards (ISO 25010) |
| **Ease of Manual Test** | Easier to execute manually | Usually requires automation + tools |
| **Examples** | Login, Add-to-cart, Search | Response time, Load capacity, Encryption strength |
| **Output** | Pass/Fail per requirement | Numerical metrics (ms, RPS, %) |
| **Customer Concern** | Functional requirements | Quality of experience (QoE) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Logic:** The KTU rubric for descriptive answers explicitly rewards students who can **contrast** functional and non-functional testing in a **tabular or bullet format**. This table is high-yield.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy Diagram: Non-Functional Testing Classification

The following Mermaid block renders a hierarchical taxonomy of all major non-functional testing types, mapped to their parent quality attributes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[NON FUNCTIONAL TESTING] --> B1[Performance Family]
    A --> B2[Security Family]
    A --> B3[User Experience Family]
    A --> B4[Compatibility Family]
    A --> B5[Reliability Family]
    B1 --> C1[Load Testing]
    B1 --> C2[Stress Testing]
    B1 --> C3[Spike Testing]
    B1 --> C4[Endurance Testing]
    B1 --> C5[Volume Testing]
    B2 --> C6[Penetration Testing]
    B2 --> C7[Vulnerability Scanning]
    B2 --> C8[Security Auditing]
    B3 --> C9[Usability Testing]
    B3 --> C10[Accessibility Testing]
    B4 --> C11[Browser Compatibility]
    B4 --> C12[OS Compatibility]
    B4 --> C13[Device Compatibility]
    B4 --> C14[Network Compatibility]
    B5 --> C15[Recovery Testing]
    B5 --> C16[Reliability Testing]
    B5 --> C17[Failover Testing]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram: Non-Functional Test Lifecycle

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **standard KTU-recognized workflow** for executing a non-functional test cycle (e.g., load testing a website).

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[Step1 Identify Quality Attribute] --> step2[Step2 Define Workload Model]
    step2 --> step3[Step3 Design Test Scenarios]
    step3 --> step4[Step4 Select Test Tools]
    step4 --> step5[Step5 Prepare Test Environment]
    step5 --> step6[Step6 Configure Test Data]
    step6 --> step7[Step7 Execute Test Load]
    step7 --> step8[Step8 Capture Metrics]
    step8 --> step9[Step9 Analyze Results]
    step9 --> step10{Regression Pass}
    step10 -- Yes --> step11[Generate NFT Report]
    step10 -- No --> step3
    step11 --> step12[Sign Off with SLA Verification]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Load Testing Tool Stack

The following Mermaid block shows the **functional architecture** of a typical load testing tool (such as JMeter or LoadRunner) and how the various components interact during a test run.

```mermaid
graph TD
    block1[BLOCK1 Test Script Repository] --> block2[BLOCK2 Script Editor and Validator]
    block2 --> block3[BLOCK3 Load Generator Cluster]
    block3 --> block4[BLOCK4 Virtual User Simulator]
    block4 --> block5[BLOCK5 Target System Under Test SUT]
    block5 --> block6[BLOCK6 Response Time Sampler]
    block6 --> block7[BLOCK7 Metrics Collector]
    block3 --> block7
    block7 --> block8[BLOCK8 Real Time Dashboard]
    block7 --> block9[BLOCK9 Raw Logs and CSV Files]
    block9 --> block10[BLOCK10 Report Generator]
    block10 --> block11[BLOCK11 Final NFT Analysis Report]
    block8 --> block10
```

## 4.4 Comparative Flow: Performance vs. Load vs. Stress (Visual Scale)

The following Mermaid block visualizes the **load intensity spectrum** across the three most commonly confused NFT types.

```mermaid
graph LR
    low[LOW Zero Load] --> p[PERFORMANCE BASELINE]
    p --> n[NORMAL Load]
    n --> l[LOAD TESTING Expected Peak]
    l --> s[STRESS TESTING Beyond Peak]
    s --> br[BREAKING POINT]
    br --> e[ENDURANCE REGION Sustained Moderate Load]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEITT862]`

**Q: Define Non-Functional Testing. List any four non-functional attributes tested under ISO/IEC 25010.**

**Model Answer:**

> Non-Functional Testing is a type of software testing performed to verify the **non-functional aspects** of a system — performance, usability, reliability, etc. — rather than specific functions. It evaluates **"how well"** the system operates under various conditions.

**Four Non-Functional Attributes (ISO/IEC 25010):**

1. **Performance Efficiency** — Response time, throughput, resource utilization.
2. **Compatibility** — Ability to coexist with other systems (browsers, OS).
3. **Usability** — Ease of learning, operability, user error protection.
4. **Reliability** — Maturity, availability, fault tolerance, recoverability.

*(Other acceptable: Security, Maintainability, Portability)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEITT862]`

**Q: Differentiate between Load Testing and Stress Testing with an example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Load Testing | Stress Testing |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Purpose** | Measure system behavior at **expected peak load** | Determine behavior **beyond** designed limits |
| **Load Level** | Normal to peak (e.g., 1000 users) | Above peak (e.g., 10,000 users) |
| **Objective** | Verify SLAs are met | Find breaking point & recovery |
| **Example** | E-commerce site during a normal sale day | E-commerce site during a flash sale with 10x traffic |

> Load testing answers *"Will the system handle our normal busy day?"* while stress testing answers *"What happens when the system is pushed past its limits?"*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEITT862]`

**Q (a)** Explain in detail the various **types of non-functional testing** with their objectives. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A web server has MTTF = 3000 hours and MTTR = 3 hours. Calculate its **availability and monthly downtime**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Types of Non-Functional Testing

**1. Performance Testing**
- **Objective:** Measure response time, throughput, and resource utilization under expected workload.
- **Example Tools:** Apache JMeter, LoadRunner.

**2. Load Testing**
- **Objective:** Verify system performance under anticipated peak concurrent user load.

**3. Stress Testing**
- **Objective:** Push the system beyond its limits to identify the breaking point and assess graceful failure.

**4. Spike Testing**
- **Objective:** Validate system elasticity when subjected to sudden, extreme bursts of load.

**5. Endurance (Soak) Testing**
- **Objective:** Detect memory leaks and resource exhaustion by running moderate load for extended hours.

**6. Security Testing**
- **Objective:** Identify vulnerabilities, test authentication, encryption, and resistance to common attacks (XSS, SQLi, CSRF).

**7. Usability Testing**
- **Objective:** Measure ease of use, intuitiveness, and user satisfaction via task completion metrics and SUS scoring.

**8. Compatibility Testing**
- **Objective:** Ensure consistent behavior across browsers, devices, OS, and network conditions.

**9. Recovery Testing**
- **Objective:** Verify the system can recover from crashes/failures and resume operation with data integrity.

> All these tests are part of **system testing** in the KTU test level hierarchy.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical Problem

**Given:**

$$MTTF = 3000 \text{ hours}$$

$$MTTR = 3 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Availability Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100 \\
  &= \frac{3000}{3000 + 3} \times 100 \\
  &= \frac{3000}{3003} \times 100 \\
  &= 0.99900 \times 100 \\
  &= 99.90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Unavailability (Downtime Fraction):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{frac} &= 1 - A \\
         &= 1 - 0.9990 \\
         &= 0.001
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Monthly Downtime (Assume 720 hours/month):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{month} &= D_{frac} \times 720 \\
          &= 0.001 \times 720 \\
          &= 0.72 \text{ hours} \\
          &= 43.2 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{A = 99.90\% \quad ; \quad D_{month} = 43.2 \text{ minutes}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not** forget to **convert decimal to percentage** (multiply by 100) — students commonly lose 1 mark here.
> - **Do not** confuse *monthly downtime* with *annual downtime* — read the question carefully.
> - **Do not** leave the answer in fraction form like "3000/3003" — always simplify and express as a percentage.
> - If the question gives MTTF in days, **convert to hours first** (×24).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEITT862]`

**Q (a)** Discuss the **metrics and parameters** used in performance testing with appropriate formulas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **process of conducting a load test** in detail, including tools used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Performance Testing Metrics

The following **quantitative metrics** are measured during performance testing:

**1. Response Time** ($T_r$)

$$T_r = T_{end} - T_{start}$$

Measured in **milliseconds (ms)**. Lower is better.

**2. Throughput** ($\lambda$)

$$\lambda = \frac{N_{req}}{T_{total}}$$

Measured in **requests/second (RPS)**. Higher is better.

**3. Latency** ($L$)

Time delay between request dispatch and first byte received. Measured in **ms**.

**4. Concurrent Users** ($N_u$)

Number of users simultaneously active during the test.

**5. Error Rate** ($E_r$)

$$E_r = \frac{N_{failed}}{N_{total}} \times 100$$

Measured in **percent (%)**. Should be close to 0%.

**6. CPU Utilization** ($U_{cpu}$)

$$U_{cpu} = \frac{CPU_{used}}{CPU_{total}} \times 100$$

Sustained > 85% indicates a **bottleneck**.

**7. Memory Utilization** ($U_{mem}$)

$$U_{mem} = \frac{Mem_{used}}{Mem_{total}} \times 100$$

A steadily increasing value during endurance testing indicates a **memory leak**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** These metrics directly drive SLA definitions. For example, *"99th percentile response time ≤ 500 ms with 0.1% error rate at 5000 concurrent users."*

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Process of Conducting a Load Test

The **standard 7-phase load testing process** recognized by the KTU syllabus is:

**Phase 1 — Identify the Test Objectives**
- Define the quality attribute (response time? throughput? both?).
- Identify the **SLAs** to be validated.

**Phase 2 — Design the Workload Model**
- Define user personas, think time, ramp-up, and ramp-down curves.
- Use **operational profiles** (e.g., 60% browse, 30% search, 10% checkout).

**Phase 3 — Prepare the Test Environment**
- Mirror production hardware, network, and database configuration.
- Use **disposable test data** — never use production data.

**Phase 4 — Select a Load Testing Tool**
- Popular open-source: **Apache JMeter, Gatling, Locust**.
- Commercial: **Micro Focus LoadRunner, NeoLoad**.

**Phase 5 — Author the Test Scripts**
- Record or script the user journeys.
- Add **parameterization** (dynamic data) and **correlation** (dynamic values).

**Phase 6 — Execute the Test**
- Run with controlled ramp-up (e.g., 0 → 1000 users over 10 minutes).
- **Monitor in real time** using dashboards.

**Phase 7 — Analyze and Report**
- Capture all metrics, compare against SLAs.
- Generate an **NFT report** with recommendations (tune DB, scale horizontally, cache, etc.).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not** list tools without **explaining their role** in the process — explaining the *workflow* matters more than naming tools.
> - **Do not** skip the **test environment preparation** phase — examiners often give 1 mark specifically for this.
> - **Do not** forget **think time** and **ramp-up** — these are the two most-missed workload-modeling parameters.
> - In (a), students often **forget the unit** of each metric — always mention **ms, RPS, or %** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Definition:** Non-functional testing verifies **HOW WELL** a system works (quality attributes), not *whether* it works (functional).
- ✅ **ISO/IEC 25010** is the standard quality model — memorize at least 5 characteristics.
- ✅ **Performance ≠ Load ≠ Stress:** Performance is the umbrella; Load is expected peak; Stress is beyond peak.
- ✅ **Endurance Testing** = long-duration moderate load (catches **memory leaks**).
- ✅ **Spike Testing** = sudden burst load (catches **elasticity** issues).
- ✅ **Availability Formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100$ — must be expressed in **%**.
- ✅ **Little's Law:** $N = \lambda \times T_r$ — relationship between users, throughput, and response time.
- ✅ **Error Rate Formula:** $E_r = \dfrac{N_{failed}}{N_{total}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Most commonly confused pair:** Load Testing vs. Stress Testing — use the keyword *"expected peak"* vs. *"beyond capacity"*.
- ✅ **Tools to remember:** JMeter (open-source), LoadRunner (commercial), Gatling, Locust.
- ✅ **Process Order:** Objectives → Workload Model → Environment → Tools → Scripts → Execute → Analyze.
- ✅ **Exam Pattern:** Part A = 3-mark definition/comparison; Part B = 14-mark with 7+7 sub-parts, expect at least one **numerical problem** (availability or throughput).
- ✅ **Board-marker-friendly:** Always include **tabular comparisons** and **numbered lists** for sub-parts in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Avoid common pitfalls:** forgetting the ×100 in percentage formulas, mixing up MTTF/MTTR units, and skipping test environment prep phase in process questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SDLC and STLC Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** SDLC and STLC Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (SDLC):** The **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** is a structured, phased framework that defines the entire process of planning, designing, building, testing, deploying, and maintaining a software system, ensuring software quality, predictability, and adherence to stakeholder requirements from inception to retirement.

The SDLC is essentially the **master blueprint** for building any software product. It is a process model used by project managers, developers, and testers alike.

**Conceptual Analogy — Building a House 🏠:**
Think of SDLC as the entire process of building a house — from the moment an architect sketches the blueprint (Requirement Analysis), to laying the foundation (Design), to constructing the walls (Coding), to inspecting every room (Testing), to handing over the keys (Deployment), and finally maintaining it over years (Maintenance). You cannot skip steps; a weak foundation means the entire house collapses.

### 1.2 What is STLC (Software Testing Life Cycle)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (STLC):** The **Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC)** is a systematic, sequential series of well-defined testing activities performed during the software development process to ensure software quality. STLC details the **phases, entry/exit criteria, deliverables, and techniques** specifically used by the Quality Assurance (QA) team.

If SDLC is building the house, STLC is the **dedicated inspection and quality-assurance process** running parallelly — the engineer who checks soil quality, tests concrete strength, verifies wiring, and ensures every brick is sound. STLC is a **subset of SDLC** but with much greater detail on testing-specific activities.

### 1.3 Why Study Their Relationship?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Understanding the relationship between SDLC and STLC is critical because:
> - It determines **when** testing activities begin within a project.
> - It governs **how test artifacts** (test plans, test cases) are aligned with development deliverables.
> - It influences the choice of **software development model** (Waterfall, V-Model, Agile, Iterative).
> - It directly impacts the **Cost of Defect Fixing** — defects caught early in SDLC are exponentially cheaper to fix.

### 1.4 The Core Conceptual Link

| Aspect | SDLC | STLC |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Entire project life | Testing sub-process |
| **Stakeholders** | Dev, BA, PM, QA, Ops | Primarily QA team |
| **Phases** | 6–7 broad phases | 6 detailed test phases |
| **Goal** | Deliver working software | Deliver defect-free software |
| **Hierarchy** | Parent process | Child / Embedded process |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Phases of SDLC (Standard KTU Framework)

SDLC is divided into the following canonical phases. Every software model — Waterfall, Iterative, V-Model, Agile — is a variation of these phases:

1. **Requirement Gathering & Analysis**
   - *Why:* Understand *what* the customer needs.
   - *Activities:* Stakeholder interviews, SRS (Software Requirement Specification) document creation.
   - *Output:* SRS document, feasibility report.

2. **System Design**
   - *Why:* Define *how* the system will be built.
   - *Activities:* HLD (High-Level Design) and LLD (Low-Level Design).
   - *Output:* System architecture diagrams, database schema.

3. **Implementation / Coding**
   - *Why:* Translate design into executable code.
   - *Activities:* Developers write source code in chosen programming language.
   - *Output:* Source code modules, unit test results.

4. **Testing**
   - *Why:* Verify that the software meets requirements and is defect-free.
   - *Activities:* Execute test cases, log defects, perform regression.
   - *Output:* Test reports, defect logs, RTM (Requirement Traceability Matrix).

5. **Deployment**
   - *Why:* Release the product to the production environment.
   - *Activities:* Installation, configuration, user training.
   - *Output:* Live production system.

6. **Maintenance**
   - *Why:* Handle post-release issues, enhancements, and patches.
   - *Activities:* Bug fixes, performance tuning, version upgrades.
   - *Output:* Updated software versions, service packs.

### 2.2 The Phases of STLC (Standard KTU Framework)

STLC is more granular because it focuses exclusively on QA activities. It contains the following six phases with strict **Entry Criteria** and **Exit Criteria** (essential for KTU 14-mark questions):

1. **Requirement Phase Testing**
   - *Goal:* Identify testable requirements and review the SRS.
   - *Entry Criteria:* Approved SRS / BRS document.
   - *Exit Criteria:* Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM) drafted.
   - *Activities:* Review requirements for ambiguity, testability, and completeness.

2. **Test Planning Phase**
   - *Goal:* Define the test strategy, scope, resources, schedule, and tools.
   - *Entry Criteria:* RTM, requirements documents.
   - *Exit Criteria:* Approved Test Plan document.
   - *Activities:* Effort estimation, risk analysis, resource planning.
   - *Output:* **Test Plan document** (the master testing artifact).

3. **Test Case Development Phase**
   - *Goal:* Design detailed test cases and test data.
   - *Entry Criteria:* Approved Test Plan.
   - *Exit Criteria:* Test cases, test scripts, test data reviewed and approved.
   - *Activities:* Write test cases, prepare test data, automate scripts (if applicable).

4. **Test Environment Setup Phase**
   - *Goal:* Establish a stable environment mirroring production.
   - *Entry Criteria:* Test case readiness, environment requirements.
   - *Exit Criteria:* Smoke test passes on the test environment.
   - *Activities:* Install software, configure servers, populate test data.

5. **Test Execution Phase**
   - *Goal:* Execute test cases and log defects.
   - *Entry Criteria:* Test cases ready, build deployed in test environment.
   - *Exit Criteria:* All test cases executed, defect logs prepared.
   - *Activities:* Manual/automated execution, defect reporting, retesting.

6. **Test Cycle Closure Phase**
   - *Goal:* Evaluate testing completeness and gather lessons learned.
   - *Entry Criteria:* Test execution complete.
   - *Exit Criteria:* Test Closure Report signed off.
   - *Activities:* Defect analysis, test metrics calculation, lessons learned.

### 2.3 The KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all key KTU 2024 high-yield concepts for this topic. **No vertical pipe `|` characters are used inside cells to preserve table rendering.**

| Concept | Definition / Standard Metric | KTU Exam Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **SDLC** | Software Development Life Cycle | Parent framework for entire project |
| **STLC** | Software Testing Life Cycle | Sub-process of SDLC focused on QA |
| **V-Model** | Extension of Waterfall where each dev phase has a parallel test phase | KTU-favorite diagram in 14-mark questions |
| **Entry Criteria** | Pre-conditions that must be met **before** a phase begins | KTU frequently asks these in 3-mark questions |
| **Exit Criteria** | Conditions that must be satisfied to **close** a phase | Often confused with Entry Criteria in exams |
| **Defect Cost Multiplier** | Cost of fixing a defect grows **exponentially** with phase progression | Classic KTU theory question |
| **RTM (Requirement Traceability Matrix)** | A table mapping requirements → test cases | Common KTU question on STLC outputs |
| **Shift-Left Testing** | Beginning testing activities earlier in the SDLC | Modern KTU 2024 syllabus concept |
| **Test Plan** | Master document defining test strategy | Key deliverable of Test Planning phase |
| **Test Closure Report** | Final document summarizing testing outcome | Deliverable of STLC closure phase |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **software industry**:
- SDLC frameworks like **Agile (Scrum)** and **DevOps** rely heavily on tight SDLC-STLC integration to deliver continuous, reliable software.
- In **safety-critical domains** (aerospace, medical devices, automotive ISO 26262), the V-Model variant of SDLC-STLC relationship is legally mandated because of strict verification and validation requirements at every phase.
- **Cost optimization:** Companies that align STLC tightly with SDLC (Shift-Left) reduce post-release defect fixing cost by up to **100x** compared to late-stage testing models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Logical Derivation: Why STLC is Embedded in SDLC

Let us mathematically and logically derive the relationship between SDLC and STLC.

**Step 1 — Define the SDLC Universe:**

The total effort of a software project can be expressed as:

$$
E_{SDLC} = E_{Req} + E_{Design} + E_{Code} + E_{Test} + E_{Deploy} + E_{Maintain}
$$

where each $E_i$ represents the effort expended in that phase.

**Step 2 — Define the STLC Universe:**

The total testing effort can be expressed as:

$$
E_{STLC} = E_{TPlan} + E_{TCase} + E_{TEnv} + E_{TExec} + E_{TClose}
$$

**Step 3 — Establish the Set-Theoretic Relationship:**

Using set theory, we can express the relationship as:

$$
E_{Test} \subseteq E_{SDLC} \quad \text{and} \quad E_{STLC} \subset E_{SDLC}
$$

This means the **testing effort is a proper subset of the total SDLC effort**, but STLC — being a more detailed breakdown of testing alone — is also a subset of the testing portion of SDLC.

**Step 4 — Translate to the Cost-of-Quality (CoQ) Equation:**

The cost of producing quality software has two components:

$$
C_{Quality} = C_{Prevention} + C_{Appraisal} + C_{Failure}
$$

where:
- $C_{Prevention}$ = cost of preventing defects (reviews, training, design checks)
- $C_{Appraisal}$ = cost of detecting defects (testing activities, inspections)
- $C_{Failure}$ = cost of defects reaching the customer (warranty, patches, brand damage)

**Step 5 — The Defect Cost Multiplier (KTU 2024 Highlight):**

If a defect is introduced in the **requirement phase** and discovered in the **maintenance phase**, its cost grows as:

$$
C_{Defect}^{Maintenance} = 100 \cdot C_{Defect}^{Requirements}
$$

$$
C_{Defect}^{Testing} \approx 10 \cdot C_{Defect}^{Coding}
$$

$$
C_{Defect}^{Design} \approx 5 \cdot C_{Defect}^{Coding}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the central mathematical justification for **Shift-Left Testing** — performing STLC activities as early as possible in the SDLC to minimize the cost of defect fixing.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SDLC Phase vs STLC Phase Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the most frequently tested analytical table in KTU 2024. It maps the **testing activity** that occurs at each **development phase**.

| SDLC Phase | Corresponding STLC Activity | Why This Mapping? |
|---|---|---|
| Requirement Gathering | Requirement Phase Testing (RTM creation) | Verifies testability of requirements |
| System Design | Test Planning (high-level strategy) | Defines what to test when coding begins |
| Coding | Test Case Development + Unit Test design | Testers prepare cases while developers code |
| Module Integration | Test Environment Setup + Integration Test design | Environment must be ready for integration tests |
| System Testing | Test Execution (System, Regression, UAT) | Bulk of test execution happens here |
| Deployment | User Acceptance Testing (UAT) | Final validation before go-live |
| Maintenance | Test Cycle Closure + Regression cycles | Ensures fixes do not break existing features |

### 3.3 Detailed Comparison: SDLC vs STLC

| Parameter | SDLC | STLC |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Entire software process | Testing sub-process |
| **Who performs** | Developers, BAs, PMs, Testers | Primarily Testers / QA Engineers |
| **Phases count** | 6 broad phases | 6 detailed test phases |
| **Goal** | Build a working software system | Ensure the built system is defect-free |
| **Entry/Exit Criteria** | Defined per phase | **More strictly defined** per phase |
| **Deliverables** | SRS, Design doc, Code, Build | Test Plan, Test Cases, Defect Log, Closure Report |
| **Cost focus** | Total project cost | Cost of Quality (CoQ) |
| **Relation** | Parent process | Subset of SDLC |

### 3.4 Integration Models — How SDLC and STLC Connect

There are three primary integration patterns:

1. **Sequential (Waterfall Style):**
   Testing begins only **after** coding is complete. STLC starts at the SDLC testing phase. High cost of late defect detection.

2. **Parallel (V-Model Style):**
   Each SDLC development phase has a corresponding STLC verification phase. This is the **gold standard** for safety-critical systems and is most commonly asked in KTU 14-mark questions.

3. **Iterative (Agile / Scrum Style):**
   STLC activities (planning, designing, executing) are performed **within every sprint**. Testing is continuous and integrated into development. Used in modern KTU 2024 industry context.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The V-Model — Visualizing SDLC-STLC Relationship

The V-Model is the **canonical KTU diagram** for this topic. It visually demonstrates that each development phase on the left leg of the V has a corresponding testing phase on the right leg of the V, with the **coding phase** sitting at the bottom vertex of the V.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef devPhase fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef testPhase fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef vertex fill:#ffe7ba,stroke:#d46b08,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    REQ["REQUIREMENT ANALYSIS"]:::devPhase
    SYS["SYSTEM DESIGN"]:::devPhase
    ARCH["ARCHITECTURE DESIGN"]:::devPhase
    MOD["MODULE DESIGN"]:::devPhase
    COD["CODING"]:::vertex

    UAT["USER ACCEPTANCE TESTING"]:::testPhase
    SYS_T["SYSTEM TESTING"]:::testPhase
    INT_T["INTEGRATION TESTING"]:::testPhase
    UNIT_T["UNIT TESTING"]:::testPhase

    REQ --> SYS --> ARCH --> MOD --> COD
    COD --> UNIT_T --> INT_T --> SYS_T --> UAT

    REQ -. Verifies .-> UAT
    SYS -. Verifies .-> SYS_T
    ARCH -. Verifies .-> INT_T
    MOD -. Verifies .-> UNIT_T
```

**Reading the V-Model:**
- The **left leg (descending)** = SDLC development phases.
- The **right leg (ascending)** = STLC verification phases.
- The **dashed lines** show that each test phase on the right verifies the corresponding development phase on the left.
- For example, **Unit Testing** verifies the **Module Design + Coding**, and **User Acceptance Testing** validates the **Requirement Analysis**.

### 4.2 STLC Phase Flow Diagram (Detailed Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef stlc fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#1890ff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#ffa940,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    A["1 REQUIREMENT PHASE"]:::stlc
    B["2 TEST PLANNING"]:::stlc
    C["3 TEST CASE DEVELOPMENT"]:::stlc
    D["4 TEST ENVIRONMENT SETUP"]:::stlc
    E["5 TEST EXECUTION"]:::stlc
    F["6 TEST CYCLE CLOSURE"]:::stlc

    O1["RTM Document"]:::output
    O2["Test Plan"]:::output
    O3["Test Cases and Scripts"]:::output
    O4["Test Environment Ready"]:::output
    O5["Defect Logs and Status Reports"]:::output
    O6["Test Closure Report"]:::output

    A --> O1 --> B --> O2 --> C --> O3 --> D --> O4 --> E --> O5 --> F --> O6
```

### 4.3 SDLC-STLC Integration Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef sdlc fill:#f6ffed,stroke:#52c41a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stlc fill:#e6f7ff,stroke:#1890ff,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef shared fill:#fff0f6,stroke:#c41d7f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    subgraph SDLC["SDLC FRAMEWORK"]
        S1["1 Requirement Analysis"]:::sdlc
        S2["2 System Design"]:::sdlc
        S3["3 Implementation Coding"]:::sdlc
        S4["4 Testing"]:::sdlc
        S5["5 Deployment"]:::sdlc
        S6["6 Maintenance"]:::sdlc
    end

    subgraph STLC["STLC FRAMEWORK"]
        T1["1 Requirement Phase"]:::stlc
        T2["2 Test Planning"]:::stlc
        T3["3 Test Case Design"]:::stlc
        T4["4 Environment Setup"]:::stlc
        T5["5 Test Execution"]:::stlc
        T6["6 Test Closure"]:::stlc
    end

    SHARED["Shared Activities: Reviews, RTM, Risk Analysis"]:::shared

    S1 --- T1
    S2 --- T2
    S3 --- T3
    S4 --- T4
    S4 --- T5
    S5 --- T5
    S6 --- T6

    STLC -. Embedded in .-> SDLC
    SHARED -. Underpins .-> SDLC
    SHARED -. Underpins .-> STLC
```

**Interpretation:** STLC phases are **not a separate linear process** — they are embedded within corresponding SDLC phases. The shared foundation includes requirement reviews, traceability, and risk analysis common to both life cycles.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define SDLC and STLC. List any two key differences between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle):** A structured framework that defines the entire software creation process from requirement gathering to maintenance, involving phases like Requirement, Design, Coding, Testing, Deployment, and Maintenance. *(1 mark)*
- **STLC (Software Testing Life Cycle):** A subset of SDLC that focuses exclusively on testing activities, comprising six phases: Requirement Analysis, Test Planning, Test Case Development, Environment Setup, Test Execution, and Test Cycle Closure. *(1 mark)*
- **Key differences:** *(1 mark)*
  1. SDLC is performed by the entire project team, while STLC is performed primarily by the QA/Test team.
  2. STLC has stricter entry and exit criteria for each phase compared to SDLC.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What are Entry Criteria and Exit Criteria in STLC? Why are they important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Entry Criteria** are the pre-conditions that must be satisfied **before** starting a particular STLC phase. Example: A signed-off Test Plan is an entry criterion for the Test Case Development phase. *(1 mark)*
- **Exit Criteria** are the conditions that must be met to **conclude** a phase and move to the next. Example: All high-priority test cases executed with a 95% pass rate is an exit criterion for Test Execution. *(1 mark)*
- **Importance:** They ensure disciplined, measurable progress, prevent premature phase transitions, and provide objective gates for the QA manager to make go/no-go decisions. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In 14-mark SDLC-STLC questions, students commonly **lose 3–4 marks** by:
> 1. Confusing the V-Model with the Waterfall model (V-Model shows parallel test phases; Waterfall is purely sequential).
> 2. Listing STLC phases without explaining entry/exit criteria.
> 3. Drawing the V-Model incorrectly (left leg development, right leg testing — these are **MUST not be reversed**).
> 4. Not mentioning the cost-of-defect multiplier — a favorite 2-mark KTU addition.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) in detail. List all phases with their activities and outputs. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC) in detail. Discuss entry and exit criteria for each phase. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — SDLC Phases (7 marks):**

SDLC is the structured process used to design, develop, test, and deploy high-quality software. It consists of six phases: *(0.5 mark for intro)*

1. **Requirement Gathering & Analysis:** Stakeholder interviews are conducted to gather business and user requirements. The output is the **SRS (Software Requirement Specification)** document. *(1 mark)*
2. **System Design:** Translates SRS into a system architecture. Two sub-activities — **HLD (High-Level Design)** for overall architecture, and **LLD (Low-Level Design)** for module-level logic. Output: Design documents, ER diagrams. *(1 mark)*
3. **Implementation / Coding:** Developers write code in the chosen programming language. Output: Source code, build artifacts. *(1 mark)*
4. **Testing:** The QA team executes test cases, identifies defects, and verifies fixes. Includes unit, integration, system, and acceptance testing. Output: Defect logs, test reports. *(1 mark)*
5. **Deployment:** The validated software is installed in the production environment. User training is conducted. Output: Live system. *(1 mark)*
6. **Maintenance:** Post-deployment support, including bug fixes, performance tuning, and version upgrades. Output: Patches, service packs. *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution for (b) — STLC Phases with Entry/Exit (7 marks):**

STLC is a systematic, six-phase testing process that is a subset of SDLC: *(0.5 mark for intro)*

1. **Requirement Phase:** Reviews requirements for testability. *Entry:* Approved BRS/SRS. *Exit:* RTM drafted. Output: RTM. *(1 mark)*
2. **Test Planning:** Defines test strategy, scope, resources, and schedule. *Entry:* RTM, requirements. *Exit:* Approved Test Plan. Output: **Test Plan document**. *(1 mark)*
3. **Test Case Development:** Detailed test cases and test data are written. *Entry:* Approved Test Plan. *Exit:* Reviewed test cases. Output: Test cases, test scripts, test data. *(1 mark)*
4. **Test Environment Setup:** Configures hardware, software, and network to mirror production. *Entry:* Test cases ready, environment specs. *Exit:* Successful smoke test. Output: Test environment. *(1 mark)*
5. **Test Execution:** Test cases are run; results are logged; defects are filed. *Entry:* Test cases ready, build deployed. *Exit:* All test cases executed, defect summary ready. Output: Defect logs, execution status report. *(1 mark)*
6. **Test Cycle Closure:** Evaluates testing completeness, calculates metrics, and documents lessons learned. *Entry:* Execution complete. *Exit:* Signed-off closure report. Output: **Test Closure Report**. *(1 mark)*

The strict entry/exit criteria make STLC a **disciplined, measurable, and traceable** process within the broader SDLC. *(0.5 mark for conclusion)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the V-Model of software development. How does it establish a clear relationship between SDLC and STLC? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare SDLC and STLC. Explain the concept of Shift-Left Testing and its significance in modern software engineering. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a) — The V-Model (7 marks):**

The V-Model (Verification and Validation Model) is an extension of the Waterfall model where each development phase on the left leg of the V is directly associated with a corresponding testing phase on the right leg. *(0.5 mark for intro)*

**Left Leg — SDLC Development Phases (Verification):** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Requirement Analysis** → produces SRS.
- **System Design** → produces HLD.
- **Architecture Design** → produces detailed architecture.
- **Module Design** → produces LLD.
- **Coding** → produces source code.

**Right Leg — STLC Testing Phases (Validation):** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Unit Testing** → verifies Module Design and Coding.
- **Integration Testing** → verifies Architecture Design.
- **System Testing** → verifies System Design.
- **User Acceptance Testing** → validates original Requirements.

**Relationship Established:** The V-Model establishes a 1:1 mapping where the testing phase on the right is explicitly linked to the development phase on the left. Each test plan for a phase is prepared **in parallel** with the corresponding development phase, not after it. This means testing activities begin at the very start of the project (during requirements), not at the end. *(2 marks)*

**Diagram:** Draw the V-shape with the four development phases descending on the left, Coding at the bottom vertex, and the four testing phases ascending on the right. Dashed horizontal lines should connect corresponding phases (Requirement ↔ UAT, System Design ↔ System Test, Architecture ↔ Integration Test, Module Design ↔ Unit Test). *(1.5 marks for neat labeled diagram)*

**Model Solution for (b) — SDLC vs STLC & Shift-Left (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table:** *(3 marks)*

| Parameter | SDLC | STLC |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Entire software project | Testing sub-process |
| Phases | 6 (Req, Design, Code, Test, Deploy, Maintain) | 6 (Req, Plan, Case, Env, Exec, Close) |
| Stakeholders | Whole project team | QA team |
| Goal | Deliver working software | Deliver defect-free software |
| Relation | Parent | Subset of SDLC |

**Shift-Left Testing Concept:** *(2.5 marks)*
Shift-Left Testing is the practice of moving testing activities **earlier** in the SDLC — to the left of the traditional timeline. Instead of beginning testing only after coding is complete, testers are involved in requirement reviews, design inspections, and early test case design from day one. This is supported by practices like **TDD (Test-Driven Development)**, **BDD (Behavior-Driven Development)**, and **CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery)**.

**Significance:** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Cost reduction:** Defects caught during requirements are 100x cheaper to fix than those caught post-deployment.
- **Faster feedback:** Developers get immediate feedback, reducing rework.
- **Higher quality:** Continuous testing improves overall product reliability.
- **Alignment with Agile/DevOps:** Shift-Left is foundational to modern iterative development models.
- **Customer satisfaction:** Fewer post-release defects translate to better user experience.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **SDLC** = Parent process with **6 broad phases**: Requirement, Design, Coding, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance.
- ✅ **STLC** = Testing sub-process with **6 detailed phases**: Requirement, Test Planning, Test Case Development, Environment Setup, Test Execution, Test Closure.
- ✅ STLC is a **subset (embedded process)** of SDLC, not a separate or parallel process.
- ✅ **Entry Criteria** = conditions to **start** a phase; **Exit Criteria** = conditions to **end** a phase.
- ✅ The **V-Model** is the most important diagram — left leg = development, right leg = testing, vertex = coding.
- ✅ **Defect cost multiplier:** Requirements-phase defect fixed in maintenance = 100x more expensive than fixing it during requirements.
- ✅ **Shift-Left Testing** = begin STLC activities as early as possible in the SDLC; foundation of Agile and DevOps.
- ✅ Key STLC deliverables to memorize: **Test Plan** (from Test Planning), **Test Cases** (from Test Case Development), **Defect Logs** (from Test Execution), **Test Closure Report** (from Test Closure).
- ✅ **RTM (Requirement Traceability Matrix)** is created in the first STLC phase and updated throughout.
- ✅ KTU 14-mark question pattern almost always combines: **Diagram (V-Model)** + **STLC phases with entry/exit** + **comparison with SDLC**.
- ✅ Always conclude STLC discussion by stating: STLC ensures **quality is built in**, not inspected in at the end.
- ✅ Agile/Scrum is the modern SDLC-STLC integration model where testing happens **within every sprint**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Testing: Black Box Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Testing: Black Box Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Black-Box Testing** is a dynamic software testing technique in which the software under test (SUT) is exercised without knowledge of its internal code structure, implementation details, internal data structures, or programming logic. Test cases are derived exclusively from the **software requirements specification (SRS)**, **design documents**, or **functional specifications** to validate input–output behavior, functional correctness, and conformance to user expectations.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Black-Box Testing is classified as a **specification-based** (or **behavioral**) testing methodology and is formally grouped under **Dynamic Testing** because the software is actively executed during the test cycle — distinguishing it from static review techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The course mandates a deep understanding of *Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)*, *Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)*, *Decision Table Testing*, *State Transition Testing*, and *Error Guessing* — all of which are specification-based Black-Box techniques.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Black Box Mystery Box":**
> Imagine a sealed, opaque vending machine in a public hallway. You cannot see its gears, wiring, or coin-collecting mechanism (the *internal code/structure*). However, you know it accepts coins (an *input*) and dispenses the beverage you select (the *expected output*). 
> 
> You test it by: inserting ₹10, pressing **B2**, and verifying a Pepsi comes out. If it dispenses a Sprite, the vending machine has a **defect** — even though its internal mechanism is perfectly intact. You, as the tester, are performing **Black-Box Testing** — you only care about the *external observable behavior* matching the requirement.

## 1.3 Why Black-Box Testing is Essential in the Software Lifecycle

- **Customer-Aligned:** Tests mirror real user interactions and business workflows.
- **Unbiased Validation:** Independent testers (who did not write the code) can find defects developers overlook (Egoless Programming principle).
- **Requirement Validation:** Catches ambiguities, omissions, and contradictions in the SRS itself.
- **Scalable for Large Systems:** Does not require source code access, making it ideal for **commercial off-the-shelf (COTS)** software, **third-party APIs**, and **system integration testing (SIT)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Misconception:** *"Black-Box Testing cannot find structural defects."* — This is **FALSE**. While it focuses on external behavior, it inherently exercises internal code paths. A wrongly implemented internal loop will surface as a Black-Box failure (e.g., incorrect output, hang, or crash).

## 1.4 Black-Box vs. White-Box vs. Grey-Box — A Quick Comparative Glance

| Dimension | **Black-Box** | **White-Box** | **Grey-Box** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Knowledge of internals | **None** | Full code access | Partial (architecture/DB) |
| Test basis | Requirements/Specs | Source code | Both |
| Performed by | Testers / Users | Developers | Testers with code insight |
| KTU Technique Examples | ECP, BVA, Decision Table | Statement, Branch, Path | Penetration, Matrix Testing |
| Phase of Use | System, Acceptance, UAT | Unit, Integration | Integration, Security |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Methodology — The Black-Box Test Cycle

The Black-Box testing life-cycle is executed in the following structured, KTU-aligned steps:

1. **Requirement Analysis:** The tester studies the SRS, use cases, user stories, and acceptance criteria.
2. **Input Domain Identification:** The set of all *legitimate* and *illegitimate* inputs is enumerated.
3. **Test Case Design:** Test cases are derived using one or more of the formal Black-Box techniques (ECP, BVA, Decision Table, etc.).
4. **Test Oracle Construction:** An *oracle* (a mechanism to determine expected output) is defined — e.g., lookup table, requirement statement, or business rule.
5. **Test Execution:** Inputs are fed to the SUT, and actual outputs are recorded.
6. **Result Comparison & Defect Reporting:** Actual vs. expected outputs are compared; deviations generate defect reports.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Black-Box Technique

### 2.2.1 Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)

**Why it exists:** Exhaustively testing every possible input (e.g., integers from 0 to 2³²–1) is infeasible. We assume that if one input in a class reveals a defect, all inputs in that class will likely reveal the same defect.

**How it works:**
- Divide the entire input domain into disjoint **equivalence classes (ECs)** such that:
  - All members of an EC are treated *equally* by the program.
  - If one test case in an EC passes, the entire EC is assumed to pass.
- Classes are of two types:
  - **Valid EC:** A set of inputs that the program *should accept*.
  - **Invalid EC:** A set of inputs that the program *should reject* (each invalid class should be tested separately to ensure it is rejected, not just one combined invalid case).

### 2.2.2 Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

**Why it exists:** Decades of defect data show that **~60–80% of input-handling defects occur at the boundaries** of equivalence classes (off-by-one errors, threshold issues, loop edge cases). ECP alone misses these because it tests *representative* values, not *edges*.

**How it works:**
- For each EC, test values at the **boundaries**: `min`, `min+1`, `nominal`, `max-1`, `max`.
- The **Robust BVA** variant also tests `min-1` and `max+1` to confirm graceful rejection.

### 2.2.3 Decision Table Testing (Cause-Effect Graphing)

**Why it exists:** Complex business logic often involves *combinations* of inputs and conditions, which ECP/BVA cannot model systematically.

**How it works:**
- Identify **causes** (input conditions) and **effects** (output actions).
- Construct a **Decision Table** with rows for condition combinations and columns for action triggers.
- Each column becomes a test case.

### 2.2.4 State Transition Testing

**Why it exists:** Some systems behave differently based on their *history* (state). The same input can yield different outputs at different states (e.g., an ATM: a $100 withdrawal behaves differently in the "Card Inserted" state vs. "PIN Verified" state).

**How it works:**
- Model the system as a **Finite State Machine (FSM)** with states and transitions.
- Test **valid sequences**, **invalid sequences**, and **all transitions**.

### 2.2.5 Error Guessing

**Why it exists:** Experienced testers intuitively know where defects "hide" — division by zero, empty fields, leap years, null pointers, etc.

**How it works:** A non-formal, experience-driven technique to enumerate *common error conditions* and craft test cases to expose them.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the formulas below — these appear frequently in KTU 2024 scheme ESE questions.**

| **#** | **Technique** | **Key Formula / Rule** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ECP — Min Test Cases** | $T_{ECP} = N_{valid} + N_{invalid}$ | Minimum tests = 1 per valid class + 1 per invalid class |
| 2 | **BVA — Standard (2-point)** | $T_{BVA} = 4 \cdot N_{var} \cdot N_{class}$ | 4 boundary values × variables × boundary classes |
| 3 | **BVA — Robust (3-point)** | $T_{Robust} = 6 \cdot N_{var} \cdot N_{class}$ | Includes on/just-above/just-below boundary |
| 4 | **Decision Table — Columns** | $T_{DT} = 2^{N_{conditions}}$ | Max test cases = all combinations of conditions |
| 5 | **State Transition — 0-switch** | $T_{0} = N_{states}$ | One test per state (0 transitions covered) |
| 6 | **State Transition — 1-switch** | $T_{1} = N_{transitions}$ | One test per transition |
| 7 | **State Transition — N-switch** | $T_{n} = N_{seq}$ where $seq$ is sequence length $n+1$ | All length-$(n+1)$ transition sequences |
| 8 | **Cyclomatic Complexity (White-Box ref.)** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Independent paths (reference for test completeness) |
| 9 | **Branch Coverage Upper Bound** | $BC \le 2^{N_{decisions}}$ | Max branches in a decision tree |
| 10 | **Defect Density** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{Size (KLOC or FP)}}$ | Quality metric from Black-Box runs |

> Where:
> - $N_{valid}$ / $N_{invalid}$ = number of valid / invalid equivalence classes
> - $N_{var}$ = number of input variables
> - $N_{class}$ = number of boundary classes per variable
> - $N_{states}$ = number of states in the FSM
> - $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = connected components in CFG

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Black-Box techniques are foundational to:
- **API Testing (REST/SOAP):** Validates endpoint behavior without examining server source.
- **Cloud SaaS Validation:** Tests multi-tenant web apps from the user’s perspective.
- **Embedded Systems:** Tests sensor-actuator I/O behavior against specification.
- **AI/ML Model Testing:** Validates model *outputs* against labeled expectations (input-output validation).
- **Cybersecurity:** Fuzz testing and penetration testing use Black-Box principles to probe external attack surfaces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Test Case Count for ECP + BVA

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A login form accepts a password with the rule: *6 ≤ length ≤ 12 characters, alphanumeric only*. Find the minimum test cases using ECP, then extend to BVA, and compute the total under Robust BVA.

### Step 1 — Equivalence Class Partitioning

Define the input domain for the password length variable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Valid EC}_1 &: \quad 6 \le L \le 12 \quad \text{(accepted lengths)} \\
\text{Invalid EC}_2 &: \quad L < 6 \quad \text{(too short)} \\
\text{Invalid EC}_3 &: \quad L > 12 \quad \text{(too long)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Pick one representative from each class:
- Representative of EC₁ → L = **8** (nominal, alphanumeric)
- Representative of EC₂ → L = **3** (invalid)
- Representative of EC₃ → L = **15** (invalid)

**Apply the formula** $T_{ECP} = N_{valid} + N_{invalid}$:

$$
T_{ECP} = 1 + 2 = 3 \text{ test cases}
$$

**Conversion logic:** We have 1 valid class and 2 invalid classes, each represented by one test case.

### Step 2 — Boundary Value Analysis (Standard 3-point BVA)

Boundary values for variable L with edges at **6** and **12**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower boundary} &: \quad 5,\ \ 6,\ \ 7 \\
\text{Upper boundary} &: \quad 11,\ \ 12,\ \ 13
\end{aligned}
$$

Pick one representative per boundary cluster (on, just-above, just-below):
- 5, 6, 7, 11, 12, 13

**Apply** $T_{BVA} = 4 \cdot N_{var} \cdot N_{class}$ where $N_{var}=1$ and $N_{class}=2$ (lower & upper):

$$
T_{BVA} = 4 \cdot 1 \cdot 2 = 8 \text{ test cases}
$$

But the actual unique values we enumerated are 6. In practice, $T_{BVA}$ counts test *cases* (with expected outcomes), and overlapping boundary checks can be merged — yielding the 6 unique BVA test cases here.

### Step 3 — Robust BVA Extension

Add the *off-limit* values beyond the boundaries: **5 (invalid)** and **13 (invalid)** are already included. Robust BVA emphasizes testing `min-1` and `max+1`, which in our case are **5** and **13** — already represented.

**Total minimum test cases (ECP ∪ BVA):**

$$
T_{total} = T_{ECP} + T_{RobustBVA \setminus ECP} = 3 + 3 = 6 \text{ unique test cases}
$$

### Step 4 — Decision Table Extension (Adding a "Special Character" condition)

If the rule extends to: *"Password must be 6–12 chars, alphanumeric, and contain at least one digit"*, then we have **2 conditions**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &: \text{Length valid?} \\
C_2 &: \text{Contains digit?}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total combinations:

$$
T_{DT} = 2^{N_{conditions}} = 2^2 = 4 \text{ test cases}
$$

This is the **maximum** test set — actual count may be reduced by eliminating impossible combinations via the *cause-effect graph*.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Black-Box Technique Selection Guide

> [!TIP]
> Use this matrix to identify the *right* Black-Box technique for a given scenario. This is a high-yield KTU question type.

| **Scenario / Symptom** | **Best Black-Box Technique** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| Range-based input validation (e.g., age 18–60) | **BVA** | Defects cluster at range edges |
| Boolean flag combinations in business rules | **Decision Table** | Models all logical interactions |
| Workflow with discrete phases (Login → Cart → Pay) | **State Transition** | State-dependent behavior |
| Large, continuous numeric input space | **ECP** | Reduces infinite inputs to finite classes |
| Testing for null/empty/zero input handling | **Error Guessing** | Heuristic experience-based |
| Validation of multi-field form submissions | **Decision Table + BVA** | Combinations of boundaries |

## 3.3 Defect Detection Yield Estimation (Industry-Standard Heuristic)

> [!NOTE]
> This heuristic is *not* typically required in KTU exams, but it provides the **engineering utility** of why Black-Box testing matters quantitatively.

Industry defect distribution after multiple test cycles follows a *defect discovery decay curve* (Goel-Okumoto or similar):

$$
D(t) = a \left( 1 - e^{-bt} \right)
$$

Where:
- $D(t)$ = cumulative defects found by time $t$
- $a$ = total latent defects in the system
- $b$ = defect detection rate (Black-Box effectiveness)

After test cycle $n$:

$$
\text{Yield}(n) = \dfrac{D(n)}{a} \times 100\%
$$

A well-designed **ECP + BVA + Decision Table** suite typically achieves **70–85%** defect yield in the first cycle, with State Transition adding another 5–10% for workflow-heavy systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Black-Box Testing — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["SRS Document"] --> node2["Identify Input Domain"]
    node2 --> node3["Apply Black Box Technique"]
    node3 --> node4["ECP Partitioning"]
    node3 --> node5["BVA Boundaries"]
    node3 --> node6["Decision Table"]
    node3 --> node7["State Transition"]
    node3 --> node8["Error Guessing"]
    node4 --> node9["Generate Test Cases"]
    node5 --> node9
    node6 --> node9
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10["Test Oracle Definition"]
    node10 --> node11["Execute on SUT"]
    node11 --> node12{"Actual equals Expected"}
    node12 -- Yes --> node13["Test PASS"]
    node12 -- No --> node14["Log Defect Report"]
    node14 --> node15["Defect Triage"]
    node15 --> node16["Fix and Retest"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow starts from the requirements document, fans out into the five formal techniques, converges into a unified test case repository, and finally executes each test case against the SUT. The decision diamond branches into PASS or DEFECT paths — the latter initiating the bug-fixing cycle.

## 4.2 Equivalence Class Partitioning — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Input Domain X"] --> node2["Valid EC 1"]
    node1 --> node3["Invalid EC 2"]
    node1 --> node4["Invalid EC 3"]
    node2 --> node5["Pick 1 Representative"]
    node3 --> node6["Pick 1 Representative"]
    node4 --> node7["Pick 1 Representative"]
    node5 --> node8["Test Case A"]
    node6 --> node9["Test Case B"]
    node7 --> node10["Test Case C"]
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — ATM Withdrawal Example

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CardInserted : insert_card
    CardInserted --> PINVerified : correct_pin
    CardInserted --> CardRetained : wrong_pin_3x
    PINVerified --> TransactionSelected : select_action
    TransactionSelected --> Dispensing : withdraw_request
    Dispensing --> Idle : cash_dispensed
    Dispensing --> InsufficientFunds : no_balance
    InsufficientFunds --> Idle : return_to_menu
    CardRetained --> [*]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** State diagrams are often tested via 0-switch, 1-switch, and N-switch coverage formulas. The ATM example above has **5 states** and **6 valid transitions** — yielding $T_0 = 5$ and $T_1 = 6$ test cases respectively.

## 4.4 Decision Table Template — Loan Eligibility Example

| **Conditions / Rules** | **R1** | **R2** | **R3** | **R4** | **R5** | **R6** | **R7** | **R8** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1: Age ≥ 21 | T | T | T | T | F | F | F | F |
| C2: Income ≥ ₹30,000 | T | T | F | F | T | T | F | F |
| C3: Credit Score ≥ 700 | T | F | T | F | T | F | T | F |
| **A1: Approve Loan** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **A2: Reject (Low Income)** | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | — | — | — | — |
| **A3: Reject (Low Credit)** | — | ✓ | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **A4: Reject (Underage)** | — | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> The table above shows the **complete decision table** for a simplified loan eligibility system. Each column (R1–R8) becomes one test case. Columns that are logically impossible (e.g., "Reject for both income AND credit when both are low" — already covered) are often collapsed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between Black-Box Testing and White-Box Testing. List any two advantages of Black-Box Testing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Black-Box Testing** is a specification-based testing approach in which the internal structure, code, and implementation of the software are *not known* to the tester. Test cases are derived from the **SRS/requirements**. In contrast, **White-Box Testing** is a structure-based approach where the tester has full access to the source code and designs test cases to exercise internal paths, branches, and conditions.

| **Basis** | **Black-Box** | **White-Box** |
|---|---|---|
| Test Basis | Requirements / Specs | Source Code |
| Tester | Independent QA | Developer |
| Defects Found | Missing/wrong functions | Logic errors, dead code |
| Techniques | ECP, BVA, Decision Table | Statement, Branch, Path |

**Two advantages of Black-Box Testing:**
1. **No programming knowledge required** — testers, domain experts, and end-users can perform it.
2. **Validates user perspective** — tests the system as a *real user* would, ensuring requirements are truly met.
3. *(Bonus)* Effective for **large and external systems** where source code is unavailable (e.g., third-party APIs, COTS software). **(2 marks)**
4. *(Bonus)* Encourages **egoless testing** since developers are not testing their own code. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Explain the concept of Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP) with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**ECP** is a Black-Box testing technique that partitions the input domain into a set of disjoint **equivalence classes (ECs)** such that testing one representative value from each class is assumed sufficient to represent the entire class. This reduces the *infinite* input space into a *finite*, manageable set of test cases.

**Rule of Thumb:**
- If the input condition specifies a **range** (e.g., 1–100), one valid class and two invalid classes (< 1, > 100) are derived.
- If the input condition specifies a **specific value** (e.g., "Y" or "N"), one valid class containing valid values and one invalid class for other values are derived.
- If the input is a **member of a set** (e.g., {A, B, C}), the set itself is the valid class and the complement is the invalid class.

**Example:** *A function accepts an integer between 1 and 100.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Valid EC} &: \quad 1 \le x \le 100 \\
\text{Invalid EC}_1 &: \quad x < 1 \\
\text{Invalid EC}_2 &: \quad x > 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Test cases: pick `x = 50` (valid), `x = 0` (invalid), `x = 150` (invalid). Only **3 test cases** are required to cover the entire domain. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Format**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**A university's online registration system requires the following:**

- A student can register for courses only if the following conditions are met:
  1. **Age** must be between **17 and 25** (inclusive).
  2. **Prior credits earned** must be **≥ 20**.
  3. **Course load** must be between **15 and 24 credits** (inclusive).
- If any of the above are violated, the system should display an appropriate error message.
- If all are satisfied, the system confirms registration.

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Identify the **Equivalence Classes** for all three input variables and design a minimal **ECP-based test suite**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Apply **Boundary Value Analysis** to derive the test cases, and construct a **Decision Table** for the registration validation logic. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — ECP Analysis \[7 Marks\]

**Step 1: Identify Equivalence Classes**

For **Age (A)** — Range constraint $17 \le A \le 25$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Valid EC}_{A1} &: 17 \le A \le 25 \quad \text{(representative: A = 21)} \\
\text{Invalid EC}_{A2} &: A < 17 \quad \text{(representative: A = 15)} \\
\text{Invalid EC}_{A3} &: A > 25 \quad \text{(representative: A = 30)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Prior Credits (PC)** — Constraint $PC \ge 20$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Valid EC}_{PC1} &: PC \ge 20 \quad \text{(representative: PC = 25)} \\
\text{Invalid EC}_{PC2} &: PC < 20 \quad \text{(representative: PC = 10)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Course Load (CL)** — Range constraint $15 \le CL \le 24$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Valid EC}_{CL1} &: 15 \le CL \le 24 \quad \text{(representative: CL = 18)} \\
\text{Invalid EC}_{CL2} &: CL < 15 \quad \text{(representative: CL = 10)} \\
\text{Invalid EC}_{CL3} &: CL > 24 \quad \text{(representative: CL = 30)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Formula**

Total valid classes $N_{valid} = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3$
Total invalid classes $N_{invalid} = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5$

$$
T_{ECP} = N_{valid} + N_{invalid} = 3 + 5 = \mathbf{8 \text{ test cases}}
$$

| TC# | Age | Prior Credits | Course Load | Expected |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TC1 | 21 | 25 | 18 | Registration confirmed |
| TC2 | 15 | 25 | 18 | Error: Age invalid |
| TC3 | 30 | 25 | 18 | Error: Age invalid |
| TC4 | 21 | 10 | 18 | Error: Prior Credits invalid |
| TC5 | 21 | 25 | 10 | Error: Course Load invalid |
| TC6 | 21 | 25 | 30 | Error: Course Load invalid |
| TC7 | 15 | 10 | 10 | Error: All invalid |
| TC8 | 30 | 10 | 30 | Error: All invalid |

> **Conversion logic:** Each invalid class is tested *individually* (per KTU standard) to ensure correct error handling — never combined into a single "all-invalid" case for ECP purposes.

### Part (b) — BVA + Decision Table \[7 Marks\]

**Step 1: Boundary Values**

For **Age** with bounds 17 and 25: test **16, 17, 18, 24, 25, 26**.
For **Prior Credits** with bound 20: test **19, 20, 21**.
For **Course Load** with bounds 15 and 24: test **14, 15, 16, 23, 24, 25**.

Total BVA test cases (unique values across all three variables):

$$
T_{BVA} = 6 + 3 + 6 = \mathbf{15 \text{ test cases}}
$$

**Step 2: Decision Table**

We have 3 conditions (C1 = Age valid, C2 = PC valid, C3 = CL valid). Maximum combinations:

$$
T_{DT} = 2^3 = \mathbf{8 \text{ test cases}}
$$

| **Rules** | **R1** | **R2** | **R3** | **R4** | **R5** | **R6** | **R7** | **R8** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1: Age valid | T | T | T | T | F | F | F | F |
| C2: PC valid | T | T | F | F | T | T | F | F |
| C3: CL valid | T | F | T | F | T | F | T | F |
| **A1: Register** | ✓ | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| **A2: Age error** | — | — | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **A3: PC error** | — | — | ✓ | ✓ | — | — | — | — |
| **A4: CL error** | — | ✓ | — | — | — | — | — | — |

> **Conversion logic:** R1 is the only success path; R2–R8 each trigger a specific error message. A "consolidated error" rule (e.g., "All conditions invalid → generic error") may also be added in a real system.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**An ATM system has the following specification:**

- The ATM has 5 states: **Idle, Card Inserted, PIN Verified, Transaction Selected, and Dispensing**.
- From *Idle*, inserting a valid card moves to *Card Inserted*.
- From *Card Inserted*, entering the correct PIN moves to *PIN Verified*; three wrong PINs moves to *Card Retained*.
- From *PIN Verified*, the user can select Withdrawal, Balance Check, or Exit.
- From *Transaction Selected*, if Withdrawal is chosen, the system moves to *Dispensing*; otherwise, it returns to *Idle*.
- From *Dispensing*, after cash is dispensed, the system returns to *Idle*.

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Draw the **State Transition Diagram** and list all valid **states** and **transitions**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Compute the minimum test cases for **0-switch, 1-switch, and 2-switch coverage**. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — State Transition Diagram \[7 Marks\]

**States:** {Idle, Card Inserted, PIN Verified, Transaction Selected, Dispensing, Card Retained}

**Transitions (events / actions):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{Idle} \xrightarrow{\text{insert\_card}} \text{Card Inserted} \\
T_2 &: \text{Card Inserted} \xrightarrow{\text{correct\_pin}} \text{PIN Verified} \\
T_3 &: \text{Card Inserted} \xrightarrow{\text{wrong\_pin\_3x}} \text{Card Retained} \\
T_4 &: \text{PIN Verified} \xrightarrow{\text{select\_withdrawal}} \text{Transaction Selected} \\
T_5 &: \text{PIN Verified} \xrightarrow{\text{select\_balance}} \text{Idle} \\
T_6 &: \text{PIN Verified} \xrightarrow{\text{exit}} \text{Idle} \\
T_7 &: \text{Transaction Selected} \xrightarrow{\text{confirm}} \text{Dispensing} \\
T_8 &: \text{Transaction Selected} \xrightarrow{\text{cancel}} \text{Idle} \\
T_9 &: \text{Dispensing} \xrightarrow{\text{cash\_dispensed}} \text{Idle}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **State count:** $N_{states} = 6$ (including Card Retained as a terminal state).
> **Transition count:** $N_{transitions} = 9$.

### Part (b) — Switch Coverage Computation \[7 Marks\]

**0-Switch Coverage** (visit each state at least once):

$$
T_0 = N_{states} = \mathbf{6 \text{ test cases}}
$$

**1-Switch Coverage** (cover every transition at least once):

$$
T_1 = N_{transitions} = \mathbf{9 \text{ test cases}}
$$

**2-Switch Coverage** (cover every pair of consecutive transitions, i.e., length-3 state sequences):

To compute 2-switch, enumerate all valid length-3 sequences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &: \text{Idle} \to \text{Card Inserted} \to \text{PIN Verified} \to \text{Transaction Selected} \\
S_2 &: \text{Card Inserted} \to \text{PIN Verified} \to \text{Transaction Selected} \to \text{Dispensing} \\
S_3 &: \text{Card Inserted} \to \text{PIN Verified} \to \text{Transaction Selected} \to \text{Idle} \\
S_4 &: \text{PIN Verified} \to \text{Transaction Selected} \to \text{Dispensing} \to \text{Idle} \\
S_5 &: \text{Card Inserted} \to \text{PIN Verified} \to \text{Idle} \to \text{Idle (via reinsert)}
\end{aligned}
$$

After eliminating unreachable or redundant sequences, we get:

$$
T_2 \approx \mathbf{5 \text{ unique test cases}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** 2-switch coverage is **expensive** — the number of valid sequences grows combinatorially. In practice, only 0-switch and 1-switch are used in most KTU-mandated test plans.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Black-Box Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting invalid classes** *(–1 to –2 marks)*: ECP requires *one test per invalid class*. Students often lump all invalid inputs into a single "negative" test case, which violates the technique's standard.
> 
> 2. **Confusing ECP with BVA** *(–1 to –3 marks)*: ECP tests *representative* values, BVA tests *boundaries*. Writing a BVA value as an ECP answer is a common error.
> 
> 3. **Missing the test oracle** *(–1 mark)*: When asked to "design test cases", KTU expects an **expected output** column. Skipping it is a partial mark loss.
> 
> 4. **Decision Table inconsistencies** *(–2 marks)*: The "T/F" entries in each row must be *logically consistent* with the action triggered. A rule claiming "Approve" while showing an "F" condition is an instant 0 for that column.
> 
> 5. **State diagram — missing terminal states** *(–1 mark)*: Always include *all* states, including absorbing/terminal states like "Card Retained" or "Session Expired".
> 
> 6. **Formula misuse** *(–1 mark)*: The ECP formula is $T = N_{valid} + N_{invalid}$, **not** $T = N_{valid} \times N_{invalid}$. The latter is a frequent mistake.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Black-Box Testing**
> 
> - ✅ **Definition:** Black-Box Testing is a *specification-based*, *dynamic* technique that validates input–output behavior without code knowledge.
> - ✅ **Five Core Techniques:** ECP, BVA, Decision Table, State Transition, Error Guessing.
> - ✅ **ECP Formula:** $T_{ECP} = N_{valid} + N_{invalid}$ — one representative per equivalence class.
> - ✅ **BVA Rule:** Test at *boundaries*: `min`, `min+1`, `nominal`, `max-1`, `max`. Robust BVA adds `min-1` and `max+1`.
> - ✅ **Decision Table Formula:** $T_{DT} = 2^{N_{conditions}}$ (max combinations).
> - ✅ **State Transition Formulas:** $T_0 = N_{states}$, $T_1 = N_{transitions}$.
> - ✅ **Error Guessing** is *non-formal* — relies on tester experience (e.g., nulls, zero, empty strings, leap years).
> - ✅ **Test Oracle** is *mandatory* — every Black-Box test case must have an expected output.
> - ✅ Black-Box Testing is the **foundation of System, Acceptance, and User Acceptance Testing (UAT)**.
> - ✅ Black-Box and White-Box are *complementary*, not substitutes — together they form the **V-Model's** verification & validation.
> - ✅ **Boundary defects** dominate (60–80% of input-handling bugs) — always prioritize BVA over pure ECP.
> - ✅ **Advantages:** Unbiased, user-centric, no code access needed.
> - ✅ **Disadvantages:** Cannot guarantee *full* coverage; limited defect localization; may repeat tests already covered by White-Box.
> - ✅ **When in doubt on a KTU exam**, label each test case with: **Test ID, Inputs, Expected Output, Actual Output, and Result (Pass/Fail)** — this structure always earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Testing: White Box Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Testing: White Box Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Dynamic Testing: White Box Testing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **White Box Testing (WBT)** — also called *Structural Testing*, *Glass Box Testing*, or *Open Box Testing* — is a dynamic software testing technique in which the tester's knowledge of the **internal structure, design, code, and implementation** of the software under test (SUT) is used to design and execute test cases.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

According to the IEEE Standard 829 and ISTQB glossary aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme, **White Box Testing** is defined as:

> *"Testing conducted with full knowledge of the internal logic/structure of the component/system, and executed against that internal structure to evaluate specific execution paths and internal workings of the software."*

The tester is expected to possess access to:
- **Source Code** (implementation visibility)
- **Control Flow Graphs** (logical navigation)
- **Data Flow Diagrams** (variable tracking)
- **Internal Variables and Data Structures** (state inspection)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Transparent Glass House"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> Imagine you are inspecting a newly built house. In **Black Box Testing**, you walk around the house, check the doors, the lights, the taps — everything from the *outside* without ever entering. In **White Box Testing**, you are given the **architectural blueprints, electrical wiring diagrams, and plumbing schematics**, and you are allowed to walk **inside the walls** with a torch. You verify that every wire is correctly connected, every pipe carries water, every load-bearing wall is structurally sound. Similarly, in White Box Testing, the tester peeks *inside* the code to validate internal logic, not just the external output.

| Aspect | Black Box Testing | White Box Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge Required** | Only requirements/specifications | Source code + design |
| **Tester Persona** | End-user / customer | Developer / QA engineer |
| **Focus** | Input $\rightarrow$ Output behavior | Internal logic, paths, conditions |
| **Also Called** | Functional / Behavioral | Structural / Glass Box |
| **Performed At** | System, Acceptance levels | Unit, Integration levels |
| **Skill Set** | Domain knowledge | Programming + Logic |

### 1.3 Why White Box Testing? (The KTU 2024 Motivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Justifications (must be remembered for 3-mark questions):**
> 1. **Catches hidden defects** that black-box testing cannot reveal (e.g., dead code, unreachable branches, memory leaks).
> 2. **Optimizes code** by exposing inefficient or redundant statements.
> 3. **Validates internal security** — anti-patterns like buffer overflows, SQL injection points, and unhandled exceptions.
> 4. **Enforces thoroughness** through quantitative coverage metrics (**100% coverage** is the gold standard for mission-critical modules).

### 1.4 Classification of White Box Testing (KTU Module 2 Hierarchy)

White Box Testing is broadly divided into the following hierarchical sub-techniques — these form the spine of Module 2:

1. **Unit Testing** — testing individual functions/methods in isolation.
2. **Integration Testing** — testing interactions between integrated units/modules.
3. **Statement Coverage Testing** — executing every executable statement at least once.
4. **Branch / Decision Coverage Testing** — executing every branch outcome.
5. **Condition Coverage Testing** — testing every boolean sub-expression.
6. **Path Coverage Testing** — exercising every independent execution path.
7. **Control Flow Testing** — derived from the program's control flow graph (CFG).
8. **Data Flow Testing** — tracking variable definitions and uses.
9. **Basis Path Testing (McCabe)** — using **Cyclomatic Complexity** $V(G)$ to derive the *basis set* of independent paths.
10. **Mutation Testing** — injecting faults (mutants) to evaluate test suite quality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Coverage Criteria — The Quantitative Backbone of WBT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> White Box Testing is fundamentally a **measurement-driven** discipline. KTU examiners love asking about these coverage metrics because they are quantifiable, mathematical, and directly tied to test thoroughness.

### 2.1.1 Statement Coverage (SC)

**Definition:** The percentage of executable statements in the source code that have been executed by the test suite.

**Why it matters:** Un-executed statements = *dead code* or *unreachable code*, which is a maintenance liability.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Identify the total number of executable statements $N_s$ in the program (exclude comments, blank lines, declarations of constants).
2. Run the test cases and count the number of statements actually executed $N_e$.
3. Compute the ratio.

**KTU High-Yield Formula (remember verbatim):**

$$
\text{Statement Coverage (SC)} = \frac{N_e}{N_s} \times 100\%
$$
