**Limitation:** Statement coverage of 100% does *not* guarantee that all branches (if/else outcomes) have been tested.

### 2.1.2 Branch Coverage (BC) / Decision Coverage (DC)

**Definition:** The percentage of decision outcomes (True/False branches) that have been exercised at least once.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Identify the total number of decision points (each `if`, `while`, `for`, `case`) — let the number of branch outcomes be $2 \times N_d$ (each decision has 2 outcomes).
2. Count branch outcomes actually traversed $N_{bt}$.
3. Compute ratio.

**KTU Formula:**

$$
\text{Branch Coverage (BC)} = \frac{N_{bt}}{2 \times N_d} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Relationship Rule (Examiner's favourite):** Branch Coverage $\geq$ Statement Coverage. Achieving 100% branch coverage usually implies 100% statement coverage, but the converse is **not** true.

### 2.1.3 Condition Coverage (CC)

**Definition:** Every boolean sub-expression (atomic condition) must evaluate to both True and False at least once.

**Example:** For `(a > 5) AND (b < 10)`, the two atomic conditions are `(a > 5)` and `(b < 10)`. Each must take both values T and F.

**KTU Formula:**

$$
\text{Condition Coverage (CC)} = \frac{N_{cc}}{2 \times N_c} \times 100\%
$$

where $N_{cc}$ = number of atomic conditions evaluated in both T and F, and $N_c$ = total atomic conditions.

### 2.1.4 Path Coverage (PC)

**Definition:** Every independent execution path from program entry to exit must be traversed at least once.

**KTU Formula:**

$$
\text{Path Coverage (PC)} = \frac{N_{ep}}{N_{tp}} \times 100\%
$$

where $N_{ep}$ = exercised paths, $N_{tp}$ = total independent paths.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Achieving 100% path coverage is often **infeasible** due to loops. For a loop executing $k$ times, the number of paths can be exponential. KTU typically asks for **Basis Path Coverage** (a tractable approximation).

### 2.2 Cyclomatic Complexity — McCabe's Masterpiece

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$** is the single most important metric in Module 2. KTU has repeated 7-mark and 14-mark questions on this in past University Exams.

**Definition:** Cyclomatic Complexity of a program/module is the number of *linearly independent* paths through the source code, equivalent to the minimum number of test cases required to exercise every path at least once.

**Three Equivalent Formulas (you must memorize all three — KTU examiners alternate):**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

$$
V(G) = \text{Number of Predicate (Decision) Nodes} + 1
$$

$$
V(G) = \text{Number of Regions (enclosed areas)} \text{ in the Control Flow Graph}
$$

where $E$ = number of edges, $N$ = number of nodes, $P$ = number of connected components (usually $P = 1$).

**Numerical Reference Scale (McCabe's Risk Classification):**

| $V(G)$ Value | Risk Level | Test Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 10 | **Simple, low risk** | Normal testing |
| 11 – 20 | **Moderate risk** | Careful testing required |
| 21 – 50 | **High risk, complex** | Refactoring recommended |
| $> 50$ | **Untestable, very high risk** | Must redesign module |

### 2.3 Basis Path Testing (McCabe's Algorithm)

**Definition:** A systematic method to derive a *basis set* of linearly independent execution paths from the source code's CFG, ensuring every statement and branch is exercised.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**
1. Draw the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** from the source code (or PDL/pseudocode).
2. Compute $V(G)$ using any of the three formulas.
3. $V(G)$ = number of paths in the *basis set*.
4. Identify the basis set — paths that introduce at least one new edge/node.
5. Derive test cases that force execution of each basis path.

### 2.4 Mutation Testing (Fault-Based Testing)

**Definition:** A fault-based testing technique where small artificial faults (*mutants*) are deliberately injected into the program, and the test suite is evaluated by its ability to *kill* (detect) these mutants.

**Key Terminology:**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Mutant** | A program copy with a single artificial fault (e.g., `>` changed to `>=`) |
| **Mutation Operator** | The rule that creates mutants (e.g., AOR — Arithmetic Operator Replacement) |
| **Killed Mutant** | A mutant whose output differs from the original program's output — i.e., the test detected the fault |
| **Live Mutant** | A mutant whose output matches the original — i.e., the test **failed** to detect the fault |
| **Mutation Score** | The effectiveness metric (computed below) |

**KTU Formula:**

$$
\text{Mutation Score (MS)} = \frac{M_k}{M_t - M_e} \times 100\%
$$

where $M_k$ = mutants killed, $M_t$ = total mutants, $M_e$ = equivalent mutants (mutants that behave identically to the original by coincidence — these are excluded from the denominator).

### 2.5 Control Flow Testing vs. Data Flow Testing

> [!NOTE]
> **Control Flow Testing:** Tests the *order of execution* — focuses on the sequence in which statements, branches, and loops are executed. Uses the **CFG** as the primary artifact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Flow Testing:** Tests the *lifecycle of variables* — focuses on how data (variables) flows from definition to usage. Uses the concept of **define-use pairs (DU-pairs)**. Anomalies include *uninitialized variable use*, *defined but never used*, *killed before use*.

### 2.6 Unit Testing (Developer-Level WBT)

**Definition:** Testing individual units (functions, methods, classes) in isolation from the rest of the system. Performed typically by the developer who wrote the code.

**Common Unit Testing Frameworks (industry-relevant for KTU viva):**

| Language | Framework |
|---|---|
| Java | JUnit, TestNG |
| Python | pytest, unittest |
| C\# | NUnit, xUnit |
| JavaScript | Jest, Mocha |
| C++ | GoogleTest, Catch2 |

### 2.7 Integration Testing (Module-Level WBT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Integration Testing combines individually tested units and tests them as a group. KTU 2024 Module 2 emphasizes four approaches:

1. **Big-Bang Integration Testing** — All units integrated at once and tested. *Pro:* Quick. *Con:* Hard to isolate failures.
2. **Top-Down Integration Testing** — Tests from the top of the call hierarchy downward using *stubs* (mock implementations of lower modules).
3. **Bottom-Up Integration Testing** — Tests from the bottom upward using *drivers* (simulated calling modules).
4. **Sandwich / Hybrid Integration Testing** — Combines top-down and bottom-up; used for large, hierarchical systems.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics (DO-178C compliance):** Requires structural coverage metrics (SC, BC, MC/DC) to be $\geq 95\%$ — White Box Testing is non-negotiable.
- **Medical Devices (FDA/IEC 62304):** Unit-level white-box testing is mandatory for Class B and C software.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** ASIL-D rated systems demand Modified Condition/Decision Coverage (MC/DC) at 100%.
- **Banking/Fintech:** Mutation testing is increasingly used to validate critical transaction logic.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| Metric | Formula | Key Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|
| Statement Coverage (SC) | $\frac{N_e}{N_s} \times 100\%$ | $N_e$ = executed statements, $N_s$ = total statements |
| Branch Coverage (BC) | $\frac{N_{bt}}{2 \cdot N_d} \times 100\%$ | $N_{bt}$ = branch outcomes traversed, $N_d$ = decisions |
| Condition Coverage (CC) | $\frac{N_{cc}}{2 \cdot N_c} \times 100\%$ | $N_{cc}$ = conditions T+F, $N_c$ = total atomic conditions |
| Path Coverage (PC) | $\frac{N_{ep}}{N_{tp}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{ep}$ = exercised, $N_{tp}$ = total independent |
| Cyclomatic Complexity (Formula 1) | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$ = edges, $N$ = nodes, $P$ = components |
| Cyclomatic Complexity (Formula 2) | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | $\pi$ = predicate nodes |
| Cyclomatic Complexity (Formula 3) | $V(G) = R$ | $R$ = regions in CFG |
| Mutation Score (MS) | $\frac{M_k}{M_t - M_e} \times 100\%$ | $M_k$ = killed, $M_t$ = total, $M_e$ = equivalent |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Cyclomatic Complexity & Basis Path Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a classic KTU 14-mark question pattern.** The following worked example covers almost every formula in the cheat sheet. Master this.

### 3.1.1 The Source Code (Sample Program)

```
PROCEDURE CalcDiscount(price, customerType, isFestival)
    BEGIN
        IF (price > 1000) THEN         // D1
            discount := price * 0.10
        ELSE
            discount := price * 0.05
        ENDIF                          // End of D1
        
        IF (customerType = "PREMIUM") THEN    // D2
            discount := discount + 50
        ENDIF                          // End of D2
        
        IF (isFestival = TRUE) THEN    // D3
            IF (price > 5000) THEN     // D4 (Nested)
                discount := discount + 200
            ENDIF                      // End of D4
        ENDIF                          // End of D3
        
        RETURN discount
    END
```

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Draw the Control Flow Graph (CFG)

We assign each node a number:

- **Node 1:** Start
- **Node 2:** `price > 1000` evaluation (Decision D1)
- **Node 3:** `discount := price * 0.10` (True branch)
- **Node 4:** `discount := price * 0.05` (False branch)
- **Node 5:** `customerType = "PREMIUM"` evaluation (Decision D2)
- **Node 6:** `discount := discount + 50` (True branch)
- **Node 7:** `isFestival = TRUE` evaluation (Decision D3)
- **Node 8:** `price > 5000` evaluation (Decision D4 — nested)
- **Node 9:** `discount := discount + 200` (True branch of D4)
- **Node 10:** `RETURN discount`
- **Node 11:** End

**Edge List (for $V(G)$ calculation):**

$$
E = \{1\!\to\!2,\; 2\!\to\!3,\; 2\!\to\!4,\; 3\!\to\!5,\; 4\!\to\!5,\; 5\!\to\!6,\; 5\!\to\!7,\; 6\!\to\!7,\; 7\!\to\!8,\; 7\!\to\!10,\; 8\!\to\!9,\; 8\!\to\!10,\; 9\!\to\!10,\; 10\!\to\!11\}
$$

Counting: $E = 14$ edges, $N = 11$ nodes, $P = 1$ (single connected graph).

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute $V(G)$ Using All Three Formulas

**Formula 1: $V(G) = E - N + 2P$**

$$
V(G) = 14 - 11 + 2(1) = 14 - 11 + 2 = 5
$$

**Formula 2: $V(G) = \text{Predicate Nodes} + 1$**

The predicate (decision) nodes are D1, D2, D3, D4. So $\pi = 4$.

$$
V(G) = 4 + 1 = 5
$$

**Formula 3: $V(G) = \text{Regions in CFG}$**

Counting the enclosed regions $R$ in the CFG (excluding the outer unbounded region) — we get $R = 5$.

$$
V(G) = 5
$$

**Verification:** All three methods yield $V(G) = 5$ ✓

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Derive the Basis Set of Independent Paths

A *basis path* is a path that introduces at least one **new edge** not previously traversed by earlier paths in the set. The basis set has exactly $V(G) = 5$ paths.

| Path | Sequence | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Path 1** | $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 5 \to 7 \to 10 \to 11$ | All True branches (D1-T, D2-T skipped via D2-F, D3-T, D4-F) — minimal execution |
| **Path 2** | $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 5 \to 6 \to 7 \to 10 \to 11$ | D1-T, D2-T, D3-T, D4-F |
| **Path 3** | $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5 \to 7 \to 10 \to 11$ | D1-F branch (new edge $2 \to 4$) |
| **Path 4** | $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 5 \to 7 \to 8 \to 9 \to 10 \to 11$ | D1-T, D3-T, D4-T (new edge $8 \to 9$) |
| **Path 5** | $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 5 \to 7 \to 8 \to 10 \to 11$ | D1-T, D3-T, D4-F (new edge $8 \to 10$) |

**Total independent paths = 5**, matching $V(G)$.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Design Test Cases for Each Path

| Test Case | price | customerType | isFestival | Expected Discount | Path Covered |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TC1 | 500 | REGULAR | FALSE | 25.00 | Path 1 |
| TC2 | 1500 | PREMIUM | FALSE | 200.00 | Path 2 |
| TC3 | 800 | REGULAR | FALSE | 40.00 | Path 3 |
| TC4 | 6000 | REGULAR | TRUE | 800.00 | Path 4 |
| TC5 | 1500 | REGULAR | TRUE | 150.00 | Path 5 |

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Compute Coverage Metrics from Test Execution

Suppose we executed **only TC1, TC2, TC3**.

**Statement Coverage (SC) Analysis:**

- Total executable statements: $N_s = 8$ (assignments, decisions, return).
- Statements exercised by TC1+TC2+TC3: $N_e = 7$ (only the D4 True branch `discount := discount + 200` at Node 9 is missed).

$$
\text{SC} = \frac{7}{8} \times 100\% = 87.5\%
$$

**Branch Coverage (BC) Analysis:**

- Total branch outcomes: $2 \times N_d = 2 \times 4 = 8$.
- Branch outcomes traversed: $N_{bt} = 7$ (missed: D4-True branch).

$$
\text{BC} = \frac{7}{8} \times 100\% = 87.5\%
$$

**Condition Coverage (CC) Analysis:**

- Atomic conditions: `(price > 1000)`, `(customerType = "PREMIUM")`, `(isFestival = TRUE)`, `(price > 5000)` — total $N_c = 4$.
- Each must take both T and F: TC1 gives `(price>1000)=F, customerType=F, isFestival=F, (price>5000)=F`; TC2 gives `(price>1000)=T, customerType=T, isFestival=F, (price>5000)=F`; TC3 gives `(price>1000)=F, customerType=F, isFestival=F, (price>5000)=F` (no new values).
- Conditions fully evaluated in both T and F: 3 out of 4 (`(price>5000)` was never True).

$$
\text{CC} = \frac{3}{4} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

### 3.2 Mutation Testing Worked Example

**Original Program (mutant parent):**
```
if (a > b)
    x = a + b
else
    x = a - b
```

**Mutation Operators Applied:**

| Mutant ID | Mutation | Changed Token |
|---|---|---|
| $M_1$ | AOR (Arithmetic Op Replace) | `+` $\to$ `-` |
| $M_2$ | ROR (Relational Op Replace) | `>` $\to$ `>=` |
| $M_3$ | UOI (Unary Op Insert) | `x = a - b` $\to$ `x = -(a - b)` |

**Test Case Evaluation:**

| Mutant | TC: $a=5, b=3$ (Expected $x=8$) | Killed/Live |
|---|---|---|
| Original | $x = 5+3 = 8$ | — (Reference) |
| $M_1$ | $x = 5-3 = 2$ | **Killed** (output differs) |
| $M_2$ | $a \geq b$ still True, $x=8$ | **Live** (test didn't detect) |
| $M_3$ | $x = -(5-3) = -2$ | **Killed** |

- $M_t = 3$ total mutants, $M_k = 2$ killed, $M_e = 0$ equivalent.

$$
\text{MS} = \frac{2}{3 - 0} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Test suite is mediocre — it failed to kill the ROR mutant. A stronger test case (e.g., $a=5, b=5$) would kill $M_2$ since `5 > 5` is False but `5 >= 5` is True.

### 3.3 Data Flow Anomaly Detection (Analytical)

Consider the sequence:

```
1. read(x)        // D: defined
2. y := x + 10    // U: used
3. x := 0         // K: killed (re-defined)
4. y := x * 2     // U: used (x is now 0, value redefined before use — valid)
5. write(y)
```

**Anomaly Check:**

- Variable `x`: Defined at line 1, used at line 2, killed at line 3, used at line 4. **No anomaly.**
- Variable `y`: Defined at line 2, used at line 5, but **re-defined** at line 4 before line 5 use. This is a **Define-Define anomaly** if intermediate use is expected, but here it is by design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Flow Anomaly Catalogue (KTU favourite):**
> - **DR (Define-Read without kill):** Safe.
> - **DU (Define-Use):** Valid computation.
> - **DK (Define-Kill):** Variable redefined before use — potential bug.
> - **UR (Undefined Read):** Critical bug — reading uninitialized variable.
> - **DD (Define-Define):** Dead code for intervening usage.

### 3.4 Coverage Relationship Proof

**Theorem:** Branch Coverage (BC) $\geq$ Statement Coverage (SC).

**Proof:**

Consider a decision `IF (X) THEN S1 ELSE S2`. To execute statement S1, we must take the True branch. To execute S2, we must take the False branch. Therefore, taking both branches implies both statements are executed. Hence, the set of test cases achieving 100% BC is a superset of those achieving 100% SC. Therefore $BC \geq SC$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for KTU Questions

| If the question asks... | Use this formula/method |
|---|---|
| "Number of independent paths?" | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ |
| "Test cases needed for full coverage?" | $V(G)$ basis paths |
| "Coverage percentage achieved?" | SC, BC, or CC formula |
| "Test suite quality?" | Mutation Score |
| "Variable lifecycle issue?" | Data Flow Anomalies (UR, DD, DK) |
| "Integration order?" | Top-Down / Bottom-Up / Sandwich |
| "Replacing missing module during testing?" | Stub (called) or Driver (calls) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define White Box Testing. List any four advantages. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** White Box Testing is a dynamic software testing technique in which the internal structure, design, and implementation of the software (including its source code, control flow, and data flow) are known to and used by the tester to design and execute test cases.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Uncovers hidden defects** — Identifies dead code, unreachable statements, and logical errors invisible to black-box testing.
2. **Maximizes coverage** — Enables quantitative metrics like 100% statement, branch, and path coverage.
3. **Optimizes code** — Detects redundant code, unused variables, and inefficient branches.
4. **Validates security** — Identifies security vulnerabilities like buffer overflows, unhandled exceptions, and injection points at the code level.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Statement Coverage and Branch Coverage with an example. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Statement Coverage | Branch Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Measures the percentage of executable statements executed | Measures the percentage of decision outcomes (True/False) executed |
| **Granularity** | Statement-level | Decision-level |
| **Strength** | Easy to achieve | Stronger; ensures all branches tested |
| **Limitation** | 100% SC does not guarantee all branches tested | Cannot detect missing conditions inside branches |
| **Formula** | $\frac{N_e}{N_s} \times 100\%$ | $\frac{N_{bt}}{2 \cdot N_d} \times 100\%$ |

**Example (1 mark):**
```
if (a > b)
    x = 1;       // S1
else
    x = 2;       // S2
```
- Test case `a=5, b=3` executes S1 (Statement Coverage = 50%, Branch Coverage = 50% — only True branch).
- A second test case `a=2, b=5` is required to execute S2 and the False branch, raising both metrics to 100%.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

#### (a) Explain Cyclomatic Complexity. Compute $V(G)$ for the following pseudocode using all three McCabe methods, and identify the basis set of independent paths. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Pseudocode:**
```
1  START
2  READ a, b
3  IF a > 10
4      IF b < 5
5          x = a + b
6      ELSE
7          x = a - b
8      ENDIF
9  ELSE
10     x = 0
11 ENDIF
12 WRITE x
13 STOP
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Cyclomatic Complexity $V(G)$ is the number of linearly independent paths through a program module, originally proposed by Thomas McCabe (1976). It quantifies the minimum number of test cases required to exercise every path at least once.

**Step 2 — Draw the CFG (2 marks):**
- Node 1: Start
- Node 2: `a > 10` (Decision D1)
- Node 3: `b < 5` (Decision D2)
- Node 4: `x = a + b` (True-True branch)
- Node 5: `x = a - b` (True-False branch)
- Node 6: `x = 0` (False branch)
- Node 7: Write
- Node 8: Stop

Edges: $E = 10$ (1→2, 2→3, 2→6, 3→4, 3→5, 4→7, 5→7, 6→7, 7→8)

Nodes: $N = 8$

**Step 3 — Apply Formulas (2 marks):**

- **Method 1:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P = 10 - 8 + 2(1) = 4$
- **Method 2:** $V(G) = \pi + 1$, where $\pi = 3$ (decisions: `a>10`, `b<5`, plus implicit loop/merge) = 3 + 1 = 4
- **Method 3:** Regions in CFG = 4 (three bounded regions inside the nested IF + one outer region)

All three yield $V(G) = 4$ ✓

**Step 4 — Basis Set of Independent Paths (2 marks):**

| Path | Sequence |
|---|---|
| P1 | 1→2→6→7→8 (D1-False, D2 skipped) |
| P2 | 1→2→3→4→7→8 (D1-T, D2-T) |
| P3 | 1→2→3→5→7→8 (D1-T, D2-F) |
| P4 | 1→2→3→7→8 (degenerate merge path) |

Number of basis paths = 4 = $V(G)$ ✓

#### (b) Explain Mutation Testing with suitable example. Compute the Mutation Score for the given test suite. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
Mutation Testing is a fault-based white-box testing technique in which the tester introduces small artificial changes (mutations) into the program source code to create *mutants*, then evaluates the test suite's ability to *kill* (detect) these mutants. It was proposed by DeMillo, Lipton, and Sayward (1978).

**Step 2 — Key Terminology (2 marks):**
- **Mutation Operator:** Rule used to inject faults (e.g., AOR — Arithmetic Operator Replacement, ROR — Relational Operator Replacement, UOI — Unary Operator Insertion).
- **Killed Mutant:** Mutant whose output differs from the original on at least one test case.
- **Live Mutant:** Mutant whose output matches the original on all test cases.
- **Equivalent Mutant:** Mutant that is functionally identical to the original (cannot be killed by any test).
- **Mutation Score:** $\text{MS} = \frac{M_k}{M_t - M_e} \times 100\%$

**Step 3 — Worked Example (2 marks):**
```
Original:    if (x > y)  z = x + y
             else        z = x - y
Mutant M1:   if (x >= y) z = x + y   (ROR: > to >=)
Mutant M2:   if (x > y)  z = x * y   (AOR: + to *)
Mutant M3:   if (x > y)  z = x - y   (AOR: + to -)
```
Test Suite: TC1: $x=5, y=3 \to z=8$ (expected).

| Mutant | TC1 Output | Killed/Live |
|---|---|---|
| M1 | $x \geq y$ True $\to z = 5+3 = 8$ | Live (test failed to detect) |
| M2 | $z = 5 \times 3 = 15$ | Killed |
| M3 | $z = 5 - 3 = 2$ | Killed |

**Step 4 — Compute Mutation Score (1 mark):**
$M_t = 3$, $M_k = 2$, $M_e = 0$.

$$
\text{MS} = \frac{2}{3 - 0} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — White Box Testing (14-mark questions):**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 (1–2 marks lost):** Students compute $V(G)$ using only one McCabe formula and skip the other two. **Always show all three methods** for full marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 (1–2 marks lost):** When drawing the CFG, students forget the **merge node** after an IF-ENDIF, leading to a wrong edge count.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 (1 mark lost):** In Mutation Testing, students forget to **subtract equivalent mutants ($M_e$)** from the denominator.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 (1 mark lost):** Confusing **Stubs** (used in Top-Down integration for lower modules not yet integrated) with **Drivers** (used in Bottom-Up integration to call lower modules).
> 5. **Common Mistake #5 (1 mark lost):** For Path Coverage, students often use the *total* number of paths instead of the *independent* paths when applying cyclomatic complexity.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

#### (a) Discuss various levels of White Box Testing. Explain Integration Testing approaches with their merits and demerits. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2022]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Levels of White Box Testing (2 marks):**

White Box Testing is applied at multiple levels of software development:
1. **Unit Level** — Testing individual functions/methods/classes in isolation. Most granular level.
2. **Integration Level** — Testing interactions between integrated units/modules.
3. **System Level (partial)** — White-box aspects (e.g., code-level security, performance instrumentation).
4. **Regression Level** — Re-executing the white-box test suite after code changes.

**Step 2 — Integration Testing Approaches (4 marks):**

**(i) Big-Bang Integration:**
- All modules integrated at once and tested together.
- *Merit:* Fast, less driver/stub overhead.
- *Demerit:* Hard to isolate failures; debugging is difficult.

**(ii) Top-Down Integration:**
- Testing begins at the top of the hierarchy and proceeds downward.
- Lower modules are replaced with **stubs** (no-op placeholders).
- *Merit:* Early prototype demonstration; major design flaws caught early.
- *Demerit:* Stubs are limited; lower-level logic not fully tested until later.

**(iii) Bottom-Up Integration:**
- Testing begins at the bottom (leaf) modules and proceeds upward.
- Higher modules are replaced with **drivers** (calling programs).
- *Merit:* No stubs needed; real lower-level logic tested thoroughly.
- *Demerit:* No working version of the system until the final integration step.

**(iv) Sandwich / Hybrid Integration:**
- Combines Top-Down (for upper layers) and Bottom-Up (for lower layers), meeting at a target middle layer.
- *Merit:* Parallel testing, faster, suitable for large systems.
- *Demerit:* More complex coordination; costlier in resources.

**Step 3 — Comparison Summary (1 mark):**

| Approach | Speed | Failure Isolation | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Big-Bang | Fastest | Poor | Low |
| Top-Down | Slow (early) | Good | Moderate |
| Bottom-Up | Slow (late) | Good | Moderate |
| Sandwich | Fastest (parallel) | Excellent | High |

#### (b) Explain the differences between Black Box and White Box Testing. Discuss four coverage criteria with their formulas. (7 marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Black vs. White Box (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Black Box Testing | White Box Testing |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge of internals | None required | Full source code access |
| Focus | Input $\to$ Output behavior | Internal logic, control flow, data flow |
| Tester | End-user, QA, customer | Developer, technical tester |
| Performed at | System, Acceptance testing | Unit, Integration testing |
| Techniques | Equivalence partitioning, BVA, decision tables, state transition | Statement, branch, condition, path coverage |
| Advantage | User perspective, unbiased | Thorough, finds hidden defects |
| Disadvantage | Misses internal defects | Cannot detect missing requirements |

**Step 2 — Four Coverage Criteria with Formulas (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Statement Coverage (SC):**
$$
\text{SC} = \frac{N_e}{N_s} \times 100\%
$$
2. **Branch Coverage (BC):**
$$
\text{BC} = \frac{N_{bt}}{2 \cdot N_d} \times 100\%
$$
3. **Condition Coverage (CC):**
$$
\text{CC} = \frac{N_{cc}}{2 \cdot N_c} \times 100\%
$$
4. **Path Coverage (PC):**
$$
\text{PC} = \frac{N_{ep}}{N_{tp}} \times 100\%
$$
where $N_{ep}$ = exercised paths, $N_{tp}$ = total independent paths (often = $V(G)$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — White Box Testing (Module 2)**

### A. Core Definitions
- ☐ **White Box Testing** = Structural/Glass Box testing with full internal knowledge.
- ☐ **Black Box Testing** = Functional testing without internal knowledge.
- ☐ **Unit Testing** = smallest testable function/class.
- ☐ **Integration Testing** = combined unit testing.

### B. Coverage Metrics (must know formulas)
- ☐ **Statement Coverage** = $\frac{N_e}{N_s} \times 100\%$ — weakest metric.
- ☐ **Branch Coverage** = $\frac{N_{bt}}{2 \cdot N_d} \times 100\%$ — stronger, implies SC.
- ☐ **Condition Coverage** = $\frac{N_{cc}}{2 \cdot N_c} \times 100\%$ — atomic conditions both T/F.
- ☐ **Path Coverage** = $\frac{N_{ep}}{N_{tp}} \times 100\%$ — strongest, often infeasible.
- ☐ **MC/DC** = Modified Condition/Decision Coverage (used in avionics DO-178C).
- ☐ **Relationship:** PC $\Rightarrow$ BC $\Rightarrow$ SC; CC $\Rightarrow$ SC (not always BC).

### C. Cyclomatic Complexity (the McCabe trio — memorize all 3 formulas)
- ☐ $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ (graph-based)
- ☐ $V(G) = \pi + 1$ (predicate-based)
- ☐ $V(G) = R$ (region-based)
- ☐ All three must give the **same answer**; show all three in 14-mark answers.
- ☐ $V(G)$ = number of basis paths = minimum test cases for basis path coverage.

### D. Basis Path Testing Steps
- ☐ Step 1: Draw CFG from code.
- ☐ Step 2: Compute $V(G)$.
- ☐ Step 3: Identify basis set of $V(G)$ independent paths.
- ☐ Step 4: Design test cases for each basis path.
- ☐ Step 5: Execute, measure coverage, augment test suite.

### E. Mutation Testing — Core Vocabulary
- ☐ **Mutant** = program with one fault injected.
- ☐ **Killed Mutant** = detected by test suite (good).
- ☐ **Live Mutant** = not detected (bad).
- ☐ **Equivalent Mutant** = identical behavior, excluded from denominator.
- ☐ **Mutation Score** = $\frac{M_k}{M_t - M_e} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ Common operators: AOR, ROR, UOI, COD (Conditional Operator Deletion).

### F. Data Flow Testing — Anomaly Catalogue
- ☐ **UR (Undefined Read)** = critical bug, use before define.
- ☐ **DD (Define-Define)** = variable redefined before intervening use — possible dead code.
- ☐ **DK (Define-Kill)** = redefinition without use — was it intended?
- ☐ **DU (Define-Use)** = normal valid lifecycle.

### G. Integration Testing Vocabulary
- ☐ **Stub** = placeholder for a *lower* module (used in Top-Down).
- ☐ **Driver** = placeholder for a *higher* module (used in Bottom-Up).
- ☐ **Big-Bang** = all at once.
- ☐ **Sandwich** = top-down + bottom-up parallel.

### H. Common KTU Pitfalls (avoid losing marks)
- ☐ Always show all **3 McCabe formulas** for $V(G)$ calculations.
- ☐ Don't forget the **merge node** in CFG after IF-ENDIF.
- ☐ In Mutation Score, **subtract $M_e$** from denominator.
- ☐ Distinguish **paths** (sequences of execution) from **branches** (decision outcomes).
- ☐ Coverge criteria are **not equal** — there is a strict partial ordering.
- ☐ White box is **complementary** to black box, not a replacement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Static Testing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Static Testing** is a software verification technique in which the program under test is **NOT executed**. Instead, the software artifacts (source code, requirements specifications, design documents, test cases, and user manuals) are examined and analyzed against predefined standards, rules, and expectations to identify defects, ambiguities, or deviations at the earliest possible stage of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEITT862 – Module 2):** > Static testing fundamentally includes two parallel pillars: > 1. **Reviews** (Manual, human-driven analysis of documents/code) > 2. **Static Analysis** (Automated, tool-driven analysis of code structure) > Together, these form the foundation of *Verification* in the V-Model. The IEEE Standard 1028 defines the formal review processes, while ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board) classifies static testing techniques into structured review types. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are a **proofreader for a newspaper article before it is printed**. - You do **not** print the newspaper to find spelling mistakes. - You simply **read the manuscript on paper**, line by line, with a red pen in hand. - You catch typos, grammatical errors, factual inconsistencies, and formatting issues. **Static testing is exactly this "proofreading" applied to software.** Instead of *running* the software to see it crash (which is called **Dynamic Testing**), we inspect the source code and documentation *before* execution, spotting the "typos" of the programming world (syntax errors, logic flaws, undefined variables, security vulnerabilities, and requirement contradictions). > [!NOTE] > **Dynamic Testing** = Driving a car to see if the brakes work. > **Static Testing** = Inspecting the brake blueprint and mechanical design before assembly. > Static testing is **preventive**; Dynamic testing is **reactive**. ## 1.3 Why Static Testing is Critical in KTU Context KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework (NEP 2020 aligned) maps testing to the following Course Outcomes: - **CO2:** Apply appropriate testing strategies and techniques to derive effective test cases. - **CO3:** Comprehend the various activities involved in software test planning and management. Static testing directly enables **CO2** by ensuring that the *basis* for test cases (the requirements and design) is itself free from defects — a phenomenon known as **"Defect Prevention at the Source."** ## 1.4 Static vs. Dynamic Testing — Quick Comparison | Parameter | Static Testing | Dynamic Testing | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Execution** | Code is NOT executed | Code IS executed | | **Phase** | Early (Requirements, Design) | Later (Coding, Integration) | | **Cost of Fix** | Very Low (30–100x cheaper) | High | | **Defect Type Found** | Coding standards, dead code, security flaws | Runtime crashes, performance issues | | **Tooling** | Linters, Review tools, Compilers | Test runners, Debuggers | | **Coverage Metric** | Statement coverage (static analysis) | Branch, Path, Condition coverage | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Static Testing Static testing is operationally divided into **two major branches** as per the ISTQB Certified Tester Foundation Level (CTFL) syllabus, which KTU follows. ### Pillar A: Reviews (Human-Driven) A review is an evaluation of a software product (or project artifact) by stakeholders to identify defects, gather improvements, and confirm completeness. Reviews are categorized by their **formality level**. #### Hierarchy of Review Formality (Least → Most Formal) 1. **Informal Review (Ad-hoc Review):** - No formal process, no documented procedure. - Pair programming, casual desk-checking. - Very low cost, variable effectiveness. 2. **Walkthrough:** - The **author** of the document leads the meeting. - The author walks the participants through the artifact to explain and gather feedback. - Goal: Knowledge transfer, defect identification, alternative solutions. 3. **Technical Review (Peer Review):** - Conducted by **qualified technical peers** (not management). - Led by a Moderator; documented by a Recorder (Scribe). - Goal: Evaluate technical content, identify defects, check conformance to standards. - Outcome: Review Log, Decision (Accept/Reject). 4. **Inspection (Most Formal):** - Defined by **IEEE 1028 Standard**. - Led by a **trained Moderator** (not the author). - Uses **checklists, entry/exit criteria, and metrics**. - Has six defined phases: Planning, Overview, Individual Preparation, Inspection Meeting, Rework, Follow-up. - Goal: Find defects using a documented process with measurable outcomes. ### Pillar B: Static Analysis (Tool-Driven) Static analysis uses automated tools (e.g., SonarQube, ESLint, FindBugs) to analyze source code against: - **Coding Standards** (e.g., MISRA-C for embedded systems) - **Data Flow Anomalies** (e.g., variable used before initialization) - **Control Flow Anomalies** (e.g., unreachable code, infinite loops) - **Cyclomatic Complexity** limits - **Security Vulnerabilities** (e.g., SQL injection, buffer overflow patterns) > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The examiner frequently asks: *"List the formal review process types."* Memorize the order: **Informal Review → Walkthrough → Technical Review → Inspection.** ## 2.2 Static Testing Defect Categories Static testing is uniquely capable of detecting the following classes of defects that dynamic testing may miss: - **Requirement defects:** Ambiguity, incompleteness, inconsistency, incorrectness. - **Design defects:** Violation of design principles, missing interfaces. - **Coding defects:** Unreachable code, unused variables, memory leaks (detected via static analysis). - **Standards violations:** Non-adherence to organizational coding conventions. - **Specification mismatches:** Code that does not match its requirement traceability. ## 2.3 Roles in a Formal Review (IEEE 1028) | Role | Responsibility | | :--- | :--- | | **Moderator** | Leads the review, mediates discussions, ensures process compliance | | **Author** | The creator of the artifact under review; fixes defects found | | **Reviewer** | Subject matter expert who identifies defects | | **Recorder/Scribe** | Logs all defects, decisions, and action items | | **Manager** | Decides on review scheduling, resource allocation, and acceptance | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following metrics are **highly testable** in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. | Metric / Formula | Symbolic Representation | Unit / Domain | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Reviewer Preparation Rate** | $\text{RPR} = \dfrac{N_{reviewed}}{T_{prep}}$ | artifacts / hour | Measures reviewer efficiency | | **Defect Density (Static)** | $\text{DD}_{static} = \dfrac{N_{defects}}{\text{LOC or FP}}$ | defects per KLOC | Defects found per unit size | | **Review Yield** | $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{N_{defects,found}}{N_{defects,total}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Effectiveness of review | | **Cost-Benefit Ratio (Royce)** | $\dfrac{C_{static}}{C_{dynamic}}$ | monetary ratio | Justifies static testing ROI | | **Effort Multiplier** | $\dfrac{E_{fix,static}}{E_{fix,dynamic}}$ | dimensionless | Shows cheaper fix in early stages | | **Inspection Rate** | $\text{IR} = \dfrac{\text{LOC reviewed}}{T_{meeting}}$ | LOC / hour | Throughput of formal inspection | | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{D_{pre-release}}{D_{pre-release} + D_{post-release}} \times 100$ | percentage | Overall quality indicator | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked "How does static testing reduce cost?", cite the famous **Boehm curve** which states that a defect fixed after release can cost **60–100x** more than the same defect fixed during the requirements phase via static review. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Static analysis is *mandatory* for Level A and B flight-critical software. - **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Requires formal code reviews and MISRA compliance via static tools. - **Medical Devices (FDA):** Review documentation is an audit artifact. - **Cybersecurity:** Static Application Security Testing (SAST) tools like Checkmarx, Fortify detect vulnerabilities *before* deployment, preventing breaches. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Derivation of the Inspection Process Phases (IEEE 1028) The formal Inspection process is the most structured review type. KTU examiners frequently ask students to "Explain the inspection process" for 7–14 marks. Below is the rigorous step-by-step logical derivation of why each phase exists. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Goal} &= \text{Maximize Defect Detection Rate (DDR)} \\ \text{DDR} &= \frac{N_{defects,found}}{N_{lines,reviewed}} \\ \text{Constraint} &= \text{Minimize Meeting Time, Ensure Process Compliance} \end{aligned} $$ ### Phase 1: Planning The **Moderator** is appointed. The **Author** submits the artifact (e.g., requirements document, code module). The Moderator forms the inspection team, distributes materials, and checks **entry criteria**. Entry Criteria typically include: - Document is syntactically correct (compiles cleanly). - Coding standards are applied. - Author has unit-tested the code. $$ \text{Entry Gate} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} C_i \quad \text{where } C_i = \text{Boolean check for criterion } i $$ If any $C_i = 0$, the inspection is **aborted** and rescheduled after fixes. ### Phase 2: Overview (Optional) The Author presents the artifact to ensure all participants have a **shared mental model**. This reduces individual preparation time. ### Phase 3: Individual Preparation Each reviewer studies the artifact **independently**, noting potential defects against a **checklist**. $$ \text{Effectiveness} \propto \sum_{k=1}^{m} P(\text{reviewer}_k \text{ finds defect}) \cdot \text{coverage}_k $$ The more reviewers, the higher the probability of finding defects — but **diminishing returns** occur beyond 4–5 reviewers. ### Phase 4: Inspection Meeting The team convenes (typically **90–120 minutes, ≤ 200 lines of code per session** per IEEE 1028). The Moderator facilitates; the **Recorder** logs every defect. No fixes are made in the meeting — this prevents scope creep. > [!NOTE] > The inspection meeting has a strict **issue rate limit** (typically ≤ 200 LOC/hour). Exceeding this reduces defect detection accuracy due to reviewer fatigue. ### Phase 5: Rework The Author fixes all logged defects. Time-boxed (e.g., < 5% of total inspection time). ### Phase 6: Follow-up The Moderator verifies that **all defects are addressed** and the **exit criteria** are met. If unmet, the document re-enters the inspection cycle. $$ \text{Exit Gate} = \bigwedge_{j=1}^{p} E_j $$ A formal report is produced, including defect logs, rework effort, and process metrics. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Cost-Benefit Analysis A KTU 2024 numerical problem may ask you to compute the cost saving from static testing. **Problem Statement:** A team performs static testing and finds **40 defects**. Without static testing, these 40 defects would have been found in dynamic testing, each costing **₹5,000** to fix. The static testing itself cost **₹80,000**. Calculate the **net savings** and the **Cost-Benefit Ratio (CBR)**. ### Solution Step-by-Step $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost of Fixing in Dynamic Testing} &= N_{defects} \times C_{dynamic,per} \\ &= 40 \times 5000 \\ &= \text{₹ } 200,000 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost of Static Testing} &= C_{static} = \text{₹ } 80,000 \\ \text{Net Savings} &= C_{dynamic} - C_{static} \\ &= 200{,}000 - 80{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ } 120{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cost-Benefit Ratio (CBR)} &= \frac{\text{Benefit}}{\text{Cost}} = \frac{200{,}000}{80{,}000} \\ &= 2.5 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** A CBR greater than 1.0 indicates **positive ROI**. For every ₹1 invested in static testing, ₹2.50 worth of downstream defect costs were avoided. ## 3.3 Worked Example: Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) **Problem Statement:** During testing, 80 defects were found before release. After release, the customer reported 20 additional defects. Calculate the DRE. ### Solution $$ \begin{aligned} D_{pre-release} &= 80 \\ D_{post-release} &= 20 \\ DRE &= \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{80}{80 + 20} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{80}{100} \times 100 \\ &= 80\% \end{aligned} $$ Industry best practice (per Capers Jones) targets **DRE ≥ 95%** for high-maturity organizations (CMMI Level 5). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Static Testing Classification Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[Static Testing] --> B[Reviews] A --> C[Static Analysis] B --> D[Informal Review] B --> E[Walkthrough] B --> F[Technical Review] B --> G[Inspection] G --> H[IEEE 1028 Process] H --> H1[Planning] H --> H2[Overview] H --> H3[Individual Preparation] H --> H4[Inspection Meeting] H --> H5[Rework] H --> H6[Follow-up] C --> I[Data Flow Analysis] C --> J[Control Flow Analysis] C --> K[Coding Standards Check] C --> L[Security Analysis SAST] ``` ## 4.2 V-Model Mapping of Static Testing Activities ```mermaid graph LR V1[Requirements] -->|Static Review| R1[Requirements Review] V2[System Design] -->|Static Review| R2[Design Review] V3[Detailed Design] -->|Static Review| R3[Detailed Design Review] V4[Coding] -->|Static Analysis| R4[Code Inspection] V5[Unit Testing] --> D1[Dynamic Testing] V6[Integration Testing] --> D2[Dynamic Testing] V7[System Testing] --> D3[Dynamic Testing] V8[Acceptance Testing] --> D4[Dynamic Testing] R1 -.->|Defects Fixed| V1 R2 -.->|Defects Fixed| V2 R3 -.->|Defects Fixed| V3 R4 -.->|Defects Fixed| V4 ``` ## 4.3 Inspection Process — State Transition Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Planning Planning --> Overview: Entry Criteria Met Planning --> Aborted: Entry Criteria Failed Aborted --> Planning: After Rework Overview --> IndividualPrep IndividualPrep --> InspectionMeeting InspectionMeeting --> Rework: Defects Logged InspectionMeeting --> Accepted: No Defects Found Rework --> Followup Followup --> Accepted: Exit Criteria Met Followup --> Planning: Exit Criteria Failed Accepted --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Defect Cost Escalation (Boehm Curve Concept) ```mermaid graph LR P1[Requirements Phase
Cost Rs 100] -->|100x| P2[Design Phase
Cost Rs 1,500] P2 -->|10x| P3[Coding Phase
Cost Rs 5,000] P3 -->|10x| P4[Testing Phase
Cost Rs 15,000] P4 -->|10x| P5[Maintenance Phase
Cost Rs 100,000+] S1[Static Review Here] -.-> P1 S2[Walkthrough Here] -.-> P2 S3[Code Inspection Here] -.-> P3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Static testing intercepts defects at the cheapest point of the cost curve. The earlier the review, the greater the financial savings. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEITT862]** **Q: Define static testing. List any two advantages of static testing.** **Model Answer:** Static testing is a software testing technique that involves the analysis and evaluation of software artifacts — such as requirements specifications, design documents, and source code — **without executing the program**. It is primarily used to identify defects early in the software development life cycle. **Two Advantages:** 1. **Early Defect Detection:** Defects are identified at the requirements or design phase, when the cost of fixing them is minimal (up to 100x cheaper than post-release fixes). 2. **Improved Code Quality:** Through static analysis, coding standards, security vulnerabilities, and maintainability issues are detected before execution, leading to higher overall software quality. *(Alternative valid advantages: Reduces development time, detects missing requirements, enables knowledge transfer among team members, no need for test environment setup.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEITT862]** **Q: Differentiate between a Walkthrough and an Inspection.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Walkthrough | Inspection | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Led by** | Author | Trained Moderator (not author) | | **Formality** | Less formal | Most formal (IEEE 1028) | | **Process** | No strict process | Six defined phases with entry/exit criteria | | **Documentation** | Optional meeting notes | Mandatory defect log, checklist, report | | **Goal** | Knowledge transfer, gather feedback | Find defects using measurable process | | **Participants** | Anyone interested | Selected technical experts | --- ## PART B — Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, PEITT862]** **(a)** Explain the formal Inspection process as defined by IEEE 1028. List all six phases and describe the role of the Moderator and Recorder. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the benefits and limitations of static testing in modern software engineering practice. **(7 Marks)** ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PEITT862]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, classify the different types of reviews used in static testing. Explain the characteristics that distinguish them in terms of formality. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A software team performed static testing and identified 45 defects at a cost of ₹90,000. Without static testing, these defects would have been detected during integration testing at ₹6,000 per defect. Calculate the net savings and the Cost-Benefit Ratio. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)** --- ## MODEL SOLUTIONS ### Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — IEEE 1028 Inspection Process The formal inspection is a structured, methodical review process defined by the IEEE 1028 standard. It consists of **six distinct phases**: **Phase 1 — Planning:** The Moderator is appointed. The author submits the artifact (requirements, design, or code). The Moderator selects the inspection team, distributes the artifact, and verifies **entry criteria** (e.g., the code compiles, standards are followed). **Phase 2 — Overview (Optional):** The author presents the artifact to the team so that all participants share a common understanding of its purpose, scope, and structure. This saves individual preparation time. **Phase 3 — Individual Preparation:** Each reviewer studies the artifact independently, typically using a checklist. They note potential defects, comments, and questions. This phase accounts for the majority of defects identified. **Phase 4 — Inspection Meeting:** The team, led by the Moderator, convenes (typically ≤ 2 hours, ≤ 200 LOC). The **Recorder** logs every defect on a standard form. **No fixes are made** during this meeting — only defect identification occurs. **Phase 5 — Rework:** The author addresses all logged defects. Time-boxed to prevent indefinite delays. **Phase 6 — Follow-up:** The Moderator verifies that rework is complete and that **exit criteria** are satisfied. A formal inspection report is produced, containing metrics such as inspection rate and defect density. **Roles:** - **Moderator:** Manages the entire inspection process, ensures compliance with IEEE 1028, mediates discussions, verifies exit criteria. - **Recorder (Scribe):** Documents all defects, decisions, and action items accurately and contemporaneously during the meeting. #### Part (b) — Benefits and Limitations of Static Testing **Benefits:** 1. **Early Defect Detection:** Catches defects at the requirements/design phase, reducing the cost multiplier. 2. **No Execution Required:** Useful even when code is incomplete, environment is unavailable, or system is too complex to run. 3. **Identifies Specific Defect Classes:** Detects coding standards violations, unreachable code, security flaws, and requirement ambiguities that dynamic testing may miss. 4. **Knowledge Transfer:** Reviews bring teams to a common understanding. 5. **Improved Communication:** Encourages collaboration across developers, testers, and analysts. 6. **Tool Support:** Static analysis tools (SonarQube, Fortify) provide automated, repeatable, and consistent checks. **Limitations:** 1. **Cannot Detect Runtime Defects:** Misses performance bottlenecks, race conditions, memory leaks at runtime. 2. **Time-Consuming for Large Artifacts:** Formal inspections have strict LOC limits per session. 3. **False Positives:** Static analysis tools may flag non-issues, wasting developer time. 4. **Skill Dependent:** Effectiveness of reviews depends on reviewer expertise. 5. **Incomplete Coverage:** Cannot guarantee that all defects are found; only those detectable by analysis. 6. **Tool Limitations:** Static analyzers cannot reason about third-party libraries or dynamic configuration. --- ### Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Classification of Reviews (Refer to Section 4.1 Diagram) Reviews are classified based on **formality level** (least to most formal): 1. **Informal Review:** Ad-hoc, no documentation. Example: Pair programming, casual desk checks. 2. **Walkthrough:** Author-led, low formality, focused on learning and alternative solutions. 3. **Technical Review:** Peer-led, technical focus, documented outcomes (Review Log). 4. **Inspection:** IEEE 1028 standard, most formal, with checklists, entry/exit criteria, and metrics. The distinguishing characteristic is the **degree of process structure**: informal reviews have minimal documentation, while inspections enforce strict phases, roles, and measurable outputs. #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem Solution **Given:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{defects} &= 45 \\ C_{static,total} &= \text{₹ } 90{,}000 \\ C_{dynamic,per} &= \text{₹ } 6{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Cost if defects were fixed in dynamic testing:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{dynamic,total} &= N_{defects} \times C_{dynamic,per} \\ &= 45 \times 6{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ } 270{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Net Savings:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Savings} &= C_{dynamic,total} - C_{static,total} \\ &= 270{,}000 - 90{,}000 \\ &= \text{₹ } 180{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Cost-Benefit Ratio (CBR):** $$ \begin{aligned} CBR &= \frac{C_{dynamic,total}}{C_{static,total}} \\ &= \frac{270{,}000}{90{,}000} \\ &= 3.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Comment:** The Cost-Benefit Ratio of **3.0** indicates that for every ₹1 invested in static testing, ₹3 worth of downstream defect remediation costs were avoided. This demonstrates a **strong positive ROI** and validates the inclusion of static testing in the project's quality assurance strategy. The organization should continue and possibly expand its static testing practice. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing static with dynamic testing:** Static testing does **NOT** execute code. If you write "the program is run to find bugs," you will lose 2–3 marks immediately. > 2. **Skipping the Moderator role:** In Inspection, the author **does NOT lead** the meeting. Always mention that the Moderator is a *trained, independent* person. > 3. **Miscounting Inspection phases:** There are exactly **six phases** (Planning, Overview, Individual Preparation, Inspection Meeting, Rework, Follow-up). Listing only 4–5 is a common error. > 4. **Numerical errors in CBR:** The formula is **Total Dynamic Cost ÷ Total Static Cost**, not the reverse. Reversing the ratio will lose 2 marks. > 5. **Forgetting IEEE 1028:** Whenever you mention "Inspection," the examiner expects the citation *IEEE Standard 1028*. Failing to cite it loses a CO-mapping mark. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Static testing = Verification without execution.** Two pillars: **Reviews** (manual) and **Static Analysis** (automated). - **Review Formality Hierarchy:** Informal Review → Walkthrough → Technical Review → Inspection. - **Inspection is the most formal review**, governed by **IEEE 1028**, with **6 phases** and strict entry/exit criteria. - **Walkthrough is led by the Author; Inspection is led by a trained Moderator (not the author).** - **Static Analysis Tools** detect: dead code, undefined variables, infinite loops, security flaws, coding standards violations. - **Cost-Benefit Ratio Formula:** $\text{CBR} = C_{dynamic,total} / C_{static,total}$. CBR > 1 indicates positive ROI. - **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE):** $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100$. Industry target: ≥ 95%. - **Boehm's Cost Principle:** Defects fixed post-release cost up to **100x more** than those caught in requirements via static review. - **Roles in Formal Review:** Moderator, Author, Reviewer, Recorder (Scribe), Manager — each has distinct, non-overlapping responsibilities. - **Static Testing in KTU 2024 Maps to:** **CO2** (testing techniques) and **CO3** (test planning/management activities). - **Static testing CANNOT detect runtime defects** like performance issues, race conditions, or memory leaks at execution time — be precise in Part A answers. - **Inspection meeting limits:** ≤ 200 LOC per session, ≤ 2 hours duration — these are IEEE 1028 best-practice thresholds frequently asked in exams.

topics.title: Validation Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Validation Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Validation Activities

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus for Software Testing, **Validation** is formally defined as a set of activities that ensure that the *built* software system satisfies the *intended use* in its real operational environment. It is the process of evaluating software at the end of development to determine whether it meets the customer's expectations, business requirements, and operational needs.

According to the **IEEE Standard 829-2008 / ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207**, validation is the *confirmation, through the provision of objective evidence, that the requirements for a specific intended use or application have been fulfilled*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Syllabus-Compliant):**
> Validation Activities are the dynamic testing and review mechanisms employed across the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) to confirm that the *final product* fulfills its *intended purpose* in the *user's environment*. These activities are primarily **High-Level Testing** activities (System Testing, Acceptance Testing) conducted from the user's perspective.

## 1.2 Validation vs. Verification — The Core Distinction

The KTU board examiner frequently asks this distinction. It is the **single most high-yield concept** in Module 2.

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | Are we building the product **right**? | Are we building the **right** product? |
| **Focus** | Internal consistency, design conformance | Fitness for use, user needs |
| **Performed At** | Earlier stages (reviews, walkthroughs) | Later stages (system, acceptance) |
| **Method** | Static (no code execution) | Dynamic (code execution) |
| **Inputs** | Specifications, design documents | Actual software, user requirements |
| **Outputs** | Defect list, review reports | Confirmation of fitness for use |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:**
> Imagine you ordered a **Paneer Butter Masala** from a restaurant.
>
> - **Verification** is the chef **tasting the dish in the kitchen**: checking if the recipe was followed, the spices are right, the salt is balanced, and the cooking time was correct. The dish meets the *recipe specification*.
> - **Validation** is **you, the customer, eating the dish at your table**: you confirm whether it satisfies your *hunger*, matches your *taste preference*, and is *fit for the occasion* (a dinner party).
>
> The chef's tasting is **Verification**. Your eating is **Validation**. The dish passes validation only when **you** are satisfied — just as software passes validation only when the **user** is satisfied.

## 1.4 Validation in the KTU Software Testing Life Cycle (STLC)

Validation activities are not isolated to the end of the project. In the **V-Model** (a high-weightage KTU topic), validation activities are mapped directly to the corresponding user-side requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Alert:**
> Examiners expect you to remember that **Validation = The Right Side of the V-Model**, while **Verification = The Left Side**. Memorizing this mapping guarantees at least 2-3 marks in any Module 2 question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Validation Activities

Validation activities, as per the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, are broadly classified into the following categories:

### A. Dynamic Validation (Execution-Based)
1. **Unit Testing** (developer-level, often considered verification-leaning but with validation aspects)
2. **Integration Testing** — validates interactions between modules.
3. **System Testing** — validates the complete system against requirements.
4. **Acceptance Testing** — final user-side validation.
5. **Performance, Load, Stress, and Usability Testing** — non-functional validation.
6. **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** — direct user validation.

### B. Static Validation (Document-Based)
1. **Requirements Reviews**
2. **Design Walkthroughs**
3. **User Manual Inspections**
4. **Prototype Evaluations**

## 2.2 Levels of Validation (KTU V-Model Mapping)

The V-Model is the **primary architectural framework** used in the KTU syllabus to explain validation. The right descending arm of the V represents validation activities.

| V-Model Left (Verification) | V-Model Right (Validation) |
|---|---|
| User Requirements | User Acceptance Testing |
| System Requirements | System Testing |
| Architectural Design | Integration Testing |
| Detailed Design | Unit Testing |
| **CODING** | **CODING** |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Validation Activity

1. **Identify Validation Goal** — Define what user need must be satisfied.
2. **Derive Validation Criteria** — Convert goals into measurable, testable acceptance criteria.
3. **Design Validation Test Cases** — Use techniques like equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis (Module 3 topic, but applied here in context).
4. **Execute Validation Tests** — Run the software in a simulated or real user environment.
5. **Compare Actual vs. Expected Outcomes** — Determine pass/fail status.
6. **Record Validation Evidence** — Maintain test logs, defect reports, and validation summary reports.
7. **Obtain User Sign-off** — Formal acceptance and closure of validation phase.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Coverage Metrics** — Frequently tested in KTU numerical questions (Part A 3-markers):

$$
\text{Validation Coverage} = \frac{\text{Number of User Requirements Validated}}{\text{Total Number of User Requirements}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Defect Detection Efficiency (DDE)} = \frac{\text{Defects Found by Validation}}{\text{Defects Found by Validation} + \text{Defects Found by User Post-Release}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{User Acceptance Rate} = \frac{\text{User-Accepted Test Cases}}{\text{Total Executed Acceptance Test Cases}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Validation Yield} = \frac{\text{Defects Resolved}}{\text{Defects Identified During Validation}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Validation} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_i \times C_i) + D_{fix}
$$

Where $T_i$ is time spent on activity $i$, $C_i$ is hourly cost, and $D_{fix}$ is defect-fix cost.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Validation activities are critical in safety-critical and mission-critical domains:
- **Aerospace**: FAA requires exhaustive validation of flight control software.
- **Medical Devices**: FDA 21 CFR Part 820 mandates validation of all production software.
- **Automotive**: ISO 26262 requires validation of all ASIL-rated automotive software.
- **Banking & FinTech**: Validation ensures transactional software complies with RBI/SEBI regulations.

## 2.6 Types of Acceptance Testing (Validation Sub-Category)

| Type | Description | Performed By |
|---|---|---|
| **Alpha Testing** | Internal testing at developer's site | Internal testers |
| **Beta Testing** | External testing at customer's site | End users |
| **Contract Acceptance Testing (CAT)** | Against contractual criteria | Both parties |
| **Regulation Acceptance Testing (RAT)** | Against regulatory standards | External auditors |
| **Operational Acceptance Testing (OAT)** | Operational readiness | System administrators |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Validation Coverage Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A banking application has 240 user requirements documented. During validation, the test team successfully validated 198 requirements. The remaining 42 are scheduled for the next release. Compute the **Validation Coverage**, **Pending Coverage**, and **Defect Density per KLOC** if the system has 35,000 lines of code and 47 validation defects were logged.

### Step 1: Identify Given Values
- Total User Requirements $R_{total} = 240$
- Validated Requirements $R_{valid} = 198$
- Pending Requirements $R_{pending} = 42$
- Lines of Code $LOC = 35{,}000$
- Defects Logged $D = 47$

### Step 2: Compute Validation Coverage

$$
\text{Validation Coverage} = \frac{R_{valid}}{R_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\text{Validation Coverage} = \frac{198}{240} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Validation Coverage} = 0.825 \times 100\% = 82.5\%
$$

### Step 3: Compute Pending Coverage (Unvalidated Fraction)

$$
\text{Pending Coverage} = \frac{R_{pending}}{R_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{42}{240} \times 100\% = 17.5\%
$$

### Step 4: Compute Defect Density

$$
\text{Defect Density} = \frac{D}{LOC / 1000} = \frac{47}{35}
$$

$$
\text{Defect Density} = 1.343 \text{ defects per KLOC}
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

A validation coverage of **82.5%** indicates the project is below the industry-standard acceptance threshold (typically **≥ 95%** for mission-critical systems). The KTU board accepts numerical answers with **2 decimal places of accuracy**.

## 3.2 Derivation: User Acceptance Rate with Weighted Criticality

**Problem Statement:** An e-commerce portal undergoes User Acceptance Testing. The following test results are observed:

| Criticality | Total Test Cases | Passed | Failed | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Critical | 25 | 23 | 2 | 0.50 |
| High | 60 | 54 | 6 | 0.30 |
| Medium | 90 | 85 | 5 | 0.15 |
| Low | 40 | 38 | 2 | 0.05 |

Compute the **Weighted User Acceptance Rate (WUAR)**.

### Step 1: Compute Per-Class Acceptance Rate

For **Critical**:
$$
AR_{critical} = \frac{23}{25} \times 100\% = 92.00\%
$$

For **High**:
$$
AR_{high} = \frac{54}{60} \times 100\% = 90.00\%
$$

For **Medium**:
$$
AR_{medium} = \frac{85}{90} \times 100\% = 94.44\%
$$

For **Low**:
$$
AR_{low} = \frac{38}{40} \times 100\% = 95.00\%
$$

### Step 2: Apply Weighted Formula

$$
WUAR = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (AR_i \times W_i)
$$

$$
WUAR = (92.00 \times 0.50) + (90.00 \times 0.30) + (94.44 \times 0.15) + (95.00 \times 0.05)
$$

$$
WUAR = 46.00 + 27.00 + 14.166 + 4.75 = 91.916\%
$$

### Step 3: Final Rounded Value

$$
WUAR \approx 91.92\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Weighted?** A failure in a critical test case (e.g., payment processing) is far more severe than a cosmetic defect. The weight factor in the formula reflects the **risk-weighted impact** of each failure category, which is the KTU examiner's preferred method for non-uniform test data.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Validation Activity

| Project Characteristic | Recommended Primary Validation Activity | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Safety-critical (medical, aviation) | Formal validation + Independent V&V | Regulatory mandate (FDA, FAA, DO-178C) |
| Commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) | Beta testing + UAT | High user base, environment variability |
| Internal enterprise application | Operational Acceptance Testing (OAT) | Focus on deployment readiness |
| Open-source community product | Beta testing + crowdsourced validation | Limited in-house QA budget |
| Embedded real-time system | Hardware-in-the-loop validation | Timing/IO validation critical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Validation Activities Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Requirements Document] --> B[Define Validation Goals]
    B --> C[Derive Acceptance Criteria]
    C --> D[Design Validation Test Cases]
    D --> E[Setup Test Environment]
    E --> F[Execute Validation Tests]
    F --> G{All Acceptance Criteria Met?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Generate Validation Summary Report]
    G -- No --> I[Log Defects and Inform Dev Team]
    I --> J[Developer Fixes Defects]
    J --> K[Re-Execute Failed Test Cases]
    K --> F
    H --> L[Obtain Formal User Sign-off]
    L --> M[Project Release Authorization]
```

## 4.2 V-Model Mapping: Verification vs. Validation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VERIF[VERIFICATION - Left Arm of V]
        V1[User Requirements]
        V2[System Requirements]
        V3[Architectural Design]
        V4[Detailed Design]
    end
    subgraph CODE[CODING - V Bottom]
        V5[Implementation Phase]
    end
    subgraph VAL[VALIDATION - Right Arm of V]
        V6[Unit Testing]
        V7[Integration Testing]
        V8[System Testing]
        V9[User Acceptance Testing]
    end
    V1 --- V2
    V2 --- V3
    V3 --- V4
    V4 --- V5
    V5 --- V6
    V6 --- V7
    V7 --- V8
    V8 --- V9
    V9 --- V1
```

## 4.3 Validation Activity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Validation Activities Master Set]
    ROOT --> DYN[Dynamic Validation]
    ROOT --> STAT[Static Validation]
    DYN --> ST[System Testing]
    DYN --> AT[Acceptance Testing]
    DYN --> NFT[Non-Functional Testing]
    DYN --> IT[Integration Testing]
    ST --> FN[Functional Testing]
    ST --> REC[Recovery Testing]
    ST --> SEC[Security Testing]
    AT --> ALPHA[Alpha Testing]
    AT --> BETA[Beta Testing]
    AT --> REG[Regulatory Acceptance]
    NFT --> PERF[Performance Testing]
    NFT --> LOAD[Load Testing]
    NFT --> USAB[Usability Testing]
    STAT --> REV[Requirements Reviews]
    STAT --> WT[Design Walkthroughs]
    STAT --> INSP[Manual Inspections]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Validation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[INPUT SIDE]
        UR[User Requirements]
        US[User Stories]
        AC[Acceptance Criteria]
    end
    subgraph PROC[VALIDATION PROCESSING UNIT]
        VP[Validation Plan Generator]
        VTC[Test Case Designer]
        VTE[Test Executor]
        VEV[Evidence Collector]
    end
    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT SIDE]
        VSR[Validation Summary Report]
        DEF[Defect Log]
        SO[Sign-off Document]
        DEC[Go or No-Go Decision]
    end
    UR --> VP
    US --> VP
    AC --> VTC
    VP --> VTC
    VTC --> VTE
    VTE --> VEV
    VEV --> VSR
    VEV --> DEF
    VSR --> DEC
    DEF --> SO
    SO --> DEC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for Students:**
> The above architecture mirrors a typical KTU lab assignment pattern for designing a test harness. The *INPUT* layer represents what the customer provides; the *PROCESSING* layer is the testing team's responsibility; the *OUTPUT* layer represents the deliverables that go back to the user.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "Validation" in software testing. How is it different from Verification?**

**Model Answer:**

Validation is the process of evaluating the **final software product** to determine whether it meets the **user's needs, expectations, and operational requirements** in the intended environment. It answers the question: *"Are we building the right product?"*

| Verification | Validation |
|---|---|
| Builds the product right | Builds the right product |
| Static analysis, reviews, inspections | Dynamic execution, system & acceptance tests |
| Performed during development | Performed after development |
| Detects specification defects | Detects requirement defects |

> **Key Point:** Verification is **specification-driven**; Validation is **usage-driven**. The KTU board expects both the *definition* and *one distinct difference* for full 3 marks.

### Question 2: List the Levels of Validation `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: List any six validation activities performed in a software project.**

**Model Answer:**

The six major validation activities are:
1. **Unit Testing** — Validates individual modules
2. **Integration Testing** — Validates module interactions
3. **System Testing** — Validates the complete integrated system
4. **Acceptance Testing** — Validates against user requirements
5. **Alpha Testing** — Internal simulated user environment
6. **Beta Testing** — Real user environment at customer site

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **Validation Activities** in software testing with reference to the **V-Model**. Discuss in detail the various levels of validation with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Validation Activities are the testing and review mechanisms used to confirm that a software system meets its *intended use* and *user requirements*. In the **V-Model** of software development, validation occupies the **right descending arm**, parallel to the verification activities on the left arm.

**Levels of Validation in V-Model:**

1. **Unit Testing** — The smallest testable units are validated against detailed design specifications. *Example*: Testing a login function with valid and invalid credentials.
2. **Integration Testing** — Validates that combined modules work together correctly. *Example*: Validating data flow between order placement and inventory modules.
3. **System Testing** — Validates the complete system against specified requirements. *Example*: Validating the entire e-commerce workflow from product search to checkout.
4. **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** — Validates the system in real-world business scenarios. *Example*: A retailer performing UAT before a festive season launch.

The key idea is that **each level of validation on the right arm corresponds to a level of verification on the left arm**, ensuring traceability from requirements to tests.

**Q: (b)** A hospital management system has **320 user requirements**. During the validation phase, **286 requirements** were successfully validated, and **34 requirements** are pending. The system has **45,000 lines of code** with **62 defects** logged. Compute:
- (i) Validation Coverage
- (ii) Pending Coverage
- (iii) Defect Density per KLOC
- (iv) Comment on the quality of the system based on these metrics. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $R_{total} = 320$
- $R_{valid} = 286$
- $R_{pending} = 34$
- $LOC = 45{,}000$
- $D = 62$

**(i) Validation Coverage:**

$$
\text{VC} = \frac{286}{320} \times 100\% = 89.375\% \approx 89.38\%
$$

**(ii) Pending Coverage:**

$$
\text{PC} = \frac{34}{320} \times 100\% = 10.625\% \approx 10.63\%
$$

**(iii) Defect Density per KLOC:**

$$
\text{KLOC} = \frac{45{,}000}{1000} = 45
$$

$$
\text{Defect Density} = \frac{62}{45} = 1.378 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

**(iv) Quality Commentary:**

- An **89.38% validation coverage** is **below the industry-recommended threshold of 95%** for healthcare applications (where regulatory standards demand higher coverage).
- A **defect density of 1.378 per KLOC** is **acceptable** (industry average is 0.5–1.0 for high-quality software, though healthcare often targets <0.5).
- **Recommendation**: The system should not be released for production; the remaining 34 requirements must be validated, and defects should be reduced further.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the various **types of acceptance testing** used as validation activities. Differentiate between **Alpha** and **Beta testing** with examples. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Acceptance testing is the **final and most user-centric** validation activity. It confirms that the system is ready for production deployment.

**Types of Acceptance Testing:**

1. **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** — Performed by end users to verify the system meets business needs.
2. **Alpha Testing** — Conducted at the developer's site by internal testers in a controlled lab environment simulating real usage.
3. **Beta Testing** — Conducted at the customer's site by actual end users in a real environment.
4. **Contract Acceptance Testing (CAT)** — Performed against contractually agreed acceptance criteria.
5. **Regulation Acceptance Testing (RAT)** — Validates compliance with legal/regulatory standards (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA).
6. **Operational Acceptance Testing (OAT)** — Validates operational readiness (backup, recovery, performance under load).

**Alpha vs. Beta Testing:**

| Parameter | Alpha Testing | Beta Testing |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Developer's site | Customer's site |
| Performed By | Internal QA team | End users |
| Environment | Controlled lab | Real production-like |
| Visibility | Confidential | Public/exposed |
| Goal | Catch defects before release | Get feedback from real users |
| Duration | Longer (thorough) | Shorter (field trial) |
| Example | An e-commerce company tests its new checkout flow in a staging lab | The same checkout is released to a subset of real customers to gather usage data |

**Q: (b)** A startup is building a **food delivery mobile app**. Identify the most suitable **validation activities** for this project. Justify each choice with a one-line reason. Compute the **Weighted User Acceptance Rate** if the test execution results are: **[7 Marks]**

| Criticality | Total | Passed | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical | 30 | 27 | 0.6 |
| Major | 50 | 44 | 0.3 |
| Minor | 20 | 19 | 0.1 |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Suitable Validation Activities for a Food Delivery App:**

1. **Functional System Testing** — Validates core flows (browse → order → pay → track).
2. **Performance/Load Testing** — Validates app behavior during peak hours (dinner rush).
3. **Usability Testing** — Validates that users across age groups can place orders easily.
4. **Security Testing** — Validates payment data protection (PCI-DSS compliance).
5. **Compatibility Testing** — Validates app on iOS, Android, various screen sizes.
6. **Beta Testing** — Real users in real conditions (traffic, weak networks, varied devices).
7. **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** — Restaurant partners validate the restaurant-side app.

**Weighted UAR Calculation:**

Critical Acceptance Rate:

$$
AR_{crit} = \frac{27}{30} \times 100\% = 90.00\%
$$

Major Acceptance Rate:

$$
AR_{maj} = \frac{44}{50} \times 100\% = 88.00\%
$$

Minor Acceptance Rate:

$$
AR_{min} = \frac{19}{20} \times 100\% = 95.00\%
$$

Weighted Calculation:

$$
WUAR = (90.00 \times 0.6) + (88.00 \times 0.3) + (95.00 \times 0.1)
$$

$$
WUAR = 54.00 + 26.40 + 9.50 = 89.90\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A WUAR of 89.90% indicates the app is **not yet ready for release**. KTU best practice requires ≥ 95% WUAR for customer-facing apps. The team must fix all critical and major defects before re-execution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Validation with Verification** — Writing "validation is checking the code" loses 2-3 marks instantly. Always emphasize *user needs* and *intended use*.
> 2. **Forgetting to Map V-Model Sides** — Validators are on the right; Verifiers are on the left. Reversing this loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Unit Rounding Errors** — Use **2 decimal places** consistently. Rounding to whole numbers loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the Justification** — When asked to "suggest validation activities," you must **justify each** in 1 line. A bare list loses 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Missing the Quality Commentary** — In numerical questions, the *interpretation* of the result carries **at least 1 mark**. Always end with a quality assessment.
> 6. **Mixing Up Alpha and Beta Locations** — Alpha = developer's site, Beta = customer's site. This is a favorite 1-mark trap question.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Validation Activities**
>
> ✅ **Definition**: Validation = Building the *right* product; ensures fitness for use.
> ✅ **Verification vs. Validation**: Right vs. Right product; Static vs. Dynamic; Specs vs. Usage.
> ✅ **V-Model Position**: Validation = Right descending arm; Verification = Left descending arm.
> ✅ **Validation Levels**: Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance (corresponds to Right Arm of V).
> ✅ **Acceptance Testing Types**: UAT, Alpha, Beta, CAT, RAT, OAT.
> ✅ **Alpha vs. Beta**: Alpha at developer's site, Beta at customer's site.
> ✅ **Key Metrics**: Validation Coverage, Defect Density, WUAR, DDE, Validation Yield.
> ✅ **Formulas to Memorize**:
>     - $\text{VC} = \frac{R_{valid}}{R_{total}} \times 100\%$
>     - $\text{DD} = \frac{D}{KLOC}$
>     - $\text{WUAR} = \sum (AR_i \times W_i)$
> ✅ **Industry Thresholds**: ≥ 95% VC for critical systems; ≤ 0.5 defects/KLOC for high-quality software.
> ✅ **Safety-Critical Domains**: Aerospace (FAA), Medical (FDA 21 CFR Part 820), Automotive (ISO 26262), Banking (RBI/SEBI).
> ✅ **KTU Hot Topics**: V-Model mapping, Alpha vs. Beta distinction, WUAR weighted calculation, Validation Coverage numerical problems.
> ✅ **Examiner's Mantra**: Always end numerical answers with a **quality interpretation**; always end descriptive answers with a **real-world example**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
