> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regression Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regression Testing** is a type of software testing that ensures that previously developed and tested software still performs correctly after a change, modification, or addition has been made to the application. It verifies that recent code changes have not adversely affected existing, unmodified features of the system.

According to the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board) Glossary** — which is the standard reference framework adopted by KTU 2024 Scheme for the Software Testing course — regression testing is defined as:

> *"Testing of changes to a previously tested program following modifications to ensure that defects have not been introduced or uncovered in unchanged areas of the software as a result of the changes made."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, regression testing is positioned as a **critical control activity** within the broader **Maintenance and Change Management** lifecycle. Whenever a defect is fixed, a new feature is added, or the code is optimized/refactored, the entire risk surface of the system can shift. Regression testing acts as a **safety net** that re-validates unchanged functionality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEITT862 – Module 2):**
> Regression testing is a **mandatory** activity, NOT optional. It is non-negotiable in any professional software development pipeline and is one of the most frequently asked topics in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you live in a large, beautiful house. You hire an electrician to fix a single broken switch in the living room. After he finishes, you would **not** assume the rest of the house is untouched, would you? You would instinctively:

1. **Re-check that switch** (to confirm the fix worked).
2. **Walk around the house** to check that the kitchen, bedroom lights, fans, and geyser are still working (because the electrician may have accidentally tripped a breaker or loosened a connection).
3. **Check related circuits** that share wiring with the original switch.

This "go-around-and-re-check" mindset is **exactly** what regression testing does in software. When a developer changes one piece of code, the change can ripple through interconnected modules, libraries, APIs, and databases. Regression testing is the systematic, automated, and prioritized re-running of old test cases to catch these unintended side-effects.

| Real-World Concept | Software Testing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Fixing one switch | Patching a defect / adding a feature |
| Walking around the house | Executing the **regression test suite** |
| Checking related circuits | **Impact analysis** + dependent test cases |
| Calling the electrician again if something broke | Logging a **new defect** and re-iterating |

---

## 1.3 Why Regression Testing Exists — The Core Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Law of Software Change:**
> *Every change to a software system has the potential to introduce new defects or re-activate previously fixed ones, even in modules that were not directly touched.*

This is not paranoia — it is statistical reality. Empirical industry data (frequently cited in KTU reference material) indicates that **over 60% of post-release defects** are actually **regressions** — defects that were introduced by well-intentioned code changes.

The three primary triggers that **mandate** regression testing are:

- **Bug Fixes**: When a defect in module A is fixed, the patch may inadvertently break module B.
- **New Features**: Adding a feature increases the public API surface and may conflict with existing flows.
- **Performance / Refactoring / Environment Changes**: Optimizing code, upgrading a library, or moving to a new OS/JDK can subtly alter runtime behavior.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Regression Testing (Operational Breakdown)

Pillar 1 — **Re-Execution of Existing Test Cases**
The tester takes a curated subset (or the full set) of previously passing test cases and runs them against the new build. The outcome is a binary verdict: *did anything break?*

Pillar 2 — **Impact / Dependency Analysis**
Before blindly re-running thousands of cases, a smart tester asks: *which modules does the change actually touch, directly or transitively?* This narrows down the regression scope.

Pillar 3 — **Test Suite Maintenance**
The regression suite is not a static artifact. It must be pruned, updated, and prioritized after every cycle. Stale, redundant, or obsolete cases must be removed.

---

## 2.2 Types of Regression Testing (KTU Board-Favorite Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> This classification is **high-yield** for KTU ESE Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorize the table below.

| # | Type | Definition | When Used | KTU Exam Cue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unit Regression** | Re-testing a single modified function/class in isolation | Right after a developer patches a unit | "Isolated module-level" |
| 2 | **Regional (Partial) Regression** | Re-testing a region of the system that contains the change plus its immediate neighbors | When a localized change has a known blast radius | "Localized area" |
| 3 | **Full Regression** | Re-testing the entire application end-to-end | Before a major release, after major refactoring, or after multiple concurrent changes | "Whole application" |
| 4 | **Selective Regression** | Running a *selected subset* of the full regression suite, based on impact analysis and code-coverage data | Every normal iteration in an Agile sprint | "Subset / chosen tests" |
| 5 | **Progressive Regression** | Test cases are updated to handle new features alongside old ones; the test suite *grows* over time | When new features are being added on top of stable code | "New test cases added" |
| 6 | **Corrective Regression** | No new changes have been introduced; only re-running existing tests to confirm stability | When the existing functionality is being verified again | "No code change, only re-verification" |

---

## 2.3 Regression Test Selection Techniques (Analytical Engine)

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically requires knowledge of techniques used to **select** which test cases to run when a full re-run is impractical. The three most cited techniques are:

### Technique 1 — **Re-test All**
- **Logic**: Run every test case in the entire suite.
- **Pros**: Maximum safety; highest defect-detection guarantee.
- **Cons**: Extremely **expensive in time and cost**; not feasible for large systems.
- **When**: Final release, regulatory audits, or small systems with <500 test cases.

### Technique 2 — **Regression Test Selection (RTS)**
- **Logic**: Select a subset of test cases that exercise code affected by the change. The classical formula is:

$$
S' = \{ t \in T \mid \text{cover}(t) \cap \text{affectedCode}(C) \neq \emptyset \}
$$

  Where:
- $T$ is the full test suite
- $S'$ is the selected subset
- $\text{cover}(t)$ is the code covered by test case $t$
- $\text{affectedCode}(C)$ is the code reachable from the change $C$

- **Pros**: Drastically reduces execution time (typically 30–60% reduction).
- **Cons**: Requires accurate **code-coverage instrumentation**.

### Technique 3 — **Test Case Prioritization**
- **Logic**: Reorder test cases so that the **most important / highest-fault-detecting** ones run first. If time runs out, the highest-value tests have already executed.
- **Common Prioritization Signals** (high-yield for KTU):
  1. Code coverage of recent changes
  2. Historical defect density of modules
  3. Customer-visible criticality (login > admin panel > logging)
  4. Frequency of execution in production
- **Pros**: Best early-defect-detection rate per unit of time.

---

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Core Purpose** | Ensure unchanged code still works after a change |
| **Primary Trigger** | Bug fix, new feature, refactor, environment upgrade |
| **Most Common Type (Industry)** | Selective Regression |
| **Most Common Type (KTU Exam)** | Full Regression / Corrective Regression |
| **Cost in SDLC** | Roughly 30–50% of total testing effort |
| **Automation Need** | Practically mandatory; manual regression is infeasible beyond 100 cases |
| **Key Risk if Skipped** | Re-emergence of old defects in production |
| **Best Practice** | Automate + Prioritize + Maintain the suite |
| **Relationship to Re-testing** | Re-testing checks *specific* failed tests; Regression checks the *whole system* |
| **Selection Formula** | $S' = \{ t \in T \mid \text{cover}(t) \cap \text{affectedCode}(C) \neq \emptyset \}$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, regression testing is the **backbone of Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (CI/CD)** pipelines. Every time a developer pushes a commit, the CI server (e.g., Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) automatically triggers a regression suite. If any test fails, the push is **blocked** from reaching production. This is how companies like Google, Microsoft, and Amazon ship code to billions of users multiple times per day while maintaining stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Process, Worked Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Standard 7-Step Regression Testing Process (Full Derivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exact flow** expected in a KTU 14-mark question. Memorize the steps and be ready to draw a flowchart.

**Step 1 — Identify the Change (Source of Modification)**
Document *what* was changed, *who* changed it, *when*, and *why*. This is done via the change request, Git commit message, or ticket (Jira/Bugzilla).

**Step 2 — Perform Impact Analysis**
Trace dependencies from the changed module outward. Ask: *Which modules call this function? Which tables does this query touch? Which UI screens consume this API?*

**Step 3 — Categorize the Change**
- Bug fix → corrective regression
- New feature → progressive regression
- Refactor → regional + selective regression
- Major release → full regression

**Step 4 — Select the Regression Test Subset**
Apply one of the three techniques (Re-test All, RTS, or Prioritization). For most modern teams, **Prioritization + RTS hybrid** is the gold standard.

**Step 5 — Prioritize the Selected Suite**
Assign priority (P0, P1, P2, P3). P0 = smoke-critical (login, payment). P3 = cosmetic.

**Step 6 — Execute the Regression Suite**
Run the tests. For large systems, this is fully automated in a CI pipeline. Manual execution is reserved for exploratory / UX regression.

**Step 7 — Report & Decide**
- **0 failures + 0 skipped** → Build is GREEN. Promote to next environment.
- **Failures detected** → Log defects, block promotion, send back to developer, then **re-run full regression after the fix** (this is the loop).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Test Case Selection (Step-by-Step)

> This is a **KTU board-style numerical** that frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Problem Statement:**

A software application has the following **5 modules**: $M_1$ (Login), $M_2$ (User Profile), $M_3$ (Shopping Cart), $M_4$ (Payment), $M_5$ (Order History).

The **regression test suite** $T$ contains the following **6 test cases** with their module coverage:

| Test Case $t$ | Modules Exercised |
|---|---|
| $t_1$ | $M_1, M_2$ |
| $t_2$ | $M_3$ |
| $t_3$ | $M_3, M_4$ |
| $t_4$ | $M_4, M_5$ |
| $t_5$ | $M_1$ |
| $t_6$ | $M_2, M_5$ |

A developer makes a **change $C$ in module $M_3$** (e.g., fixes a bug in the shopping cart). Apply the **Regression Test Selection formula** to determine which test cases must be re-run.

---

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Affected Code**

$$
\text{affectedCode}(C) = \{ M_3 \}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Coverage of Each Test Case**

We compute $\text{cover}(t_i) \cap \text{affectedCode}(C)$ for every test case $t_i \in T$.

**For $t_1$:**
$$
\text{cover}(t_1) \cap \{M_3\} = \{M_1, M_2\} \cap \{M_3\} = \emptyset
$$

Result: **$t_1$ is NOT selected.**

**For $t_2$:**
$$
\text{cover}(t_2) \cap \{M_3\} = \{M_3\} \cap \{M_3\} = \{M_3\}
$$

Result: **$t_2$ IS selected.**

**For $t_3$:**
$$
\text{cover}(t_3) \cap \{M_3\} = \{M_3, M_4\} \cap \{M_3\} = \{M_3\}
$$

Result: **$t_3$ IS selected.**

**For $t_4$:**
$$
\text{cover}(t_4) \cap \{M_3\} = \{M_4, M_5\} \cap \{M_3\} = \emptyset
$$

Result: **$t_4$ is NOT selected.**

**For $t_5$:**
$$
\text{cover}(t_5) \cap \{M_3\} = \{M_1\} \cap \{M_3\} = \emptyset
$$

Result: **$t_5$ is NOT selected.**

**For $t_6$:**
$$
\text{cover}(t_6) \cap \{M_3\} = \{M_2, M_5\} \cap \{M_3\} = \emptyset
$$

Result: **$t_6$ is NOT selected.**

**Step 3 — Assemble the Selected Subset $S'$**

$$
S' = \{ t_2, t_3 \}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Efficiency Metrics**

$$
\text{Selection Ratio} = \frac{\vert S' \vert}{\vert T \vert} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333 \text{ (or } 33.3\%\text{)}
$$

$$
\text{Time Saved} = \left(1 - \frac{\vert S' \vert}{\vert T \vert}\right) \times 100\% = 66.7\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Out of 6 test cases, only **2 must be re-executed**, saving **66.7%** of regression effort while still covering every code path affected by the change.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Scheme Tip:** When solving this type of question, always show the **set-intersection operation explicitly** as done above. Examiners award marks for each correct intersection, not just the final answer.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Regression Type

| Situation | Recommended Regression Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| A typo fix in a CSS file | Smoke Regression only | Blast radius is purely visual; no logic touched |
| A bug fix in payment calculation | Full Regression of payment + related modules | Money is involved; zero tolerance for side-effects |
| A new "Wishlist" feature added | Progressive Regression | New feature + all existing flows must be re-verified |
| Library upgrade (e.g., React 17 → 18) | Full Regression | API contracts and runtime behavior can change globally |
| Refactoring internal helper functions | Regional Regression with strong unit test coverage | If unit tests pass, risk is contained |
| End of sprint in Agile | Selective Regression (prioritized) | Time-boxed; cannot run everything |
| Final release to production | Full Regression (Re-test All) | Maximum safety; non-negotiable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Regression Testing Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Change Identified: Bug Fix or New Feature] --> B[Impact Analysis: Map Dependencies]
    B --> C[Categorize Change Type]
    C --> D1[Bug Fix]
    C --> D2[New Feature]
    C --> D3[Refactor]
    C --> D4[Major Release]
    D1 --> E[Corrective Regression Suite]
    D2 --> F[Progressive Regression Suite]
    D3 --> G[Regional Regression Suite]
    D4 --> H[Full Regression Suite]
    E --> I[Apply Test Selection Technique]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Prioritize Tests: P0 to P3]
    J --> K[Execute Regression Suite Automated or Manual]
    K --> L{All Tests Pass}
    L -->|Yes| M[Build GREEN: Promote to Next Environment]
    L -->|No| N[Log Defects and Block Promotion]
    N --> O[Developer Fixes Defects]
    O --> P[Re-run Full Regression]
    P --> K
    M --> Q[Update Regression Test Suite for Future Cycles]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CI/CD Regression Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source Control Layer]
        repoA[Git Repository: Developer Push]
    end
    subgraph CI[Continuous Integration Server]
        triggerA[Webhook Trigger]
        buildA[Compile and Build]
        unitA[Run Unit Tests]
        staticA[Static Analysis and Lint]
    end
    subgraph RT[Regression Test Engine]
        selA[Regression Test Selector]
        prioA[Priority Queue P0 to P3]
        execA[Test Execution Engine]
        reportA[Result Reporter]
    end
    subgraph DEC[Decision Gate]
        gateA{Quality Gate Passed}
    end
    subgraph DEPLOY[Deployment Layer]
        stageA[Staging Environment]
        prodA[Production Environment]
    end
    repoA --> triggerA
    triggerA --> buildA
    buildA --> unitA
    unitA --> staticA
    staticA --> selA
    selA --> prioA
    prioA --> execA
    execA --> reportA
    reportA --> gateA
    gateA -->|Pass| stageA
    gateA -->|Fail| notifyA[Notify Developer and Block]
    stageA --> prodA
```

## 4.3 Regression Test Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Change C in Module M] --> q1{Is the change localized to one module?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Is the module safety or money critical?}
    q1 -->|No| q3[Run Full Regression]
    q2 -->|Yes| q4[Run Full Regression of related modules]
    q2 -->|No| q5[Run Selective Regression of affected code]
    q3 --> endA[End: Tests Selected]
    q4 --> endA
    q5 --> endA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q1. Define Regression Testing. When is it triggered in a software project?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Regression Testing is a type of software testing conducted to verify that a recent change in the code — such as a bug fix, new feature addition, performance optimization, or environment change — has not adversely affected the existing, previously working functionality of the application.

It is **triggered** in the following situations:
1. **After a bug fix** is applied to any module.
2. **After a new feature** is added or an existing one is enhanced.
3. **After refactoring** or code optimization is performed.
4. **After environment changes** such as database migration, OS upgrade, or third-party library update.
5. **Before a major release** as a final safety check.

> *Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if the student gives BOTH a clear definition AND at least 2–3 trigger scenarios. A definition alone fetches 1 mark.*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**Q2. Distinguish between Re-testing and Regression Testing.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Re-testing | Regression Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | To verify that a *specific* defect that was previously failed has been fixed | To verify that *unchanged* functionality still works after a change |
| **Scope** | Narrow — only the failed test cases | Broad — covers the entire affected area or whole system |
| **Defect Type** | Targets a known, logged defect | Targets *new, unintended* side-effects |
| **Trigger** | After a specific bug is fixed | After *any* change (bug fix, new feature, refactor) |
| **Order** | Usually performed **before** regression testing | Usually performed **after** re-testing |
| **Automation** | Often manual initially, then automated | Almost always automated in modern pipelines |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the concept of Regression Testing in detail. Discuss the different types of regression testing with suitable examples.
*(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **Regression Test Selection** technique using the formal selection formula. How does it help in reducing the testing effort?
*(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a)

Regression Testing is a quality assurance activity that re-runs previously executed test cases against a modified build to confirm that no existing functionality has been broken.

**The major types of regression testing are:**

**1. Corrective Regression**
Used when **no new functionality** is added and the test cases remain unchanged. The objective is simply to re-validate the existing behavior. *Example: Re-running the test suite of a calculator app after no code change, just to confirm ongoing stability.*

**2. Progressive Regression**
Used when **new functionality is added**. The test suite is progressively expanded to cover the new feature along with the existing ones. *Example: Adding a "Pay Later" option to a checkout flow. Existing test cases for checkout are re-run and new test cases for the Pay Later option are added.*

**3. Selective Regression**
A carefully **chosen subset** of the full regression suite is executed based on impact analysis. *Example: After fixing a bug in the search filter, only those test cases that exercise the search module and its dependent modules are re-run.*

**4. Regional (Partial) Regression**
The change is **localized** to a region of the system (e.g., a single service). Only that region and its direct neighbors are tested. *Example: Modifying the inventory microservice; only inventory-related and order-placement tests are run.*

**5. Full Regression**
The **entire test suite** is re-executed end-to-end. Used before major releases. *Example: Before launching version 5.0 of a banking application, the full 10,000-test-case suite is run overnight.*

**6. Unit Regression**
The lowest-level regression, done at the **class/function level** by the developer before committing. *Example: A developer modifies a `calculateTax()` function and re-runs the JUnit tests for that class.*

> *Examiner's note: Full 7 marks require a clear definition (1 mark) + 5–6 types explained with examples (5–6 marks). Students who list types without examples are capped at 4 marks.*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b)

**The Formal Selection Formula**

Let:
- $T$ = the complete regression test suite
- $C$ = the change applied to the code
- $\text{affectedCode}(C)$ = the set of code components reachable from $C$
- $\text{cover}(t)$ = the set of code components exercised by test case $t$
- $S'$ = the selected subset for re-execution

The selection rule is:

$$
S' = \{ t \in T \mid \text{cover}(t) \cap \text{affectedCode}(C) \neq \emptyset \}
$$

In simple English: a test case $t$ is selected for regression if and only if it covers **at least one** piece of code that is reachable from the change $C$.

**Worked Example**

A system has 4 modules: $A, B, C, D$. The test suite has 5 cases:

| Test Case $t$ | Modules Covered |
|---|---|
| $t_1$ | $A, B$ |
| $t_2$ | $B$ |
| $t_3$ | $C, D$ |
| $t_4$ | $D$ |
| $t_5$ | $A, C$ |

A bug is fixed in module $B$. Therefore $\text{affectedCode}(C) = \{B\}$.

Applying the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1: \quad &\{A,B\} \cap \{B\} = \{B\} \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow t_1 \in S' \\
t_2: \quad &\{B\} \cap \{B\} = \{B\} \neq \emptyset \Rightarrow t_2 \in S' \\
t_3: \quad &\{C,D\} \cap \{B\} = \emptyset \Rightarrow t_3 \notin S' \\
t_4: \quad &\{D\} \cap \{B\} = \emptyset \Rightarrow t_4 \notin S' \\
t_5: \quad &\{A,C\} \cap \{B\} = \emptyset \Rightarrow t_5 \notin S'
\end{aligned}
$$

So the selected regression subset is:

$$
S' = \{t_1, t_2\}
$$

**Effort Reduction Calculation**

$$
\text{Selection Ratio} = \frac{\vert S' \vert}{\vert T \vert} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40
$$

$$
\text{Time Saved} = 60\%
$$

Only **2 out of 5 test cases** need to be re-run, saving **60%** of testing effort while still covering every code path affected by the change in module $B$. This is the core practical benefit of the Regression Test Selection technique.

> *Examiner's note: Full 7 marks require the formula (2 marks) + correct set intersections shown step-by-step (3 marks) + final calculation and conclusion (2 marks).*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **7-step process** of regression testing. Why is impact analysis the most critical step?
*(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast the three **regression test selection techniques**: Re-test All, Regression Test Selection, and Test Case Prioritization. When would you choose each?
*(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(a)

The 7-step process of regression testing is:

1. **Identify the change** — Document what was changed and why.
2. **Impact analysis** — Map all modules transitively affected by the change.
3. **Categorize the change** — Bug fix, new feature, refactor, or release.
4. **Select the regression subset** — Apply Re-test All / RTS / Prioritization.
5. **Prioritize the suite** — Assign P0 (smoke-critical) to P3 (cosmetic).
6. **Execute the regression suite** — Automated in CI/CD pipelines.
7. **Report and decide** — Green build promotes; red build blocks.

**Why impact analysis is the most critical step:**

Without accurate impact analysis, the team either:
- **Over-selects** test cases → wastes 40–70% of testing time, slowing releases and increasing cost.
- **Under-selects** test cases → misses critical side-effects, leading to production defects and post-release hot-fixes.

Impact analysis directly determines the **precision** of the entire regression effort. It is the single decision that controls the trade-off between speed and safety.

---

#### Model Solution for Q(b)

| Parameter | Re-test All | Regression Test Selection (RTS) | Test Case Prioritization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core Idea** | Run every test in the suite | Run only tests covering affected code | Run all tests but in a smart order |
| **Effort** | 100% | 30–60% | 100% (execution) + sorting cost |
| **Defect Detection Guarantee** | Highest | High (misses non-affected latent bugs) | Highest *early*; total is unchanged |
| **Requires Coverage Data** | No | Yes (code coverage tool) | No (uses heuristics) |
| **Best For** | Final release, small suites | Normal CI cycles | Time-boxed sprints |
| **Risk** | Slow | May miss edge cases | May not finish if too many tests |
| **Cost** | Very High | Medium | Medium |

**When to choose each:**
- **Re-test All** → Final pre-release, regulatory compliance, suite <500 cases.
- **RTS** → Daily CI builds, large suites, when code coverage is instrumented.
- **Prioritization** → When the time budget is hard-capped (e.g., a 2-hour regression window) and partial coverage is acceptable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Re-testing with Regression Testing** in Part A. They are NOT synonyms. Always present the comparison table.
> 2. **Skipping the worked numerical** in the Selection Technique question. KTU examiners award marks for the *intersection steps*, not just the final set. A correct final answer without intermediate work gets only 2 out of 7 marks.
> 3. **Listing regression types without examples.** Memorizing names is half the battle; one realistic example per type doubles the mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the formula** $S' = \{ t \in T \mid \text{cover}(t) \cap \text{affectedCode}(C) \neq \emptyset \}$. Writing the words without the formula costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not justifying the choice of regression type** in analytical questions. Always end with a one-line "therefore, this type is appropriate because…" sentence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam):**

- **Definition**: Regression testing re-runs old test cases after a change to confirm no breakage in existing functionality.
- **Triggers**: Bug fix, new feature, refactor, environment upgrade, pre-release.
- **Six Types**: Corrective, Progressive, Selective, Regional, Full, Unit.
- **Three Selection Techniques**: Re-test All, RTS, Prioritization.
- **Selection Formula**: $S' = \{ t \in T \mid \text{cover}(t) \cap \text{affectedCode}(C) \neq \emptyset \}$
- **Re-testing ≠ Regression**: Re-testing = specific failed test. Regression = whole affected area.
- **Cost Share**: 30–50% of total testing effort in a typical project.
- **Automation**: Practically mandatory; manual regression is infeasible beyond ~100 cases.
- **Order in Test Cycle**: Re-testing (specific fix) → Regression (broader impact).
- **CI/CD Hook**: Triggered on every Git push in modern pipelines.
- **Golden Rule**: If unsure, run **Full Regression** — when in doubt, over-test rather than under-test.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid**: Treating regression testing as the same activity as system testing — it is a **distinct, change-driven** activity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unit Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Testing** is a level of software testing in which individual *units* or *components* of a software application are tested in isolation from the rest of the system. The primary purpose is to validate that each unit of the software code performs as designed. A *unit* is the smallest testable part of any software, typically a function, method, class, or module.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT862 - Software Testing)**, Unit Testing occupies the foundational tier of the **Testing Pyramid** introduced by Mike Cohn, where the three classical levels are:

1. **Unit Tests** (base, largest volume)
2. **Integration Tests** (middle layer)
3. **System / End-to-End Tests** (apex, smallest volume)

Formally, a unit test can be represented as a triplet:
$$ T = \langle I, S, O \rangle $$

where $I$ represents the **Inputs** (including parameters and preconditions), $S$ represents the **System Under Test (SUT)**, and $O$ represents the **Expected Outputs** (including postconditions and observable state changes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** As per the 2024 Scheme, Unit Testing sits within *Module 2: Functional and Structural Testing* and emphasizes techniques like **Equivalence Partitioning (EP)**, **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)**, **Control Flow Testing**, and **Data Flow Testing** as direct techniques to design unit-level test cases.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of unit testing like a **chef inspecting every ingredient before cooking a gourmet meal**:

- Before combining flour, eggs, sugar, and butter into a cake, the chef tastes each ingredient individually to ensure freshness, correct quantity, and proper quality.
- If a single ingredient is spoiled, the entire cake fails — even if all other ingredients are perfect.
- Similarly, in software, if a single function (the smallest testable part) behaves incorrectly, it can corrupt the entire application downstream.

**Another analogy:** Imagine building a house. Unit testing is like checking each brick for cracks, measuring its load-bearing capacity, and verifying its dimensions **before** it is placed into the wall. You don't wait for the entire wall to collapse to discover a defective brick.

### 1.3 Why Unit Testing? — The Strategic Value

> [!TIP]
> Unit tests are often the **cheapest, fastest, and most localized** tests in the entire software testing hierarchy. Industry research (including studies cited in the KTU syllabus) indicates that bugs caught at the unit level are typically **15× to 100× cheaper** to fix than those caught during system testing or post-deployment.

Key benefits include:

- **Early Defect Detection:** Catches bugs at the earliest stage of the SDLC.
- **Facilitates Refactoring:** A safety net for code modification.
- **Executable Documentation:** Unit tests serve as living, runnable documentation of code behavior.
- **Improved Design:** Writing testable code naturally encourages modular, decoupled design.
- **Faster Debugging:** Failed unit tests point directly to the offending line of code.

### 1.4 Distinguishing Unit Testing from Other Levels

| Characteristic | Unit Testing | Integration Testing | System Testing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single function/class | Multiple modules combined | Entire application |
| **Speed of Execution** | Milliseconds | Seconds to minutes | Minutes to hours |
| **Test Doubles Required?** | Almost always | Sometimes | Rarely |
| **Performed By** | Developer (primarily) | Developer/Tester | Independent QA team |
| **Failure Localization** | Very precise | Moderate | Difficult |
| **External Dependencies** | None (isolated) | Some | All |

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Characteristics of a Good Unit Test — The FIRST Principles

The **FIRST** acronym (popularized by Robert C. Martin in the KTU-recommended curriculum) defines five attributes of high-quality unit tests:

1. **F — Fast:** Tests should execute in milliseconds. Slow tests discourage frequent execution.
2. **I — Independent:** Tests must not depend on each other; any test can run in any order.
3. **R — Repeatable:** The same result every time, in any environment (no flaky tests).
4. **S — Self-Validating:** Tests automatically determine pass/fail without manual inspection.
5. **T — Timely:** Tests are written in a timely manner — ideally *before* the production code (TDD).

### 2.2 The Anatomy of a Unit Test Case (The AAA Pattern)

A well-structured unit test follows the **Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA)** pattern:

- **Arrange:** Set up the preconditions, create the SUT, configure test doubles, and prepare inputs.
- **Act:** Invoke the method/function being tested with the arranged inputs.
- **Assert:** Verify that the actual outcome matches the expected outcome (using assertions like `assertEquals`, `assertTrue`, etc.).

### 2.3 Test Doubles — The Isolation Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since unit tests must run in **isolation**, dependencies (databases, APIs, file systems, other classes) must be replaced with **Test Doubles**. Gerard Meszaros' classical taxonomy (which forms part of the KTU reference framework) defines five types:

| Test Double Type | Purpose | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Dummy** | Fill parameter lists | Never actually used; passed but ignored |
| **Stub** | Provide canned answers | Returns hardcoded values for specific calls |
| **Spy** | Record how it was called | A stub that also captures call information |
| **Mock** | Verify interactions | Pre-programmed with expectations; fails if not met |
| **Fake** | Working lightweight implementation | E.g., in-memory database replacing a real one |

### 2.4 Test-Driven Development (TDD) Cycle — Red-Green-Refactor

The TDD methodology mandates the iterative cycle:

1. **Red:** Write a failing test for the next bit of functionality.
2. **Green:** Write the minimum production code to make the test pass.
3. **Refactor:** Clean up both the production and test code, removing duplication.

This cycle is typically completed in **5 to 10 minutes** per increment, providing continuous regression safety.

### 2.5 Unit Testing Techniques (KTU Module 2 High-Yield Focus)

#### 2.5.1 Equivalence Partitioning (EP)
Inputs are divided into **equivalence classes** such that testing one representative value from each class is sufficient to infer correctness across the entire class. This exploits the principle that a program typically handles all values within an equivalence class identically.

#### 2.5.2 Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)
Focuses on testing the **boundaries** between equivalence partitions, as defects frequently cluster at edges. For a valid range $[a, b]$, BVA typically tests: $a-1, a, a+1, b-1, b, b+1$.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all critical formulas and metrics for Unit Testing as required by the KTU 2024 Scheme assessment.

| # | Metric / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Statement Coverage** | $C_{stmt} = \frac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of executed statements to total statements | Percentage |
| 2 | **Branch (Decision) Coverage** | $C_{branch} = \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of executed branches to total branches | Percentage |
| 3 | **Condition Coverage** | $C_{cond} = \frac{C_{evaluated\_true} + C_{evaluated\_false}}{2 \cdot C_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Evaluates each boolean sub-expression independently | Percentage |
| 4 | **Path Coverage** | $C_{path} = \frac{P_{executed}}{P_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of executed paths to total paths | Percentage |
| 5 | **Mutation Score** | $M_{score} = \frac{M_{killed}}{M_{total} - M_{equivalent}} \times 100\%$ | Effectiveness of test suite against mutations | Percentage |
| 6 | **Test Effectiveness** | $E_{test} = \frac{D_{caught}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Proportion of defects detected by the test suite | Percentage |
| 7 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \frac{D_{pre\_release}}{D_{pre\_release} + D_{post\_release}} \times 100\%$ | Effectiveness of testing phase in finding defects | Percentage |
| 8 | **Code Coverage (general)** | $CC = \frac{L_{covered}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Lines of code executed by tests | Percentage |
| 9 | **Boundary Off-by-One Test Count** | $n_{bva} = 4n + 1$ (for $n$ variables with ranges) | Number of BVA test cases for $n$ variables | Count |
| 10 | **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity** | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Independent paths in a control flow graph | Integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $S$ = statements, $B$ = branches, $C$ = conditions, $P$ = paths, $M$ = mutants, $D$ = defects, $E$ = edges in CFG, $N$ = nodes in CFG, $P$ = connected components, $L$ = lines of code.

### 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

Unit testing is the cornerstone of **Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (CI/CD)** pipelines. In modern DevOps workflows (used by companies like Google, Amazon, Microsoft):

- Every code commit triggers **automated unit test execution**.
- Coverage gates enforce minimum thresholds (e.g., **80%** statement coverage).
- A failing unit test **blocks the deployment pipeline**.
- Unit tests enable **safe large-scale refactoring** across millions of lines of code.
- They provide **living specifications** — the executable contract of the code's behavior.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Statement, Branch, and Path Coverage

**Scenario:** Consider the following pseudocode function `gradeClassifier(score)`:

```
1. FUNCTION gradeClassifier(score)
2.     IF score >= 90 THEN
3.         grade = "A"
4.     ELSE IF score >= 75 THEN
5.         grade = "B"
6.     ELSE IF score >= 50 THEN
7.         grade = "C"
8.     ELSE
9.         grade = "F"
10.    RETURN grade
11. END FUNCTION
```

**Step 1: Construct the Control Flow Graph (CFG)**

The control flow graph contains the following nodes and edges:
- **Nodes (N):** 1 (entry), 2 (decision1), 3 (assign A), 4 (decision2), 5 (assign B), 6 (decision3), 7 (assign C), 8 (assign F), 9 (return), 10 (exit). Total $N = 10$.
- **Edges (E):** entry→D1, D1→A (T), D1→D2 (F), D2→B (T), D2→D3 (F), D3→C (T), D3→F (F), A→return, B→return, C→return, F→return, return→exit. Total $E = 12$.
- **Connected Components (P):** $P = 1$.

**Step 2: Calculate Cyclomatic Complexity using McCabe's Formula**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P = 12 - 10 + 2(1) = 4$$

> This tells us there are **4 independent paths** through the function, which is the minimum number of test cases required for **100% path coverage**.

**Step 3: Design a Test Suite and Compute Coverage Metrics**

Let us evaluate the test suite: $T = \{ T_1(95), T_2(80), T_3(60), T_4(40) \}$.

For each test case, trace the execution:

| Test Case | Input | Path Taken | Statements Executed | Branches Executed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 95 | score≥90 → A | 1,2,3,9,10,11 | score≥90 (T) |
| $T_2$ | 80 | score<90, score≥75 → B | 1,2,4,5,9,10,11 | score≥90 (F), score≥75 (T) |
| $T_3$ | 60 | score<90, score<75, score≥50 → C | 1,2,4,6,7,9,10,11 | score≥90 (F), score≥75 (F), score≥50 (T) |
| $T_4$ | 40 | All false → F | 1,2,4,6,8,9,10,11 | All false branches |

**Step 4: Calculate Statement Coverage**

Total statements $S_{total} = 11$ (lines 1–11). 
Executed statements across all tests $S_{executed} = 11$ (all lines covered).

$$C_{stmt} = \frac{11}{11} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Step 5: Calculate Branch Coverage**

Total branches $B_{total} = 6$ (three decisions × two branches each). 
Executed branches $B_{executed} = 6$ (all true and false outcomes triggered).

$$C_{branch} = \frac{6}{6} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Step 6: Verify Path Coverage**

With 4 test cases and exactly 4 independent paths (matching $V(G) = 4$):

$$C_{path} = \frac{4}{4} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Mutation Score Calculation

**Scenario:** A function `isEven(n)` returns `true` if `n` is even.

The original function:
```
isEven(n) = (n % 2 == 0)
```

**Step 1: Generate Mutants (fault-injected versions)**

| Mutant ID | Mutation | Code |
|---|---|---|
| $M_1$ | Relational operator `==` → `!=` | `(n % 2 != 0)` |
| $M_2$ | Constant `0` → `1` | `(n % 2 == 1)` |
| $M_3$ | Operator `%` → `/` | `(n / 2 == 0)` |
| $M_4$ | Return `true` → `false` | `return false` |
| $M_5$ | Logical NOT added | `(!(n % 2 == 0))` |

**Step 2: Test Suite Used**

$T = \{ T_1(4), T_2(7) \}$ — testing an even and an odd number.

**Step 3: Execute Test Suite Against Each Mutant**

| Mutant | $T_1(4)$ | $T_2(7)$ | Killed? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M_1$ | Fails (4%2!=0 is false, but original true) | Passes | ✅ **KILLED** |
| $M_2$ | Fails (4%2==1 is false, but original true) | Passes | ✅ **KILLED** |
| $M_3$ | Fails (4/2==0 is false, but original true) | Passes | ✅ **KILLED** |
| $M_4$ | Fails (returns false, but original true) | Passes | ✅ **KILLED** |
| $M_5$ | Fails (returns false, but original true) | Passes | ✅ **KILLED** |

**Step 4: Compute Mutation Score**

Assume no equivalent mutants (i.e., mutants that always behave identically to the original):
$M_{total} = 5$, $M_{killed} = 5$, $M_{equivalent} = 0$.

$$M_{score} = \frac{M_{killed}}{M_{total} - M_{equivalent}} \times 100\% = \frac{5}{5 - 0} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A mutation score of **100%** indicates a highly effective test suite — it can detect virtually all artificially introduced faults, suggesting strong fault-detection capability in production scenarios.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Boundary Value Analysis Test Case Design

**Scenario:** A function `validateAge(age)` accepts ages between 18 and 60 (inclusive). It rejects any age outside this range.

**Step 1: Identify Equivalence Classes**

- **Valid class:** $V_1 = [18, 60]$ — ages that should be accepted.
- **Invalid class (low):** $I_1 = (-\infty, 17]$ — ages below the minimum.
- **Invalid class (high):** $I_2 = [61, +\infty)$ — ages above the maximum.

**Step 2: Apply BVA — Identify Boundary Points**

For a single variable with range $[a, b] = [18, 60]$, BVA test points are:

- **Just below minimum:** $a - 1 = 17$
- **At minimum:** $a = 18$
- **Just above minimum:** $a + 1 = 19$
- **A nominal mid value:** $a + \frac{b-a}{2} = 39$ (representative)
- **Just below maximum:** $b - 1 = 59$
- **At maximum:** $b = 60$
- **Just above maximum:** $b + 1 = 61$

**Step 3: Construct the BVA Test Suite**

$$T_{BVA} = \{ T_1(17), T_2(18), T_3(19), T_4(39), T_5(59), T_6(60), T_7(61) \}$$

**Step 4: Validate Coverage with the $n_{bva} = 4n + 1$ Formula**

With $n = 1$ variable:
$$n_{bva} = 4(1) + 1 = 5 \text{ (minimum BVA test cases)}$$

We used 7 test cases (3 invalid + 1 nominal + 3 invalid), exceeding the minimum to cover both boundary sides robustly.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Unit Testing Techniques to Coverage Goals

| Technique | Targets | Best Suited For | Coverage Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equivalence Partitioning** | Input domain | Data validation functions | Reduces redundant test cases |
| **Boundary Value Analysis** | Class edges | Numeric range checks | Catches off-by-one errors |
| **Statement Coverage** | Code lines | Identifying dead code | Weakest coverage criterion |
| **Branch Coverage** | Control decisions | Ensuring both paths tested | Stronger than statement |
| **Path Coverage** | All execution paths | Complex algorithms | Exponentially large for loops |
| **Mutation Testing** | Artificial faults | Measuring test suite quality | Gold standard for test effectiveness |
| **Data Flow Testing** | Variable def-use | Detecting improper state usage | Catches anomalies missed by control flow |

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Unit Testing Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Unit Under Test] --> B[Design Test Cases using EP and BVA]
    B --> C[Write Test Code with Assertions]
    C --> D[Arrange: Setup SUT and Test Doubles]
    D --> E[Act: Invoke Method Under Test]
    E --> F[Assert: Compare Expected vs Actual Output]
    F --> G{Test Passes?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Log Success and Update Coverage]
    G -->|No| I[Capture Failure and Stack Trace]
    I --> J[Report Defect to Developer]
    J --> K[Developer Fixes Production Code]
    K --> E
    H --> L{All Tests Executed?}
    L -->|No| D
    L -->|Yes| M[Generate Coverage and Mutation Reports]
    M --> N[Refactor Test Code and Re-execute]
    N --> O[Integration with CI and CD Pipeline]
```

### 4.2 Test Doubles Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TD[Test Double Root Category] --> A1[Dummy]
    TD --> A2[Stub]
    TD --> A3[Spy]
    TD --> A4[Mock]
    TD --> A5[Fake]
    A2 --> B1[Canned Hardcoded Returns]
    A3 --> C1[Captures Call Information]
    A4 --> D1[Verifies Expected Interactions]
    A5 --> E1[Working Lightweight Implementation]
    A1 --> F1[Placeholder Passed but Never Used]
```

### 4.3 Test-Driven Development Red-Green-Refactor Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Write a Failing Test RED] --> P2[Run Test Confirms Failure]
    P2 --> P3[Write Minimum Production Code]
    P3 --> P4[Run Test Confirms Pass GREEN]
    P4 --> P5[Refactor Production and Test Code]
    P5 --> P6[Run All Tests to Confirm No Regression]
    P6 --> P1
```

### 4.4 Testing Pyramid Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PY[Testing Pyramid] --> L1[System End to End Tests Few and Slow]
    PY --> L2[Integration Tests Moderate Volume]
    PY --> L3[Unit Tests Largest Volume Fast and Isolated]
    L1 --> M1[Validates Full User Workflows]
    L2 --> M2[Validates Module Interactions]
    L3 --> M3[Validates Individual Functions and Classes]
```

### 4.5 Coverage Criteria Subsumption Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SS[Path Coverage Strongest] --> BB[Multiple Condition Coverage]
    BB --> CC[Condition Coverage]
    CC --> DD[Branch Decision Coverage]
    DD --> EE[Statement Coverage Weakest]
    EE --> F1[Path subsumes Branch subsumes Statement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:** The subsumption diagram above formalizes the hierarchy of structural coverage criteria. A test suite achieving **100% path coverage** automatically guarantees **100% statement, branch, and condition coverage**, but the converse is not true. This relationship is critical in KTU Module 2 questions on control flow testing.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of Unit Testing. List any four characteristics of a good unit test.**

**Model Answer:**

Unit Testing is the lowest level of software testing where individual components or functions of a software application are tested in isolation to verify that each unit operates as designed. It is typically performed by developers during the coding phase and forms the base of the testing pyramid.

The four characteristics of a good unit test (FIRST principles) are:

1. **Fast:** Tests should execute in milliseconds to encourage frequent running.
2. **Independent:** Tests must not depend on each other and can run in any order.
3. **Repeatable:** Tests produce the same result every time, in any environment.
4. **Self-Validating:** The test automatically reports pass or fail without manual interpretation.

*(Any four characteristics, full 3 marks for definition plus four points.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Stubs and Mocks in unit testing with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Stub | Mock |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Purpose** | Provides canned, pre-defined responses to calls | Verifies that specific interactions occurred |
| **Verification Style** | State-based (output check) | Behavior-based (interaction check) |
| **Configuration** | Hardcoded return values | Pre-programmed expectations |
| **Example Use** | A stub returns a fixed user object when a database call is made | A mock verifies that `sendEmail()` was called exactly once with the correct parameters |
| **Failure Trigger** | Wrong return value during Act phase | Expected method not called or called incorrectly |

Stubs answer "**What to return?**" while Mocks answer "**Was it called correctly?**"
