# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Smoke Testing. List any four of its characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Smoke Testing is a preliminary testing technique performed on every new build to verify that the **critical and core functionalities** of the application are working correctly. It is a *shallow and wide* test that decides whether the build is **stable enough** to be accepted for further, detailed testing. It is also called **Build Verification Testing (BVT)**.

**Four Characteristics (1 Mark — ¼ each):**
1. **Shallow and wide** — covers many modules briefly without deep testing.
2. **Quick execution** — completes within **15 to 60 minutes**.
3. **Performed on every new build** before detailed testing begins.
4. **Acts as a gatekeeper** — failed smoke rejects the build outright.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Smoke Testing and Sanity Testing (any 4 points).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — ¾ Mark per point):**

| # | Smoke Testing | Sanity Testing |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Performed on **every new build** | Performed after a **specific bug fix** |
| 2 | Scope is **wide and shallow** (all modules) | Scope is **narrow and deep** (1–2 modules) |
| 3 | **Predefined and documented** test suite | Often **ad-hoc and undocumented** |
| 4 | **Usually automated** in CI/CD pipelines | **Usually manual** execution |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of Smoke Testing in detail. Discuss its **purpose, characteristics, advantages, and limitations**.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept (2 Marks):**
Smoke Testing, also known as **Build Verification Testing (BVT)**, is a preliminary testing approach performed on a freshly deployed build. The objective is to ensure that the **critical end-to-end workflows** of the application are operational. It is named after the hardware practice of powering up a circuit to check if **smoke** escapes — a basic viability check.

**2. Purpose (2 Marks):**
- To **verify build stability** before committing QA effort.
- To **catch show-stopper defects early** in the SDLC.
- To act as a **gatekeeper** for the build acceptance process.
- To provide **quick feedback** to developers on code quality.
- To save **time, cost, and resources** by rejecting broken builds early.

**3. Characteristics (1 Mark):**
- Shallow and wide, fast (≤ 60 min), automated in mature teams, focused on critical paths, repeatable, stable across sprints.

**4. Advantages (1 Mark):**
- Early defect detection, reduced integration risk, faster feedback, improved build quality, minimized QA effort on broken builds.

**5. Limitations (1 Mark):**
- Does not find **deep functional or non-functional defects**, requires a **maintained test suite**, may give a **false sense of security** if too shallow.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider an LMS (Learning Management System) application with **6 critical modules**: Student Registration, Login, Course Browsing, Video Playback, Quiz Submission, and Certificate Download. A QA team has **30 detailed test cases**. Design an effective **smoke test suite** and calculate the following metrics:
- (i) Smoke Test Coverage
- (ii) Smoke Test Efficiency Ratio
- (iii) Smoke Test Execution Time (assuming 4 min per test)
- (iv) Build Acceptance Rate (assume 4 builds received, 3 passed smoke)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Smoke Suite Design (1 Mark):**
Select **one happy-path test case per critical module** (industry standard).

$$
\text{Smoke Test Cases} = 6
$$

**Step 2 — Calculations (6 Marks — 1.5 each):**

**(i) Smoke Test Coverage (STC):**
$$
\text{STC} = \left( \frac{6}{6} \right) \times 100\% = \mathbf{100\%}
$$

**(ii) Smoke Test Efficiency Ratio (STER):**
$$
\text{STER} = \left( \frac{6}{30} \right) \times 100\% = \mathbf{20\%}
$$

**(iii) Smoke Test Execution Time (STET):**
$$
\text{STET} = 6 \times 4 \text{ min} = \mathbf{24 \text{ minutes}}
$$

**(iv) Build Acceptance Rate (BAR):**
$$
\text{BAR} = \left( \frac{3}{4} \right) \times 100\% = \mathbf{75\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion (½ Mark):**
The smoke suite is **highly efficient** (100% module coverage at only 20% effort) and the build stability is **acceptable (75% acceptance rate)**. The QA lead may recommend improving the developer testing process to push acceptance rate above **80%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a **neat diagram**, explain the **Smoke Testing process** in a CI/CD pipeline. How does it differ from **Sanity Testing** in terms of trigger, scope, and documentation?

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (3 Marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid flowchart — student should draw a clean block diagram of: Developer Commit → Build → Smoke Test Gate → Accept/Reject → System Test / Reject to Dev.)

**Process Explanation (3 Marks):**
1. Developer commits code to version control (Git).
2. CI/CD tool (Jenkins/GitLab) triggers **automated build**.
3. Build is deployed to a test environment.
4. **Predefined smoke test suite** is auto-executed.
5. If **all pass** → build moves to System/Regression testing.
6. If **any fail** → build is **rejected**, developers are notified, and the cycle restarts.

**Smoke vs Sanity Comparison (1 Mark):**
- **Trigger:** Smoke = new build; Sanity = bug fix.
- **Scope:** Smoke = wide/shallow; Sanity = narrow/deep.
- **Documentation:** Smoke = documented suite; Sanity = ad-hoc.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A banking application has **10 critical modules** and **100 detailed test cases**. The QA team executes a smoke test suite of **10 test cases** averaging **6 minutes per test**. Over **5 builds**, the smoke results are: Build 1 (10/10), Build 2 (8/10), Build 3 (10/10), Build 4 (9/10), Build 5 (10/10). Calculate:
- (i) Total Smoke Execution Time
- (ii) Average Smoke Pass Rate across 5 builds
- (iii) Build Acceptance Rate
- (iv) Smoke Test Efficiency Ratio

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Smoke Execution Time (2 Marks):**
$$
\text{Time per build} = 10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ min}
$$
$$
\text{Total Time} = 5 \times 60 = \mathbf{300 \text{ minutes} = 5 \text{ hours}}
$$

**Step 2 — Pass Rate per Build (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{STPR}_1 = 100\%,\ \text{STPR}_2 = 80\%,\ \text{STPR}_3 = 100\%,\ \text{STPR}_4 = 90\%,\ \text{STPR}_5 = 100\%
$$

**Step 3 — Average Pass Rate (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{Avg STPR} = \frac{100 + 80 + 100 + 90 + 100}{5} = \frac{470}{5} = \mathbf{94\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Build Acceptance Rate (1 Mark):**
- Builds with 100% pass rate: 1, 3, 5 → **3 accepted**.
- Builds 2 (80%) and 4 (90%) → **rejected** (since 100% is the acceptance threshold).
$$
\text{BAR} = \left( \frac{3}{5} \right) \times 100\% = \mathbf{60\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Smoke Test Efficiency Ratio (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{STER} = \left( \frac{10}{100} \right) \times 100\% = \mathbf{10\%}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The acceptance rate of 60% is **below the ideal 80%**, indicating the development team needs to improve unit testing and pre-commit checks. The smoke suite is **highly efficient at 10% effort** covering all 10 critical modules.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Mark-Losing Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing Smoke with Sanity** — This is the **single biggest mark-deduction point**. If a question asks for smoke and you write sanity characteristics, expect **at least 3–4 marks lost** immediately.
> 2. **Writing definitions without examples** — KTU examiners reward "**definition + example + advantage**" format. Writing only the definition forfeits the *application* marks.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram** — In any 7-mark sub-question, a **neatly labeled diagram** (even a hand-drawn one) fetches **1.5–2 marks minimum**. Always draw one.
> 4. **Not mentioning the gatekeeper / BVT terminology** — KTU 2024 scheme expects **standard ISTQB-aligned terminology**.
> 5. **Forgetting to compute all sub-parts in numerical questions** — Each sub-part (i), (ii), (iii), (iv) is typically worth **1–2 marks**. Missing one loses marks proportionally.
> 6. **Writing "smoke testing is done only once"** — **WRONG**. It is done on **every build**. This is a tested KTU trap.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Smoke Testing = Build Verification Test (BVT)** — a *shallow and wide* preliminary test.
- Performed on **EVERY new build** in the project lifecycle.
- Purpose: **verify critical paths** and act as a **gatekeeper** for detailed testing.
- **Failure → Build REJECTED** → sent back to developers.
- **Pass → Build ACCEPTED** → proceeds to System/Integration/Regression Testing.
- Typical suite size: **20–50 test cases** covering **all critical modules**.
- Execution time: **15 to 60 minutes** (strict time-boxed).
- **Shallow & wide** in nature — never deep.
- **Difference from Sanity:**
  - Smoke = new build, wide, documented, automated.
  - Sanity = bug fix, narrow, ad-hoc, manual.
- **Difference from Regression:**
  - Regression = re-execute *all relevant* tests after a change.
  - Smoke = execute only *critical path* tests on a *new build*.
- **Key Metrics:** STPR, BAR, DLST, STET, STC, STER.
- **Ideal BAR** ≥ 80%; **Ideal DLST** ≤ 5%; **Ideal STC** = 100% of critical modules.
- **Automation tools:** Jenkins, Selenium, TestNG, JUnit, PyTest, Postman (for APIs).
- **Named after** hardware smoke test — early circuit power-on check.
- **KTU 2024 tag:** Maps directly to **CO2** of PEITT862 Software Testing syllabus.
- **Always include a diagram** in 7-mark sub-answers — it is a **guaranteed mark-grabber**.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Performance Testing — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Testing** is a type of non-functional software testing technique used to determine the responsiveness, throughput, reliability, and scalability of a system under a specified workload. It is a critical subset of **Software Quality Assurance (SQA)** that measures quantitative attributes such as **response time**, **throughput**, **latency**, and **resource utilization** against predefined **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** and performance benchmarks.

According to the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board)** and KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, performance testing is broadly classified into several sub-categories:
- **Load Testing**
- **Stress Testing**
- **Endurance / Soak Testing**
- **Spike Testing**
- **Volume Testing**
- **Scalability Testing**

The objective is **not** to find functional defects, but to **eliminate performance bottlenecks** before deployment.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Stress Test"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Imagine a newly built 4-lane highway bridge. Before opening it to the public, engineers pour thousands of trucks (virtual load) onto it simultaneously. They measure: 
> 1. How long does it take a truck to cross? (**Response Time**)
> 2. How many trucks pass per minute? (**Throughput**)
> 3. At what point does the bridge begin to wobble or crack? (**Bottleneck Identification**)
> 4. How does it behave during a sudden rush-hour flood of traffic? (**Spike Test**)
> 5. Will it still hold up after 72 straight hours of traffic? (**Endurance Test**)

> Performance Testing is the **software equivalent** of this bridge stress test. Instead of trucks, we use **virtual users (VUsers)** to drive realistic load through the application.

---

## 1.3 Why is Performance Testing Critical?

| Perspective | Business Impact |
|-------------|-----------------|
| **End User** | Slow page loads → user abandonment (e.g., Amazon loses 1% sales per 100ms delay) |
| **Business** | SLA violations → financial penalties & reputation damage |
| **Operations** | Unidentified bottlenecks → costly production outages |
| **Architecture** | Validates horizontal/vertical scaling strategies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** — A formal contract defining acceptable performance thresholds such as *"95% of transactions must complete within 2 seconds under 1000 concurrent users."*

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold = Standard Industry Terms)

- **Response Time (RT):** Total time from sending a request to receiving the final response.
- **Throughput (TP):** Number of requests/transactions processed per unit time (e.g., requests/sec).
- **Latency:** Time delay between cause and effect (network/DB call delay).
- **Concurrent Users (VUsers):** Number of simultaneous active users.
- **Resource Utilization:** Percentage use of **CPU, Memory, Disk I/O, Network Bandwidth**.
- **Error Rate (%):** Percentage of failed requests under load.
- **P95 / P99 Percentiles:** 95th/99th percentile response time (used to identify tail latencies).
- **Hits per Second (HPS):** Raw HTTP request rate hitting the server.
- **Transactions per Second (TPS):** Logical business-level transaction rate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Performance Testing (Detailed Breakdown)

### 2.1.1 Load Testing
- **Definition:** Tests the system under expected real-world load conditions.
- **Goal:** Measure response time, throughput, and stability under **normal** and **peak** load.
- **Example:** A banking app expected to handle **5,000 concurrent users** during salary day.

### 2.1.2 Stress Testing
- **Definition:** Pushes the system **beyond** normal operational capacity to identify the **breaking point**.
- **Goal:** Determine how the system fails and whether it **recovers gracefully**.
- **Example:** Doubling the load to 10,000 users to find where the server crashes.

### 2.1.3 Endurance / Soak Testing
- **Definition:** Sustained load over a **long duration** (typically 8–72 hours).
- **Goal:** Detect **memory leaks**, **resource exhaustion**, and **degradation over time**.
- **Example:** Running 2,000 VUsers for 24 hours to see if RAM usage creeps upward.

### 2.1.4 Spike Testing
- **Definition:** Sudden, extreme bursts of load (spike up or spike down).
- **Goal:** Validate system resilience to **abrupt traffic surges**.
- **Example:** Flash sale on Flipkart — traffic jumps 10x in 30 seconds.

### 2.1.5 Volume Testing
- **Definition:** Tests the system with **large volumes of data** in the database.
- **Goal:** Check behavior when DB tables contain millions of rows.
- **Example:** Uploading 10 million records into the orders table.

### 2.1.6 Scalability Testing
- **Definition:** Measures the system's ability to **scale up (vertical)** or **scale out (horizontal)** as load increases.
- **Goal:** Identify the optimal scaling strategy.

---

## 2.2 The Performance Testing Life Cycle (PTLC)

1. **Requirement Gathering & Analysis** — Identify SLAs, workload profiles, target metrics.
2. **Test Planning** — Select tool, design test scenarios, define acceptance criteria.
3. **Test Scripting** — Record/enhance scripts using a performance tool (JMeter, LoadRunner).
4. **Test Data Preparation** — Generate realistic datasets (parameterization + correlation).
5. **Test Execution** — Run baseline → load → stress → endurance → spike tests.
6. **Monitoring & Profiling** — Capture server-side metrics (CPU, memory, DB queries).
7. **Analysis & Reporting** — Identify bottlenecks, compare against SLAs, suggest tuning.
8. **Re-test & Validation** — Verify that tuning fixes resolved the bottlenecks.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Purpose |
|---|------------------|----------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Throughput** | $TP = \frac{N_{req}}{T_{total}}$ | requests/sec | Transaction processing rate |
| 2 | **Average Response Time** | $RT_{avg} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} RT_i$ | seconds (s) | Mean latency |
| 3 | **Percentile (P95)** | $P_{95} = RT_{sorted}[0.95 \times N]$ | seconds (s) | Tail latency |
| 4 | **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda \times W$ | users | Concurrency relationship |
| 5 | **CPU Utilization** | $U_{CPU} = \frac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Server load |
| 6 | **Error Rate** | $ER = \frac{N_{fail}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Reliability under load |
| 7 | **Memory Utilization** | $U_{mem} = \frac{M_{used}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Leak detection |
| 8 | **Requests per User** | $R_{pu} = \frac{N_{req}}{N_{users}}$ | req/user | Workload modeling |
| 9 | **Think Time** | $T_{think} = T_{session} - T_{active}$ | seconds (s) | Realistic pacing |
| 10 | **Pacing (Hammerhead model)** | $P = \frac{T_{think} + T_{exec}}{N_{iterations}}$ | seconds/iter | Iteration delay |

> [!NOTE]
> **Little's Law** (John Little, 1961) is the foundation of performance engineering:
> - $L$ = Average number of users in the system
> - $\lambda$ = Arrival rate (throughput)
> - $W$ = Average time in system (response time)
>
> **Real-world utility:** Used by cloud architects (AWS, Azure) to plan **auto-scaling policies** and **capacity planning** for data centers.

---

## 2.4 Popular Performance Testing Tools

| Tool | Type | License | KTU Relevance |
|------|------|---------|---------------|
| **Apache JMeter** | Open-source | Free | ⭐ Most asked in exams |
| **LoadRunner (Micro Focus)** | Commercial | Paid | Industry standard |
| **Gatling** | Open-source (Scala) | Free | Modern, code-driven |
| **Locust** | Open-source (Python) | Free | Developer-friendly |
| **k6 (Grafana)** | Open-source (Go) | Free | Cloud-native |
| **BlazeMeter** | Cloud JMeter | Freemium | CI/CD integration |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Little's Law Applied to a Web Application

> **Given scenario:** An e-commerce website is observed during a 1-hour peak window. 
> - $\lambda$ (throughput) = **120 transactions/minute**
> - $W$ (avg response time) = **3 seconds**
> - Find $L$ (concurrent users).

### Step 1 — State Little's Law
$$L = \lambda \times W$$

### Step 2 — Unify Units
Convert $\lambda$ from per-minute to per-second to match $W$ in seconds:
$$\lambda = \frac{120 \text{ transactions}}{60 \text{ s}} = 2 \text{ transactions/s}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into Little's Law
$$L = 2 \text{ txn/s} \times 3 \text{ s} = 6 \text{ concurrent users}$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
> At any given moment during the peak hour, **6 users** are actively engaged with the website (waiting for a response or browsing).

### Step 5 — Reverse Application
If marketing projects **500 concurrent users** during a sale, what is the required throughput?
$$\lambda = \frac{L}{W} = \frac{500}{3} \approx 166.67 \text{ requests/s}$$

This is precisely the figure architects use to size backend pods and database connection pools.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Test Types

| Test Type | Load Level | Duration | Primary Goal | Failure Mode Studied |
|-----------|------------|----------|--------------|----------------------|
| **Load Test** | Expected peak | 1–2 hours | SLA validation | Slow response |
| **Stress Test** | 1.5× to 5× peak | 30 min – 1 hour | Breaking point | System crash |
| **Endurance Test** | Normal | 8–72 hours | Memory leak | Gradual degradation |
| **Spike Test** | Sudden burst | Seconds–minutes | Resilience | Sudden outage |
| **Volume Test** | Normal, big data | Varies | DB scalability | Query timeout |
| **Scalability Test** | Incrementally scaled | Varies | Growth planning | Capacity ceiling |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Throughput vs Response Time Trade-off

> **Scenario:** A REST API is benchmarked at three load levels. Results:

| VUsers | Throughput (req/s) | Avg Response Time (s) | CPU (%) |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|---------|
| 100 | 95 | 0.5 | 35 |
| 500 | 420 | 1.2 | 70 |
| 1000 | 480 | 4.8 | 95 |

### Analysis:

1. **Linear region (100 → 500 users):** Throughput scales (95 → 420) with near-proportional response time growth. System is **healthy**.

2. **Saturation region (500 → 1000 users):** Throughput grows only **14%** (420 → 480) while response time **quadruples** (1.2s → 4.8s). System is **saturated** — adding users no longer helps.

3. **Bottleneck:** CPU at 95% indicates **CPU-bound bottleneck** → solutions:
   - Vertical scaling (bigger CPU cores)
   - Code optimization (algorithmic complexity)
   - Caching (Redis/Memcached) to reduce CPU cycles
   - Horizontal scaling (load balancer + multiple pods)

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Percentile Calculation (P95)

> **Given:** 1000 response time samples sorted ascending. Find P95.

**Step 1 — Compute rank position:**
$$P_{95} = \lceil 0.95 \times N \rceil = \lceil 0.95 \times 1000 \rceil = 950$$

**Step 2 — Pick the value at that position:** The 950th sample in the sorted list is the **P95 response time**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why P95 not average?** Average masks outliers. If 99 users get a 1s response and 1 user gets a 60s response, the average is misleading (1.59s). P95 = 1s reflects the **typical user experience**.

---

## 3.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **E-commerce** | Pre-Black Friday traffic surge testing |
| **Banking** | NEFT/RTGS batch processing at month-end |
| **Healthcare** | EHR systems during shift change hours |
| **Streaming** | Netflix/YouTube CDN load at IPL final |
| **Gaming** | Server capacity for game launch day |
| **IoT** | Million-device telemetry ingestion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Performance Testing Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Identify Performance Requirements SLAs] --> B[Step2: Plan Test Scenarios and Workload Model]
    B --> C[Step3: Design Test Scripts with Parameterization]
    C --> D[Step4: Prepare Test Data and Correlation]
    D --> E[Step5: Execute Baseline Test Single User]
    E --> F{Baseline OK?}
    F -- No --> G[Fix Script and Environment] --> C
    F -- Yes --> H[Step6: Execute Load Test Expected Peak]
    H --> I[Step7: Execute Stress Test Beyond Peak]
    I --> J[Step8: Execute Endurance Test Long Duration]
    J --> K[Step9: Execute Spike Test Sudden Burst]
    K --> L[Step10: Collect Metrics RT TP CPU MEM]
    L --> M{SLAs Met?}
    M -- No --> N[Step11: Bottleneck Analysis and Tuning] --> H
    M -- Yes --> O[Step12: Generate Performance Test Report]
    O --> P[Sign-off and Production Release]
```

---

## 4.2 Performance Test Architecture (Client–Server–Monitor)

```mermaid

    LG1 --> LB

```

## 4.3 Bottleneck Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Performance Issue Detected] --> Q1{High Response Time?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{CPU at 90 percent or more?}

    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Memory at 90 percent or more?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> C2[Memory Bottleneck - Heap analysis, leak fix]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Disk I/O at saturation?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> C3[I/O Bottleneck - Index optimization, SSD]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Database slow queries?}
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Performance Testing. List any four types of performance testing.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Performance Testing** is a non-functional testing technique used to evaluate the speed, responsiveness, throughput, and stability of a system under a defined workload to verify if it meets the **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**.

**Four Types:**
1. **Load Testing** — validates system under expected load.
2. **Stress Testing** — finds the breaking point beyond expected load.
3. **Endurance Testing** — checks for memory leaks over long duration.
4. **Spike Testing** — verifies behavior under sudden traffic bursts.
5. (Bonus) Volume Testing — tests with large datasets.
6. (Bonus) Scalability Testing — tests ability to scale.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the terms: Response Time, Throughput, and P95 Latency with example.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Response Time (RT):** Total time taken from sending a request to receiving the full response. *Example: API returns in 1.2 seconds.*
- **Throughput:** Number of requests processed per unit time. *Example: Server handles 450 requests/second.*
- **P95 Latency (95th Percentile):** The value below which 95% of all response times fall. *Example: P95 = 2.5s means 95% of users get a response within 2.5s.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinguishing Tip:** RT measures **one user**, throughput measures **system capacity**, and P95 captures **user-perceived experience** fairly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **Performance Testing Life Cycle (PTLC)** in detail with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Load Testing, Stress Testing, and Spike Testing** with real-world scenarios and expected outcomes. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) PTLC Detailed Steps:**

**1. Requirement Gathering & Analysis (1 mark)**
- Collect SLAs, peak user count, acceptable response time, throughput targets.
- Identify business-critical transactions (e.g., checkout, login).

**2. Test Planning (1 mark)**
- Choose tool (JMeter/LoadRunner), test environment, hardware.
- Define workload model — VUser count, ramp-up time, test duration.

**3. Test Scripting (1 mark)**
- Record business flows in the tool.
- Apply **parameterization** (replace hard-coded values with CSV data) and **correlation** (capture dynamic session IDs).

**4. Test Data Preparation (1 mark)**
- Generate thousands of unique user credentials, product IDs, etc.
- Use a **data pool** for realistic data variety.

**5. Test Execution (1.5 marks)**
- Execute tests in phases:
  - **Smoke test** (2–3 VUsers to validate script).
  - **Baseline test** (1 VUser to set reference).
  - **Load test** (expected peak).
  - **Stress / spike / endurance tests.**

**6. Monitoring & Profiling (1 mark)**
- Use **APM tools** (Dynatrace, New Relic) to capture server CPU, memory, DB query time, GC pauses.

**7. Analysis & Reporting (0.5 marks)**
- Compare results vs SLAs; identify **bottlenecks**; generate graphs and recommendations.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — Test Types Comparison:**

| Test Type | Real-World Scenario | Expected Outcome |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Load Testing** | Salary day on banking app — 5,000 concurrent users | Verify 95% of transactions complete within 2s |
| **Stress Testing** | Simulating 20,000 users (4× expected) | System degrades gracefully; logs meaningful errors; auto-recovers when load drops |
| **Spike Testing** | Flash sale launch — traffic jumps from 500 to 8,000 in 30s | Auto-scaling triggers; no data corruption; queue absorbs excess |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse Load Testing with Stress Testing — load = expected, stress = beyond expected.
> - Always **state the metric measured** (RT, throughput, CPU%) in your answer.
> - Diagram (block diagram of PTLC) carries **1.5 marks**; skipping it loses easy marks.
> - Vague answers like *"testing for speed"* get **zero** — use exact terms: response time, throughput, SLA.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain **JMeter's architecture and components** for performance testing. Describe how you would design a script to test a **login and checkout workflow**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What are **performance bottlenecks**? Discuss with a neat diagram, the systematic approach to identify and resolve CPU, memory, database, and network bottlenecks. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — JMeter Architecture & Script Design:**

**JMeter Components (1.5 marks each sub-bullet):**
- **Test Plan** — root container.
- **Thread Group** — defines VUsers, ramp-up, loop count.
- **Samplers** — HTTP Request, JDBC Request, FTP Request.
- **Logic Controllers** — Loop, If, While, Transaction Controller.
- **Listeners** — View Results Tree, Summary Report, Aggregate Report.
- **Config Elements** — CSV Data Set Config, HTTP Header Manager.
- **Timers** — Constant Timer (think time), Gaussian Random Timer.
- **Assertions** — Response Assertion, Duration Assertion.

**Script Design for Login + Checkout (3.5 marks):**
1. Add **Thread Group** with 100 users, 60s ramp-up, 2 iterations.
2. Add **HTTP Request Defaults** with target server URL.
3. Add **CSV Data Config** with 100 unique usernames/passwords.
4. Add **HTTP Request — GET /login** (load login page).
5. Add **Regular Expression Extractor** to capture **session ID** (correlation).
6. Add **HTTP Request — POST /login** with parameterized credentials.
7. Add **Response Assertion** to verify "Welcome" in response.
8. Add **Transaction Controller** wrapping the entire flow.
9. Add **Constant Timer** (think time = 5s).
10. Add **Summary Report Listener** to capture RT, TPS, error %.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — Bottleneck Analysis:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A **performance bottleneck** is a component in the system where the overall throughput is limited by its capacity.

**Systematic Approach (with diagram, 6 marks):**
1. **Establish baseline** metrics.
2. **Run load test** and observe degraded metric.
3. **Drill down** layer by layer:

| Bottleneck Layer | Symptom | Diagnostic Tool | Resolution |
|------------------|---------|-----------------|------------|
| **CPU** | High CPU%, low throughput | `top`, `perf`, JFR | Code profiling, horizontal scaling |
| **Memory** | Increasing heap, GC pauses | `jmap`, JConsole | Memory leak fix, increase heap |
| **Database** | Slow queries, high lock waits | `EXPLAIN PLAN`, slow log | Indexing, query rewrite, sharding |
| **Network** | High latency, packet loss | `ping`, `traceroute`, Wireshark | CDN, compression, HTTP/2 |
| **Disk I/O** | High await time | `iostat` | SSD upgrade, async writes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students often write *"bottleneck = bug"* — wrong. A bottleneck is a **capacity limitation**, not a defect. Also, **always correlate** client-side RT with server-side metrics before concluding a bottleneck.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — KTU 2024**

- [x] **Performance Testing = Non-functional**, focused on **speed, stability, scalability**.
- [x] **6 Sub-types:** Load, Stress, Endurance, Spike, Volume, Scalability.
- [x] **Key Metrics:** Response Time, Throughput (req/s), P95/P99, CPU%, Memory%, Error Rate.
- [x] **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda \times W$ — backbone of capacity planning.
- [x] **SLAs** define the pass/fail criteria — without them, performance testing is meaningless.
- [x] **PTLC Phases:** Requirement → Plan → Script → Data → Execute → Monitor → Analyze → Report.
- [x] **JMeter = Open-source tool** — most likely in KTU practical/lab exams.
- [x] **Correlation** captures dynamic values; **Parameterization** injects varied data — don't mix them up.
- [x] **P95 is more meaningful than average** for user-experience evaluation.
- [x] **Bottleneck Resolution Order:** DB → App Code → App Server → Load Balancer → Network.
- [x] **Endurance testing** specifically targets **memory leaks** — runs for 8–72 hours.
- [x] **Spike test** validates **auto-scaling** and **circuit breaker** patterns.
- [x] **Stress test** answer must include: what was the breaking point, did it **fail gracefully** (proper error pages, no data corruption), and did it **recover** when load dropped.
- [x] **Tools Summary:** JMeter (open-source), LoadRunner (industry commercial), Gatling (modern).
- [x] Always draw the **PTLC block diagram** in 7-mark questions — it carries ~1.5 marks.
- [x] For calculations, **show unit conversions** (per minute → per second) explicitly to fetch full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Load Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Load Testing** is a non-functional software testing technique that is a sub-category of **Performance Testing**, designed to evaluate the behavior of a system under a specified expected load. The primary objective is to measure the **response time**, **throughput**, **resource utilization** (CPU, memory, disk I/O, network bandwidth), and **stability** of the system when subjected to concurrent user requests, transactions, or data volumes that simulate real-world or peak production conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, load testing is positioned within the broader discipline of **Performance Engineering**, where the System Under Test (SUT) is exercised against pre-defined Service Level Agreements (SLAs) to identify performance bottlenecks **before** deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Load testing answers the question: *"Will my system perform acceptably under the expected real-world load?"* — it does **NOT** test the breaking point (that is **Stress Testing**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Traffic Test

Imagine a newly constructed **4-lane flyover** in Kerala before it is opened to the public. The engineering team wants to know:

- Will 2,000 cars per hour pass smoothly?
- How long does it take to clear a traffic signal?
- Where do bottlenecks (junctions, toll booths) form?

The engineers simulate the traffic by driving **1,000 test cars** in a controlled manner during off-peak hours. They measure average speed, queue length, and fuel consumption. That simulation **is** load testing. Pushing 5,000 cars until the flyover cracks would be **stress testing**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Mandatory KTU Glossary)

> [!NOTE]
> The following terms appear frequently in KTU board exams and must be memorized verbatim.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Virtual User (VUser)** | A simulated user thread that mimics real user actions. |
| **Think Time** | The deliberate pause a real user takes between actions (e.g., reading a page). |
| **Pacing** | The interval between iterations of the same user scenario. |
| **Ramp-up** | The gradual increase in the number of virtual users over time. |
| **Ramp-down** | The gradual decrease in virtual users at the end of the test. |
| **Throughput** | The number of requests/transactions the system processes per unit of time (usually per second). |
| **Response Time** | The total time elapsed between sending a request and receiving the complete response. |
| **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** | A contractual performance threshold (e.g., 95th percentile response time < 2 seconds). |

### 1.4 Load Testing vs. Other Performance Tests

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Load**, **Stress**, **Spike**, and **Endurance** testing. Memorize the distinction below.

| Test Type | Load Condition | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Load Testing** | Expected production load | Verify SLAs are met under normal-to-peak conditions. |
| **Stress Testing** | Load **beyond** expected peak | Identify the breaking point of the system. |
| **Spike Testing** | Sudden, sharp increase then decrease | Check recovery from sudden traffic bursts. |
| **Endurance (Soak) Testing** | Expected load sustained for hours/days | Detect memory leaks and resource degradation. |
| **Volume Testing** | Large amount of data | Test database/IO performance with massive datasets. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Phases of Load Testing

A KTU 2024 board answer must present the process in **five logical phases**. Examiners award marks for clear phase demarcation.

1. **Test Environment Identification**
   - Hardware specs (servers, load generators, network).
   - Software stack (OS, web server, DB, middleware versions).
   - Network topology and bandwidth.
   - *Why:* Results are only reproducible when the environment is fully documented.

2. **Workload Characterization (Acceptance Criteria)**
   - Identify **key business transactions** (e.g., login, search, checkout).
   - Determine the **user distribution** — what % of users perform what action.
   - Establish **performance SLAs** (e.g., response time, throughput, error rate).
   - *Why:* You cannot test for what you have not defined.

3. **Test Scenario Design**
   - Define **ramp-up profile** (linear, stepped, spike).
   - Configure **think time** distribution (constant, random, recorded).
   - Decide on **test data strategy** (parameterized data pools).
   - *Why:* A well-designed scenario directly maps to real user behavior.

4. **Test Execution & Monitoring**
   - Execute the test from geographically distributed load generators.
   - Capture real-time metrics: response time, throughput, CPU, memory, network I/O.
   - *Why:* Real-time data is essential for diagnosing bottlenecks during the run.

5. **Analysis, Reporting & Tuning**
   - Compare results against SLAs.
   - Identify bottlenecks (database, application code, network).
   - Recommend optimizations and **re-test** after fixes.
   - *Why:* The final deliverable is actionable insight, not raw graphs.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas appear in KTU numerical problems. Memorize units carefully.

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Average Response Time (ART)** | $ART = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i}{N}$ | seconds (s) or ms | $T_i$ = response time of $i$-th transaction; $N$ = total transactions. |
| 2 | **Throughput (TP)** | $TP = \frac{\text{Total Requests Completed}}{\text{Total Test Duration}}$ | requests/second (RPS) | Also called *Transactions Per Second (TPS)*. |
| 3 | **Hits per Second (HPS)** | $HPS = \frac{\text{Total HTTP Hits}}{\text{Test Duration (s)}}$ | hits/s | Each page may generate multiple hits (HTML, CSS, JS, images). |
| 4 | **Concurrent Users (CU)** | $CU = \frac{\text{Peak Hour Visits} \times \text{Avg Session Time (s)}}{\text{Test Window (s)}}$ | users | Used to convert business projections to test load. |
| 5 | **Error Rate (ER)** | $ER = \frac{\text{Failed Transactions}}{\text{Total Transactions}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | SLAs typically demand $ER < 0.1\%$ for critical systems. |
| 6 | **Percentile Response Time** | $P_{x} = $ value below which $x\%$ of samples fall | seconds (s) | $P_{90}$, $P_{95}$, $P_{99}$ are common KTU 2024 favorites. |
| 7 | **CPU Utilization** | $CPU_{\%} = \frac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Sustainable threshold is generally $\leq 70\%$. |
| 8 | **Memory Utilization** | $Mem_{\%} = \frac{\text{Used Memory}}{\text{Total Memory}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Watch for steady growth → memory leak. |
| 9 | **Bandwidth Utilization** | $BW_{\%} = \frac{\text{Actual Throughput (Mbps)}}{\text{Max Bandwidth (Mbps)}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Should remain $\leq 80\%$ to avoid congestion. |
| 10 | **Little's Law (Queueing)** | $L = \lambda \times W$ | users | $L$ = users in system; $\lambda$ = arrival rate; $W$ = wait time. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule:** In the table above, the pipe character `|` was deliberately replaced with `$\vert$` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in exam answer sheets when typesetting such formulas.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Load testing is mandatory in:
- **Banking & FinTech:** Ensuring 10,000 concurrent UPI transactions succeed during salary days.
- **E-Commerce:** Surviving festive sales (Big Billion Days, Amazon Great Indian Festival).
- **Government Portals:** Kerala PSC, KSEB bill payment during month-end rush.
- **Healthcare Systems:** AIIMS patient registration under epidemic-load scenarios.
- **Telecom:** 5G core network element validation under subscriber density.

The cost of **NOT** load testing is captured in the famous industry saying: *"Performance is a feature. If it's not tested, it doesn't exist."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Average Response Time

**Problem [KTU 2024 ESE Model]:**
During a 10-minute load test, a web server processes **1,200 transactions**. The response times (in milliseconds) recorded for 6 sample transactions are: 220, 340, 410, 295, 380, 255.
Calculate (a) Average Response Time, (b) Throughput in TPS, and (c) comment on SLA compliance if the SLA demands response time $\leq 400$ ms.

**Solution:**

**(a) Average Response Time (ART):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ART &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i}{N} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{220 + 340 + 410 + 295 + 380 + 255}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1900}{6} \\[6pt]
&= 316.67 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We sum all six sample response times directly, then divide by the count of samples (6). The unit remains in **milliseconds** because the input was in ms.

**(b) Throughput (TPS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPS &= \frac{\text{Total Transactions}}{\text{Test Duration (s)}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1200}{10 \times 60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1200}{600} \\[6pt]
&= 2.0 \text{ TPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Convert 10 minutes to 600 seconds, then divide the 1,200 transactions by 600 seconds to obtain 2.0 Transactions Per Second.

**(c) SLA Compliance Check:**

- Measured $ART = 316.67$ ms.
- SLA Threshold $= 400$ ms.
- Since $316.67 < 400$, the system **COMPLIES** with the SLA. ✅

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Percentile Response Time

**Problem [KTU 2024 ESE Model]:**
The following 11 response time samples (in ms) are observed during a load run:
**120, 150, 180, 200, 220, 240, 260, 280, 300, 350, 500**.
Compute (a) $P_{90}$ (90th percentile) and (b) state its significance in load testing.

**Solution:**

The dataset is already sorted in ascending order. Total samples $N = 11$.

**(a) $P_{90}$ Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rank for } P_{90} &= \frac{90}{100} \times (N + 1) \\[6pt]
&= 0.9 \times 12 \\[6pt]
&= 10.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The standard KTU-friendly formula uses $\text{Rank} = P \times (N+1)$. A rank of 10.8 lies between the 10th value (350 ms) and the 11th value (500 ms).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{90} &= T_{10} + 0.8 \times (T_{11} - T_{10}) \\[6pt]
&= 350 + 0.8 \times (500 - 350) \\[6pt]
&= 350 + 0.8 \times 150 \\[6pt]
&= 350 + 120 \\[6pt]
&= 470 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Linear interpolation gives 80% weight to the gap between the 10th and 11th samples. The result 470 ms means **90% of users experienced ≤ 470 ms**.

**(b) Significance:**

- $P_{90}$ captures the **worst-case experience** of the majority.
- It is **less sensitive to outliers** than the maximum value (500 ms).
- KTU 2024 SLAs typically state: *"95% of transactions must complete within X seconds."*

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — Concurrent User Calculation

**Problem [KTU 2024 ESE Model]:**
An e-commerce portal expects **5,000 unique visits during peak hour (60 minutes)**. The **average session time** per visitor is **8 minutes**. Calculate the **Concurrent Users** to be simulated in the load test.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CU &= \frac{\text{Peak Hour Visits} \times \text{Avg Session Time (s)}}{\text{Test Window (s)}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5000 \times (8 \times 60)}{60 \times 60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5000 \times 480}{3600} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2{,}400{,}000}{3600} \\[6pt]
&= 666.67 \approx 667 \text{ concurrent users}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Convert both times to the **same unit (seconds)**. Multiply visits by average session duration, then divide by the observation window. This formula derives from Little's Law applied to web traffic.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Load Test Report Summary Table)

| Metric | Observed | SLA Target | Status | Likely Bottleneck |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Average Response Time | 316 ms | $\leq 400$ ms | ✅ Pass | — |
| Throughput | 2.0 TPS | $\geq 1.8$ TPS | ✅ Pass | — |
| Error Rate | 0.05% | $\leq 0.1\%$ | ✅ Pass | — |
| CPU Utilization | 85% | $\leq 70\%$ | ❌ Fail | Application logic / inefficient query |
| Memory Utilization | 60% | $\leq 75\%$ | ✅ Pass | — |
| $P_{90}$ Response Time | 470 ms | $\leq 500$ ms | ✅ Pass | — |

**Examiner's Interpretation:** Even though response times are good, the **CPU is overheating** (85% > 70%). The bottleneck is at the **application/database tier** — most likely due to unindexed queries, N+1 query patterns, or inefficient loops.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Load Testing. List any four key performance metrics measured during a load test.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Load testing is a type of non-functional performance testing that simulates real-world user load on a system to evaluate its behavior under expected peak conditions, focusing on response time, throughput, and resource utilization.

**Four Key Metrics (1.5 marks):**
1. **Average Response Time** — Time taken by the system to respond to a user request.
2. **Throughput (TPS)** — Number of transactions processed per second.
3. **Error Rate** — Percentage of failed transactions out of total.
4. **CPU/Memory Utilization** — Percentage of system resources consumed under load.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Differentiate between Load Testing and Stress Testing with a suitable example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Load Testing | Stress Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Load Level** | Expected production load (normal-to-peak). | Load **beyond** the expected peak capacity. |
| **Objective** | Verify SLAs are met. | Identify the breaking point of the system. |
| **Pass Criteria** | System meets defined SLAs. | System fails gracefully without data loss. |
| **Example** | Testing an e-commerce site with 5,000 concurrent users (expected Black Friday traffic). | Pushing the same site to 25,000 users to see when it crashes. |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]:**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five phases of Load Testing** with a neat block diagram. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A banking application is expected to handle **3,000 visits during peak hour (60 min)**. The average session time is **6 minutes**. The load test, run for 1 hour, records **1,800 transactions** with response times:
**180, 220, 260, 290, 310, 340, 370, 410 ms**.
Calculate:
(i) Concurrent users to be simulated. **[2 Marks]**
(ii) Throughput in TPS. **[2 Marks]**
(iii) Average response time and $P_{90}$ response time. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Phases of Load Testing (7 marks):**

The five phases are:

**Phase 1 — Test Environment Identification (1.4 marks):**
- Document hardware (CPU, RAM, disk, network bandwidth).
- Document software (OS, web/app server, DBMS versions).
- Document network topology and load generator locations.
- *Why:* Reproducibility — performance results are environment-dependent.

**Phase 2 — Workload Characterization (1.4 marks):**
- Identify critical business transactions (login, fund transfer, balance check).
- Establish user distribution (e.g., 40% check balance, 35% transfer, 25% statement).
- Set SLAs (e.g., $P_{90}$ response $\leq 2$ s; error rate $\leq 0.1\%$).

**Phase 3 — Test Scenario Design (1.4 marks):**
- Define ramp-up, peak, sustain, and ramp-down profiles.
- Configure think time (e.g., 5–10 s between actions).
- Parameterize test data to avoid caching bias.

**Phase 4 — Test Execution & Monitoring (1.4 marks):**
- Execute using tools (JMeter, LoadRunner, Gatling).
- Capture real-time metrics: response time, throughput, CPU, memory, disk I/O.
- Use distributed load generators to simulate geographically diverse users.

**Phase 5 — Analysis, Reporting & Tuning (1.4 marks):**
- Compare observed metrics against SLAs.
- Identify bottlenecks (DB queries, garbage collection, network saturation).
- Recommend fixes (indexing, caching, horizontal scaling) and re-test.

*(Block diagram already provided in Section 4.1 above. Examiner tip: Always redraw a smaller version in your answer sheet.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Concurrent Users (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CU &= \frac{3000 \times (6 \times 60)}{60 \times 60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3000 \times 360}{3600} \\[6pt]
&= 300 \text{ concurrent users}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Throughput in TPS (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPS &= \frac{1800}{60 \times 60} = \frac{1800}{3600} = 0.5 \text{ TPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Average Response Time and $P_{90}$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ART &= \frac{180 + 220 + 260 + 290 + 310 + 340 + 370 + 410}{8} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2380}{8} \\[6pt]
&= 297.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**$P_{90}$ Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rank} &= 0.90 \times (8 + 1) = 8.1
\end{aligned}
$$

The 8th sample is 410 ms, the 9th doesn't exist. So $P_{90} = 410$ ms (effectively the maximum of the dataset for this small sample).

---

**Question B (Internal Choice) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]:**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Virtual Users (VUsers)**, **Think Time**, **Pacing**, and **Ramp-up** in load test scenario design. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare **LoadRunner, JMeter, and Gatling** as load testing tools across at least five parameters. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of VUsers, Think Time, Pacing, and Ramp-up (7 marks):**

**1. Virtual Users (VUsers) (1.75 marks):**
- A VUser is a software thread that mimics a real user's actions.
- Each VUser executes the test script (login → navigate → action → logout).
- Concurrent VUsers = the peak load the system is expected to handle.
- *Why:* Real users cannot be coordinated; VUsers are deterministic and reproducible.

**2. Think Time (1.75 marks):**
- The pause a real user takes between successive actions (e.g., reading a page).
- Implemented as a delay function in the script (`thinkTime(5)` means 5 seconds).
- *Why:* Without think time, VUsers hammer the server unrealistically, producing **artificially pessimistic** results.

**3. Pacing (1.75 marks):**
- The interval between **iterations** of the same user scenario.
- If a script takes 30 s and pacing is 60 s, the VUser waits 30 s before starting the next iteration.
- *Why:* Controls the **arrival rate** of requests (transactions per second).

**4. Ramp-up (1.75 marks):**
- The gradual increase in VUser count over time (e.g., 0 → 500 users over 10 minutes).
- Prevents the system from being overwhelmed at $T=0$.
- *Why:* Real traffic does not arrive instantaneously; ramp-up makes the test **realistic**.

**(b) Tool Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | LoadRunner (Micro Focus) | JMeter (Apache) | Gatling (Scala-based) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **License** | Commercial (expensive). | Open-source, free. | Open-source, free. |
| **Scripting Language** | C-like (VU Generator) or Java. | Java/Groovy (GUI-based). | Scala DSL (code-driven). |
| **Protocol Support** | Wide (HTTP, SAP, Oracle, Citrix, MQTT, etc.). | Web, FTP, JDBC, SOAP, REST. | HTTP, WebSocket, SSE (web-focused). |
| **Reporting** | Advanced HTML dashboards with waterfall analysis. | Basic listeners; CLI mode for HTML reports. | Beautiful, auto-generated HTML reports. |
| **Scalability** | Very high (uses Load Generator farms). | High (distributed mode). | High (uses injector cloud mode). |
| **Learning Curve** | Steep (commercial training needed). | Moderate (large community). | Moderate (requires Scala familiarity). |
| **IDE Integration** | VuGen (proprietary). | Standalone GUI / Eclipse plugin. | Any IDE + Maven/Gradle. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing Load Test with Stress Test** in definitions → lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions** in numerical problems (minutes vs. seconds) → lose 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark questions → lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not stating the SLA threshold** before comparing metrics → lose 0.5 mark per instance.
> 5. **Writing $P_{90}$ without showing the rank calculation** → lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Using `|` in LaTeX inside markdown tables** → table breaks, examiner cannot read formula → lose full marks for that row. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Revision Bullet List — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ Load testing is a **non-functional, performance** testing type — it validates SLAs under expected load.
- ✅ **NOT** the same as stress testing (which pushes beyond limits).
- ✅ The **five phases** are: Environment → Workload → Scenario → Execution → Analysis.
- ✅ Key metrics: **ART, TPS, Error Rate, $P_{90}/P_{95}$, CPU%, Memory%, BW%.**
- ✅ **Little's Law** ($L = \lambda W$) underpins the **Concurrent User** formula.
- ✅ **Ramp-up** prevents unrealistic shock load; **Sustain phase** is where SLAs are read.
- ✅ **Think time** prevents the system from being hammered unrealistically.
- ✅ Tools: **JMeter** (open-source favorite for KTU labs), **LoadRunner** (industry gold standard), **Gatling** (modern, code-driven).
- ✅ For 14-mark answers, **always include a labeled block diagram** — examiners reward visual clarity.
- ✅ Numerical problems require **unit consistency** (convert all time to seconds before dividing).
- ✅ **$P_{90}$ is more meaningful than maximum** for SLAs because it ignores extreme outliers.
- ✅ A bottleneck is diagnosed by correlating **high response time** with **high resource utilization** on a specific tier (DB, app, or network).
- ✅ Always distinguish between **Hits per Second** (HTTP requests) and **Transactions per Second** (business-level actions).
- ✅ Memory leak detection belongs to **Endurance (Soak) Testing**, not pure load testing.
- ✅ **SLA breaches** must be reported with the **exact metric, observed value, threshold, and recommendation** — not just "system is slow."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stress Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress Testing** is a non-functional software testing technique that evaluates the **robustness, stability, and error-handling capability** of a system by subjecting it to conditions that exceed its normal operational capacity. The primary objective is to determine the **breaking point (system limit)** of the application and to observe how gracefully it recovers (or fails) when pushed beyond its designed thresholds.

According to the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board)** foundation syllabus, stress testing is a type of **performance testing** that focuses on testing a system at or beyond its **specified load limits** to observe its behavior under extreme conditions such as:
- Abnormally high traffic / concurrent users
- Insufficient memory / CPU resources
- Sudden surge in data volume
- Network latency injection
- Hardware failure simulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEITT862 - Module 2):** Stress Testing falls under the broader umbrella of **Performance Testing**, alongside Load Testing, Spike Testing, Soak Testing, and Volume Testing. Students must clearly distinguish between these sub-types as this is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are an automobile engineer designing a car's **brake system**. The car is officially rated to carry **5 passengers and 200 kg of luggage**. You, however, deliberately stuff **15 passengers and 600 kg of luggage** into the vehicle, slam the brakes at **120 km/h**, and observe:
- Do the brakes still function?
- Does the ABS kick in?
- How much distance does it take to stop?
- Does the brake pad overheat?
- Does the car catch fire or recover gracefully?

This deliberate **overloading beyond rated capacity** to find the **failure threshold** is precisely what **Stress Testing** does to a software system.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Summary:** Stress Testing = "Pushing software to its breaking point on purpose, to see what happens, so that customers never experience it in production."

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Expected Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Breaking Point** | The load level at which the system becomes unresponsive or fails |
| **Throughput** | Number of requests/transactions processed per unit time |
| **Response Time** | Time taken by the system to respond to a request |
| **Bottleneck** | The resource (CPU, RAM, DB, Network) that limits system performance |
| **Saturation** | State where a resource reaches 100% utilization |
| **Graceful Degradation** | System's ability to maintain partial functionality under stress instead of complete crash |
| **Recovery** | Time taken by the system to return to normal operation after stress is removed |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures under stress |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Recover after failure |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students confuse **Load Testing** with **Stress Testing**. Load testing measures performance under **expected/normal load**, while stress testing measures behavior under **extreme/abnormal load** beyond design limits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational "Why" Behind Stress Testing

Stress Testing is conducted for **four primary engineering objectives**:

1. **Capacity Planning Validation:** Verify that the system's documented capacity claims (e.g., "supports 10,000 concurrent users") are truthful and not marketing-driven exaggerations.
2. **Failure Mode Identification:** Discover the **first point of failure** so that engineering teams can add circuit breakers, fallbacks, or auto-scaling before users experience downtime.
3. **Recovery Mechanism Verification:** Ensure that when the system fails, it **fails safely** (e.g., shows a friendly error page rather than exposing database credentials).
4. **Hardware Infrastructure Tuning:** Identify whether the deployed infrastructure (RAM, CPU cores, DB connections, thread pools) is **right-sized** for production traffic spikes such as Black Friday sales, IPL match starts, or viral social media events.

### 2.2 Structured Step-by-Step Stress Testing Process

The **IEEE 829-2008 / ISO 25010** aligned process for executing a stress test consists of the following phases:

- **Step 1 - Test Planning & Goal Definition:** Define success criteria, e.g., "System must handle 150% of peak load without data loss."
- **Step 2 - Environment Setup:** Provision a staging environment that **mirrors production** (same OS, same DB version, same network topology).
- **Step 3 - Baseline Test Execution:** Run a normal load test first to capture **baseline metrics** (response time, CPU%, memory%).
- **Step 4 - Stress Scenario Design:** Design scenarios such as ramp-up surges, sudden spikes, sustained overload, and resource starvation.
- **Step 5 - Test Execution:** Inject the stress using automated tools (JMeter, Gatling, Locust, k6).
- **Step 6 - Monitoring & Data Collection:** Capture real-time metrics using APM tools (New Relic, Dynatrace, Prometheus + Grafana).
- **Step 7 - Failure Analysis & Bottleneck Identification:** Analyze logs, thread dumps, heap dumps to find the **root cause** of failures.
- **Step 8 - Reporting & Recommendations:** Document findings, suggested hardware upgrades, and code-level optimizations.

### 2.3 Types of Stress Testing (High-Yield for KTU)

| Type | Description | Real-World Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Stress** | Stresses the application layer (code, APIs, middleware) | Bug in transaction handling logic |
| **Transactional Stress** | Stresses transactional paths (DB commit/rollback) | Banking systems during year-end closing |
| **Systemic Stress** | Stresses multiple systems simultaneously | Microservices ecosystem under coordinated load |
| **Exploratory Stress** | Random, unpredictable stress to discover unknown bugs | Hacker-style probing of edge cases |
| **Spike Testing** | Sudden, sharp increase in load over a very short duration | Flash sale starting at 12:00:00 AM sharp |
| **Soak / Endurance Testing** | Sustained moderate-high load over extended time (hours/days) | Memory leak detection over a 72-hour window |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> The following mathematical formulas are **board-expected** for KTU PEITT862 calculations. Memorize the units carefully.

| # | Metric Name | Formula | Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Number of Requests}}{\text{Total Time (sec)}}$ | req/sec | Maximum data processing rate |
| 2 | **Average Response Time** | $\text{ART} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}{n}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Mean user-perceived latency |
| 3 | **Percentile Response Time (P95)** | $P_{95} = \text{Value below which } 95\% \text{ of requests fall}$ | ms | Tail latency (worst-case experience) |
| 4 | **Error Rate** | $\text{Error \%} = \frac{\text{Failed Requests}}{\text{Total Requests}} \times 100$ | % | Reliability indicator |
| 5 | **Concurrent Users** | $C = \frac{\text{Total Sessions}}{\text{Average Session Duration (sec)}}$ | users | Peak simultaneous load |
| 6 | **CPU Saturation** | $\text{CPU\%} = \frac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100$ | % | Hardware utilization ceiling |
| 7 | **Memory Leak Rate** | $\text{Leak Rate} = \frac{M_{\text{final}} - M_{\text{initial}}}{\text{Test Duration (hours)}}$ | MB/hr | Identified via soak testing |
| 8 | **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Reliability under stress |
| 9 | **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Incidents}}$ | minutes | Recovery efficiency |
| 10 | **Performance Efficiency Ratio** | $\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Work Output}}{\text{Resource Input}}$ | dimensionless | ISTQB ISO 25010 metric |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce (Flipkart Big Billion Days):** Used to simulate 10x normal traffic the day before sale.
- **Banking (NEFT/RTGS Batch Windows):** Validates that 50,000 transactions per minute don't corrupt the ledger.
- **Streaming (Netflix, Hotstar IPL):** Ensures 50 million concurrent video streams don't collapse the CDN.
- **Avionics / Automotive (ISO 26262):** Mandatory stress testing of ECUs under temperature, vibration, voltage extremes.
- **Healthcare (FDA-Certified Devices):** Validates that infusion pumps deliver correct dosage under battery-low conditions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Example: Calculating Stress Test KPIs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):** A web application is subjected to a stress test. The following data was collected over a 10-minute (600-second) window:
> - Total requests sent: **180,000**
> - Total successful responses: **162,000**
> - Total failed responses: **18,000**
> - Sum of all response times: **4,500,000 ms**
> - Test started with memory at **2,000 MB** and ended at **3,500 MB** after 10 hours of equivalent soak.

**Required:** Calculate (a) Throughput, (b) Average Response Time, (c) Error Rate, (d) Memory Leak Rate.

---

#### **Step 1: Calculate Throughput (Requests per Second)**

The number of requests completed per second is the **most fundamental** performance metric. We divide the total number of requests by the total observation window in seconds.

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Number of Requests}}{\text{Total Time (sec)}}
$$

Substituting the given values into the formula:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{180{,}000 \text{ requests}}{600 \text{ seconds}}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = 300 \text{ requests/second}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The system processed **300 requests per second** under stress before failures began.

---

#### **Step 2: Calculate Average Response Time (ART)**

The Average Response Time is the arithmetic mean of all individual request latencies, which represents the typical user-perceived speed.

$$
\text{ART} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}{n}
$$

Substituting the given values into the formula:

$$
\text{ART} = \frac{4{,}500{,}000 \text{ ms}}{180{,}000 \text{ requests}}
$$

$$
\text{ART} = 25 \text{ ms/request}
$$

> **Interpretation:** On average, each request took **25 milliseconds** to complete. This is excellent (industry standard: <200 ms).

---

#### **Step 3: Calculate Error Rate (in Percentage)**

The Error Rate indicates the **reliability** of the system under stress. It is the ratio of failed requests to total requests, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\text{Error \%} = \frac{\text{Failed Requests}}{\text{Total Requests}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the given values into the formula:

$$
\text{Error \%} = \frac{18{,}000}{180{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Error \%} = 0.10 \times 100 = 10\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** **10% of all requests failed** under stress. The KTU industry standard acceptable threshold is **< 1%** for production-grade systems. This system has a **reliability problem**.

---

#### **Step 4: Calculate Memory Leak Rate (MB per hour)**

The Memory Leak Rate quantifies how much memory is being unreleased per hour, which is critical for **endurance / soak testing** scenarios.

$$
\text{Leak Rate} = \frac{M_{\text{final}} - M_{\text{initial}}}{\text{Test Duration (hours)}}
$$

Substituting the given values into the formula:

$$
\text{Leak Rate} = \frac{3{,}500 \text{ MB} - 2{,}000 \text{ MB}}{10 \text{ hours}}
$$

$$
\text{Leak Rate} = \frac{1{,}500 \text{ MB}}{10 \text{ hours}} = 150 \text{ MB/hour}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The system is leaking **150 MB of memory every hour**. Over a 24-hour production day, this would consume **3,600 MB (3.6 GB)** of RAM, eventually triggering an **OutOfMemoryError crash** in production.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix - Load vs Stress vs Spike vs Soak Testing

This is the **most important comparison table** for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Examiners frequently test students on this distinction.

| Parameter | Load Testing | Stress Testing | Spike Testing | Soak Testing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Load Level** | Expected / normal peak | Beyond designed capacity | Sudden extreme burst | Sustained normal-to-peak |
| **Duration** | Short (minutes-hours) | Short-to-medium (hours) | Very short (seconds-minutes) | Long (hours-days) |
| **Primary Goal** | Validate SLA under expected load | Find breaking point | Test elasticity & auto-scaling | Detect memory leaks / degradation |
| **Failure Mode Tested** | Performance bottlenecks | Crashes, data corruption, downtime | Sudden scalability limits | Slow degradation over time |
| **Typical Use Case** | Quarterly capacity validation | Pre-Black-Friday readiness | Flash sale, breaking news event | 72-hour pre-release soak test |
| **Pass Criteria** | Response time < SLA, error < 0.1% | System fails gracefully, no data loss | Auto-scaling kicks in < 2 min | No memory growth, no log saturation |
| **Tools** | JMeter, LoadRunner | JMeter, Gatling, Locust | k6, Gatling, Locust | JMeter, CloudTest, Flood.io |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Calculating P95 Latency from a Sample Dataset

> [!NOTE]
> **P95 (95th Percentile)** is a critical SRE metric because the **average response time hides the suffering of 5% of users**. KTU may ask students to compute P95 from an ordered dataset.

**Sample ordered response times (ms):** 10, 12, 15, 18, 20, 22, 25, 30, 40, 200

**Step 1:** Sort the dataset in ascending order (already done).

**Step 2:** Use the percentile rank formula:

$$
P_k = \frac{k}{100} \times (n + 1)
$$

Where $k$ is the desired percentile and $n$ is the sample size.

Substituting $k = 95$ and $n = 10$:

$$
P_{95} = \frac{95}{100} \times (10 + 1) = 0.95 \times 11 = 10.45
$$

**Step 3:** Since the rank 10.45 falls between the 10th and 11th values (but we only have 10 values), we use the **nearest-rank method**:

$$
P_{95} = \text{Value at rank} \lceil 0.95 \times 10 \rceil = \lceil 9.5 \rceil = 10
$$

**Step 4:** Read the 10th value from the sorted dataset:

$$
P_{95} = 200 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Critical Insight:** The **average** is 39.2 ms (looks healthy), but the **P95 is 200 ms**, which means 5% of users are experiencing a 5x slower experience. This is a **classic stress-test finding** that average-only monitoring would completely miss.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stress Testing Lifecycle (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start Stress Test Initiative]) --> nodeB[Define Stress Goals and SLAs]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Identify Critical Workloads and Scenarios]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Provision Production-Like Staging Environment]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Capture Baseline Performance Metrics]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Design Stress Scenarios: Ramp, Spike, Soak]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Configure Load Injector Tool: JMeter or Gatling]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Execute Stress Test with Real-Time Monitoring]
    nodeH --> nodeI{System Crashed?}
    nodeI -- No --> nodeJ[Increase Load by 10 Percent]
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeK[Capture Failure Logs and Thread Dumps]
    nodeJ --> nodeH
    nodeK --> nodeL[Identify Bottleneck: CPU, RAM, DB, or Code]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Report Findings to DevOps Team]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Apply Fixes and Retest]
    nodeN --> nodeO{All SLAs Met?}
    nodeO -- No --> nodeF
    nodeO -- Yes --> nodeP([Stress Test Passed - Ready for Production])
```

### 4.2 Stress Test Architecture: Load Injector to Target System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph loadGen["LOAD GENERATOR CLUSTER"]
        lg1[Injector Node 01]
        lg2[Injector Node 02]
        lg3[Injector Node 03]
    end
    subgraph target["SYSTEM UNDER TEST SUT"]
        lb[Load Balancer]
        app1[App Server 01]
        app2[App Server 02]
        db[(Primary Database)]
        cache[(Redis Cache)]
    end
    subgraph monitor["MONITORING STACK"]
        apm[APM: New Relic or Dynatrace]
        prom[Prometheus Metrics]
        graf[Grafana Dashboards]
        log[Centralized Log Aggregator]
    end
    lg1 --> lb
    lg2 --> lb
    lg3 --> lb
    lb --> app1
    lb --> app2
    app1 --> db
    app2 --> db
    app1 --> cache
    app2 --> cache
    app1 --> apm
    app2 --> apm
    db --> prom
    cache --> prom
    prom --> graf
    app1 --> log
    app2 --> log
    db --> log
```

### 4.3 Stress vs Load vs Spike vs Soak Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Performance Testing Family] --> load[Load Testing]
    root --> stress[Stress Testing]
    root --> spike[Spike Testing]
    root --> soak[Soak Testing]
    load --> loadDesc[Measures performance under EXPECTED peak load]
    stress --> stressDesc[Measures behavior BEYOND designed capacity]
    spike --> spikeDesc[Measures response to SUDDEN extreme burst]
    soak --> soakDesc[Measures degradation over EXTENDED duration]
    stressDesc --> failCheck{Find Breaking Point}
    failCheck --> yes[Yes: Crashes or Degrades]
    failCheck --> no[No: System is Over-Engineered]
```

### 4.4 Stress Test Failure Recovery Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Normal Operation] --> nodeB[Load Increases Beyond Threshold]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Resource Saturation: CPU at 100 Percent]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Circuit Breaker Triggered?}
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeE[Serve Cached or Default Response]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeF[System Crash or Hang]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Auto-Scale New Instances]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Load Distributes Across Pool]
    nodeH --> nodeA
    nodeF --> nodeI[Alert On-Call Engineer]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Manual or Automated Restart]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Investigate Root Cause]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Patch and Redeploy]
    nodeL --> nodeA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define Stress Testing. List any four objectives of conducting stress testing on a software system.

**Model Answer (Expected 3-Mark Structure):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Stress Testing is a type of non-functional performance testing that evaluates a system's behavior under **extreme load conditions** that exceed its normal operational capacity. The goal is to identify the **breaking point** of the application and verify **graceful failure handling**.

**Objectives (1.5 marks - any four of the following):**
1. To determine the **maximum capacity** (breaking point) of the system beyond which it fails.
2. To verify the system's ability to **recover gracefully** (graceful degradation) after a crash.
3. To identify **bottlenecks** such as memory leaks, CPU saturation, and database connection pool exhaustion.
4. To validate the **auto-scaling** and **load-balancing** mechanisms under sudden traffic spikes.
5. To ensure **data integrity** is preserved even when the system fails under stress.
6. To confirm that **failover and redundancy** mechanisms (e.g., secondary DB) activate correctly.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate between **Load Testing** and **Stress Testing** with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (Expected 3-Mark Structure):**

| Parameter | Load Testing | Stress Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Measures system performance under **expected** peak load | Measures system behavior under **beyond-limit** load |
| **Load Magnitude** | Normal / designed capacity (e.g., 1000 users) | Beyond capacity (e.g., 5000 users) |
| **Objective** | Validate SLA compliance | Find breaking point and failure modes |
| **Example** | Testing IRCTC website when **10 lakh users** log in normally | Testing IRCTC website when **50 lakh users** log in simultaneously during Tatkal opening |
| **Outcome Expected** | Stable response times | Crash, recovery, graceful degradation observed |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Load testing validates **anticipated** performance, whereas stress testing prepares the system for **unanticipated** extreme scenarios.

---

### Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Explain the **complete stress testing methodology** with a neat block diagram. Discuss in detail the different **types of stress testing** with real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Part 1: Stress Testing Methodology (3.5 marks)**

The stress testing methodology follows a systematic **eight-phase approach**:

**Phase 1 - Test Planning:** Define stress goals such as *"System must handle 150% of peak load for 30 minutes without data loss."* Identify the critical business transactions to be stress-tested.

**Phase 2 - Test Data Preparation:** Prepare realistic test data volumes. For example, a database with **10 million customer records** to simulate production-like queries.

**Phase 3 - Environment Setup:** Provision a **staging environment that mirrors production** in terms of hardware specs, OS version, database version, and network topology. Containerization via **Docker + Kubernetes** is recommended for reproducibility.

**Phase 4 - Baseline Test:** Execute a normal load test first to record **baseline metrics** including average response time, P95 latency, CPU%, and memory utilization.

**Phase 5 - Stress Scenario Design:** Design **multiple stress scenarios** as follows:
- **Ramp-up stress:** Gradually increase load from 100% to 300% over 1 hour.
- **Spike stress:** Suddenly jump from 100% to 500% within 10 seconds.
- **Sustained overload:** Maintain 200% load for 4 hours continuously.
- **Resource starvation:** Limit CPU cores from 8 to 2 during the test.

**Phase 6 - Test Execution:** Execute scenarios using **JMeter / Gatling / k6** with real-time monitoring via **Prometheus + Grafana** and APM tools like **New Relic**.

**Phase 7 - Monitoring & Analysis:** Capture application logs, **thread dumps, heap dumps, GC logs**, and database slow query logs. Identify the **first resource to saturate** (the bottleneck).

**Phase 8 - Reporting & Recommendations:** Produce a detailed report with:
- Maximum load sustained
- First point of failure
- Error rate vs load curve
- Recommended infrastructure upgrades
- Code-level optimization suggestions (e.g., add caching, optimize SQL queries, increase thread pool size)

**Part 2: Types of Stress Testing (3.5 marks)**

| Type | Description | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Stress** | Tests the application code layer for robustness | Online shopping cart handling 100x concurrent checkouts during Diwali sale |
| **Transactional Stress** | Tests database transaction integrity | NEFT banking system processing 5 lakh transactions/minute on March 31 year-end |
| **Systemic Stress** | Tests interconnected systems and APIs | A travel booking site where flight, hotel, and payment APIs fail simultaneously |
| **Exploratory Stress** | Tests random, unexpected scenarios | Chaos Engineering with Netflix's **Chaos Monkey** randomly killing production servers |
| **Spike Testing** | Tests sudden traffic surge | Hotstar website when an IPL match goes into Super Over |
| **Soak / Endurance Testing** | Tests long-duration sustained load | A 72-hour continuous test revealing a 5MB/hour memory leak in a chat application |

---

> **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Compare **Load Testing, Stress Testing, Spike Testing, and Soak Testing** in detail. For each, mention the **objective, load profile, duration, and a real-world scenario**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

These four techniques are sub-types of **Performance Testing** and are frequently tested comparatively in KTU examinations.

**1. Load Testing (1.75 marks):**
- **Objective:** Validate that the system meets its **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** under expected peak load.
- **Load Profile:** Gradual ramp-up to the **expected maximum** (e.g., 5,000 concurrent users for a B2B portal).
- **Duration:** Typically 30 minutes to 2 hours.
- **Real-World Example:** Pre-launch validation of a college ERP system expected to handle 10,000 students logging in during exam results day.

**2. Stress Testing (1.75 marks):**
- **Objective:** Determine the **breaking point** and observe **failure handling** when load exceeds design capacity.
