- **Load Profile:** Push load to **150%–300%** of designed maximum, often with sudden spikes.
- **Duration:** 1–4 hours.
- **Real-World Example:** Testing IRCTC's tatkal booking server at **10x normal load** (50 lakh concurrent users) to find when the queueing system collapses.

**3. Spike Testing (1.75 marks):**
- **Objective:** Validate **auto-scaling elasticity** when traffic suddenly bursts and then drops.
- **Load Profile:** Abrupt jump from baseline to extreme (e.g., 1,000 → 50,000 users in 30 seconds), then back to baseline.
- **Duration:** Short, repeated bursts over 1–2 hours.
- **Real-World Example:** Flipkart's Big Billion Days flash sale that opens at 12:00 PM sharp with millions of users clicking simultaneously.

**4. Soak / Endurance Testing (1.75 marks):**
- **Objective:** Detect **memory leaks, log file growth, database connection leaks, and slow performance degradation** over extended periods.
- **Load Profile:** Sustained **moderate-to-high** load (e.g., 70% of peak capacity).
- **Duration:** **Long** - typically 8 hours to 7 days.
- **Real-World Example:** A 72-hour continuous test of a video conferencing tool (like Zoom) to detect memory leaks that would crash the app after a 4-hour meeting in production.

**Conclusion:** Each performance testing type serves a unique purpose. A mature engineering organization conducts **all four** before major production releases.

---

> **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** A banking application is subjected to stress testing. The following data was observed: Total users simulated = 5,000; Number of failed login attempts = 250; Total successful transactions = 8,000; Sum of all transaction response times = 2,400,000 ms; System crashed at 4,200 concurrent users. Calculate: (i) Error Rate, (ii) Average Transaction Response Time, (iii) Throughput, (iv) Breaking Point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Total users simulated = 5,000
- Failed login attempts = 250
- Successful transactions = 8,000
- Sum of response times = 2,400,000 ms
- Breaking point = 4,200 concurrent users

**(i) Error Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Error \%} = \frac{\text{Failed Requests}}{\text{Total Requests}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Error \%} = \frac{250}{5{,}000} \times 100 = 5\%
$$

> The system has a **5% failure rate**, which exceeds the banking industry standard of **< 0.5%**. This is a **critical reliability defect**.

**(ii) Average Transaction Response Time (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ART} = \frac{\sum T_i}{n} = \frac{2{,}400{,}000 \text{ ms}}{8{,}000 \text{ transactions}} = 300 \text{ ms/transaction}
$$

> Each transaction takes **300 milliseconds**. For a banking app, the acceptable limit is **< 1000 ms**, so this is within SLA.

**(iii) Throughput (1.5 marks):**

Assuming the test ran for 1 hour (3,600 seconds):

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{8{,}000 \text{ transactions}}{3{,}600 \text{ sec}} \approx 2.22 \text{ transactions/sec}
$$

> The system processes approximately **2.22 transactions per second** under stress.

**(iv) Breaking Point (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Breaking Point} = 4{,}200 \text{ concurrent users}
$$

> The system crashes when concurrent users exceed **4,200**. Recommendation: **horizontal scaling** (add 2 more app servers) to raise the breaking point to **12,000+ users**.

---

> **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Discuss the role of **automation tools** in stress testing. Compare **JMeter, Gatling, and Locust** as stress testing tools, listing their key features, programming language support, and ideal use cases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Automation in Stress Testing (2 marks):**

Manual stress testing is **practically impossible** because:
- Simulating 10,000+ concurrent users requires distributed load generation.
- Real-time metric collection, thread dump capture, and log aggregation demand automation.
- Repeatability for **regression stress testing** in CI/CD pipelines requires scripted execution.

Automation tools provide **scripted load generation, distributed testing, real-time reporting, and CI/CD integration** with Jenkins, GitHub Actions, and GitLab CI.

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | **Apache JMeter** | **Gatling** | **Locust** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Language** | Java (GUI-based) | Scala (code-based DSL) | Python (code-based) |
| **Protocol Support** | HTTP, FTP, JDBC, SOAP, REST, LDAP | HTTP, WebSocket, Server-Sent Events | HTTP, custom protocols via Python |
| **Scripting Style** | XML-based test plans (or JSR223 Groovy) | Scala code (Gatling DSL) | Pure Python classes |
| **UI Mode** | Yes (rich GUI) | No (code-only, with optional Recorder) | No (code-only, with Web UI) |
| **Distributed Testing** | Yes (master-slave) | Yes (Gatling Frontline - commercial) | Yes (master-worker, native) |
| **Reporting** | HTML dashboard, JMeter Plugins | Excellent auto-generated HTML reports | Basic Web UI, requires extension |
| **CI/CD Integration** | Strong (Maven, Gradle, Jenkins) | Strong (Maven, sbt, Jenkins) | Strong (Python ecosystem, Jenkins) |
| **Learning Curve** | Medium (GUI helps beginners) | High (requires Scala knowledge) | Low (Python is widely known) |
| **Ideal Use Case** | Enterprise teams needing protocol variety and UI | High-performance, code-driven CI/CD pipelines | Python shops, developer-friendly teams |
| **License** | Open-source (Apache 2.0) | Open-source core (Apache 2.0) | Open-source (MIT) |

**Conclusion:** Choose **JMeter** for protocol diversity and beginners; **Gatling** for developer-led, code-driven high-scale testing; **Locust** for Python-centric teams needing simplicity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Load and Stress Testing:** Many students write "stress testing is testing with more users" without specifying "**beyond** designed capacity" — this loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Skipping the "Why":** When listing objectives, do not just write bullet points. Add **one-line justification** for each, e.g., "Identify bottlenecks — to right-size infrastructure."
> 3. **Forgetting Real-World Examples:** KTU examiners award **bonus marks** (0.5–1 mark) for citing relatable Indian examples (IRCTC, Flipkart, Hotstar IPL).
> 4. **Numerical Errors:** Always show **formula → substitution → answer** in three distinct steps. Skipping the substitution step loses 1 mark.
> 5. **No Block Diagram in Methodology:** Methodology questions **must** include a labeled diagram (mermaid/hand-drawn). Missing it costs 1.5 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Stress Testing = Pushing software BEYOND its design limits to find the breaking point.**
- **Goal:** Find failure, observe graceful degradation, validate recovery, identify bottlenecks.
- **Types:** Application, Transactional, Systemic, Exploratory, Spike, Soak/Endurance.
- **Key Distinction:** Load = expected load; Stress = beyond-limit load; Spike = sudden burst; Soak = long duration.
- **Critical Metrics:** Throughput (req/sec), ART (ms), P95 (ms), Error Rate (%), CPU%, Memory Leak Rate (MB/hr).
- **MTBF & MTTR** quantify reliability and recovery efficiency respectively.
- **Process:** Plan → Setup → Baseline → Design → Execute → Monitor → Analyze → Report.
- **Tools:** JMeter (Java, GUI-rich), Gatling (Scala, code-driven), Locust (Python, developer-friendly), k6 (Go, modern).
- **ISTQB Classification:** Stress Testing is a **sub-type of Performance Testing**, which is a **sub-type of Non-Functional Testing**.
- **Memory Leaks** are detected only via **Soak/Endurance Testing**, not regular stress tests.
- **P95 latency** is more meaningful than **average latency** for user experience evaluation.
- **Chaos Engineering** (Netflix's Chaos Monkey) is the **modern extreme** of stress testing in production.
- **Real-World Triggers:** IRCTC Tatkal, Flipkart Big Billion Days, Hotstar IPL, Banking NEFT batch, NEET result declaration.
- **Acceptable Thresholds (Industry Standard):** Error Rate < 1%, ART < 200 ms, P95 < 500 ms, CPU < 80%, Memory stable.
- **Always include** baseline metrics, environment parity with production, and bottleneck analysis in your stress test report.
- **For KTU Numericals:** Memorize the 10 formulas in the Cheat Sheet table — they cover 95% of calculation questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Usability Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Usability Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Usability Testing** is a *black-box, user-centric* evaluation technique used in software engineering to measure how *easy, efficient, and satisfying* a software product is to use for its intended end users under controlled test conditions. It is formally defined by the **ISO 9241-11** international standard as *"the extent to which a product can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction in a specified context of use."*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, Usability Testing sits under **Non-Functional Testing** (a verification & validation activity) and is a sub-category of **User Experience (UX) Testing**. It focuses on the *human-computer interaction (HCI)* layer rather than the code logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Usability Testing is examined as a structured process — from **test planning → participant selection → task design → execution → analysis & reporting**. Examiners expect the *test process* to be explained, not just the definition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Usability Testing like a "Supermarket Checkout Test"** 🛒

Imagine a supermarket redesigns its checkout counter layout. Before rolling it out to 500 branches, they ask 20 regular shoppers to actually try buying groceries at the new counter while observers watch. They time how long it takes (efficiency), count how many mistakes occur (effectiveness), and ask shoppers to rate the experience (satisfaction). The feedback is then used to fix confusing button placements or unclear signs.

**Usability Testing works identically for software:**
- **Shoppers** = Real or representative *users* (test participants)
- **Buying groceries** = Realistic *test tasks/scenarios*
- **Timing the purchase** = Measuring *efficiency* (time-on-task)
- **Counting mistakes** = Measuring *effectiveness* (error rate, task completion)
- **Asking for feedback** = Measuring *satisfaction* (via SUS, questionnaires, interviews)

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Effectiveness** | Accuracy and completeness with which users achieve goals | Core metric (ISO 9241-11) |
| **Efficiency** | Resources expended in relation to effectiveness | Core metric (ISO 9241-11) |
| **Satisfaction** | Freedom from discomfort, positive attitudes | Measured via SUS, QUIS |
| **Heuristic Evaluation** | Expert-based review against usability principles | Nielsen's 10 Heuristics |
| **Think-Aloud Protocol** | User narrates thoughts while performing tasks | Data collection method |
| **SUS Score** | System Usability Scale — industry-standard questionnaire | Industry metric |
| **Test Participant** | Representative end-user recruited for the test | Sampling concern |

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **5 ± 2** — Number of evaluators typically required for Nielsen's Heuristic Evaluation (Nielsen's recommendation).
- **68%** — Acceptance threshold; SUS scores above **68** are considered above average.
- **80.3%** — Industry benchmark mean SUS score.
- **ISO 9241-11** — The international standard governing usability definition.
- **3 to 5** — Typical number of test participants needed to uncover ~85% of usability issues in a single user group (Nielsen's "Diminishing Returns" curve).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Goals & Objectives of Usability Testing

The principal goals, structured for KTU-style answers:

1. **Identify usability problems** in the user interface (UI) before release.
2. **Gather quantitative metrics** — task completion rate, time-on-task, error counts.
3. **Gather qualitative data** — user preferences, frustrations, confusions.
4. **Establish a usability baseline** for regression comparison across releases.
5. **Validate design decisions** against user expectations and task workflows.
6. **Ensure regulatory/compliance accessibility** (e.g., WCAG 2.1 alignment in healthcare/aviation software).
7. **Improve user satisfaction (UX)** and reduce training/support costs post-deployment.

## 2.2 Types / Categories of Usability Testing

| # | Type | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Exploratory / Formative** | Loosely structured; user explores freely to find issues | Early design phase (wireframes, prototypes) |
| 2 | **Assessment / Summative** | Structured; metrics-based evaluation against goals | Late design / pre-release |
| 3 | **Comparative** | Two or more designs compared side-by-side (A/B) | Selecting between alternatives |
| 4 | **Hallway / Guerrilla** | Quick tests with opportunistic users (e.g., in cafés) | Rapid feedback loops |
| 5 | **Remote Usability Testing** | Users tested via screen-share, video, or unmoderated tools | Distributed user base |
| 6 | **Heuristic Evaluation** | Expert inspectors review UI against Nielsen's 10 heuristics | When users are unavailable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Heuristic Evaluation is *not* user testing** — it is an *expert-based inspection* method. KTU questions sometimes mix these up; remember the distinction.

## 2.3 The Usability Test Process (Step-by-Step)

The KTU-recommended process (often asked as a 7-mark question):

### Step 1: **Plan the Test**
- Define **test objectives** (what to measure).
- Identify **target user profile** (demographics, experience level, accessibility needs).
- Select the **usability test method** (moderated, unmoderated, remote, in-person).
- Prepare the **test plan document** (objectives, scope, schedule, deliverables).

### Step 2: **Recruit Test Participants**
- Determine **sample size** — typically 5–10 users per user group.
- Use **screening questionnaires** to filter representative users.
- Offer **incentives** (gift cards, discounts) to encourage participation.

### Step 3: **Design Test Tasks / Scenarios**
- Tasks must be **realistic, actionable, and goal-oriented**.
- Example: *"You want to send a ₹2,000 payment to your friend Ravi. Show how you would do it."*
- Avoid leading the user; tasks should not hint at UI features.

### Step 4: **Prepare the Test Environment**
- Set up **lab equipment**: computer, screen recorder, audio recorder, eye-tracker (if used).
- Configure **logging software** (e.g., Morae, Lookback, UserTesting).
- Pilot test with a colleague to refine scripts.

### Step 5: **Conduct the Test Sessions**
- Welcome the participant, obtain **informed consent**.
- Brief them: *"Think aloud — say what you are looking at, thinking, doing."*
- Observe without intervening (unless the user is stuck for too long).
- Record **observations, timings, errors, and verbatim quotes**.

### Step 6: **Analyze Data & Identify Issues**
- Tabulate **quantitative data**: completion rates, time-on-task, error counts.
- Code **qualitative data** for themes (confusion points, delight moments).
- Assign **severity ratings** to each issue (Critical, Major, Minor).

### Step 7: **Report Findings & Recommend Fixes**
- Produce a **Usability Test Report** with executive summary, methodology, findings, severity, and recommendations.
- Prioritize fixes using a **Severity × Frequency** matrix.

## 2.4 Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these 10 heuristics — they are a frequent short-answer question (3 marks).**

1. **Visibility of system status** — Keep users informed (loading bars, confirmations).
2. **Match between system and the real world** — Speak the user's language.
3. **User control and freedom** — Provide undo/redo, cancel, exit.
4. **Consistency and standards** — Same words/situations mean the same thing.
5. **Error prevention** — Prevent problems before they occur.
6. **Recognition rather than recall** — Minimize memory load; show options.
7. **Flexibility and efficiency of use** — Accelerators for expert users.
8. **Aesthetic and minimalist design** — No irrelevant information.
9. **Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors** — Plain-language error messages.
10. **Help and documentation** — Easy-to-search, task-focused help.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **No traditional math formulas apply here**, but the following metric equations are essential for KTU 14-mark derivations.

| Metric | Formula | Units / Threshold | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** | $\text{TCR} = \frac{\text{Tasks Completed Successfully}}{\text{Total Tasks Attempted}} \times 100$ | **%** (Higher is better; target $\geq 80\%$) | Measures *Effectiveness* |
| **Time-on-Task (ToT)** | $\text{ToT} = t_{\text{end}} - t_{\text{start}}$ | **Seconds / Minutes** (Lower is better) | Measures *Efficiency* |
| **Errors per Task** | $\text{Errors} = \frac{\sum \text{Deviations from Optimal Path}}{\text{Total Tasks}}$ | **Count** (Lower is better) | Direct effectiveness measure |
| **SUS Score (System Usability Scale)** | $\text{SUS} = \sum (\text{Odd items} - 1) + \sum (5 - \text{Even items})$ then $\times 2.5$ | **0–100 scale** (Above **68** = Acceptable) | Industry-standard satisfaction measure |
| **Effectiveness Ratio (E)** | $E = \frac{\text{Goals Achieved}}{\text{Goals Attempted}}$ | **0 to 1** (1 = perfect) | ISO 9241-11 metric |
| **Efficiency Ratio (Ef)** | $E_f = \frac{E}{\text{Time Spent}}$ | **Goals per second** | ISO 9241-11 metric |
| **Sample Size (Nielsen)** | $N \approx 5 \pm 2$ per user group | **Number of users** | Uncovers ~85% of issues |
| **Issue Discovery Rate** | $D(n) = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | **Probability** | Cumulative discovery formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipes replaced with `\vert` or description text** in all table cells to comply with markdown safety rules.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics (DO-178C):** Pre-deployment usability testing reduces pilot workload errors.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62366-1):** Summative usability testing is *legally mandated* before FDA/CE clearance.
- **Banking & FinTech:** A/B comparative usability tests reduce cart-abandonment and call-center load.
- **E-Governance (India):** Usability testing of UMANG, DigiLocker portals improves citizen adoption.
- **Automotive (ISO 15005):** In-vehicle infotainment systems undergo usability testing to prevent driver distraction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sample Size Argument (Nielsen's "Why 5 Users?")

This derivation is a classic KTU 14-mark analytical question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Problem:** How do we mathematically justify that only 5 users are needed to find most usability problems?

### Step 1 — Define the Discovery Probability

Let $p$ be the probability that a *single* randomly chosen user discovers a *specific* usability problem during a test session.

$$
0 < p \leq 1
$$

For a *new* user, the probability of **not** finding the problem is:

$$
P(\text{miss}) = 1 - p
$$

### Step 2 — Extend to n Users

If we test $n$ independent users, and each has the same probability $p$ of finding a given problem, then the probability that **all** $n$ users miss the problem is:

$$
P(\text{all miss}) = (1 - p)^n
$$

Therefore, the probability that **at least one** of the $n$ users discovers the problem (the cumulative discovery function) is:

$$
D(n) = 1 - (1 - p)^n
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Empirical Discovery Rate

Nielsen (1993) empirically observed that the *average* usability problem has a discovery rate of approximately:

$$
p \approx 0.31 \quad \text{(i.e., 31% of users encounter any given issue)}
$$

This is the empirical mean of the binomial distribution fitted to Nielsen's experimental data.

### Step 4 — Evaluate D(n) at Different n

Substitute $p = 0.31$ into the formula:

$$
D(n) = 1 - (1 - 0.31)^n = 1 - 0.69^n
$$

Calculate the discovery probability for different sample sizes:

$$
D(1) = 1 - 0.69^1 = 1 - 0.69 = 0.31 \;\; (\text{31\%})
$$

$$
D(3) = 1 - 0.69^3 = 1 - 0.328 = 0.672 \;\; (\text{67.2\%})
$$

$$
D(5) = 1 - 0.69^5 = 1 - 0.156 = 0.844 \;\; (\text{84.4\%})
$$

$$
D(10) = 1 - 0.69^{10} = 1 - 0.0244 = 0.9756 \;\; (\text{97.56\%})
$$

$$
D(15) = 1 - 0.69^{15} = 1 - 0.00381 = 0.9962 \;\; (\text{99.62\%})
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the Diminishing Returns

The marginal gain in discovery probability decreases sharply:

$$
\Delta D(1 \to 5) = 0.844 - 0.31 = 0.534
$$

$$
\Delta D(5 \to 10) = 0.9756 - 0.844 = 0.132
$$

$$
\Delta D(10 \to 15) = 0.9962 - 0.9756 = 0.021
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Testing the first 5 users uncovers ~**84.4%** of usability issues. Going from 5 → 10 users only adds ~**13.2%** more discoveries, and 10 → 15 only ~**2.1%**. This is Nielsen's *Law of Diminishing Returns*, justifying the **5 ± 2** user heuristic.

## 3.2 Derivation of the SUS Score (System Usability Scale)

The SUS is a 10-item Likert questionnaire (1 = Strongly Disagree, 5 = Strongly Agree). Items alternate: **Odd** items are positively worded, **Even** items are negatively worded.

### Step 1 — Collect Raw Item Scores

For each user $i$, collect scores $S_{i,1}, S_{i,2}, \ldots, S_{i,10}$ where each $S_{i,k} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$.

### Step 2 — Compute the User's SUS Contribution

For a single user's response, the score contribution is:

$$
\text{SUS}_i = \left( \sum_{k \text{ odd}} (S_{i,k} - 1) \right) + \left( \sum_{k \text{ even}} (5 - S_{i,k}) \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Scale to 0–100

Each item can contribute a maximum of 4 points, so the max per user is **40**. Multiply by **2.5** to normalize to 0–100:

$$
\text{SUS}_i^{\text{final}} = 2.5 \times \left[ \sum_{k \text{ odd}} (S_{i,k} - 1) + \sum_{k \text{ even}} (5 - S_{i,k}) \right]
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Group Mean

$$
\overline{\text{SUS}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{SUS}_i^{\text{final}}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a user responds: [4, 2, 5, 1, 4, 2, 5, 1, 4, 2].

Odd items (1, 3, 5, 7, 9): $4 - 1 = 3$, $5 - 1 = 4$, $4 - 1 = 3$, $5 - 1 = 4$, $4 - 1 = 3$.

$$
\text{Sum}_{\text{odd}} = 3 + 4 + 3 + 4 + 3 = 17
$$

Even items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10): $5 - 2 = 3$, $5 - 1 = 4$, $5 - 2 = 3$, $5 - 1 = 4$, $5 - 2 = 3$.

$$
\text{Sum}_{\text{even}} = 3 + 4 + 3 + 4 + 3 = 17
$$

$$
\text{SUS} = 2.5 \times (17 + 17) = 2.5 \times 34 = 85
$$

This user's SUS = **85** (Excellent — above the **68** threshold).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Usability Test Method

| Decision Criterion | Moderated Lab | Remote Unmoderated | Heuristic Evaluation | A/B Comparative |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Available Budget** | High | Low | Low | High |
| **Need for Qualitative Depth** | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Number of Participants** | 5–10 | 50–200 | 3–5 experts | 100+ |
| **Stage in Design Lifecycle** | Mid–Late | Any | Early | Pre-launch |
| **Geographically Distributed Users** | No | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Quantitative Comparison** | Limited | Strong | None | Very Strong |
| **Discovery of Subtle UX Issues** | High | Medium | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Usability Test Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Plan the Test] --> B[Step 2: Recruit Participants]
    B --> C[Step 3: Design Test Tasks]
    C --> D[Step 4: Prepare Test Environment]
    D --> E[Step 5: Conduct Test Sessions]
    E --> F[Step 6: Analyze Data]
    F --> G[Step 7: Report Findings]
    G --> H{Issues Found?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Prioritize by Severity x Frequency]
    I --> J[Recommend UI Fixes]
    J --> K[Retest in Next Cycle]
    H -- No --> L[Release Approved]
    K --> A
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This flowchart shows the iterative nature of usability testing. Each cycle feeds back into planning, embodying the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** quality cycle.

## 4.2 Data Collection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Test Participant] -->|Performs Task| UI[Software Under Test]
    P -->|Think Aloud| OBS[Observer / Moderator]
    UI -->|Logs Events| LOG[Logging Software e.g. Morae]
    OBS -->|Notes Quotes| DB[(Qualitative Data Store)]
    LOG --> DB
    P -->|Fills SUS Form| SUS[SUS Questionnaire]
    SUS --> DB
    DB --> ANA[Analyst]
    ANA --> RPT[Usability Test Report]
    RPT --> DEV[Development Team]
    DEV --> UI
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This shows the *three primary data streams* in a moderated usability test — (1) behavioural logs from the application, (2) qualitative observer notes from the think-aloud protocol, and (3) subjective SUS scores — all converging into a unified data store for the analyst to triangulate findings.

## 4.3 Nielsen's 10 Heuristics — Visual Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Nielsens 10 Usability Heuristics))
    H1[Visibility of System Status]
    H2[Match System and Real World]
    H3[User Control and Freedom]
    H4[Consistency and Standards]
    H5[Error Prevention]
    H6[Recognition not Recall]
    H7[Flexibility and Efficiency]
    H8[Aesthetic Minimalist Design]
    H9[Help Recognize and Recover Errors]
    H10[Help and Documentation]
```

## 4.4 Severity vs Frequency Risk Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SEVERITY_HIGH[High Severity]
      SH1[Critical: Data Loss / Cannot Complete Task] --> SH2[Major: Major Friction, Workaround Exists]
    end
    subgraph SEVERITY_MED[Medium Severity]
      SM1[Moderate: Slows User Noticeably] --> SM2[Minor: Cosmetic but Recurring]
    end
    subgraph SEVERITY_LOW[Low Severity]
      SL1[Trivial: Cosmetic Edge Case] --> SL2[Suggestion: Enhancement Idea]
    end
    FREQ[Frequency of Occurrence Across Users] --> SEVERITY_HIGH
    FREQ --> SEVERITY_MED
    FREQ --> SEVERITY_LOW
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Issues in the **High Severity + High Frequency** quadrant are the highest priority for fixing. This is the standard KTU "severity-frequency prioritization matrix" used in Usability Test Reports.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Usability Testing. List any four goals of usability testing.**

**Model Answer:**

Usability Testing is a user-centric evaluation technique that measures how effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily specified users can achieve specified goals using a software product in a specified context of use. It is formally defined by the **ISO 9241-11** standard.

**Four Goals:**
1. Identify usability problems in the UI before release.
2. Measure task completion rates, time-on-task, and error counts.
3. Measure user satisfaction via standardized questionnaires (e.g., SUS).
4. Establish a usability baseline for regression comparison across versions.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for goals. Examiners award 1 mark per correctly stated goal, max 4.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six of Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics.**

**Model Answer:**

1. Visibility of system status.
2. Match between system and the real world.
3. User control and freedom.
4. Consistency and standards.
5. Error prevention.
6. Recognition rather than recall.

*(0.5 mark per heuristic, 6 × 0.5 = 3 marks. Students commonly lose marks by paraphrasing without using the canonical heuristic names.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Choice 1

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **usability test process**. Describe each step with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The usability test process is a systematic, seven-step methodology:

1. **Plan the Test:** Define test objectives (e.g., "Measure checkout completion rate above 80%"). Identify the user profile, test method, and prepare a test plan document.
2. **Recruit Participants:** Select 5–10 representative users per user group. Use a screener questionnaire; for example, "Do you shop online at least once a month?"
3. **Design Test Tasks:** Create realistic scenarios such as "You want to return a defective product purchased 3 days ago. Show how you would do it."
4. **Prepare the Environment:** Set up a quiet lab with screen recording (Morae), audio capture, and a moderator console. Conduct a pilot test.
5. **Conduct Sessions:** Welcome the user, obtain consent, brief them on the think-aloud protocol, observe silently, and take notes on errors, hesitations, and verbal frustrations.
6. **Analyze Data:** Compute Task Completion Rate, Time-on-Task, and Error counts. Code qualitative observations by theme.
7. **Report Findings:** Produce a report with severity-rated issues, severity-frequency matrix, and prioritized recommendations to the dev team.

*(Allocate ~1 mark per step + 1 mark for the example narrative.)*

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **types of usability testing** with examples. Compare **heuristic evaluation** and **user-based testing**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Usability Testing:**
- **Exploratory:** Used early with low-fidelity prototypes; user explores freely.
- **Assessment:** Used late with metrics; structured tasks and SUS.
- **Comparative:** A/B testing two designs.
- **Remote:** Unmoderated tests via UserTesting.com.
- **Hallway:** Quick opportunistic tests with passers-by.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Heuristic Evaluation | User-Based Testing |
|---|---|---|
| Evaluators | 3–5 UX experts | 5–10 real users |
| Cost | Low | Moderate to High |
| Speed | Fast (hours) | Slow (days–weeks) |
| Realism | Low (experts ≠ users) | High |
| Discovers Severe Issues | Moderate | High |
| Best Stage | Early design | Mid–late design |

*(4 marks for types + 3 marks for the comparison table with explicit contrasts.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**Q. (a)** Define the **ISO 9241-11** standard. Explain the three usability metrics it defines. Show how the **Task Completion Rate** and **SUS Score** are calculated with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**ISO 9241-11** is the international standard that defines usability in terms of three measurable components:

1. **Effectiveness:** The accuracy and completeness with which users achieve specified goals. Measured as the *Task Completion Rate*.
2. **Efficiency:** The resources expended (typically time) in relation to effectiveness. Measured as *Time-on-Task per goal achieved*.
3. **Satisfaction:** Freedom from discomfort and positive attitudes toward the product. Measured via the **System Usability Scale (SUS)**.

**TCR Calculation Example:**

Out of 20 users testing a mobile banking app, 16 successfully completed the bill-payment task.

$$
\text{TCR} = \frac{16}{20} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

This is at the acceptable threshold.

**SUS Calculation Example:**

A user responds to the 10-item SUS as: **[4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2]**

Odd items sum: $(4-1) \times 5 = 15$. Even items sum: $(5-2) \times 5 = 15$.

$$
\text{SUS} = 2.5 \times (15 + 15) = 2.5 \times 30 = 75
$$

Score **75** > 68 → above-average usability.

*(2 marks ISO + 2 marks three metrics + 1.5 marks each calculation.)*

---

**Q. (b)** What is **Nielsen's "Law of Diminishing Returns"** in usability testing? Derive mathematically that testing 5 users uncovers ~85% of usability issues. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept:** Nielsen's empirical observation states that as you test more users, the marginal discovery of *new* usability issues decreases sharply. The first few users uncover most problems; subsequent users add little.

**Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 for full derivation.)*

Let $p = 0.31$ be the average discovery rate per user. The cumulative discovery function is:

$$
D(n) = 1 - (1 - p)^n = 1 - 0.69^n
$$

Evaluating at $n = 5$:

$$
D(5) = 1 - 0.69^5 = 1 - 0.156 = 0.844 \approx 84.4\%
$$

**Interpretation:** ~85% of issues are uncovered with just 5 users. Adding a 6th user adds only ~5% new discoveries. This justifies the *5 ± 2* user rule for cost-effective usability testing.

*(2 marks concept + 3 marks derivation + 2 marks interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing Heuristic Evaluation with User Testing.** Heuristic Evaluation uses *experts*, not real users. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Skipping severity ratings.** Reports must categorize issues (Critical/Major/Minor) — a flat list of issues loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the SUS threshold (68).** Simply giving a SUS score without context loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Miswriting the SUS formula.** The multiplier is **2.5** (not 2 or 5). Odd items use $(S - 1)$, even items use $(5 - S)$. Mixing these up costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Omitting informed consent and ethics.** Usability testing is research on human subjects; examiners deduct marks if you skip ethics in the process.
> 6. **No screenshots or examples in 14-mark answers.** Always illustrate heuristics with a real-world UI example (e.g., Google's autosuggest = H1: Visibility of System Status).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Usability Testing**

- 📘 **Definition:** ISO 9241-11 → Effectiveness, Efficiency, Satisfaction.
- 📊 **Three core metrics:** Task Completion Rate, Time-on-Task, SUS Score.
- 🧪 **Test Process (7 steps):** Plan → Recruit → Design Tasks → Prepare Environment → Conduct → Analyze → Report.
- 👥 **Sample Size:** 5 ± 2 users per group (uncovers ~85% of issues via $D(n) = 1 - 0.69^n$).
- 🧠 **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics:** Memorize all 10 — frequently asked.
- 📈 **SUS Score:** Range 0–100, threshold **68**, multiply raw sum by **2.5**.
- ⚖️ **Severity-Frequency Matrix:** Use to prioritize fixes in the report.
- 🔍 **Heuristic Evaluation ≠ User Testing** — expert-based vs. user-based.
- 🌐 **Types:** Exploratory, Assessment, Comparative, Remote, Hallway, Heuristic.
- 📝 **Report must include:** Executive summary, methodology, findings (severity-rated), recommendations, raw data appendix.
- ⚖️ **Ethics:** Informed consent, anonymity, right to withdraw — mandatory.
- 🏥 **Domain Applications:** Avionics (DO-178C), Medical (IEC 62366-1), Automotive (ISO 15005), FinTech.
- 💡 **Best Practice:** Test early, test often — iterative usability testing in each sprint.
- 🚫 **Pitfall:** Do not lead the user; tasks should be open-ended, not instructional.
- ✅ **Acceptance Threshold:** TCR $\geq$ 80%, SUS $\geq$ 68, Error rate $\leq$ 1 per task.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compatibility Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compatibility Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compatibility Testing** is a type of non-functional software testing that evaluates whether a software application can run consistently and correctly across **different computing environments**, including operating systems, web browsers, hardware platforms, network configurations, mobile devices, database systems, and other software applications. It ensures that the application behaves as expected regardless of the environmental variations in which it is deployed.

According to the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board)** and **IEEE 829** standards adopted in the KTU 2024 Scheme, compatibility testing is classified as a specialized subset of **System Testing** that verifies the *interoperability*, *portability*, and *co-existence* attributes of the system under test (SUT).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 2 — Testing Types):** Compatibility testing is grouped with other non-functional testing types such as **Usability, Performance, and Security Testing**, and is assessed under **Course Outcome CO2**: *Apply various testing techniques and strategies for real-world software projects.*

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Compatibility Testing

| Pillar | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Co-existence** | Ability of the application to coexist with other software without interfering with their functionality | Running MS Word and Adobe Reader simultaneously on Windows 11 |
| **Interoperability** | Ability of the application to interact and exchange data seamlessly with other systems | Web app exchanging JSON data with a third-party payment gateway API |
| **Portability** | Ability of the application to transfer/run from one environment to another with minimal changes | A Java-based web app running on both Windows and Linux servers |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Universal Travel Adapter"** 🌍🔌
> Imagine a person carrying a **laptop** (your software) while traveling from country to country (different environments). In the USA, sockets use **Type A/B** plugs; in the UK, **Type G**; in India, **Type D/M**; in Europe, **Type C/F**. The laptop is *internally* the same machine — but if it does not come with a **universal travel adapter** (compatibility layer), it simply will not function. 
> 
> **Compatibility Testing** is exactly this: ensuring that your *software* (the laptop) works correctly when plugged into *every conceivable environment* (socket type) — operating systems, browsers, devices, and networks — without losing its core functionality.

A simpler everyday example: A **YouTube video link** you share with a friend must open and play correctly whether the friend uses:
- Chrome on Windows 11
- Safari on macOS Sonoma
- Firefox on Ubuntu Linux
- The YouTube mobile app on an iPhone 15
- A Smart TV's built-in browser

If the video plays on Chrome but freezes on Safari, the application has **failed compatibility testing**.

## 1.4 Why Compatibility Testing is Indispensable in KTU/Industry Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme project guidelines, students developing their **Mini-Project and Main Project** must demonstrate cross-platform deployment. Failure to conduct compatibility testing typically results in **15–25% grade deduction** in the project evaluation rubric under the *"Deployment & Quality"* criterion.

- **Market Reach**: A web app that fails on iOS Safari loses nearly **27% of global mobile users**.
- **Defect Cost Multiplier**: A compatibility bug discovered in production costs **30x** more to fix than one caught during the testing phase.
- **User Retention**: **88% of users** abandon an application that fails to render correctly on their device, according to industry usability benchmarks.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Taxonomy of Compatibility Testing (Layered View)

Compatibility testing is structurally organized into **six distinct environmental layers**. Mastering this taxonomy is essential for the KTU Module 2 theory questions.

### Layer 1 — Hardware Compatibility Testing
Validates software execution across varied hardware configurations:
- **CPU Architectures**: x86, x86-64, ARM, Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3), RISC-V
- **RAM**: 2 GB, 4 GB, 8 GB, 16 GB, 32 GB
- **GPU**: Integrated vs. discrete, VRAM availability
- **Storage**: HDD vs. SSD, available disk space
- **Peripherals**: Printers, scanners, webcams, USB devices

### Layer 2 — Operating System (OS) Compatibility Testing
Validates the application on different OS families and versions:
- **Windows**: Windows 10, 11, Windows Server 2019/2022
- **macOS**: Ventura, Sonoma, Sequoia
- **Linux**: Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, CentOS, Red Hat
- **Mobile OS**: Android (12, 13, 14, 15), iOS (16, 17, 18)
- **Others**: ChromeOS, HarmonyOS, KaiOS

### Layer 3 — Browser Compatibility Testing
Ensures consistent rendering and functionality across browsers:
- **Engines**: Blink (Chrome/Edge), WebKit (Safari), Gecko (Firefox)
- **Browser Versions**: Latest stable, latest-1, latest-2
- **Mobile Browsers**: Chrome Mobile, Safari Mobile, Samsung Internet
- **Critical Concerns**: CSS rendering, JavaScript engine differences, HTML5 support

### Layer 4 — Database Compatibility Testing
Tests application behavior with various database systems:
- **SQL**: MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle DB, MS SQL Server, SQLite
- **NoSQL**: MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis, Couchbase
- **Cloud DBs**: AWS RDS, Azure Cosmos DB, Google Cloud Spanner

### Layer 5 — Network Compatibility Testing
Validates functionality under different network conditions:
- **Bandwidth**: 2G, 3G, 4G LTE, 5G, Wi-Fi, Broadband
- **Latency**: Low (<50ms) to High (>500ms)
- **Protocols**: IPv4, IPv6, HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, WebSockets

### Layer 6 — Mobile/Device Compatibility Testing
Tests on physical and virtual mobile devices:
- **Screen Resolutions**: HD, FHD, QHD, 4K, Foldable screens
- **Form Factors**: Phones, tablets, phablets, smartwatches
- **Manufacturers**: Samsung, Apple, Google Pixel, OnePlus, Xiaomi

## 2.2 Two Strategic Variants of Compatibility Testing

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme repeatedly tests students on the distinction between these two variants. Memorize this contrast.

| Variant | Direction | Purpose | KTU 2-Mark Favorite |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Compatibility Testing** | Old software ↔ New environment | Verifies the current software works on **future/upcoming** OS, browser, or hardware versions | "Testing current build on beta Windows 12" |
| **Backward Compatibility Testing** | New software ↔ Old environment | Verifies the **newer version** of the software still supports **legacy** data, file formats, APIs, and older systems | "Testing MS Word 2024 opening a .doc file from Word 2003" |

### Industry Standard Formula for Compatibility Test Coverage

$$
\text{Compatibility Coverage (CC)} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \cdot P_i \cdot t_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \cdot P_i} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $E_i$ = Number of executable test cases on environment $i$
- $P_i$ = Priority weight of environment $i$ (1 = low, 5 = critical)
- $t_i$ = Time allocated for environment $i$ (in hours)
- $n$ = Total number of test environments

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet** — commit this single equation to memory; it appears in nearly every numerical/analytical question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol/Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Total Test Combinations | $TC = \prod_{i=1}^{k} V_i$ | Where $V_i$ = number of variants in dimension $i$, $k$ = number of dimensions |
| **Pairwise Reduction** | $TC_{pair} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{k} V_i \cdot (V_i - 1) / 2$ | Each pair tested at least once |
| Compatibility Coverage | $CC = \frac{\text{Passed Compat. Tests}}{\text{Total Compat. Tests}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (0–100%) |
| Defect Density (Compatibility) | $DD_c = \frac{\text{Compatibility Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Defects per thousand lines of code |
| Environment Failure Rate | $EFR = \frac{\text{Environment-specific failures}}{\text{Total failures}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| Test Effort Estimation | $E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_i \cdot R_i) + B$ | $T_i$ = test cases, $R_i$ = replays, $B$ = buffer time (hours) |
| Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) | $DRE = \frac{\text{Defects found pre-release}}{\text{Defects found pre-release + post-release}} \times 100\%$ | Industry benchmark $\geq 95\%$ |
| Configuration Matrix Cardinality | $\vert C \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \vert C_i \vert$ | Total valid configurations |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud-Native DevOps**: Compatibility testing is integrated into CI/CD pipelines (e.g., GitHub Actions matrix builds) to validate deployments across multi-cloud environments (AWS, Azure, GCP).
- **Progressive Web Apps (PWAs)**: Service workers and manifest files must be tested across browsers with differing PWA support levels.
- **IoT Systems**: Compatibility testing ensures firmware interacts correctly with heterogeneous sensor networks and gateway devices.
- **Automotive Software**: AUTOSAR-based infotainment systems require strict compatibility validation across vehicle hardware variants.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Case Walkthroughs

## 3.1 The Compatibility Test Configuration Matrix (CTM)

A **CTM** is a multi-dimensional matrix used in KTU laboratory exams and industry practice to systematically enumerate all environment combinations to be tested.

### Example Scenario (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A KTU B.Tech final-year project team has developed a web-based **Online Examination Portal**. The team must plan compatibility tests for:
> - **3 Browsers**: Chrome, Firefox, Safari
> - **2 Operating Systems**: Windows 11, macOS Sonoma
> - **2 Devices**: Desktop, Mobile
>
> **Compute the total number of possible test combinations using exhaustive and pairwise techniques.**

### Step 1 — Identify Dimensions and Variants

$$
D_1 = \{\text{Chrome}, \text{Firefox}, \text{Safari}\} \Rightarrow V_1 = 3
$$

$$
D_2 = \{\text{Windows 11}, \text{macOS Sonoma}\} \Rightarrow V_2 = 2
$$

$$
D_3 = \{\text{Desktop}, \text{Mobile}\} \Rightarrow V_3 = 2
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Exhaustive Combination Count

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{exhaustive} &= \prod_{i=1}^{k} V_i \\
&= V_1 \times V_2 \times V_3 \\
&= 3 \times 2 \times 2 \\
&= 12 \text{ test cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical Note:** This is the total *Cartesian product* of all environment variants. Every combination must be executed in theory, but this is **time-prohibitive** in real projects.

### Step 3 — Apply Pairwise Reduction

Pairwise testing guarantees that **every pair of parameter values appears in at least one test case**, drastically reducing the matrix.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{pairwise} &\leq \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{V_i \cdot (V_i - 1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{3 \cdot 2}{2} + \frac{2 \cdot 1}{2} + \frac{2 \cdot 1}{2} \\
&= 3 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 5 \text{ minimum test cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Construct the Pairwise Test Set (Orthogonal Array Approach)

| **Test ID** | **Browser** | **OS** | **Device** |
|---|---|---|---|
| TC-01 | Chrome | Windows 11 | Desktop |
| TC-02 | Chrome | macOS Sonoma | Mobile |
| TC-03 | Firefox | Windows 11 | Mobile |
| TC-04 | Firefox | macOS Sonoma | Desktop |
| TC-05 | Safari | Windows 11 | Mobile |
| TC-06 | Safari | macOS Sonoma | Desktop |

> **Logical Note:** A commonly used tool is the **All-Pairs Algorithm** (e.g., PICT, ACTS, Hexawise), which generates the minimal covering array. The above 6 cases already cover all 12 pairwise interactions, while the minimum theoretical bound is 5 (the "3,2,2" case requires 6 in practice due to orthogonality).

### Step 5 — Calculate Compatibility Coverage

Suppose the team has 8 hours of testing time and prioritizes Chrome at weight 5, Firefox at 4, and Safari at 3.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CC &= \frac{\sum E_i \cdot P_i \cdot t_i}{\sum P_i \cdot t_i} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{(6 \cdot 5 \cdot 4) + (4 \cdot 4 \cdot 2.5) + (2 \cdot 3 \cdot 1.5)}{(5 \cdot 4) + (4 \cdot 2.5) + (3 \cdot 1.5)} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{120 + 40 + 9}{20 + 10 + 4.5} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{169}{34.5} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 489.86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Correction Note:** The numerator and denominator units differ, so the actual coverage is normalized differently. The standard simplified coverage formula is:

$$
CC = \frac{\text{Test cases executed successfully}}{\text{Total test cases planned}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
CC &= \frac{11}{12} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 91.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** An 91.67% coverage means 1 out of 12 combinations failed or could not be tested — a strong indicator that further investigation is needed on the failing combination (e.g., Safari on mobile).

## 3.2 Analytical Workflow — The Compatibility Test Process

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Step 1: Define Compatibility Scope (CTM Construction)        │
│ Step 2: Prioritize Environments (User Analytics + Market)    │
│ Step 3: Design Test Cases (Functional in Each Environment)   │
│ Step 4: Set Up Test Lab (VMs, Emulators, Real Devices)       │
│ Step 5: Execute Tests & Capture Defects                      │
│ Step 6: Report Compatibility Matrix & Coverage               │
│ Step 7: Regression on Fixed Configurations                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Defect Density Calculation Walkthrough

> **Problem:** A 50,000 LOC (lines of code) web application has 8 documented compatibility defects. Compute the **Compatibility Defect Density**.

$$
DD_c = \frac{\text{Compatibility Defects}}{\text{KLOC}} = \frac{8}{50} = 0.16 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

> **Industry Benchmark:** A $DD_c < 0.5$ defects/KLOC is considered **acceptable** for compatibility; $>1.0$ is **critical**.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Compatibility Testing. Differentiate between Forward Compatibility Testing and Backward Compatibility Testing with a real-time example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Compatibility testing is a type of non-functional testing that verifies the application's ability to run correctly across different hardware, software, network, and browser environments.
- **Forward (1 mark):** Testing the *current* version of software to ensure it works on *future* environments. *Example:* Testing the current MS Office 2021 on the upcoming beta release of Windows 12.
- **Backward (1 mark):** Testing the *new* version of software to ensure it still supports *older* environments, file formats, or APIs. *Example:* Testing MS Office 2024 to confirm it can open .doc files created in MS Word 2003.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain any THREE types of compatibility testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Browser Compatibility Testing (1 mark):** Validates web applications across multiple browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) and their versions to ensure consistent rendering and functionality.
2. **Operating System Compatibility Testing (1 mark):** Verifies software behavior on different OS platforms (Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS) and their respective versions.
3. **Mobile/Device Compatibility Testing (1 mark):** Tests applications across various mobile devices, screen sizes, resolutions, and manufacturers (Samsung, Apple, OnePlus).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either Question A OR Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Q-A(a). **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain in detail the various types of compatibility testing. Discuss the challenges faced during compatibility testing with suitable examples.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Compatibility testing is a non-functional testing methodology that validates the software's ability to function seamlessly across heterogeneous environments — operating systems, browsers, hardware, networks, and devices. As per ISTQB standards, it ensures *co-existence*, *interoperability*, and *portability*.

**Types of Compatibility Testing (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Hardware Compatibility Testing:** Verifies the software runs correctly on different hardware configurations (CPU architectures — x86 vs. ARM, varying RAM, GPU, peripherals). *Example:* Adobe Photoshop demanding at least 8 GB RAM; testing on both 4 GB and 16 GB machines.

2. **Operating System Compatibility Testing:** Validates behavior on different OS families and versions. *Example:* A .NET application tested on Windows 10, 11, and Linux (via .NET Core).

3. **Browser Compatibility Testing:** Ensures consistent rendering across browsers using different rendering engines (Blink, WebKit, Gecko). *Example:* CSS Grid layout breaking in older Safari versions.

4. **Network Compatibility Testing:** Tests functionality across various bandwidths, protocols (IPv4 vs. IPv6), and latency conditions. *Example:* A video conferencing app degrading gracefully on 2G networks.

5. **Mobile/Device Compatibility Testing:** Validates on various devices with different screen sizes, OS versions, and manufacturers. *Example:* Testing a banking app on Samsung Galaxy S23 and Apple iPhone 15.

**Challenges (1 mark):**
- **Combinatorial Explosion:** Number of test combinations grows exponentially ($3 \times 2 \times 2 = 12$ becomes $5 \times 3 \times 3 \times 2 = 90$).
- **Cost of Physical Devices:** Procuring every device variant is impractical; reliance on emulators may miss real-world bugs.
- **Rapid OS/Browser Updates:** New versions released frequently, making exhaustive testing infeasible.
- **Third-Party Dependency Variability:** Library updates may break compatibility silently.

---

#### Q-A(b). **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Describe the compatibility testing process. Explain the Configuration Matrix with a suitable example.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step-by-Step Process (4 marks — 0.5 mark per step):**

1. **Define Scope:** Identify the application type (web, mobile, desktop) and the environment dimensions to be tested.
2. **Analyze User Base:** Use analytics to determine the most-used environments (e.g., 60% Chrome on Windows, 25% Safari on iOS).
3. **Build Configuration Matrix (CTM):** Enumerate all variants per dimension.
4. **Prioritize Combinations:** Apply risk-based or pairwise reduction.
5. **Set Up Test Lab:** Configure VMs, emulators, or use cloud platforms like BrowserStack.
6. **Design Test Cases:** Write environment-specific functional and UI test cases.
7. **Execute and Log Defects:** Record environment-specific failures with screenshots/logs.
8. **Report Coverage and Sign Off:** Generate a compatibility matrix report indicating pass/fail per cell.

**Configuration Matrix Example (3 marks):**

Consider an e-commerce web app tested across 3 browsers × 2 OS × 2 devices:

| **Browser \ Device** | **Windows + Desktop** | **Windows + Mobile** | **macOS + Desktop** | **macOS + Mobile** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chrome | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Firefox | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Safari | N/A | N/A | ✅ | ✅ |

> **Note:** "N/A" indicates an infeasible combination (Safari on Windows was discontinued after Safari 5 in 2012). The total executable combinations: $2 \times 2 \times 2 + 2 \times 2 = 12$, but only 10 are valid.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

#### Q-B(a). **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Discuss the role of virtualization and cloud-based platforms in compatibility testing. List any four popular tools used for browser and mobile compatibility testing with their key features.**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Virtualization (3 marks):**
- **Virtualization** (via VMware, VirtualBox, Hyper-V) allows testers to instantiate multiple OS instances on a single physical machine, drastically reducing hardware costs.
- **Emulators/Simulators** (Android Studio Emulator, Xcode Simulator) replicate mobile devices on a workstation.
- **Cloud platforms** (BrowserStack, Sauce Labs, LambdaTest) offer **on-demand** access to thousands of real device-browser-OS combinations, eliminating the need for in-house device labs.
- Supports **parallel execution** of test suites, reducing compatibility test cycle time by up to **70%**.

**Four Popular Tools (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Tool | Type | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Selenium Grid** | Browser | Open-source; supports parallel cross-browser testing with WebDriver |
| **BrowserStack** | Browser + Mobile | Real device cloud; supports 3000+ browser-OS combinations |
| **Appium** | Mobile | Open-source; supports native, hybrid, and mobile web apps on iOS and Android |
| **LambdaTest** | Browser | Cloud-based; offers AI-powered visual regression testing |

---

#### Q-B(b). **[7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**A web application is to be tested across 4 browsers, 3 operating systems, and 2 device types. Calculate the number of exhaustive and pairwise test combinations. Construct a Configuration Matrix and propose a risk-based prioritization strategy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Exhaustive Combinations (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{exhaustive} &= V_1 \times V_2 \times V_3 \\
&= 4 \times 3 \times 2 \\
&= 24 \text{ test cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Pairwise Minimum (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{pairwise} &\leq \frac{4 \cdot 3}{2} + \frac{3 \cdot 2}{2} + \frac{2 \cdot 1}{2} \\
&= 6 + 3 + 1 \\
&= 10 \text{ test cases (theoretical minimum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, the **All-Pairs tool** (e.g., PICT) typically yields **12 test cases** for a 4×3×2 matrix.

**Step 3 — Configuration Matrix (2 marks):**

| **Test ID** | **Browser** | **OS** | **Device** |
|---|---|---|---|
| TC-01 | Chrome | Windows 11 | Desktop |
| TC-02 | Chrome | macOS Sonoma | Mobile |
| TC-03 | Chrome | Ubuntu 24.04 | Desktop |
| TC-04 | Firefox | Windows 11 | Mobile |
| TC-05 | Firefox | macOS Sonoma | Desktop |
| TC-06 | Firefox | Ubuntu 24.04 | Mobile |
| TC-07 | Edge | Windows 11 | Mobile |
| TC-08 | Edge | macOS Sonoma | Desktop |
| TC-09 | Safari | Windows 11 | N/A |
| TC-10 | Safari | macOS Sonoma | Mobile |
| TC-11 | Safari | Ubuntu 24.04 | N/A |
| TC-12 | Edge | Ubuntu 24.04 | Desktop |

**Step 4 — Risk-Based Prioritization (1 mark):**
- **High Priority (P1):** Chrome + Windows 11 + Desktop (most common user combo per analytics).
- **Medium Priority (P2):** Safari + macOS + Mobile (significant Apple user base).
- **Low Priority (P3):** Edge + Ubuntu, Safari + Windows (infeasible/infeasible — skip).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Forward vs. Backward Compatibility** — many students swap the definitions. Memorize: *Forward = current build → future environment*; *Backward = new build → old environment*.
> 2. **Do not forget to list Co-existence, Interoperability, and Portability** when defining compatibility testing. Missing these three pillars costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Do not confuse "compatibility" with "portability"** as synonyms. Portability is a *sub-attribute* of compatibility.
> 4. **In numerical problems**, always show the formula before substitution. Marks are awarded for the *method*, not just the final number.
> 5. **Configuration Matrix tables must be properly aligned** in your answer sheet; messy tables lead to 1–2 mark deductions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final-Day Quick Recall)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Compatibility testing = validating software across varied environments to ensure consistent behavior.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Co-existence, Interoperability, Portability.
- ✅ **Six Layers:** Hardware, OS, Browser, Database, Network, Mobile/Device.
- ✅ **Forward Compatibility:** Current software ↔ **future** environment (e.g., current app on beta OS).
- ✅ **Backward Compatibility:** New software ↔ **legacy** environment (e.g., Word 2024 opening .doc from Word 2003).
- ✅ **Combinatorial Formula:** $TC = \prod_{i=1}^{k} V_i$ (Cartesian product of all environment variants).
- ✅ **Pairwise Bound:** $TC_{pair} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{V_i(V_i - 1)}{2}$.
- ✅ **Coverage Formula:** $CC = \frac{\text{Passed Tests}}{\text{Total Tests}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Defect Density:** $DD_c = \frac{\text{Compat. defects}}{KLOC}$ — keep < **0.5** for production readiness.
- ✅ **Popular Tools (Browser):** Selenium Grid, BrowserStack, LambdaTest, Sauce Labs.
- ✅ **Popular Tools (Mobile):** Appium, Espresso, XCUITest, Firebase Test Lab.
- ✅ **Virtualization Tools:** VMware, VirtualBox, Hyper-V — reduce hardware cost by 60–80%.
- ✅ **Configuration Matrix (CTM):** Multi-dimensional grid listing all environment combinations.
- ✅ **KTU Module Mapping:** This topic falls under **CO2** of the PEITT862 syllabus.
- ✅ **ISTQB Classification:** Compatibility testing is a *System-level non-functional test type*.
- ✅ **Industry Benchmark:** DRE for compatibility bugs ≥ **95%** pre-release.
- ✅ **Common Defect Categories:** Rendering issues, font inconsistencies, JavaScript engine differences, OS-specific file path separators, locale/regional settings, DPI scaling.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing the Test Process: Test Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Managing the Test Process: Test Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Management (TM)** is the **systematic, continuous process of planning, organizing, directing, controlling, and monitoring the test activities** within a software project to ensure that the test objectives are achieved within the constraints of **time, budget, scope, and quality**. It encompasses the coordination of test resources, test artifacts, test teams, and the evaluation of the overall test process for continual improvement.

According to the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board) Foundation Level Syllabus** and aligned with **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT862 - Software Testing, Module 3)**, Test Management is treated as a **sub-discipline of project management** that focuses specifically on the *test process*. It is the responsibility of the **Test Manager / Test Lead** and is executed across all test levels (Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance) and test types (Functional, Non-Functional).

### 1.1.1 Core Components of Test Management

| # | Component | Purpose |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Test Organization** | Defines who does the testing (in-house, independent, outsourced) |
| 2 | **Test Planning** | Decides what, when, how, and by whom testing is performed |
| 3 | **Test Monitoring & Control** | Tracks progress and initiates corrective actions |
| 4 | **Test Estimation** | Forecasts effort, cost, and duration of testing |
| 5 | **Test Configuration Management (TCM)** | Manages test artifacts and their versions |
| 6 | **Test Process Improvement (TPI/TMM/CTP/STEP)** | Continually elevates the maturity of the testing process |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Inspector" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Test Management = Construction Site Inspection Management**

Imagine you are constructing a 30-floor skyscraper. The Test Manager is the **Chief Building Inspector** who:

1. **Plans** — Decides *which floors* to inspect, *what materials* to test, and *when* inspections happen.
2. **Organizes** — Hires inspectors, assigns zones (plumbing, electrical, structural).
3. **Estimates** — Calculates the *number of test days* based on the size of each floor.
4. **Monitors** — Tracks how many floors have been inspected vs. the plan.
5. **Controls** — If the concrete quality fails on Floor 12, he halts construction and orders rework.
6. **Improves** — After each project, he refines his checklist based on the defects found previously.

Just as a building cannot be handed over without a Chief Inspector's sign-off, **a software product cannot be released without a Test Manager's "Test Closure Report"**.

### 1.3 Key Industry-Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard Test Management Metrics that the KTU examiner expects you to memorize:
> - **DRE (Defect Removal Efficiency)** = `E / (E + D)` where E = Defects found before release, D = Defects found after release.
> - **Test Coverage** = `T_executed / T_total` (test cases executed ÷ total test cases).
> - **Defect Density** = `Defects_found / Size_of_Module` (usually per KLOC or per Function Point).
> - **Test Effectiveness** = `(Bugs found by test team) / (Bugs found in total)`.

These metrics form the **KPI (Key Performance Indicators)** of the Test Manager and are essential for KTU 14-mark problems.

---

## 1.4 Why is Test Management a Separate Discipline?

> [!NOTE]
> Testing is **not** just an "activity at the end of coding". It is a **lifecycle process** that requires:
> - Dedicated **budget allocation** (typically 25%–40% of total project cost).
> - Specialized **human resources** (test analysts, automation engineers, performance testers).
> - **Tooling** (JIRA, TestRail, Zephyr, Selenium Grid, JMeter).
> - **Governance** (Test Policy, Test Strategy, Test Plans).

Without Test Management, testing becomes **ad-hoc, unmeasured, and uncontrollable**, leading to project failure, customer dissatisfaction, and post-release defects.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Test Management (ISTQB / IEEE 829 Standard)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the following five logical pillars. Each pillar is a *continuous activity* that runs throughout the project lifecycle.

### Pillar 1: Test Organization

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Decides *WHO* performs the testing.

Three organizational models are recognized by IEEE 1008 (Standard for Software Unit Testing) and ISTQB:

1. **In-house / Developer Testing** — Developers test their own code. *Cheap, but suffers from author-bias.*
2. **Independent Testers within the Project Team** — A separate QA group within the project. *Removes bias, still close to the team.*
3. **Independent Test Group / Specialist Test Team** — A dedicated test department outside the project (e.g., a Test Center of Excellence). *Maximum independence, highest objectivity.*

**Why the "Why" matters:** Author bias is the *single biggest cause* of missed defects. Independent testing increases the **probability of finding defects** by introducing fresh perspectives.

### Pillar 2: Test Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Defines the *scope, approach, resources, schedule, and deliverables* of testing.

The Test Plan is the **single most important document** in Test Management. It is guided by **IEEE 829-2008 / IEEE 29119-2** standards and contains:

- Test Plan Identifier
- Test Items (what to test)
- Test Strategy (how to test)
- Entry & Exit Criteria
- Test Deliverables
- Environmental Needs
- Responsibilities
- Schedule
- Risks & Contingencies
- Approval Sign-off

**Master Test Plan (MTP)** vs. **Level Test Plan (LTP)**:
- MTP covers the entire project at a high level.
- LTP details testing at one level (e.g., System Test Plan, Acceptance Test Plan).

### Pillar 3: Test Monitoring & Control

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Monitoring* is the *continuous observation* of test activities; *Control* is the *corrective action* taken when results deviate from the plan.

- **Monitoring** = Collect metrics (Test Cases Executed, Pass/Fail Rate, Defects Logged).
- **Control** = Re-prioritize, add resources, extend schedule, or stop testing.

### Pillar 4: Test Estimation

Forecasting **effort, cost, and duration**. Methods include:
1. **Expert Judgment / Delphi Method** — Consensus from experienced testers.
2. **Analogy-Based Estimation** — Compare with similar past projects.
3. **Test Point Analysis (TPA)** — Based on Function Points / Use Case Points.
4. **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** — Decompose testing into smaller tasks.
5. **COCOMO II for Testing** — Algorithmic model.

### Pillar 5: Test Process Improvement (TPI / TMM / CTP / STEP)

Models used to *mature* the test process over time. (Detailed comparison in Section 2.4.)

---

## 2.2 Test Monitoring — The S-Curve & Burn-Down Charts

> [!NOTE]
> Test progress is visualized through **Cumulative Defect Curves (S-Curves)** and **Test Burn-Down Charts**. These are *high-yield* topics in KTU 14-mark questions.

**Ideal S-Curve:** Slow start → Rapid defect detection in mid-phase → Tails off near completion.

If the actual curve flattens too early, the Test Manager must investigate (false negatives? Insufficient test data?).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Test Estimation & Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAM — MEMORIZE THESE.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{E}{E + D} \times 100\%$ | E = Pre-release defects, D = Post-release defects. Aim for **> 95%**. |
| 2 | **Test Case Effectiveness (TCE)** | $TCE = \dfrac{Defects\ Found}{Test\ Cases\ Executed}$ | Average defects per executed test case. |
| 3 | **Test Coverage (TC)** | $TC = \dfrac{T_{executed}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Measures completeness of testing. |
| 4 | **Requirements Coverage (RC)** | $RC = \dfrac{R_{tested}}{R_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of requirements covered by tests. |
| 5 | **Test Effort Estimation (TEE)** | $E = \dfrac{N \times T_{avg}}{P}$ | E = Effort, N = Number of test cases, T_avg = Avg time per case, P = Productivity. |
| 6 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{Defects\ Found}{Size}$ | Size in **KLOC** (per 1000 lines) or **FP** (Function Point). |
| 7 | **Cost of Testing (CT)** | $CT = \sum (T_i \times R_i) + Tools + Infra$ | T_i = hours of tester i, R_i = hourly rate. |
| 8 | **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum (T_{detect} - T_{inject})}{N_{defects}}$ | Average time a defect lives before detection. |
| 9 | **Test Case Productivity (TCP)** | $TCP = \dfrac{N_{test\ cases}}{Effort\ (person-hrs)}$ | How many test cases a tester designs per hour. |
| 10 | **Percentage of Reopened Defects (PRD)** | $PRD = \dfrac{D_{reopened}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Measures quality of the test process. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the **units** in the final answer. For example, write *"DD = 0.5 defects per KLOC"*, not just *"0.5"*. Marks are awarded for unit awareness.

---

## 2.4 Test Process Improvement Models — Comparative Analysis

This is a **favourite 7-mark sub-question** in KTU Module 3.

| Aspect | **TMM (Test Maturity Model)** | **TPI (Test Process Improvement)** | **CTP (Critical Test Process)** | **STEP (Systematic Test & Evaluation Process)** |
|--------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| **Origin** | Illinois Institute of Technology (IIT), 1996 | Sogeti (Netherlands), 1997 | Boehmer & Associates, 1996 | DOE (US Department of Energy), 2005 |
| **Structure** | 5 Maturity Levels (Initial → Optimizing) | 14 Key Areas × 5 Maturity Levels (A → E) | 12 Critical Process Areas | 5 Maturity Levels (Ad-hoc → Improving) |
| **Model Type** | CMM-like, level-based | Continuous, area-based | Goal-driven, level-based | Quality-model-based, criteria-driven |
| **Focus** | Institutional maturity | Test process optimization | Critical success areas | Testing + Evaluation of software products |
| **Assessment** | Formal appraisers | TPI Next maturity matrix | Self-assessment with checklists | Appraisals using STEP criteria |
| **KTU Weight** | High (most asked) | High | Medium | Low-Medium |

### 2.4.1 TMM — 5 Levels (Memorize!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TMM Level Names** (Modified from CMM):
> - **Level 1 — Initial:** Ad-hoc, chaotic, no defined process.
> - **Level 2 — Phase Definition:** Testing is integrated into each phase; goals defined.
> - **Level 3 — Integration:** Test process is integrated across the lifecycle; training programs exist.
> - **Level 4 — Management & Measurement:** Test process is measured, controlled, and managed quantitatively.
> - **Level 5 — Optimization / Defect Prevention:** Focus on *preventing* defects rather than finding them.

### 2.4.2 TPI Next — 14 Key Areas

> [!NOTE]
> Test Strategy, Test Lifecycle, Stakeholders, Team Competence, Communication, Reporting, Test Planning, Test Design, Test Execution, Defect Management, Test Environment, Test Automation, **Metrics**, **Testware Management**. (Mnemonic: *SLS TCR DDP MMT*)

### 2.4.3 CTP — 12 Critical Processes (Selected Highlights)

- Test Planning
- Test Case Design
- Test Environment Setup
- Test Execution
- Defect Tracking
- Test Measurement

### 2.4.4 STEP — 5 Maturity Levels (Also called "Test Process Improvement using STEP")

- **Level 1 — Ad-hoc / Chaotic**
- **Level 2 — Repeatable / Test Planning Present**
- **Level 3 — Defined / Standard Test Process**
- **Level 4 — Managed / Measured Process**
- **Level 5 — Optimizing / Continually Improved**

---

## 2.5 Test Configuration Management (TCM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TCM** is the discipline of tracking and controlling *test artifacts* (test plans, test cases, test data, test scripts, defect logs, test reports). It ensures that testers always work on the **correct, approved version** of the testware.

Real-world utility: In a banking application, if the test team accidentally tests against **v1.2** of the requirements instead of **v1.3**, the entire test cycle is wasted. TCM prevents this.
