## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part A: short-answer questions testing definitions, advantages, and direct recall.

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a Test Management Tool. Mention any two advantages of using Jira as a test management tool.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Test Management Tool (TMT)** is a software application used to manage the testing process—including test planning, test case creation, test execution, defect tracking, and reporting. It centralizes testing artifacts and automates traceability between requirements, test cases, and defects.

**Two advantages of Jira as a TMT:**
1. **Centralized Issue Tracking** – Jira provides a single repository for storing bugs, test cases (via plugins), and test execution results, reducing data silos and improving cross-team visibility.
2. **Seamless CI/CD Integration** – Jira's REST API and built-in integrations with Jenkins, Bamboo, and Bitbucket enable automated bug creation upon build failure, accelerating the defect lifecycle.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any six important features of Jira relevant to software testing.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Issue Tracking** with customizable issue types (Bug, Story, Task, Epic, Test).
2. **Customizable Workflows** allowing states like *Open → In Progress → Resolved → Closed*.
3. **Agile Boards** (Kanban and Scrum) for sprint planning and backlog grooming.
4. **Advanced Search (JQL)** to filter issues by any field.
5. **Dashboards with Gadgets** (CFD, Burndown, Velocity, Pie Charts).
6. **Test Management Plugins** (Zephyr, Xray, TestRail) for formal test case management.
7. **REST API** for integration with external tools.
8. **Audit Trail** with immutable change history for compliance.

*(Any six earn full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B: full 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The two options below are **mutually exclusive alternatives**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **core architectural components** of Jira with a neat block diagram. Discuss how the **Workflow Engine** and **REST API** contribute to test management in a distributed QA team.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A QA team recorded the following sprint velocities over 5 sprints: 22, 26, 24, 28, 30 story points. The upcoming Sprint 6 has 12 working days. Compute (i) the team's **average velocity**, (ii) the **standard deviation**, (iii) the **ideal burndown equation** assuming the team commits to the average velocity, and (iv) the **ideal remaining work** at the end of day 4. Also, briefly explain the significance of burndown charts in test release readiness.

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Core Architectural Components of Jira**

Jira's architecture can be visualized in five logical layers:

1. **Presentation Layer** – A web-based UI accessed via browser (Atlassian Cloud) or via the desktop client. It renders dashboards, boards, and issue screens.
2. **Application Layer** – Java-based business logic implementing the **Workflow Engine**, **Permission Schemes**, **Notification Schemes**, and the **Plugin SDK** (e.g., OSGi-based extensions for Zephyr/Xray).
3. **Persistence Layer** – A relational database (PostgreSQL/MySQL/MS SQL Server) that stores all issues, comments, work logs, attachments, and configuration. The **change history** is append-only, ensuring audit compliance.
4. **Integration Layer** – The **REST API** (with OAuth 2.0) and webhooks that allow external systems (Jenkins, Selenium, GitHub) to read/write Jira data and trigger events.
5. **Reporting Layer** – Dashboards and gadgets that aggregate data from the persistence layer into visual artifacts (CFD, Burndown, Velocity).

**Workflow Engine Contribution to Test Management:**
- Defines the **lifecycle states** of a bug/test case (e.g., *Open → In Progress → Resolved → Verified → Closed*).
- Enforces **transition guards** (only a user in the *QA Lead* project role can transition from *Resolved* to *Closed*).
- Automates **post-functions** (sending email to the reporter when status changes to *Resolved*).
- In distributed QA teams, the Workflow Engine ensures **standardized defect handling** across geographies, eliminating ambiguity.

**REST API Contribution to Test Management:**
- Allows **automated test execution frameworks** (e.g., Selenium + Jenkins) to **auto-create bugs** in Jira when a test fails, capturing stack traces and screenshots.
- Enables **bi-directional sync** with TestRail or Zephyr—tests run outside Jira get reported back with status.
- Facilitates **dashboards for non-Jira users** (e.g., management viewing live QA metrics in Power BI via API).
- Powers **chatops** (e.g., Slack bot that listens for `/jira create` to file bugs during stand-ups).

*(Include a labelled block diagram from Section 4.1 for full marks.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Burndown Analysis**

Given data: velocities $v_1=22, v_2=26, v_3=24, v_4=28, v_5=30$; $n=5$; $S=12$ days.

**(i) Average Velocity:**

$$
\bar{v} = \frac{22 + 26 + 24 + 28 + 30}{5} = \frac{130}{5} = 26 \;\text{story points}
$$

**(ii) Standard Deviation:**

Compute squared deviations from the mean $\bar{v}=26$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(22-26)^2 &= 16 \\
(26-26)^2 &= 0 \\
(24-26)^2 &= 4 \\
(28-26)^2 &= 4 \\
(30-26)^2 &= 16
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum (v_i - \bar{v})^2 = 16 + 0 + 4 + 4 + 16 = 40
$$

Apply Bessel-corrected sample standard deviation:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{40}{5-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{40}{4}} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 \;\text{story points}
$$

**(iii) Ideal Burndown Equation:**

With $W_0 = \bar{v} = 26$ story points and $S = 12$ days:

$$
I(t) = W_0 - \frac{W_0}{S} \cdot t = 26 - \frac{26}{12} \cdot t
$$

Simplify the slope coefficient:

$$
\frac{26}{12} = 2.1\overline{6} \approx 2.167
$$

So the ideal equation is:

$$
I(t) = 26 - 2.167 \cdot t \;\;\text{story points}
$$

**(iv) Ideal Remaining Work at Day 4:**

$$
I(4) = 26 - 2.167 \times 4 = 26 - 8.667 = 17.333 \;\text{story points}
$$

**Significance of Burndown Charts in Test Release Readiness:**
- A burndown chart **visually compares actual progress against the ideal line**. If the actual remaining work is consistently above the line, the team is at risk of missing the release deadline.
- For **test release readiness**, the QA Lead uses the burndown to determine whether the planned test cases (and their story points) will be executed by the end of the sprint.
- A flatlined or upward-trending burndown near the sprint end signals **scope creep, blockers, or under-resourced QA**, prompting a release delay or scope reduction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a **neat diagram**, explain the **Jira issue hierarchy** (Epic, Story, Task, Bug, Sub-task) as it applies to test management. Discuss how **issue linking** provides **traceability** between requirements, test cases, and defects in a real-world project.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the **features, advantages, and limitations** of using **native Jira** versus **Jira with the Zephyr plugin** for test management. Give a justified recommendation for (i) a startup with 5 developers and (ii) a BFSI enterprise with 200+ testers.

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Jira Issue Hierarchy and Traceability**

Jira organizes work into a five-level hierarchy:

1. **Initiative** (portfolio level, optional) – A strategic theme spanning multiple projects.
2. **Epic** – A large feature or capability spanning multiple sprints. *Example: "Implement Online Payments Module"*.
3. **Story** – A user-centric, independently deliverable requirement. *Example: "As a user, I want to pay via UPI"*.
4. **Task / Bug** – A non-functional unit of work or a defect. *Example: "Integrate Razorpay SDK" or "Payment fails for amount > ₹10,000"*.
5. **Sub-task** – A granular, often time-bound child of a Task/Bug. *Example: "Write unit tests for payment handler"*.

*(Include the hierarchy diagram from Section 4.3 for full marks.)*

**Issue Linking for Traceability:**
Jira provides four primary link types relevant to testing:
- **Tests / Tests against** – Links a test case to the story/requirement it validates.
- **Blocks / Is blocked by** – Links a bug to a story, indicating the bug must be fixed before the story can be completed.
- **Relates to** – Loose association between artifacts.
- **Cloners / Duplicate** – Marks duplicate issues to avoid double work.

**Real-World Traceability Example:**
Consider a UPI payment story. A QA creates a Test Case `ECOM-501` with three test steps. This test case is linked to Story `ECOM-100` via the **Tests** link. When the test fails, a Bug `ECOM-602` is created and linked to `ECOM-501` via **Tests** and to `ECOM-100` via **Blocks**. This chain (`ECOM-100 ← Tests ← ECOM-501 ← Tests ← ECOM-602`) provides a **complete audit trail** showing exactly which requirement, test, and defect are connected—essential for ISO/CMMI audits and root-cause analysis.

---

**Part (b) — Native Jira vs. Jira + Zephyr**

**Native Jira (Out-of-the-box):**
- *Features:* Issue tracking, customizable workflows, Kanban/Scrum boards, dashboards, REST API, basic search (JQL).
- *Advantages:* No additional cost, quick setup, sufficient for bug tracking and Agile ceremonies.
- *Limitations:* **No native test case authoring, no test cycles, no formal traceability, no test execution reports.**

**Jira with Zephyr (Squad or Scale):**
- *Features:* All native features PLUS dedicated *Test* issue type, test steps, test cycles, test plans, test execution tracking, traceability matrix, BDD support, test coverage reports, integration with CI/CD.
- *Advantages:* **Formal test case management, requirement traceability, automated test result aggregation, regulatory compliance ready.**
- *Limitations:* **Additional licensing cost, learning curve, performance overhead at scale, vendor lock-in.**

**Justified Recommendations:**

**(i) Startup with 5 developers:**
- *Recommendation:* **Native Jira is sufficient.**
- *Justification:* Small teams prioritize speed and cost. A startup rarely has formal test case documentation needs; bugs are tracked via Slack or email and filed directly in Jira. Adding Zephyr would add cost and complexity without proportional benefit. They can adopt Zephyr later as they scale.

**(ii) BFSI Enterprise with 200+ testers:**
- *Recommendation:* **Jira + Zephyr Scale (or Xray).**
- *Justification:* Banking, Financial Services, and Insurance (BFSI) industries are subject to strict regulations (RBI, SEBI, ISO 27001, SOX) requiring **traceability of every requirement to a test case, every test case to an execution record, and every defect to its root cause**. With 200+ testers, manual tracking is impossible. Zephyr Scale's enterprise features—**test plan versioning, multi-project dashboards, API-level automation, and audit-grade reports**—are essential. The licensing cost is justified by the cost of a single regulatory non-compliance penalty, which can run into millions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in the Jira/TMT question (costing 2–5 marks):**
>
> 1. **Conflating Jira Software with Jira Core** – Jira Software (with Agile) ≠ Jira Core (issue tracking only). Always specify which edition your answer refers to.
> 2. **Forgetting plugin dependency** – Many students claim Jira "natively supports test case management." It does **NOT**. Always clarify that test case management requires **Zephyr, Xray, or TestRail**.
> 3. **Mixing up Priority and Severity** – *Priority* is a **business** decision (when to fix); *Severity* is a **technical** decision (how badly the system is broken). Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark questions** – A block diagram or workflow diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in Part B. Text-only answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Incorrect velocity formula** – Use the *arithmetic mean of past sprints*, not the most recent sprint. Some students erroneously take the *max* velocity.
> 6. **Missing units in burndown** – Always specify *story points* on the y-axis and *days* on the x-axis. A unitless equation is treated as incomplete.
> 7. **Forgetting boundary verification** – In derivation questions, always verify $I(0) = W_0$ and $I(S) = 0$. Examiners award 1 mark for this sanity check.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Introduction to Test Management Tool (Jira)**

- **Jira** is developed by **Atlassian**; it is the de-facto industry standard for **issue and test tracking** in Agile projects.
- **Native Jira is NOT a full TMT**; it requires **Zephyr, Xray, or TestRail** for formal test case management.
- The **five-tier hierarchy** is: *Initiative → Epic → Story → Task/Bug → Sub-task*.
- The **four primary issue link types** are: *Tests, Blocks, Relates, Cloners*—these provide traceability.
- **Workflow States** for a typical bug: *Open → In Progress → Resolved → Closed*, with optional *Reopened* loop.
- **Jira Architecture** consists of five layers: *Presentation → Application → Persistence → Integration → Reporting*.
- **JQL (Jira Query Language)** allows advanced searches like `project = ECOM AND type = Bug AND priority = Highest`.
- **Velocity** $\bar{v} = \frac{1}{n}\sum v_i$; **Standard Deviation** $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum(v_i-\bar{v})^2}{n-1}}$.
- **Ideal Burndown** $I(t) = W_0 - \frac{W_0}{S} \cdot t$; verify boundaries at $t=0$ and $t=S$.
- **Test Coverage** $TC = \frac{T_{executed}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$.
- **Defect Removal Efficiency** $DRE = \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre}+D_{post}} \times 100\%$.
- **Defect Density** $DD = \frac{D_{module}}{KLOC_{module}}$ defects per thousand lines of code.
- **Priority** is a *business* (when-to-fix) attribute; **Severity** is a *technical* (impact) attribute.
- **Kanban** = continuous flow with WIP limits; **Scrum** = time-boxed sprints with velocity tracking.
- **Atlassian Marketplace** hosts 1000+ plugins; **Zephyr Squad, Zephyr Scale, Xray, QMetry** are top TMT plugins.
- **REST API** enables CI/CD integration—Jenkins/Bamboo can auto-create bugs on build failure.
- **Dashboards** support gadgets: *CFD, Burndown, Velocity, Pie Chart, Created-vs-Resolved, 2D Filter*.
- **Audit Trail** (immutable change history) makes Jira suitable for **ISO, CMMI, FDA, SOX** compliance.
- **Jira Cloud** vs. **Jira Data Center** — Cloud is SaaS-hosted; Data Center is self-hosted for enterprises.
- For **KTU 14-mark answers**, always include a **diagram** (block/workflow) and **numerical example** if the question permits.
- For **startups**, prefer **native Jira**; for **enterprises (BFSI, healthcare, defense)**, prefer **Jira + Zephyr Scale/Xray**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automation and Testing Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automation and Testing Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Test Automation — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Test Automation** is the use of specialized software tools, scripts, and frameworks to **execute test cases automatically**, compare actual outcomes against predicted outcomes, manage test data, and generate test reports — with **minimal human intervention** — in order to improve testing efficiency, repeatability, accuracy, and coverage across the software development life cycle (SDLC).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, test automation is positioned as a **strategic engineering activity** (not merely a coding task). It sits at the intersection of three disciplines:

- **Software Engineering** → automation frameworks, CI/CD integration
- **Quality Engineering** → coverage metrics, defect detection rates
- **Project Management** → ROI calculations, cost-benefit analysis

### 1.2 Testing Tools — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Testing Tools** are software applications that support one or more testing activities such as test planning, test case generation, test execution, test evaluation, defect tracking, and test management. Tools can be classified as **commercial** (e.g., UFT, LoadRunner), **open-source** (e.g., Selenium, JMeter), or **in-house developed**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **quality inspector at a car manufacturing plant**. Manually inspecting every car (visual checks, brake tests, emission tests) is:

- **Slow** → Each car takes 30 minutes
- **Error-prone** → After 4 hours, the inspector's eyes tire
- **Inconsistent** → Different inspectors may judge differently
- **Expensive** → You need 50 inspectors for 100 cars/day

Now install a **robotic inspection system** with sensors, cameras, and pre-programmed checklists:

- The robot inspects every car in **5 minutes**
- It never gets tired
- It applies **identical** standards
- You only need **2 human supervisors**

**Test automation is exactly this robotic inspector for software.** Instead of humans running the same test cases 100 times during regression, a script does it perfectly every time.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Standard Metrics)

- **Automation Coverage (AC)** = percentage of test cases that are automated
- **Defect Detection Efficiency (DDE)** = defects found by automation / total defects
- **Mean Time To Test (MTTT)** = average time to execute one automated test
- **Tool Reliability** = successful runs / total runs (target: **≥ 99%**)
- **ROI Threshold** = industry standard breakeven is typically **within 3 release cycles**

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often credit students who explicitly mention that **"automation does NOT replace manual testing"** — it augments it. The famous 80/20 rule (80% of defects come from 20% of features) still applies to automation candidate selection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Automate? — The Strategic Logic

- **Repeatability** → The same script runs the same way on every build.
- **Speed** → A regression suite of 500 cases that takes 2 weeks manually may take 4 hours automated.
- **Reusability** → The same script works across multiple versions, OS, and browsers.
- **Data-Driven Capability** → One script can run with thousands of input combinations.
- **Continuous Integration Enablement** → Triggers automatically on every commit (e.g., Jenkins, GitHub Actions).

### 2.2 Classification of Testing Tools

Tools are classified along **six orthogonal dimensions**:

| Dimension | Categories | Example Tools |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **By Activity Supported** | Test Management, Test Design, Test Execution, Defect Tracking | JIRA, TestRail, Selenium |
| **By Testing Type** | Functional, Performance, Security, API, UI | JMeter, Postman, OWASP ZAP |
| **By License Model** | Open-Source, Commercial, Freemium | Selenium (OSS), UFT (Commercial) |
| **By Architecture** | Record-Playback, Script-Based, Model-Based, Keyword-Driven | Selenium WebDriver (Script) |
| **By Execution Layer** | Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance | JUnit, REST Assured, Cypress |
| **By Platform** | Web, Mobile, Desktop, Embedded | Appium (Mobile), Katalon (Cross) |

### 2.3 The Automation Testing Pyramid (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Test Automation Pyramid** (Mike Cohn) is a mandatory KTU 2024 concept. It dictates WHERE to invest automation effort for maximum ROI.

```
                /\
               /  \         ← UI / E2E Tests (10%)
              /----\           Few, slow, brittle
             /      \
            / Integ- \      ← Integration / API Tests (30%)
           /  ration   \       Medium count, medium speed
          /------------\
         /              \
        /    Unit Tests   \  ← Unit Tests (60%)
       /__________________\    Many, fast, stable
```

- **Unit Tests (Base)** → Fast, isolated, written by developers using JUnit, pytest, NUnit.
- **Integration/API Tests (Middle)** → Verify interactions; use Postman, REST Assured, SoapUI.
- **UI/E2E Tests (Top)** → Simulate real user flows; use Selenium, Cypress, Playwright.

### 2.4 Test Automation Framework — Anatomy

A **framework** is a *reusable architectural skeleton* that defines:

1. **Driver Layer** → Browser/device initialization (e.g., ChromeDriver, GeckoDriver)
2. **Page Object Layer** → Encapsulates UI elements (POM — Page Object Model)
3. **Test Data Layer** → External files (Excel, JSON, YAML) for data-driven testing
4. **Test Logic Layer** → Assertions, verifications, test runners
5. **Reporting Layer** → HTML/PDF reports (Extent Reports, Allure, TestNG)
6. **CI/CD Hook Layer** → Integration with Jenkins, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps

### 2.5 Tool Selection Process — KTU 2024 Model

> [!NOTE]
> The **Tool Selection Process** is a 7-step decision framework commonly tested in KTU Module 4. It is derived from the IEEE 829-2008 and ISTQB Foundation guidelines.

1. **Identify the Need** → What testing problem are we solving?
2. **Define Requirements** → Functional, non-functional, integration, budget
3. **Shortlist Candidates** → Market scan, vendor evaluation
4. **Evaluate via POC (Proof of Concept)** → Run a pilot project
5. **Assess Cost-Benefit & ROI** → Quantitative + qualitative analysis
6. **Make Decision** → Selection OR build-in-house OR no automation
7. **Pilot + Rollout** → Phased deployment, training, support

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Cost-Benefit Analysis

| Symbol | Definition | Formula | Unit |
|--------|------------|---------|------|
| $C_{manual}$ | Cost of manual execution per cycle | $C_{manual} = T_{h} \times H_{r} \times N_{tc}$ | Currency |
| $C_{auto}$ | One-time automation cost (tool + dev + training) | $C_{auto} = C_{tool} + C_{dev} + C_{train}$ | Currency |
| $C_{maint}$ | Annual maintenance cost of scripts | $C_{maint} = 0.10$ to $0.30$ of $C_{auto}$ | Currency/yr |
| $T_{h}$ | Average time to execute one test case manually | — | Hours |
| $H_{r}$ | Hourly rate of tester | — | Currency/hr |
| $N_{tc}$ | Number of test cases | — | Count |
| $N_{cyc}$ | Number of execution cycles per year | — | Count |
| $S$ | Annual savings | $S = (C_{manual} \times N_{cyc}) - C_{maint}$ | Currency/yr |
| $ROI$ | Return on Investment (%) | $ROI = \frac{S - C_{auto}}{C_{auto}} \times 100$ | Percent |
| $BP$ | Breakeven Point (cycles) | $BP = \frac{C_{auto}}{C_{manual} - C_{maint}/N_{cyc}}$ | Cycles |
| $AC$ | Automation Coverage | $AC = \frac{N_{auto}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Percent |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical exam pitfall:** In the ROI formula, the $C_{auto}$ is a **one-time** cost, while $C_{maint}$ is **recurring**. Students often mistakenly divide annual savings by annual cost and miss the one-time amortization.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Software** → Automates 10,000+ regulatory test cases for compliance (Basel III, GDPR)
- **E-Commerce** → Automates cart/checkout flows across 50 device-browser combinations
- **Avionics** → Automates DO-178C certification tests
- **Healthcare (FDA)** → Validates safety-critical medical device software

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cost-Benefit Analysis (Complete Working)

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Model):**
A startup has 200 test cases that take **0.5 hours each** to execute manually. The tester rate is **₹600/hour**. The automation suite costs **₹2,00,000** to build, with **₹40,000/year** maintenance. There are **4 release cycles per year**. Find the **annual savings, ROI, and breakeven point**.

**Step 1 — Calculate Manual Cost per Cycle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{manual} &= T_{h} \times H_{r} \times N_{tc} \\
C_{manual} &= 0.5 \;\text{hr} \times 600 \;\text{\text₹/hr} \times 200 \\
C_{manual} &= 60{,}000 \;\text{\text₹ per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* 200 test cases each taking half an hour at ₹600/hr gives 100 tester-hours, multiplied by the hourly rate.

**Step 2 — Calculate Annual Manual Cost (Before Automation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{manual}^{annual} &= C_{manual} \times N_{cyc} \\
C_{manual}^{annual} &= 60{,}000 \times 4 \\
C_{manual}^{annual} &= 2{,}40{,}000 \;\text{\text₹ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Four release cycles per year, each costing ₹60,000 to execute manually.

**Step 3 — Calculate Annual Savings (After Automation)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= (C_{manual} \times N_{cyc}) - C_{maint} \\
S &= 2{,}40{,}000 - 40{,}000 \\
S &= 2{,}00{,}000 \;\text{\text₹ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Automation replaces manual effort entirely, but we still pay ₹40,000/yr to maintain scripts.

**Step 4 — Calculate Return on Investment (Year 1)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI &= \frac{S - C_{auto}}{C_{auto}} \times 100 \\
ROI &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
ROI &= 0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* In Year 1, savings exactly equal the one-time setup cost → **breakeven achieved in exactly 1 year (4 cycles)**.

**Step 5 — Calculate ROI (Year 2 onwards)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI_{y2} &= \frac{S - 0}{C_{auto}} \times 100 \\
ROI_{y2} &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
ROI_{y2} &= 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* From Year 2, the setup cost is sunk — pure profit against the original investment.

**Step 6 — Breakeven Point in Cycles**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{C_{auto}}{C_{manual} - C_{maint}/N_{cyc}} \\
BP &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{60{,}000 - 10{,}000} \\
BP &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} \\
BP &= 4 \;\text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Each cycle now saves ₹50,000 (₹60K manual minus ₹10K amortized maintenance). ₹2,00,000 / ₹50,000 = 4 cycles → **breakeven in exactly 4 cycles or 1 year**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Automation Coverage Calculation

**Problem:** A project has 1,000 total test cases. 320 are automated. Of these, 280 are passing and 40 are flaky. Find the **Automation Coverage** and **Effective Automation Coverage**.

**Step 1 — Automation Coverage (AC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AC &= \frac{N_{auto}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
AC &= \frac{320}{1000} \times 100 \\
AC &= 32\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective Automation Coverage (EAC) — Excluding Flaky Tests**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{N_{auto} - N_{flaky}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
EAC &= \frac{320 - 40}{1000} \times 100 \\
EAC &= 28\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* KTU examiners award bonus marks for recognizing that **flaky tests are unreliable** and should be excluded from coverage metrics.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Automate vs. Manual

| Criterion | Automate ✅ | Manual ✋ |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| Frequency of execution | High (regression, daily) | Low (one-time) |
| Data volume | Large / data-driven | Small |
| Stability of requirement | Stable | Frequently changing |
| Test type | Unit, API, regression, load | Usability, exploratory, ad-hoc |
| Criticality | Mission-critical paths | Edge cases, UX subjective |
| ROI horizon | Pays back in 3 cycles | Never pays back |

### 3.4 Tool Selection Scoring Matrix (Worked Example)

A team scores 4 candidate tools (A, B, C, D) on 5 criteria (1–5 scale, weighted):

| Criterion | Weight | Tool A | Tool B | Tool C | Tool D |
|-----------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Functionality | 0.30 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 4 |
| Ease of use | 0.20 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| Integration | 0.20 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 3 |
| Cost | 0.15 | 2 | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Support | 0.15 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 5 |

**Weighted Score for Tool A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_A &= (5 \times 0.30) + (3 \times 0.20) + (4 \times 0.20) + (2 \times 0.15) + (4 \times 0.15) \\
W_A &= 1.50 + 0.60 + 0.80 + 0.30 + 0.60 \\
W_A &= 3.80
\end{aligned}
$$

Repeat for all tools; the highest score wins. This is a **frequently asked 14-mark problem** in KTU Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Test Automation Framework Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CI["CI/CD Pipeline Layer - Jenkins / GitLab"]
        repoA["Source Code Repository"]
        triggerA["Build Trigger on Commit"]
    end

    subgraph FW["Automation Framework Core"]
        driverA["Driver Layer - ChromeDriver, GeckoDriver"]
        pomA["Page Object Model Layer"]
        dataA["Test Data Layer - Excel / JSON / DB"]
        logicA["Test Logic Layer - TestNG / pytest / JUnit"]
        reportA["Reporting Layer - Allure / Extent Reports"]
    end

    subgraph APP["System Under Test SUT"]
        webA["Web Application"]
        apiA["REST API Endpoints"]
        mobileA["Mobile App - Android / iOS"]
    end

    repoA --> triggerA
    triggerA --> driverA
    driverA --> pomA
    dataA --> logicA
    pomA --> logicA
    logicA --> reportA
    logicA --> webA
    logicA --> apiA
    logicA --> mobileA
    webA --> reportA
    apiA --> reportA
    mobileA --> reportA
```

### 4.2 Tool Selection Process Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["Step 1: Identify Testing Need"] --> s2["Step 2: Define Functional Requirements"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 3: Shortlist Candidate Tools"]
    s3 --> s4["Step 4: Conduct Proof of Concept POC"]
    s4 --> s5{"Step 5: Cost-Benefit and ROI Favorable?"}
    s5 -- Yes --> s6["Step 6: Select Tool and Procure License"]
    s5 -- No --> s7["Step 7: Build In-House OR Reject Automation"]
    s6 --> s8["Step 8: Pilot Rollout on Subset"]
    s8 --> s9["Step 9: Full Deployment and Training"]
    s9 --> s10["Step 10: Continuous Monitoring and Improvement"]
```

### 4.3 Testing Tool Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["Software Testing Tools"]

    root --> t1["Functional Tools"]
    root --> t2["Performance Tools"]
    root --> t3["Security Tools"]
    root --> t4["Test Management Tools"]
    root --> t5["Static Analysis Tools"]

    t1 --> t1a["Selenium"]
    t1 --> t1b["UFT / QTP"]
    t1 --> t1c["Cypress"]
    t1 --> t1d["Playwright"]

    t2 --> t2a["JMeter"]
    t2 --> t2b["LoadRunner"]
    t2 --> t2c["Gatling"]

    t3 --> t3a["OWASP ZAP"]
    t3 --> t3b["Burp Suite"]
    t3 --> t3c["Nessus"]

    t4 --> t4a["JIRA + Zephyr"]
    t4 --> t4b["TestRail"]
    t4 --> t4c["Quality Center ALM"]

    t5 --> t5a["SonarQube"]
    t5 --> t5b["ESLint"]
    t5 --> t5c["PMD"]
```

### 4.4 Automation Decision Flow (Candidate Test Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["Is the test case executed more than 3 times?"] --> q2{"Yes"}
    q1 --> n1["Keep Manual"]
    q2 --> q3["Are requirements stable?"]
    q3 -- No --> n1
    q3 -- Yes --> q4["Is data complex or repeatable?"]
    q4 -- Yes --> q5["Automate - High Priority"]
    q4 -- No --> q6["Automate - Medium Priority"]
    q5 --> done["Add to Automation Suite"]
    q6 --> done
    n1 --> stop["Stop - Manual Execution"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define **Test Automation**. List any **four advantages** of test automation over manual testing.

**Model Answer:**
Test automation is the use of software tools and scripts to execute test cases, compare actual vs. expected outcomes, and generate reports with minimal human intervention.

**Four Advantages:**
- **Speed** — Automated tests run faster than manual execution.
- **Repeatability** — Same test executes identically every time.
- **Reusability** — Scripts can be reused across multiple builds and environments.
- **Accuracy** — Eliminates human errors caused by fatigue or oversight.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** What is the **Test Automation Pyramid**? State the recommended proportion of tests at each layer.

**Model Answer:**
The Test Automation Pyramid (Mike Cohn) is a strategic model that recommends the **distribution of test cases across three layers** to maximize ROI.

| Layer | Recommended % | Purpose |
|-------|--------------|---------|
| Unit Tests (Base) | **60%** | Fast, stable, developer-written |
| Integration / API Tests (Middle) | **30%** | Verify service interactions |
| UI / E2E Tests (Top) | **10%** | Simulate user journeys |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Test Tool Selection Process** in detail. List the criteria used to evaluate testing tools.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain the **Cost-Benefit Analysis** of test automation. Derive the ROI formula.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Test Tool Selection Process** is a structured 7-step methodology for identifying, evaluating, and adopting the most suitable testing tool for an organization.

**Step 1 — Identify the Need:** Clearly articulate the testing problem (e.g., regression taking 2 weeks). Define the scope: which test types, platforms, and lifecycle phases need support.

**Step 2 — Define Requirements:** Categorize requirements into:
- **Functional** → supported test types, scripting language, platform coverage
- **Non-Functional** → performance, scalability, security, ease of use
- **Integration** → compatibility with CI/CD, JIRA, ALM tools
- **Economic** → license cost, training cost, support cost

**Step 3 — Shortlist Candidates:** Scan the market for tools (Selenium, UFT, Katalon, TestComplete). Eliminate those that fail critical requirements.

**Step 4 — Proof of Concept (POC):** Run a 2–4 week pilot on a real project. Measure: ease of scripting, test execution time, defect detection rate, support responsiveness.

**Step 5 — Cost-Benefit Analysis:** Quantify savings (manual cost avoided) vs. costs (tool + dev + training + maintenance). Compute ROI.

**Step 6 — Decision:** Select the tool, build in-house, or defer automation.

**Step 7 — Pilot Rollout:** Phased deployment with team training, documentation, and continuous feedback.

**Evaluation Criteria:**
- **Functionality** — Does it support our test types?
- **Usability** — Learning curve for the team
- **Integration** — CI/CD, ALM, defect tracking
- **Vendor Support** — SLA, documentation, community
- **Cost** — License, training, maintenance
- **Scalability** — Future growth support

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Example:** A company has 500 regression test cases, each taking 0.4 hours manually. Tester rate: ₹500/hr. Cycles per year: 4. Automation setup cost: ₹3,00,000. Maintenance: ₹60,000/yr.

**Step 1 — Manual Cost per Cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{manual} &= 0.4 \times 500 \times 500 = 1{,}00{,}000 \;\text{\text₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Manual Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{manual}^{annual} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times 4 = 4{,}00{,}000 \;\text{\text₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual Savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 4{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000 = 3{,}40{,}000 \;\text{\text₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — ROI Derivation (General Form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI &= \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100 \\
ROI &= \frac{(C_{manual} \times N_{cyc}) - C_{maint} - C_{auto}}{C_{auto}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Breakeven (Cycles):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}000} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{85{,}000} \approx 3.53 \;\text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Automation pays back within **4 cycles (~1 year)** with a **13.3% Year 1 ROI** and **113% Year 2+ ROI**.

---

#### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Page Object Model (POM)** design pattern with a diagram. State its advantages.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Selenium, QTP/UFT, and JMeter** across at least 6 dimensions.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Page Object Model (POM)** is a design pattern in test automation where each **web page** of the application is represented as a **separate class**, and the UI elements (locators) and user interactions (methods) are encapsulated within that class.

**Structure:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    t1["Test Case 1 - LoginTest"] --> p1["Page Object - LoginPage"]
    t2["Test Case 2 - SearchTest"] --> p2["Page Object - SearchPage"]
    t3["Test Case 3 - CheckoutTest"] --> p3["Page Object - CheckoutPage"]
    p1 --> e1["Element: txtUsername"]
    p1 --> e2["Element: btnLogin"]
    p2 --> e3["Element: txtSearch"]
    p3 --> e4["Element: btnPay"]
```

**Example (Conceptual):**
- `LoginPage` class → has methods `enterUsername()`, `enterPassword()`, `clickLogin()`
- `Test_Login` class → calls these methods, no locator hardcoding

**Advantages:**
- **Reusability** → Locators defined once, used many times
- **Maintainability** → UI change requires update in one place
- **Readability** → Tests look like business workflows
- **Reduced Duplication** → No copy-paste of XPath/CSS

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Dimension | Selenium | QTP / UFT | JMeter |
|-----------|----------|-----------|--------|
| **Test Type** | Functional, regression | Functional, regression | Performance, load |
| **License** | Open-source | Commercial (HP/Micro Focus) | Open-source |
| **Language Support** | Java, Python, C#, JS, Ruby | VBScript only | Java (GUI), Beanshell |
| **Platform** | Web only (with Appium for mobile) | Web, Desktop, API | Web protocols (HTTP, FTP, JDBC) |
| **Browser Support** | All major browsers | IE, Chrome, Firefox, Edge | N/A (headless protocol level) |
| **Record-Playback** | Yes (Selenium IDE) | Yes (strong feature) | No |
| **CI/CD Integration** | Excellent (Jenkins, GitHub) | Limited | Excellent |
| **Best Use Case** | Web automation | Enterprise GUI | Load & stress testing |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often **confuse Selenium with TestComplete** or list tools without **justifying** the comparison criterion.
> - **Always state the testing TYPE first** before recommending a tool. Selenium cannot do load testing efficiently — use JMeter.
> - In Cost-Benefit problems, students forget to **amortize** the one-time setup cost across multiple years, which yields **inflated ROI values** and loses 2–3 marks.
> - For POM questions, **draw the diagram** — examiners award 2 marks specifically for the architecture sketch.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Automation and Testing Tools**

- **Test Automation** = Script-driven execution of test cases with minimal human effort.
- **Testing Tools** are categorized by **activity, test type, license, and architecture**.
- **Automation Pyramid**: **60% Unit, 30% Integration, 10% UI** — invert this and you fail the test strategy.
- **POM (Page Object Model)** is the **mandatory** design pattern for scalable Selenium frameworks.
- **Data-Driven Testing** separates test logic from test data (Excel/JSON/DB).
- **Keyword-Driven Testing** uses action words (Click, Type, Verify) — separates technical code from business logic.
- **Tool Selection Process** has **7 steps**: Need → Requirements → Shortlist → POC → Cost-Benefit → Decision → Rollout.
- **ROI Formula**: $ROI = \frac{(C_{manual} \times N_{cyc}) - C_{maint} - C_{auto}}{C_{auto}} \times 100$
- **Breakeven Point**: $BP = \frac{C_{auto}}{C_{manual} - C_{maint}/N_{cyc}}$ cycles.
- **Automation Coverage** $AC = (N_{auto} / N_{total}) \times 100$ — exclude flaky tests for **Effective AC**.
- **Tool Categories to Remember**:
  - Functional: **Selenium, UFT, Cypress, Playwright**
  - Performance: **JMeter, LoadRunner, Gatling**
  - Security: **OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Nessus**
  - Management: **JIRA, TestRail, ALM/Quality Center**
  - Static Analysis: **SonarQube, ESLint, PMD**
- **CI/CD Integration**: Jenkins, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps, GitHub Actions.
- **Golden Rule**: **Automate stable, repetitive, data-intensive, critical-path tests.** Keep exploratory, usability, and ad-hoc tests **manual**.
- **Common Exam Traps**:
  - Confusing **record-playback** tools with **script-based frameworks**
  - Forgetting that **automation finds KNOWN defects** (regression) — not new ones
  - Claiming 100% automation is the goal — it is **not feasible or desirable**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing for Specialized Environment: Testing Object Oriented Software, Testing Web-based Systems, Tracking the Bug: Debugging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT862 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Testing for Specialized Environment: Testing Object Oriented Software, Testing Web-based Systems, Tracking the Bug: Debugging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Testing Object-Oriented Software (OOT)

#### Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Testing Object-Oriented Software (OOT)** is a specialized testing methodology that addresses the unique characteristics of object-oriented (OO) systems—such as **encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and dynamic binding**—by employing techniques that validate class interactions, integration of class hierarchies, and behavioral correctness of reusable components.

The KTU 2024 syllabus positions OOT as a distinct paradigm from conventional procedural testing because:

- **Traditional testing** focuses on functions/modules in isolation.
- **Object-Oriented testing** must validate tightly coupled classes whose behavior changes polymorphically at runtime.

#### Intuitive Analogy: The Lego Castle

Imagine you are building a **Lego Castle** (the software). Conventional testing is like checking each Lego brick individually (does it click? does it fit?). However, Object-Oriented testing is like checking whether a *Turret Brick* (subclass) properly inherits from a *Base Brick* (parent class), can be swapped with another turret of a different shape (polymorphism), and hides its internal connector pins (encapsulation) while still functioning. You cannot test a Lego piece in isolation—you must test how it interacts with the *entire* castle.

---

### 1.2 Testing Web-Based Systems

#### Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Web-based System Testing** is the process of validating distributed, network-dependent, multi-tiered applications accessed via browsers (client-side) and executed on remote servers (server-side), covering dimensions such as **functionality, compatibility, usability, security, performance, and database integrity**.

#### Intuitive Analogy: The Restaurant Website

Imagine ordering food from a **restaurant's website**:
- Does the menu load correctly on **your phone** (compatibility)?
- Does the **payment gateway** securely process your card (security)?
- Does the page load within **2 seconds** even at peak dinner time (performance)?
- Does clicking "Order" correctly update the **inventory database** (database testing)?
- Can you easily navigate from menu → cart → checkout (usability)?

A bug in **any one layer**—HTML, CSS, JavaScript, server logic, or database—breaks the entire experience.

---

### 1.3 Tracking the Bug: Debugging

#### Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Debugging** is the systematic, post-execution process of **locating, analyzing, and removing** the root cause of a defect (bug) identified during testing or operation. It is a diagnostic activity that follows detection, distinct from testing itself.

#### Intuitive Analogy: The Detective

Once a *crime* (defect) is reported (by testing), a **detective** (debugger) arrives at the *crime scene* (the failing module). The detective:
1. **Reproduces** the crime (re-create the failure).
2. **Gathers clues** (logs, traces, dumps).
3. **Forms hypotheses** (induction).
4. **Rules out suspects** (deduction).
5. **Apprehends the culprit** (fixes the bug).
6. **Ensures the crime cannot recur** (regression testing).

Testing says *"something is wrong."* Debugging says *"here is what is wrong, why, and how to fix it."*

---

### 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Glossary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Defect vs. Fault vs. Failure vs. Error — The KTU Quadruple**
>
> | Term | Definition |
> |------|------------|
> | **Error** | Human mistake made during development (syntactic or logic). |
> | **Defect (Bug)** | The encoded representation of an error in software artifacts (code, design, docs). |
> | **Fault** | The activated/manifested defect during execution. |
> | **Failure** | The observable deviation from expected behavior by the end user. |
> | **Severity** | Impact of the defect on system operation (critical, major, minor). |
> | **Priority** | Urgency to fix (high, medium, low). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Object-Oriented Testing Strategies (Hierarchical Layered Approach)

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the following **four-tier OOT strategy**:

#### Tier 1: Testing OO Analysis & Design Models
- Verify **use-case diagrams** for completeness.
- Verify **class diagrams** for proper generalization, aggregation, association, and composition.
- Verify **sequence/collaboration diagrams** for correct message ordering.
- Check **state-chart diagrams** for proper state transitions.

#### Tier 2: Testing Class-Level (Unit Testing in OO Context)
A **class** is the smallest testable unit in OOT (replacing the *function/module* of procedural testing). Testing methods:

**(a) Random Testing**
- Test methods of a class in random order with random inputs.
- Identify **minimum test cases** to cover all methods.

**(b) Partition Testing**
- Divide input domain into equivalence partitions.
- Reduces number of test cases vs. exhaustive testing.

**(c) Hierarchical Testing**
- Test superclass methods → subclass methods → aggregated classes.
- Ensures inherited behavior is correct.

#### Tier 3: Integration Testing in OO Context
- **Thread-based testing**: Integrates classes required to respond to one input/event.
- **Use-based testing**: Begins by testing *independent* classes (those using few server classes), then progresses to *dependent* classes.
- **Cluster testing**: Groups cooperating classes, then tests at the cluster level.

#### Tier 4: System, Regression, and Acceptance Testing
- Validates complete OO application against requirements.
- Regression ensures new class additions do not break inherited behavior.

---

### 2.2 Testing Methods Applicable at Class Level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Methods Triad:**
> 1. **Fault-Based Testing** — Design test cases that intentionally exercise known fault hypotheses (e.g., inheritance-related faults).
> 2. **Class Testing via Equivalence Partitioning** — Use integration testing within a class (intra-class) and across classes (inter-class).
> 3. **Scenario-Based Testing** — Use use-case scenarios to derive test sequences that exercise multiple objects.

---

### 2.3 Web-Based System Testing — Dimensions (The "Big Seven")

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates mastery of these **seven web testing dimensions**:

| # | Dimension | What is Tested | Common Tools |
|---|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Content Testing** | Text, graphics, links, database-derived values | HTML validators, link checkers |
| 2 | **Database Testing** | Data integrity, schema correctness, query performance | SQL validators, DBUnit |
| 3 | **Functional Testing** | Business logic, form processing, transactions | Selenium, QTP/UFT |
| 4 | **Usability Testing** | Navigation, ergonomics, accessibility (WCAG) | User surveys, accessibility scanners |
| 5 | **Security Testing** | Authentication, authorization, SQL injection, XSS | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite |
| 6 | **Performance Testing** | Load, stress, scalability, response time | JMeter, LoadRunner |
| 7 | **Compatibility Testing** | Browser, OS, device, resolution, plug-in | BrowserStack, CrossBrowserTesting |

---

### 2.4 The Bug Life Cycle (Defect Life Cycle)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Bug Life Cycle** (also called the *Defect Life Cycle*) is a state-transition model that describes the stages a defect passes through from its detection to closure.

**Standard KTU State Diagram Stages:**

1. **New** — Defect is logged for the first time.
2. **Assigned** — Bug is assigned to a developer by the lead.
3. **Open** — Developer begins investigation.
4. **Fixed** — Developer writes a patch and commits.
5. **Retest** — Tester re-executes the failing test case.
6. **Verified (Closed)** — Test passes; bug is resolved.
7. **Reopened** — Retest fails; developer must re-fix.
8. **Deferred / Postponed** — Bug acknowledged but scheduled for a later release.
9. **Rejected** — Developer deems it not a bug (with justification).
10. **Duplicate** — Same defect already exists in the system.

---

### 2.5 Debugging Process — Stepwise Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus outlines **5 formal debugging steps**:

1. **Reproduce the Failure** — Create a minimal, reliable test case that consistently triggers the failure.
2. **Diagnose the Cause** — Use scientific methods (induction, deduction) to isolate the root cause.
3. **Fix the Code** — Apply the minimum, targeted code change.
4. **Retest** — Verify the fix passes the original failing case.
5. **Regression Test** — Ensure no other functionality is broken by the fix.

---

### 2.6 Debugging Strategies (The 5 KTU-Mandated Strategies)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Brute Force / Shotgun Debugging**
> Load the program with print statements, memory dumps, or breakpoints wherever suspicion leads. Used when cause is unknown.
>
> **2. Induction (Bottom-Up Reasoning)**
> Observe specific symptoms → infer general cause. Use a *cause-hypothesis loop*: data → hypothesis → verification.
>
> **3. Deduction (Top-Down Reasoning)**
> Start from a general hypothesis → derive specific predictions → verify by test. Often based on *first principles*.
>
> **4. Backtracking**
> Trace backward from the failure point through the code path until the source of incorrect state is found. Effective for small programs.
>
> **5. Testing (Cause-Effect)**
> Design test cases targeting suspected causes; results confirm or refute hypotheses.

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Testing metrics are frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorize the formulas.

| Metric | Formula | Description | Unit |
|--------|---------|-------------|------|
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{N_{defects}}{Size}$ | Defects per unit size (KLOC or function points). | defects/KLOC |
| **Test Effectiveness (TE)** | $TE = \dfrac{B_{test}}{B_{test} + B_{released}} \times 100$ | % of bugs caught before release. | % |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{runtime}}{N_{failures}}$ | Average uptime between failures. | hours |
| **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{repair}}{N_{repairs}}$ | Average time to fix one defect. | hours |
| **Test Coverage (TC)** | $TC = \dfrac{T_{executed}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | % of test cases executed. | % |
| **Code Coverage** | $CC = \dfrac{L_{covered}}{L_{total}} \times 100$ | % of lines/paths/branches exercised. | % |
| **Bug Rejection Ratio** | $BRR = \dfrac{B_{rejected}}{B_{total}}$ | Ratio of invalid bug reports. | dimensionless |

**Where:**
- $N_{defects}$ = total defects found
- $B_{test}$ = bugs caught in testing
- $B_{released}$ = bugs caught post-release
- $T_{runtime}$ = total operational time
- $L_{covered}$ = covered code lines

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OOT** is fundamental in Java/Spring, C++/Qt, Python/Django enterprise systems where polymorphic dispatch creates non-linear defect patterns.
- **Web Testing** underpins CI/CD pipelines in e-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart) and banking (NetBanking), where a single security flaw can leak millions of records.
- **Debugging** is the daily bread of software engineers—Google's internal SRE teams use structured debugging methodologies to maintain 99.999% uptime on services like Search and Gmail.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Class Test Case Reduction Using Random Testing

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation mirrors the **Binder's Random Testing Model** for class-level OO testing—a high-yield KTU topic.

**Problem Statement:** A class has $m$ methods. Each method requires a minimum of $n$ arguments. Determine the **minimum number of test cases** $T_{min}$ to achieve full method coverage.

**Step 1: Define the Input Space**

Each test instance is a tuple $(obj, m_i, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)$ where:

- $obj$ is the instantiated object.
- $m_i$ is the method being invoked.
- $a_j$ is the $j$-th argument to $m_i$.

**Step 2: Total Combinatorial Possibilities**

The total number of random test cases possible is:

$$
T_{total} = M \times A^n
$$

where:
- $M$ = number of methods
- $A$ = domain size of each argument
- $n$ = number of arguments per method call

**Step 3: Apply Operation Sequence Simplification**

For each test case, only **one method** is invoked per call (object-level). To cover all $M$ methods, we need at minimum:

$$
T_{min} \ge M
$$

**Step 4: Account for State Mutations**

Each method call may change the object's state. To exercise all state transitions, the **operation sequence length** $L$ must satisfy:

$$
L \ge M
$$

**Step 5: Final Formula (Binder Reduction)**

$$
T_{min} = M + \lceil \log_2(S) \rceil
$$

where $S$ = number of distinct object states.

**Numerical Example:**

> A class has $M = 8$ methods and $S = 16$ distinct object states.

$$
T_{min} = 8 + \lceil \log_2(16) \rceil = 8 + 4 = 12 \text{ test cases}
$$

Compared to exhaustive testing $T_{total} = 8 \times A^n$ (which could be millions), this is a **99.99%+ reduction**.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Bug Severity-Priority Matrix (Analytical)

The KTU 2024 scheme requires mapping defects onto a **2×2 Severity-Priority Matrix**.

**Step 1:** Classify the **Severity** based on impact:
- **Critical (S1)** — System crash, data loss.
- **Major (S2)** — Major function fails; no workaround.
- **Minor (S3)** — Minor function fails; workaround exists.
- **Cosmetic (S4)** — UI/typo issues only.

**Step 2:** Classify the **Priority** based on business urgency:
- **High (P1)** — Must fix before release.
- **Medium (P2)** — Should fix; may defer to next release.
- **Low (P3)** — Nice to have; cosmetic backlog.

**Step 3:** Plot onto the matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Defect Category} &= f(\text{Severity}, \text{Priority}) \\
\text{Cell}_{(S_i, P_j)} &\in \{(S1P1), (S1P2), \ldots, (S4P3)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **KTU Default Triage Rule**:

| Cell | Typical Action |
|------|----------------|
| S1P1 | Fix immediately; block release. |
| S1P2 | Fix in current sprint; escalate. |
| S2P1 | Fix before next release. |
| S2P2 | Fix in next release. |
| S3P3 | Cosmetic backlog. |
| S4P3 | Documentation ticket. |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Calculating Defect Density for a Web Application

**Scenario:** A web application is **45,000 LOC** in size. During system testing, **180 defects** are logged. After release, **20 additional defects** are reported by users in the first 3 months.

**Step 1: Compute Defect Density (Pre-Release)**

$$
DD_{pre} = \frac{N_{defects}}{KLOC} = \frac{180}{45} = 4.0 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Test Effectiveness**

$$
TE = \frac{B_{test}}{B_{test} + B_{released}} \times 100 = \frac{180}{180 + 20} \times 100
$$

$$
TE = \frac{180}{200} \times 100 = 90\%
$$

**Step 3: Compute Test Coverage**

Assume **320 test cases** exist; **288 were executed**.

$$
TC = \frac{T_{executed}}{T_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{288}{320} \times 100 = 90\%
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Matrix:**
> - $DD = 4.0$/KLOC is **above industry average** (typical good software: 1-3/KLOC). Indicates weak upstream review.
> - $TE = 90\%$ is **acceptable** (industry standard: >85%).
> - $TC = 90\%$ implies **10% untested paths**—risk of latent bugs.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Debugging Strategy Selection

| Strategy | Best Used When | Skill Required | Cost |
|----------|---------------|----------------|------|
| Brute Force | Cause unknown; small program | Low | High (instrumentation overhead) |
| Induction | Symptoms observable; data available | Medium | Medium |
| Deduction | Strong domain knowledge | High | Low |
| Backtracking | Linear code; recent failure | Low | Low (small programs only) |
| Testing/Hypothesis | Suspected causes; available test infra | High | Medium |

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Effectiveness of Random Class Testing (Statistical Confidence)

**Goal:** Determine probability that random testing catches at least one fault.

Given:
- $p$ = probability a randomly selected test triggers the fault.
- $T$ = number of random test cases run.

**Step 1:** Probability that a single test does **not** trigger the fault:

$$
P_{miss,1} = 1 - p
$$

**Step 2:** Probability that **all T** tests miss the fault:

$$
P_{miss,all} = (1 - p)^T
$$

**Step 3:** Probability that **at least one** test catches the fault:

$$
P_{catch} = 1 - (1 - p)^T
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical Example:

If $p = 0.05$ and $T = 50$:

$$
P_{catch} = 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{50} = 1 - (0.95)^{50}
$$

$$
P_{catch} = 1 - 0.0769 = 0.9231 = 92.31\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Doubling $T$ to 100 raises $P_{catch}$ to **99.40%** — a classic diminishing-returns tradeoff in OO testing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bug Life Cycle — State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : Defect Detected
    New --> Assigned : Lead Triage
    Assigned --> Open : Developer Investigates
    Open --> Fixed : Code Patched
    Fixed --> Retest : Tester Re-runs Case
    Retest --> Verified : Test Passes
    Retest --> Reopened : Test Fails
    Verified --> Closed : Marked Resolved
    Reopened --> Open : Re-assigned
    New --> Rejected : Not a Bug
    New --> Duplicate : Already Logged
    New --> Deferred : Postponed
    Closed --> [*]
    Rejected --> [*]
    Duplicate --> [*]
    Deferred --> [*]
```

---

### 4.2 Object-Oriented Testing Strategy — Pyramid Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Testing\nOO Application vs Requirements] --> B[Regression Testing\nNew Classes vs Inherited]
    B --> C[Integration Testing\nThread-based / Use-based / Cluster]
    C --> D[Class Testing\nRandom / Partition / Hierarchical]
    D --> E[OO Design Verification\nClass, Sequence, State Diagrams]
    E --> F[OO Analysis Verification\nUse-Case Models]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style D fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style E fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style F fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.3 Web Application Testing — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client[Client Browser\nHTML CSS JS] --> FrontEnd[Front-End Layer]
    FrontEnd --> Validation[Input Validation]
    Validation --> Business[Business Logic Layer]
    Business --> DB[Database Layer\nSQL NoSQL]

    subgraph TestDimensions[The Big Seven Dimensions]
        T1[Content Testing]
        T2[Functional Testing]
        T3[Usability Testing]
        T4[Security Testing]
        T5[Performance Testing]
        T6[Compatibility Testing]
        T7[Database Testing]
    end

    Client -.-> T1
    Client -.-> T3
    FrontEnd -.-> T4
    FrontEnd -.-> T6
    Business -.-> T2
    Business -.-> T5
    DB -.-> T7

    style Client fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style Business fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style DB fill:#2ecc71,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.4 Debugging Process — Closed-Loop Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Bug Detected\nby Testing]) --> R1[Step 1: Reproduce Failure\nMinimal Test Case]
    R1 --> R2{Reproducible?}
    R2 -- No --> R1
    R2 -- Yes --> D1[Step 2: Diagnose Cause\nInduction / Deduction]
    D1 --> D2[Step 3: Apply Fix\nTargeted Code Change]
    D2 --> D3[Step 4: Retest Original Case]
    D3 --> D4{Fixed?}
    D4 -- No --> R1
    D4 -- Yes --> D5[Step 5: Regression Testing\nFull Suite Re-run]
    D5 --> D6{No Regression?}
    D6 -- No --> R1
    D6 -- Yes --> End([Bug Closed])

    style Start fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style End fill:#27ae60,color:#fff
    style D1 fill:#f39c12,color:#fff
    style D5 fill:#3498db,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.5 Defect Severity-Priority Triage Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CriticalHigh[Critical / High]
        A1[System Crash\nData Loss]
    end
    subgraph CriticalMedium[Critical / Medium]
        B1[Major Function\nFails No Workaround]
    end
    subgraph MajorHigh[Major / High]
        C1[Function Fails\nWorkaround Exists]
    end
    subgraph MinorLow[Minor / Low]
        D1[Cosmetic\nUI Typo]
    end

    A1 --> Fix1[Block Release\nFix Immediately]
    B1 --> Fix2[Fix in Sprint\nEscalate]
    C1 --> Fix3[Fix in Next\nRelease]
    D1 --> Fix4[Backlog Item]

    style A1 fill:#c0392b,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#e67e22,color:#fff
    style C1 fill:#f1c40f,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#16a085,color:#fff
```

---

### 4.6 Class Testing — Method Coverage Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Input Domain of Class] --> Partition[Equivalence Partitioning]
    Input --> Random[Random Test Generation]
    Input --> Hier[Hierarchical Inheritance Walk]

    Partition --> Cases[Reduced Test Set]
    Random --> Cases
    Hier --> Cases

    Cases --> Execute[Execute on Class Instance]
    Execute --> Oracle[Compare Against Oracle]
    Oracle --> Pass{Pass?}
    Pass -- Yes --> Close[Test Case Closed]
    Pass -- No --> Bug[Log Bug\nReturn to Debugging]

    style Input fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style Cases fill:#9b59b6,color:#fff
    style Oracle fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Differentiate between Fault, Error, Failure, and Defect in software testing. Provide one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction (KTU Standard):**
>
> | Term | Meaning | Example |
> |------|---------|---------|
> | **Error** | A human mistake in code or design. | A developer writes `if (x = 5)` (using assignment `=` instead of comparison `==`). |
> | **Defect (Bug)** | The encoded result of an error in a software artifact. | The committed source code containing `if (x = 5)`. |
> | **Fault** | A defect activated at runtime, causing incorrect program state. | When the compiled code runs, the assignment `x = 5` *overwrites* `x` every iteration. |
> | **Failure** | The externally observable deviation from expected behavior. | The `while` loop never terminates, and the program hangs. |

> All four terms are **causally linked**: Error → Defect → Fault → Failure.

**[Marks Allocation: 3 = 1 (definition table) + 1 (example table) + 1 (causal chain explanation)]**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model Question]**

**List and briefly explain any THREE testing methods applicable at the class level in object-oriented software.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Class-Level Testing Methods:**
>
> **1. Random Testing**
> Generates random method-call sequences with random input arguments. The objective is to minimize the test set while maximizing method coverage. **Binder's formula** $T_{min} = M + \lceil \log_2 S \rceil$ gives the minimum test cases required.
>
> **2. Partition Testing**
> The input domain of each method is divided into **equivalence partitions** (valid, invalid, boundary). One representative value per partition reduces the test set substantially while preserving fault-detection power.
>
> **3. Hierarchical Testing**
> Tests follow the **inheritance hierarchy** — first the superclass methods are tested, then subclass-specific methods, then methods that exercise inherited + overridden behavior. This catches inheritance-specific faults like *omitted method binding* and *incorrect polymorphic dispatch*.

**[Marks Allocation: 3 = 1 (each method explanation)]**

### PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **complete bug life cycle**. Discuss the significance of each state in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **five debugging strategies** prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus. For each strategy, state one **engineering scenario** where it is most suitable. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) Bug Life Cycle — Detailed Explanation [7 Marks]**

**Diagram (Mermaid State Machine):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> New : Defect Logged
    New --> Assigned : Triage by Lead
    Assigned --> Open : Developer Starts
    Open --> Fixed : Patch Applied
    Fixed --> Retest : Re-execution
    Retest --> Verified : Pass
    Retest --> Reopened : Fail
    Verified --> Closed
    Reopened --> Open
    New --> Rejected : Not a Bug
    New --> Duplicate : Exists Already
    New --> Deferred : Postponed
    Closed --> [*]
    Rejected --> [*]
    Duplicate --> [*]
    Deferred --> [*]
```

**Significance of Each State:**

| State | Significance |
|-------|--------------|
| **New** | Initial state — defect is formally reported in the tracking tool (e.g., JIRA, Bugzilla). Cannot be acted upon until triaged. |
| **Assigned** | Bug is assigned to a developer by the lead/test manager with a deadline (SLA). |
| **Open** | Developer actively investigates the root cause using debugging strategies. |
| **Fixed** | Developer has committed a code patch; awaiting verification. |
| **Retest** | Tester re-runs the originally failing test case against the patched build. |
| **Verified** | Retest passed — bug is resolved. |
| **Reopened** | Retest failed — the fix did not address the root cause; sent back to developer. |
| **Closed** | Bug is fully resolved and accepted; no further action required. |
| **Rejected** | Developer/lead rejects the bug with justification (e.g., *"works as designed"*). |
| **Duplicate** | An identical defect is already logged elsewhere; the new ticket is closed as a copy. |
| **Deferred** | Acknowledged but scheduled for a future release due to time/risk constraints. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Rejected** with **Deferred**. Rejected = *Not a defect at all*; Deferred = *Genuine defect, but postponed*.

---

**(b) Five Debugging Strategies — Detailed Explanation [7 Marks]**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Brute Force (Shotgun) Debugging — [1.5 Marks]**
> The developer instruments the code with `printf` statements, breakpoints, or memory dumps at suspicious locations and observes the runtime values. **Scenario:** A legacy C program with no documentation crashes intermittently; cause unknown. The developer floods `printf` calls in suspect modules to narrow down the crash point.

> **2. Induction (Bottom-Up) Debugging — [1.5 Marks]**
> Specific symptoms (data values, log entries) are observed → a general hypothesis is proposed → the hypothesis is verified by additional tests. **Scenario:** A web application returns *"500 Internal Server Error"* whenever a user uploads a file >2MB. By inducing from log entries showing memory spikes, the developer hypothesizes an *insufficient buffer allocation*.

> **3. Deduction (Top-Down) Debugging — [1.5 Marks]**
> A general hypothesis is proposed from first principles → specific predictions are derived → verified by targeted tests. **Scenario:** Based on knowledge of the Java Memory Model, a developer deduces that a `NullPointerException` must arise from a non-volatile flag being read without synchronization in a multithreaded context.

> **4. Backtracking — [1 Mark]**
> The developer traces backward from the failure point through the call stack until the source of the incorrect state is found. **Scenario:** A small embedded C program (~500 LOC) outputs `42` instead of `48`. Tracing back from `printf` through 4 function calls locates the `*` vs `+` typo.

> **5. Cause-Effect (Testing-Based) Debugging — [1.5 Marks]**
> Test cases are systematically designed to test hypotheses about causes. **Scenario:** A database returns wrong row counts. The developer writes specific SQL test queries targeting suspected index corruption; results confirm the cause.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Students frequently forget to **state the scenario** for each strategy. Without an application context, marks are deducted (typically 0.5 per strategy). Always tie the strategy to a real engineering situation.

**(a)** Explain the **seven dimensions of web-based system testing** prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus. For each dimension, mention **two specific test techniques** and **one tool** commonly used. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) Seven Dimensions of Web-Based System Testing [7 Marks]**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Seven" with Techniques & Tools:**
>
> **1. Content Testing — [1 Mark]**
> Validates textual, graphical, and structural correctness of web pages.
> - Techniques: *Link checking, spelling/grammar verification, HTML validation, alt-text audit.*
> - Tool: **W3C Markup Validator** / *LinkChecker*.
>
> **2. Database Testing — [1 Mark]**
> Validates data integrity, schema correctness, query response.
> - Techniques: *ACID property tests, schema mapping verification, query performance benchmarking.*
> - Tool: **DBUnit / SQLVal**.
>
> **3. Functional Testing — [1 Mark]**
> Validates business logic correctness against specifications.
> - Techniques: *Black-box functional testing, form validation, workflow testing.*
> - Tool: **Selenium / QTP**.
>
> **4. Usability Testing — [1 Mark]**
> Validates ease-of-use, navigation, accessibility (WCAG compliance).
> - Techniques: *Heuristic evaluation, user surveys, accessibility scans.*
> - Tool: **WAVE / Axe DevTools**.
>
> **5. Security Testing — [1 Mark]**
> Validates authentication, authorization, encryption, and resistance to attacks.
> - Techniques: *Penetration testing, SQL injection tests, XSS tests, session hijacking simulation.*
> - Tool: **OWASP ZAP / Burp Suite**.
>
> **6. Performance Testing — [1 Mark]**
> Validates response time, throughput, scalability under load.
> - Techniques: *Load tests, stress tests, soak/endurance tests, spike tests.*
> - Tool: **Apache JMeter / LoadRunner**.
>
> **7. Compatibility Testing — [1 Mark]**
> Validates correct behavior across browsers, OS, and devices.
> - Techniques: *Cross-browser testing, cross-device testing, plug-in compatibility, resolution testing.*
> - Tool: **BrowserStack / CrossBrowserTesting**.

**(b) Object-Oriented Testing Strategy — With Diagram [7 Marks]**

**Diagram (Mermaid Pyramid):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S4["Level 4: System & Regression Testing\nWhole application vs SRS"]
    S3["Level 3: Integration Testing\nThread-based / Use-based / Cluster"]
    S2["Level 2: Class Testing\nFault-based / Scenario-based / Random"]
    S1["Level 1: OO Model Verification\nClass, Sequence, State Diagrams"]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4

    style S1 fill:#8c564b,color:#fff
    style S2 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
```

**Detailed Explanation of Each Level [4 Marks]:**

- **Level 1 — OO Model Verification:** Before code is written, the OO *models* themselves are reviewed. Correctness of class diagrams, sequence diagrams, and state-chart diagrams is verified against the use-case specification.
- **Level 2 — Class Testing:** Each class is the smallest testable unit. Methods are exercised using fault-based, scenario-based, or random testing. **State coverage** and **method coverage** are key metrics.
- **Level 3 — Integration Testing:** Classes are integrated using *thread-based* testing (tests one thread of system response from input to output), *use-based* testing (tests independent classes first, then dependent), or *cluster* testing (tests cooperating classes).
- **Level 4 — System & Regression Testing:** The complete OO application is tested against requirements; regression ensures new additions do not break existing (especially inherited) behavior.

**Integration Testing in OO vs Conventional Systems — Differences [3 Marks]:**

| Aspect | Conventional Systems | OO Systems |
|--------|---------------------|------------|
| **Smallest unit** | Function / module | Class |
| **Control flow** | Top-down / Bottom-up | Thread-based / Use-based |
| **Test driver** | Stubs and drivers needed | Polymorphic messages can substitute |
| **Reuse** | Function call reuse | Inheritance + dynamic binding |
| **Failure pattern** | Localized | Can cascade via polymorphism |
| **Regression scope** | Module-level | Inheritance chain must be re-tested |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently **omit the inheritance-based regression consideration**. In OO systems, a fix in a superclass requires regression testing of **all subclasses**, which is a unique OO concern absent in conventional testing.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**
>
> - **OOT Strategy Pyramid:** Analysis → Class Testing → Integration → System/Regression.
> - **Class Testing Methods:** Random (Binder's formula), Partition (equivalence), Hierarchical (inheritance walk).
> - **OO Integration Methods:** Thread-based, Use-based, Cluster testing.
> - **OO Fault Sources:** Encapsulation leaks, inheritance misuse, polymorphic ambiguity, dynamic binding errors.
> - **Web Testing Big Seven:** Content, Database, Functional, Usability, Security, Performance, Compatibility.
> - **Web Test Plan Sections:** Test environment, test scope, schedule, deliverables, entry/exit criteria.
> - **Bug Life Cycle States:** New → Assigned → Open → Fixed → Retest → Verified → Closed (with branches: Rejected, Duplicate, Deferred, Reopened).
> - **Defect vs Fault vs Failure:** Error (human) → Defect (artifact) → Fault (activated) → Failure (observed).
> - **Severity vs Priority:** Severity = technical impact; Priority = business urgency. Independent axes.
> - **Debugging 5 Strategies:** Brute Force, Induction, Deduction, Backtracking, Cause-Effect Testing.
> - **Debugging Process:** Reproduce → Diagnose → Fix → Retest → Regression.
> - **Key Metrics:** Defect Density, Test Effectiveness, MTBF, MTTR, Code Coverage.
> - **Binder's Formula:** $T_{min} = M + \lceil \log_2 S \rceil$.
> - **Probability of Detection:** $P_{catch} = 1 - (1-p)^T$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - Web Dimensions: **"C-D-F-U-S-P-C"** = Content, Database, Functional, Usability, Security, Performance, Compatibility.
> - Debugging Strategies: **"BID-Back"** = Brute force, Induction, Deduction, Backtracking, (Cause-Effect) Testing.
> - Bug States: **"N-A-O-F-R-V-C"** = New, Assigned, Open, Fixed, Retest, Verified, Closed.
